notes/9/note_text:
Session 10

Parent/Caregiver Report:
- Mother stated that speech overall seems easier to understand for familiar listeners, mainly due to the clearer /f/ sounds. She feels /s/ and word endings are "about the same" as last week. Child recently started a new preschool routine; no concerns reported from teachers yet.

Behaviour/Engagement:
- Child initially talked a lot about preschool but settled into therapy tasks with minimal support. Remained engaged in both table-top and floor activities. Showed good willingness to attempt harder words when encouraged.

Goals / Therapy Activities:
1. /f/ in sentences and conversation
   - Continued practice of /f/ in connected speech. Child maintained high accuracy with /f/ across initial, medial, and final positions in sentences. Very little cueing was required, and errors were infrequent. Performance remained stable.

2. Initial /s/ in words and short phrases
   - Practiced a familiar set of /s/ words and added a few new ones ("sun, soup, sock, seal, sand, soap, seat"). The child showed similar accuracy to the previous session, with some correct productions and some distortions or substitutions. Cueing level remained about the same; visual and verbal reminders continued to be beneficial.

3. Initial /s/ in structured play (emerging spontaneous use)
   - Embedded /s/ targets into a pretend play scenario (e.g., setting up a "sand" area, washing with "soap"). Child produced a few clearer /s/ words spontaneously but also reverted to less accurate productions. No significant shift in independence was noted this week.

4. Final consonant production in CVC words
   - Continued with CVC words and short phrases emphasizing final /p, m, t/. The child’s performance was comparable to recent sessions: increased success with final consonants in imitation, but spontaneous use of final sounds remained limited. No major change in overall pattern.

Plan / Recommendations:
- Maintain focus on consolidating /s/ at word and short phrase levels while continuing to monitor stable /f/ skills.
- Continue final consonant work in highly functional words that are relevant at preschool (e.g., "hat," "cup," "bus").
- Parent encouraged to check in with preschool staff about any intelligibility concerns and to share strategies (e.g., slowing down, repeating key words) if needed.
notes/10/note_text:
Session 11

Parent/Caregiver Report:
- Mother reported that preschool staff have mentioned that the child is generally understandable but sometimes "drops the ends" of words. They also occasionally notice unclear /s/ sounds. Mother feels skills are "holding steady" since last week.

Behaviour/Engagement:
- Child came in with good energy and quickly engaged in pretend play. Cooperative throughout the session with only brief redirection needed. Enjoyed using a mirror to "watch" sounds.

Goals / Therapy Activities:
1. /f/ in conversation and carryover tasks
   - Used open-ended conversation and picture description tasks. Child continued to produce /f/ accurately in most contexts without cues. Occasional slips occurred but did not significantly affect intelligibility. Performance stable compared to prior weeks.

2. Initial /s/ in words and short phrases
   - Practiced a mix of familiar and new /s/ words ("sun, soup, sock, seal, sand, seat, soup, sad"). Accuracy remained similar to the last session. Child responded well when reminded to place the tongue "behind the teeth," but still needed these cues fairly often. Phrase-level accuracy ("my sock," "see sun") was variable.

3. Initial /s/ in structured play
   - Embedded /s/ targets into a board game and pretend play. Child produced some spontaneous /s/ words with clearer articulation, but these were interspersed with less accurate productions. Overall level of independence was consistent with recent weeks.

4. Final consonant production in CVC words and short phrases
   - Continued focus on final /p, m, t/ in high-frequency words. Child could produce final consonants reliably in imitation within structured tasks but omitted them more frequently when shifting to brief phrases or unstructured play. Error patterns and cueing needs were stable.

Plan / Recommendations:
- Continue with current targets, emphasizing consistent placement cues for /s/ and gradual increase in phrase-level practice.
- Maintain collaboration with preschool staff to support carryover (e.g., modeling clear /s/ and final sounds during small group activities).
- Parent encouraged to continue brief, fun practice at home and to reinforce when the child uses clearer /s/ or final consonants in everyday situations.
notes/11/note_text:
Session 12

Parent/Caregiver Report:
- Mother reported that the child’s speech remains easier to understand than at the start of the block, particularly with /f/. She notes that /s/ and final consonants are still "a work in progress" but feels the child is more aware of sounds. No new concerns.

Behaviour/Engagement:
- Child was relaxed and talkative, transitioning easily into therapy activities. Participated well in both structured and play-based tasks. Demonstrated good rapport with the clinician and responded well to feedback.

Goals / Therapy Activities:
1. Review of /f/ across word positions in conversation
   - Conducted a review of /f/ in initial, medial, and final positions during picture description and conversational tasks. Child maintained strong accuracy for /f/ with minimal cueing required. Occasional errors were self-corrected. This skill appears well established at this time.

2. Initial /s/ in words and short phrases
   - Revisited core /s/ words and short phrases ("sun, soup, sock, seal, sand," "see sun," "my sock"). Performance was consistent with recent sessions: a mix of accurate and distorted /s/ productions, with better accuracy when the child was attentive and used placement cues. Still needs regular reminders for tongue position but is increasingly tolerant of cueing.

3. Initial /s/ in simple sentences (imitation)
   - Introduced short sentences with /s/ in initial position (e.g., "The sun is hot," "I see soup"). Child could imitate these sentences with fair accuracy when provided with a clear model and slowed rate. Independence at the sentence level is not yet established, but the child managed several recognizable productions.

4. Final consonant production in functional CVC words
   - Targeted final /p, m, t/ in functional words used at home and preschool ("cup, nap, mom, game, hat, seat"). In imitation, the child produced many accurate final consonants. Spontaneous carryover remains limited but awareness of final sounds appears improved, as the child occasionally attempted to "add the end" when cued.

Plan / Recommendations:
- Recommend continued therapy to further develop /s/ accuracy and final consonant production, with a focus on increasing independence at the phrase and sentence levels.
- Provide parent with a short list of high-frequency /s/ words and CVC words with final consonants for home practice, emphasizing brief, positive practice times.
- Plan next block to prioritize /s/ refinement and final consonant carryover, while monitoring maintenance of strong /f/ skills in connected speech.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Caregiver shared that they understand therapy is focusing on speech sounds. No major concerns reported this week aside from others occasionally having difficulty understanding the child.
- Parent noted that the child is very talkative at home and uses short sentences (3–4 words).

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned into the therapy room with some initial hesitation but separated from caregiver with reassurance.
- Once engaged with toys, the child was cheerful and cooperative. Attention to adult-directed tasks was variable; required frequent redirection from self-directed play back to speech activities.

Goals / therapy activities:
1. Establish production of /s/ in isolation
- Introduced "snake sound" and visual cue (smiling lips, teeth together, long air). With maximal verbal and visual cues, the child was able to sustain /s/ intermittently but often added a vowel (e.g., "suh").
- Accuracy for clear, isolated /s/ remained low; productions were inconsistent and often backed or distorted.

2. Initial /s/ in CV and VC syllables
- Practiced syllables such as "see, soo, ess, iss" at the imitation level. Child required frequent modeling and visual cueing.
- Some approximations were heard, but the child often substituted /t/ or omitted the sound entirely.

3. Establish production of /f/ in isolation
- Introduced "bunny teeth on lip" cue with mirror. Child was able to place the lower lip under the top teeth with hand-over-hand support.
- Airflow was inconsistent; often produced a stop-like sound (/p/). A few clearer /f/ sounds were heard when the clinician slowed the pace and exaggerated the model.

4. Functional communication observation
- During play, child produced many spontaneous short phrases (e.g., "my turn," "I want that," "go car"). Intelligibility was reduced at times due to sound substitutions and omissions, especially in initial position.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to build awareness of /s/ and /f/ at the isolation and simple syllable levels with heavy cueing.
- Encourage caregiver to model slow, clear "snake" (/s/) and "bunny teeth" (/f/) sounds during daily routines without pressure to copy.
- Plan to continue at the sound and simple syllable level next session before moving to words.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent reported no major changes since the last visit. They continue to notice that familiar family members understand the child better than unfamiliar listeners.
- Caregiver stated they tried some of the "snake" and "bunny teeth" practice at home and the child was willing to watch but did not consistently copy.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child separated from caregiver more easily this week and entered the room holding the clinician's hand.
- Engaged well with turn-taking games and tolerated structured tasks for short periods. Needed breaks between drill activities but returned with reminders.

Goals / therapy activities:
1. /s/ in isolation and simple syllables (CV, VC)
- Reviewed "snake sound" in the mirror. Child produced clearer /s/ in isolation with maximal visual and verbal support; fewer vowel additions than last session.
- In CV syllables ("see, soo, sah"), the child achieved more consistent frication when directly imitating the clinician, though accuracy decreased when cues were faded.

2. Initial /s/ in single words (imitation)
- Trialed a small set of words ("sun, soup, sock, sit") at the imitated level. Child was able to approximate /s/ in initial position in several attempts when given a slow model and visual cue.
- Errors included substituting /t/ for /s/ or omitting the sound. Some emerging correct productions were observed, especially for "sun" and "sock."

3. /f/ in isolation
- Continued use of "bunny teeth on lip" with mirror. The child showed improved ability to place articulators independently after a model, though airflow remained inconsistent.
- A few strong, accurate /f/ sounds were produced when the clinician slowed the pace and provided verbal reminders to "keep the air going."

4. Functional phrases
- During play, child produced multiple spontaneous 3–4 word phrases (e.g., "I want more," "put it here," "go in there"). Errors in consonants persisted but message was generally understood in context.

Plan / recommendations:

- Encourage caregiver to continue short, positive practice opportunities (5 minutes) using a mirror and the "snake" and "bunny teeth" cues.

notes/2/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent reported that the child is starting to notice their own speech, occasionally saying "that funny" or repeating words when not understood.
- Caregiver feels the child is trying harder to be clear but still becomes frustrated when not understood by others.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly and appeared eager to participate, asking for preferred toys.
- Sustained attention to clinician-directed tasks improved; able to stay with table-top speech practice for slightly longer periods with minimal redirection.

Goals / therapy activities:
1. /s/ in isolation and CV/VC syllables
- Child produced more consistent, clear /s/ sounds in isolation with moderate cues (visual plus brief verbal prompt). Less need for maximal support compared to previous sessions.
- In CV syllables ("see, soo, say") and VC syllables ("ess, iss, oss"), accuracy improved with imitation. Child was able to maintain the "snake sound" for several correct attempts in a row before reverting to substitutions.

2. Initial /s/ at the imitated word level
- Target words included "sun, soup, sit, sock, sand." Child produced initial /s/ with improved accuracy in imitation, especially when allowed extra time to set up the articulators.
- Fewer omissions of /s/ were noted. Some spontaneous attempts to self-correct occurred when the child heard an error.

3. /f/ in isolation and CV syllables
- With the mirror and "bunny teeth" cue, the child demonstrated clearer, more consistent /f/ in isolation, needing fewer tactile prompts.
- Began practicing simple CV syllables ("fee, foo, fah") at the imitation level. Accuracy was emerging; the child sometimes reverted to /p/ but was able to correct with a verbal reminder to "keep the air."

4. Functional communication
- During play, child produced multiple spontaneous phrases and short sentences (3–4 words). Intelligibility was slightly improved, particularly when slowing down after clinician reminders.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to strengthen /s/ in syllables and initial word position in imitation, and /f/ in isolation/CV.
- Introduce a small set of familiar /s/ words for repeated practice and begin monitoring for occasional spontaneous use in structured play.
- Caregiver encouraged to reinforce slow, clear speech and praise efforts to self-correct rather than focusing on errors.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent reported noticing more clear "snake" sounds at home during play, especially when they remind the child.
- Caregiver shared that the child sometimes asks, "Is that my snake sound?" indicating increased awareness.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child arrived in a good mood, transitioned independently, and quickly joined the clinician at the table.
- Participated well in both structured drills and play-based tasks. Needed only occasional prompts to stay on task.

Goals / therapy activities:
1. /s/ in initial position at the imitated word level
- Target words: "sun, soup, sock, sand, sit, sad." Child demonstrated increased accuracy in producing initial /s/ when imitating, with less cueing required.
- Able to maintain correct production over short series of trials (3–4 words in a row) with a simple verbal reminder to "make the snake sound" at the start.

2. Initial /s/ in simple carrier phrases (imitation)
- Introduced short phrases such as "my ___," "see ___" using /s/ words (e.g., "see sun," "my sock").
- Child could imitate several phrases with acceptable /s/ production when given a model. Accuracy decreased without a model, but this represents a step up in the hierarchy.

3. /f/ in isolation and initial CV syllables
- Continued practice with mirror and visual cue. Child produced /f/ in isolation with moderate cueing and showed less reliance on tactile support.
- Practiced CV syllables ("fee, foo, fah, fuh") in imitation. Accuracy was variable but improved compared to last week; more consistent frication and fewer /p/ substitutions.

4. General speech intelligibility
- In spontaneous play, the child’s short phrases were often understandable with context. Sound substitutions persisted, but listener understanding appeared slightly improved when the child slowed down.

Plan / recommendations:
- Begin to incorporate more phrase-level practice for initial /s/ while continuing to reinforce accuracy at the word level.
- Maintain focus on /f/ at isolation and simple syllable levels until accuracy is more stable.
- Home program: caregiver to practice a small set of /s/ words in short phrases (e.g., "see sun," "see sock") using a slow, playful model and plenty of praise.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:

- Parent feels speech is a bit clearer in familiar routines (bath time, bedtime stories), though still challenging for new listeners.

Behaviour/engagement:

- Showed good participation in both table-top and floor-based activities. Needed occasional movement breaks but returned to tasks with gentle prompting.

Goals / therapy activities:

- In carrier phrases ("see ___," "my ___"), the child imitated phrases with generally accurate /s/ in the target word, though prosody and speed occasionally affected clarity.

2. Initial /s/ in structured play (supported spontaneous attempts)
- During a picture game and toy play, the clinician modeled and then encouraged the child to name /s/ words (e.g., "sock, sand, soap").
- The child attempted some productions without direct imitation, with mixed accuracy. Some spontaneous correct /s/ productions were noted when the child was focused.

3. /f/ in isolation and CV syllables

- Practiced CV syllables ("fee, foo, fah, foe"). Accuracy was slightly better than previous sessions, though the child still intermittently substituted /p/.

4. Functional communication
- Child used several spontaneous 3–5 word utterances during play (e.g., "put it in there," "I want more bubbles"). Consonant errors remained but did not prevent basic understanding.

Plan / recommendations:

notes/5/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent reported that the child is now sometimes correcting themselves when they hear an error on /s/ (e.g., repeating a word more clearly after a reminder).
- Caregiver noted that teachers at preschool have mentioned slight improvements in clarity during circle time.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly and appeared comfortable with the routine.
- Demonstrated good engagement with speech games and was motivated by earning small rewards (stickers, choosing a game). Occasional silliness but easily redirected.

Goals / therapy activities:
1. Initial /s/ at word and short phrase levels (imitation and emerging independent)
- Child produced initial /s/ in familiar words with good accuracy in imitation and required fewer cues than in previous weeks.
- In short phrases ("see sun," "my sock," "more soup"), the child imitated phrases with relatively consistent /s/ production. When prompted to try without a model, accuracy was emerging but variable.

2. Initial /s/ in structured play (independent attempts)
- Used picture cards and a simple board game to elicit /s/ words. The child attempted to name many /s/ items independently.
- Some correct spontaneous productions were observed; however, the child still frequently reduced or substituted the sound when more excited or speaking quickly.

3. /f/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Continued to practice /f/ in the mirror. The child is becoming more independent in setting up the articulators and maintaining airflow.
- CV syllables ("fee, foo, fah, foe, fun") were produced with mixed accuracy. Self-corrections were observed when the clinician gave a brief reminder to "keep the air going."

4. Generalization to conversation
- In natural play, the child’s overall intelligibility showed slight improvement when using slower, more deliberate speech. Consonant errors persisted, but the child was more responsive to prompts such as "use your snake sound."

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to reinforce accurate /s/ productions at the word and phrase levels and increase opportunities for independent productions in structured activities.
- Maintain current /f/ targets in isolation and syllables until accuracy stabilizes further.
- Caregiver encouraged to use simple verbal prompts ("snake sound," "bunny teeth") and to praise attempts at self-correction rather than perfection.
notes/6/note_text:

Goals / therapy activities:

2. Initial /s/ in structured play
- Used a picture-based game to elicit /s/ words. The child attempted many targets but frequently needed verbal prompts ("remember your snake sound") to maintain accuracy.
- Performance was consistent with previous session; no major change in level of support required.

3. /f/ in isolation and CV syllables

- CV syllables ("fee, foo, fah") showed stable performance; some correct productions and some /p/ substitutions.

4. Overall intelligibility

Plan / recommendations:

notes/7/note_text:

3. /f/ in isolation and CV syllables

4. Functional communication

notes/8/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Caregiver noted that the child is starting to remind them about the "snake sound" during story time, asking to "do my sounds."
- Parent has not noticed a big change in overall clarity this week but feels the child is more interested in practicing.

Behaviour/engagement:

Goals / therapy activities:

- Independent phrase attempts were variable but consistent with recent performance.

2. Initial /s/ in structured play
- Used a turn-taking board game to elicit /s/ words. Child attempted target words independently and responded well to gentle reminders to slow down.

notes/9/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent reported that the child has been practicing /s/ words more regularly this week and seems proud of their "snake sound."
- Caregiver feels that some words are now easier to understand, particularly those practiced frequently (e.g., "sun," "sock").

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly and appeared highly motivated, asking "What game today?" on arrival.
- Demonstrated good sustained attention to structured tasks with minimal redirection and responded positively to praise.

Goals / therapy activities:
1. Initial /s/ at word and phrase levels (increased independence)
- Child produced familiar /s/ words in imitation with high accuracy and needed only occasional verbal reminders.
- In short phrases ("see sun," "my sock," "more soup"), the child imitated with good consistency and was able to produce some phrases independently with acceptable /s/ productions.

2. Initial /s/ in structured play and early conversation
- During a pretend play routine (kitchen set, toy animals), the clinician embedded /s/ words and encouraged the child to use them spontaneously.
- The child produced several independent /s/ words accurately within play (e.g., "soup," "sunny," "sit") and occasionally used the "snake sound" without prompting.

3. /f/ in isolation, CV syllables, and initial word attempts (imitation)
- Child showed improved stability in /f/ production in isolation and CV syllables, requiring slightly fewer cues.
- Introduced a small set of initial /f/ words at the imitated level (e.g., "fish," "feet," "fan"). Accuracy was emerging, with some clear /f/ productions and some continued /p/ substitutions.

notes/10/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent reported that the child is using clearer /s/ in some everyday words ("sock," "sun," "sit") without being reminded, especially during morning routines.
- Caregiver shared that others have commented that the child is a "little easier to understand" recently.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child came into the session with good energy and quickly joined the clinician.
- Maintained attention to activities well, though needed occasional prompts to slow down when excited.

Goals / therapy activities:

notes/11/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Caregiver reported that the child is now often reminding themselves to "do my snake" when saying certain words and appears proud of their progress.
- Parent feels that overall speech is somewhat clearer, particularly for familiar topics, though they still notice errors on less-practiced words.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned independently and demonstrated good rapport with the clinician.
- Participated actively in both structured and play-based tasks. Needed minimal prompting to stay on topic and responded well to visual and verbal feedback.

Goals / therapy activities:
1. Initial /s/ in phrases and structured conversation (independent)
- Child produced familiar /s/ words in short phrases with generally good accuracy, often without needing a direct model.
- In semi-structured conversation about preferred topics (e.g., favorite games, family), the child produced several spontaneous /s/ words accurately. Some errors persisted on novel words, but self-monitoring was evident.

2. Initial /s/ in more varied phrase structures
- Expanded practice to include a wider range of phrase patterns (e.g., "I see ___," "put on ___," "look at ___") with /s/ targets.
- Child imitated these phrases with good accuracy and was able to produce some independently after brief practice.

3. /f/ in isolation, CV syllables, and initial words (continued practice)

- Continued work on initial /f/ words ("fish," "feet," "food," "fun"). Some clear /f/ productions were observed, particularly when the child was reminded to "keep the air going," though consistency remains an area for growth.

notes/0/note_text:
Session 1

Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent shared that they are most concerned about others not understanding child’s speech, especially with new people. Reported that child often leaves off ending sounds and that “s” and “f” are hard to understand. No recent changes in health or hearing.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned with mild hesitation but separated from caregiver with support. Once in the room, engaged well with play-based tasks. Attention span was brief and required frequent redirection, but child was generally cheerful and willing to attempt imitation when given clear models.

Goals / therapy activities:
1) Establish production of early developing consonants in isolation and simple syllables (focus on /p, b, m, t, d/)
- Practiced CV and VC shapes (e.g., “pa,” “bee,” “me,” “up,” “eat”) in play with cars and animals.
- Child could imitate /p, b, m/ in CV syllables with moderate verbal and visual cueing; accuracy was inconsistent and often reduced to open vowels.
- Final consonants were usually omitted even with direct models (e.g., “up” → “uh,” “eat” → “ee”).

2) Introduce awareness of final consonants in simple words
- Used picture cards and a “quiet sound at the end” game (e.g., up, in, out, home).
- Child occasionally produced a final consonant with heavy modeling and simultaneous production but was not yet able to do so independently.
- Noted some awareness when SLP exaggerated final sound; child sometimes smiled and attempted to copy, but accuracy remained low.

3) Initial /f/ in isolation and simple syllables
- Introduced /f/ using mirror, “bite lip, blow air” cue, and visual model.
- Child could blow air but often did not maintain lip placement; productions frequently approximated /h/ or were silent airflow.
- Very few clear /f/ productions despite maximal verbal, visual, and tactile cueing.

4) Initial /s/ in isolation
- Briefly probed /s/ with snake toy and mirror.
- Child tended to substitute /t/ or /d/ for /s/ in attempts, with difficulty maintaining a long “hissy” sound.
- Able to produce a short, inconsistent /s/-like sound when SLP provided hand cue and slowed rate, but this was not yet reliable.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to build consistency for early consonants and increase awareness of final sounds.
- Maintain focus on /f/ and /s/ at isolation and simple syllable level with high support.
- Parent encouraged to model clear final sounds in everyday words (e.g., up, in, out) without asking child to repeat yet, just to increase exposure.
- Next session: re-check /p, b, m, t, d/ in simple syllables; continue shaping /f/ and /s/ in isolation.
notes/1/note_text:
Session 2

Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent noted no major changes since last week. Reported that child is “trying to copy more words” but is still hard to understand, especially at the end of words. No concerns with hearing or behaviour at home.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child came into therapy room more easily today and warmed up quickly with toys. Participated well in structured turn-taking games. Some impulsivity and talking over the clinician, but generally cooperative. Took short breaks between tasks and returned with light prompting.

Goals / therapy activities:
1) Early consonants in CV/VC syllables (/p, b, m, t, d/)
- Repeated practice with picture cards and a pretend picnic activity.
- Child imitated CV forms (e.g., “bee,” “tea,” “moo”) with increased consistency compared to last session; accuracy for initial consonants was generally good in imitation when focused.
- VC forms (e.g., “up,” “eat,” “in”) remained more challenging, but child produced several clearer final consonants with simultaneous production.

2) Final consonant awareness and production in simple words
- Used a “put the sound on” game with minimal pairs (e.g., “bee/beep,” “tea/teeth” where appropriate for age).
- Child produced a few accurate final consonants in imitation (e.g., “up,” “in”) with maximal verbal cueing and visual emphasis on the last sound.
- Still frequently omitted final consonants in spontaneous attempts, but showed more frequent attempts to “add something at the end” when reminded.

3) Initial /f/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Continued mirror work with “bite lip, blow” cue, using a feather to visualize airflow.
- Child produced several approximations that were closer to /f/ today; still often reverted to /h/ or labial closure, but there were more clear fricative sounds than last week.
- Needed consistent modeling and reminders; no independent /f/ yet, but emerging awareness.

4) Initial /s/ in isolation and simple CV syllables
- Practiced /s/ with a toy snake and train, emphasizing “long, quiet snake sound.”
- Child could hold a short /s/-like sound in imitation more often than last session, though productions were brief and sometimes stopped to /t/.
- Able to imitate a few CV syllables (e.g., “see,” “soo”) with partial accuracy when watching SLP’s mouth.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue current targets as child is beginning to show emerging improvements, especially with early consonants and attempts at final sounds.
- Parent encouraged to play simple “sound on/off” games at home (e.g., “up” vs. “uh”) with exaggerated models, no pressure for perfect copying.
- Next session: continue building final consonants at VC level, and consolidate /f/ and /s/ in isolation before moving to more complex syllables.
notes/2/note_text:
Session 3

Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent reported that child has started to occasionally add a “p” or “t” at the end of some words at home (e.g., “up,” “eat”), but this is not consistent. Still worries about strangers understanding child.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly and appeared excited to come in. Remained seated for longer stretches during table activities, with minimal redirection. Showed good willingness to imitate and enjoyed being praised for “strong sounds.”

Goals / therapy activities:
1) Final consonant production in VC words
- Focused on VC items (e.g., up, in, out, eat) using picture cards and a “feed the animal” game where the animal only “ate” the word with the ending sound.
- Child produced final consonants more consistently in imitation today. With moderate verbal and visual cues, many productions were clear enough to distinguish from the open-vowel form.
- Still needed support to carry this over to spontaneous attempts, but accuracy within structured imitation tasks showed a small increase.

2) Initial /f/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Continued mirror practice and lip-biting cues, paired with blowing cotton balls.
- Child produced several clear /f/ sounds in isolation when watching the clinician and following step-by-step instructions.
- Began to imitate a few CV syllables (e.g., “fee,” “fo”) with approximate /f/ at the start. Still inconsistent, but more frequent successful attempts compared to last week.

3) Initial /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Used “snake sound” in a board game, requiring /s/ productions to move pieces.
- Child’s ability to sustain a short /s/ in imitation is emerging; more attempts were audible and closer to target.
- Imitated several CV syllables (e.g., “see,” “soo,” “suh”) with partial accuracy; substitutions (/t/ or /d/) still occurred but slightly less often.

4) Early consonant CV syllables in play
- Practiced /p, b, m, t, d/ in simple CV words within play (e.g., “bee,” “moo,” “tea,” “do”).
- Child maintained fairly good initial consonant accuracy during imitation tasks, though some omissions occurred as play became faster or more spontaneous.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain focus on final consonants at VC level and continue building consistency before moving to full CVC words.
- Continue shaping /f/ and /s/ in CV syllables; accuracy is slowly increasing but not yet stable enough to move up a level.
- Parent encouraged to highlight final consonants in a few routine words (e.g., up, in, out) and to praise any attempts at “end sounds.”
- Next session: re-check VC and simple CVC forms; continue /f/ and /s/ CV practice in play.
notes/3/note_text:
Session 4

Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent shared that child is “using more sounds” and occasionally corrects themselves when leaving off an ending. Still reports that longer sentences are hard to understand. No other concerns today.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered easily and was eager to show toys. Remained engaged in structured games for most of the session, with brief periods of silliness that responded well to gentle redirection. Demonstrated good effort when given specific feedback about “strong ending sounds.”

Goals / therapy activities:
1) Final consonants in VC and emerging CVC words
- Continued VC practice (up, in, out, eat) and introduced simple CVC items (e.g., “cup,” “pop,” “mat”) using a matching game.
- Child produced VC targets with moderate accuracy in imitation, often achieving a clearly audible final consonant.
- In CVC, child frequently produced the initial and vowel but sometimes reduced or omitted the final consonant. With verbal cues and slow rate, several accurate CVC productions were observed.

2) Initial /f/ in CV and CVC words (imitated)
- Practiced /f/ in CV words (fee, four, fun) and a few simple CVC words (e.g., “fat,” “fun”) during a fishing game.
- Child was able to imitate /f/ in CV form with growing consistency when given a visual model and reminder to “bite lip and blow.”
- CVC forms with /f/ were more challenging, but a small number of accurate productions were observed when segmented into parts (e.g., “fuh” + “n” for “fun”).

3) Initial /s/ in CV and early word-level practice
- Used snake-themed picture cards to practice /s/ in CV syllables and simple words (e.g., “see,” “sun,” “sock”) at the imitated level.
- Child produced /s/ more reliably in isolation and short syllables; substitutions to /t/ still occurred but less frequently.
- In simple words, child often produced approximations that were close to /s/, especially when watching SLP’s mouth; accuracy remains variable but improving.

4) Monitoring early consonant accuracy in play
- During free play, SLP listened for /p, b, m, t, d/ in spontaneous words.
- Child’s initial consonants were generally clearer in imitation than in spontaneous speech; some omissions persisted in longer utterances.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to strengthen final consonant production in CVC words and build consistency before advancing to phrases.
- Maintain /f/ and /s/ at word level in imitation, gradually increasing the variety of words.
- Parent encouraged to choose 2–3 practice words with final consonants (e.g., up, in, cup) and practice briefly each day using slow, clear models.
- Next session: continue CVC work for final consonants, and expand /f/ and /s/ word lists as tolerated.
notes/4/note_text:
Session 5

Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent reported that child has begun saying “up” and “out” more clearly at home, especially when reminded. Noted that some new words with /f/ are starting to sound a bit clearer, though still inconsistent.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly and quickly engaged with the chosen game. Demonstrated improved attention span and was able to participate in short drill-like activities between play turns. Responded well to visual supports and verbal praise.

Goals / therapy activities:
1) Final consonants in CVC words (imitated)
- Practiced a set of CVC words with /p, t, m/ in final position (e.g., cup, hat, ham, map) using picture cards and a “treasure hunt” game.
- Child produced clearer final consonants in imitation with moderate cueing, especially when SLP slowed the word and emphasized the end.
- Occasional omissions persisted, but the child showed more frequent accurate productions and some self-corrections when cued to “put the sound on the end.”

2) Initial /f/ in CVC words
- Used a picture game (e.g., “fun,” “fish,” “food”) at the imitated word level.
- Child demonstrated increased success with /f/ at the beginning of words when provided with a visual model and the “bite lip, blow” cue.
- Accuracy remained variable across different words, but there were more consistent approximations that were clearly recognizable as /f/.

3) Initial /s/ in simple CVC words
- Targeted words such as “sun,” “sock,” “sit,” “soap” at the imitated level using a turn-taking board game.
- Child produced several recognizable /s/ productions at the start of words with visual and verbal cues (e.g., “long quiet sound first”).
- Some substitutions (/t/ or /d/) continued, but there was a slight increase in consistent /s/ productions relative to previous sessions.

4) General intelligibility in short phrases (informal observation)
- During structured play, child used short spontaneous phrases (e.g., “my turn,” “I want that,” “more please”).
- Intelligibility for familiar phrases was fair to good when context was known, but final consonant omission and sound substitutions still reduced clarity for unfamiliar listeners.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue working on final consonants in a consistent set of CVC words to build reliability before moving to phrases.
- Maintain /f/ and /s/ practice at the imitated word level; focus on a limited set of high-frequency words.
- Parent encouraged to practice 3–5 CVC words with clear final consonants in short, fun bursts (e.g., during snack or bath time).
- Next session: re-check accuracy for current CVC list and consider introducing simple carrier phrases if consistency continues to improve.
notes/5/note_text:

Goals / therapy activities:
1) Final consonants in CVC words and early carrier phrases

- In short carrier phrases, child often maintained the final consonant when the phrase was slowed and segmented, though accuracy was still less consistent than at the single-word level.

2) Initial /f/ in CVC words and short phrases (imitated)
- Practiced /f/ words (fish, fun, food, foot) and embedded them in simple phrases (e.g., “big fish,” “my foot”) during a picture description task.
- Child produced /f/ in the initial position more consistently than previous weeks; fewer substitutions to /p/ or /h/ were observed.

3) Initial /s/ in CVC words and short phrases

Plan / recommendations:

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother reported that intelligibility is "hit or miss" at home, especially when he is excited. She notices he often leaves off sounds at the ends of words and that unfamiliar listeners frequently ask for repetition. No major changes since the initial evaluation.

Behavior/engagement:
- 4-year-old male. Transitioned to therapy room with brief hesitation but separated from caregiver with reassurance. Engaged well in play-based activities, especially with cars and animal figures. Attention was fair; needed intermittent redirection from self-directed play back to speech tasks. Accepted visual and verbal cues without distress.

Goals / Observations:
1. Final consonant production (CVC words: /p, t, m/)
- Targeted final consonants in simple CVC words (e.g., "cup, cap, cat, mat, map, mom") at the imitated word level.
- Frequently omitted final sounds or replaced them with a neutral vowel (e.g., "cu-" for "cup").
- With simultaneous production and visual cueing (touching mouth, tapping picture), able to mark final consonant in some trials, but accuracy was inconsistent and required maximal cueing.

2. Initial /k/ vs /t/ (fronting)
- Focused on contrasting "back sound" /k/ with "front sound" /t/ in isolation and simple CV syllables ("koo, key, kah") using mirror and tactile cue at throat.
- Tended to substitute /t/ for /k/ in spontaneous attempts (e.g., "tar" for "car").
- In isolation, able to approximate /k/ in several trials with strong verbal modeling and slowed rate; productions remained inconsistent at the syllable level.

3. Initial /s/ in single words
- Introduced initial /s/ in isolation and in a few highly familiar words ("sun, soup, sock") using visual cue for "long, quiet air."
- Frequently produced /d/ or omitted the sound entirely in words.
- Could hold a prolonged /s/ in isolation in a few attempts when watching clinician's mouth, but carryover to words was limited.

4. Syllable shapes (CV, CVCV)
- Observed in play: primarily using CV and CVCV (same vowel) syllable shapes ("mama, dada, nana"). Final consonant deletion noted frequently in spontaneous speech.

Plan / Recommendations:
- Continue to target final consonants in simple CVC words at the imitated level with maximal cues.
- Maintain focus on establishing /k/ in isolation and CV syllables before moving into more complex word forms.
- Provide caregiver with simple home practice: 3–4 picture cards for CVC words emphasizing "putting the sound on the end" with slow, clear models. Encourage short, playful practice rather than drilling.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother reported that he has started trying to "fix" some words when she models them (e.g., she says "cup" and he repeats again more carefully). She continues to notice missing end sounds but feels he is "trying harder" to be understood. No new concerns.

Behavior/engagement:
- Entered room more readily today; greeted clinician with a quiet "hi". Participated well in turn-taking games and tolerated structured table-top tasks for brief periods. Required occasional breaks with preferred toys to maintain engagement. Accepted visual, verbal, and minimal tactile cues.

Goals / Observations:
1. Final consonant production (CVC words: /p, t, m/)
- Practiced final /p, t, m/ in high-interest CVC words ("cup, cap, cat, mat, map, mom, ham") at the imitated word level.
- Compared to last session, he more consistently attempted to add an ending sound rather than omitting it, though accuracy was still variable.
- With visual cue (tapping picture at end of word) and slowed models, he produced perceptible final consonants in a greater proportion of trials. Still required frequent cueing but showed increased awareness and some self-correction attempts.

2. Initial /k/ vs /t/
- Continued work on /k/ in isolation and CV syllables using mirror and "back sound" cue.
- Demonstrated more consistent ability to produce a clear /k/ in isolation when given multiple practice opportunities.
- In CV syllables ("key, car, cow"), still often reverted to /t/, but there were several accurate imitated productions with maximal verbal cueing and slowed rate. Improvement from baseline but not yet stable.

3. Initial /s/ in single words
- Targeted initial /s/ in isolation and in a limited set of words ("sun, soup, sock, seal") at the imitated level.
- Sustained /s/ in isolation more reliably than last session when watching clinician's mouth.
- In words, productions remained inconsistent with frequent /d/ substitutions, but there were a few clearer approximations with combined gestural (finger to lip) and verbal cues.

4. Syllable shapes / word structure
- In spontaneous play, continued use of CV, CVCV, and some CVC words. Final consonant deletion still prominent, though he occasionally marked a final consonant in familiar words ("up, out") when excited.

Plan / Recommendations:
- Continue focusing on final consonants at the imitated CVC word level; reinforce any spontaneous attempts to add end sounds.
- Maintain intensive practice for /k/ in isolation and CV syllables; not yet ready to systematically target at phrase level.
- Provide caregiver with visual cue ideas (tapping at end of word) for home practice with 3–5 CVC words and a few initial /k/ words ("car, key") with slow models.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother shared that he is now saying "cup" and "up" more clearly at home when reminded, and she hears the final /p/ sometimes without cues. She still notices difficulty with "car" and "cat." No changes in health or behavior.

Behavior/engagement:
- Transitioned smoothly into the session, appeared comfortable and curious about materials. Attention to structured tasks was improved; he was able to remain with clinician-led activities for slightly longer periods before seeking toys. Responded well to praise and seemed proud when he perceived himself as "talking clearly."

Goals / Observations:
1. Final consonant production (CVC words: /p, t, m/)
- Targeted a broader set of CVC words at the imitated word level ("cup, cap, cat, coat, mat, map, mom, meat").
- Showed a meaningful increase in accuracy compared to previous sessions, particularly for final /p/ and /m/.
- With moderate verbal cueing and occasional visual cues, he marked final consonants in the majority of trials. Some spontaneous attempts to correct when he omitted an ending sound (e.g., "cu-...cup"). Appears to be consolidating at this level.

2. Initial /k/ in CV and CVC words
- Continued practice of /k/ in isolation and CV syllables, then moved into a small set of CVC words ("key, car, cow, coat") at the imitated level.
- Demonstrated more consistent /k/ in isolation and CV positions, requiring fewer repetitions to achieve a clear back sound.
- In simple words, he produced /k/ accurately in many imitated trials with verbal cueing. Occasional fronting to /t/ persisted, but overall accuracy and confidence improved. Appropriate to continue word-level practice.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and simple words
- Practiced sustained /s/ in isolation, then in target words ("sun, soup, sock, seal, soap") at the imitated word level.
- Isolation /s/ was produced more reliably, with longer duration and better control of airflow.
- Word-level productions still variable; some approximations sounded closer to /s/, while others remained /d/ or omitted. Needed consistent modeling and gestural cues, but he attended well to the clinician's mouth and appeared to understand the difference.

4. General intelligibility / syllable shapes
- In play, used a mix of CV, CVCV, and some clearer CVC forms. Still evident final consonant deletion in spontaneous speech, but more attempts to add endings in highly familiar words.

Plan / Recommendations:
- Continue working on final consonants in CVC words; consider beginning to probe for carryover into short carrier phrases ("my cup," "big cat") if accuracy remains stable.
- Maintain practice of initial /k/ at the imitated word level; monitor readiness to introduce simple two-word combinations with /k/ ("go car").
- Provide caregiver with a short list of CVC practice words and a few /k/ words, encouraging brief daily practice with positive feedback and modeling, not correction-heavy drills.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother reported that family members are noticing he is easier to understand for certain words ("cup, up, mom"). She still sees him dropping end sounds in longer or less familiar words. She also mentioned he starts JK in the fall and would like him to be understood by teachers.

Behavior/engagement:
- Arrived in good spirits and transitioned easily. Highly engaged in a board game and car ramp activities used for turn-taking with speech targets. Needed minimal redirection. Demonstrated good tolerance for repeated practice and enjoyed earning tokens/stickers.

Goals / Observations:
1. Final consonant production in CVC words and short phrases
- Practiced final /p, t, m/ in CVC words ("cup, cap, cat, coat, mat, map, mom, meat") at imitated and then simple carrier phrase level ("my cup," "big cat," "mom's map").
- At the imitated word level, accuracy for marking final consonants was high with light verbal reminders.
- When moved into short phrases, he maintained final consonants in many trials but showed some omissions as phrases became longer or faster. Overall, demonstrated emerging ability to carry final consonants into two-word phrases with moderate cues.

2. Initial /k/ in CVC words and emerging phrases
- Targeted initial /k/ in words ("car, key, coat, cow, cup") at the imitated word level and then in simple two-word combinations ("blue car, my key").
- Accuracy at word level was noticeably improved; fewer substitutions to /t/ and needed less intensive cueing.
- In short phrases, he could maintain /k/ in the initial position in several attempts with verbal modeling and a reminder to use his "back sound." Some reversion to /t/ when excited, but overall trend is positive.

3. Initial /s/ in words
- Continued work on initial /s/ in isolation and words ("sun, soup, sock, seal, soap, sand") at the imitated level.
- Isolation /s/ is now more consistent, with good airflow and minimal distortion when he is focused.
- Word-level productions remain mixed: some clear /s/ productions were heard, but he still often substitutes /d/ or blends /s/ with the following vowel. Required frequent gestural cues and slowed rate.

4. Intelligibility / syllable structure in play
- In spontaneous play, used more recognizable CVC words, especially those practiced in therapy. Final consonant deletion continues for many words, but there is emerging awareness; he occasionally stopped and tried again when not understood.

Plan / Recommendations:
- Continue strengthening final consonant production in both single words and short carrier phrases to increase stability.
- Maintain focus on initial /k/ in words and simple two-word combinations; not yet ready for more complex phrases.
- Keep targeting initial /s/ at the imitated word level; consider introducing minimal pair contrasts (e.g., "see/tea") in future sessions.
- Home practice: encourage parent to use short phrases with known CVC words ("my cup," "big map") and model clear, slow speech, praising any attempts to add final sounds.
notes/4/note_text:

notes/5/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother reported small but noticeable improvements with final sounds in a few everyday words ("cup, bed, up"). She also feels he is "catching himself" more often and trying again when people do not understand him. She would like to know when to start focusing on phrases at home.

Behavior/engagement:
