notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
Mother reported that child is "hard to understand" for unfamiliar listeners and often leaves off sounds at the ends of words. She notices more clarity when the child is talking slowly or repeating after her. No major changes since recent assessment; child starting junior kindergarten in the fall.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child transitioned to the therapy room with brief hesitation, then engaged well in play-based activities. Needed some redirection away from preferred toys but responded to visual schedules and clear expectations. Participated in imitation tasks with good effort and responded well to specific praise.

Goals/activities:
1. Final consonant production (CVC words: /p, m, t/)
- Targeted simple CVC words (e.g., "map, mop, mat, up, cup, mom") at the imitated word level.
- Child frequently omitted final consonants in spontaneous speech (e.g., "ma" for "map"), but with direct imitation and visual cueing (tapping for the last sound), produced clearer final consonants in some trials.
- Accuracy was inconsistent; often added a vowel after the final sound ("map-uh"). Continued to require models and reminders to "stop the sound at the end."

2. Initial /k/ vs /t/
- Practiced initial /k/ in simple words ("key, car, cow") using a "back sound" cue and mirror work.
- Child tended to substitute /t/ for /k/ (e.g., "tea" for "key") on first attempts.
- With maximal verbal and visual cueing (hand to throat, "tongue up and back"), was able to imitate /k/ in isolation in several attempts; carryover to words remained limited.

3. Initial /s/ in single words
- Introduced initial /s/ in isolation and then in a few single words ("sun, soup, sock").
- Child was able to approximate /s/ in isolation when given a "snake sound" cue and encouraged to keep the tongue behind the teeth.
- In words, productions were variable, with some substitutions (e.g., "dun" for "sun"). Required frequent models and visual cues.

Plan/recommendations:
Continue to focus on final consonant production in simple CVC words and establishing consistent /k/ and /s/ at the imitated word level. Parent was shown how to emphasize last sounds in everyday words (e.g., "up, in, out") and encouraged to model slow, clear speech without pressuring the child to repeat. Will reassess readiness to increase complexity once accuracy improves.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
Mother reported that child is "copying more" at home and will sometimes repeat short words when prompted, but final sounds are still often missing. She has been trying the "tap the last sound" strategy and feels the child is starting to notice when words "have an ending."

Behaviour/engagement:
Child entered the room more easily today and quickly engaged with a car and animal set. Attention was generally good for short, structured tasks, with brief breaks for play. Showed good willingness to imitate and enjoyed listening for "silly" vs "right" words.

Goals/activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words (/p, m, t/)
- Revisited CVC words at the imitated word level using picture cards and a simple board game.
- Child showed increased awareness of final sounds, often pausing and attempting to "add the end" after a model.
- Still inconsistent, but more frequent accurate productions of final /p/ and /m/ when provided with a visual cue (tapping or pointing to the final sound picture).

2. Initial /k/ in words
- Practiced /k/ in isolation and in initial position of simple words ("key, car, cow, cake").
- Child produced /k/ in isolation more consistently with a "cough"/"back sound" cue.
- At the word level, substitutions to /t/ still occurred, but there were several clear productions of "key" and "car" after a direct model. Needed moderate to maximal cueing but demonstrated more attempts and fewer refusals.

3. Initial /s/ in words and simple phrases
- Targeted initial /s/ in words ("sun, soup, sock, seat") using a "quiet snake" visual and mirror.
- Child was able to produce /s/ in isolation in most attempts with verbal reminders to "keep teeth together."
- At the word level, accuracy improved slightly compared to last session, with more consistent approximations. Still required frequent modeling; occasional substitution to /d/ or omission of the sound.

Plan/recommendations:
Continue current focus on final consonants and initial /k/ and /s/ at the imitated word level. Accuracy is beginning to emerge but is not yet consistent. Parent encouraged to play short listening games at home ("Do you hear the end in 'up'?") and to keep practice brief and fun. Will consider introducing simple carrier phrases once word-level productions become more reliable.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
Mother shared that the child has been "trying to fix" words when she gives a gentle reminder, especially with words like "up" and "out." She feels speech is "a tiny bit clearer" with close family, though unfamiliar listeners still have difficulty. No new concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child transitioned smoothly and appeared eager to show toys. Attention to table-top tasks improved; tolerated slightly longer practice sets when activities were embedded in games. Showed some self-monitoring, laughing when hearing "silly" productions.

Goals/activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words (/p, m, t, k/)
- Practiced a larger set of CVC words ("map, mop, mat, cup, cut, back, book, mom") at the imitated word level and in short carrier phrases ("my ___").
- Child demonstrated noticeably better consistency with final /p/ and /m" in imitation, often producing the ending without an additional vowel.
- Final /t/ and /k/ remained more challenging, but there were several accurate attempts when given a visual cue (pointing to the final sound icon) and slowed rate.
- Able to maintain some final consonants within simple phrases (e.g., "my cup") with moderate cues.

2. Initial /k/ in words and CV syllables
- Continued work on initial /k/ using CV syllables ("ku, ki, ko") and simple words.
- Child produced /k/ in isolation with good success after initial cueing, needing fewer repetitions.
- In words, substitutions to /t/ decreased slightly; there were multiple clear productions of "key" and "cow" in imitation. Still needed verbal reminders about "back sound," but less physical prompting.

3. Initial /s/ in words and emerging phrases
- Targeted /s/ in initial position of familiar words ("sun, soup, sock, sand, seat") during a picture lotto game.
- Child produced /s/ in isolation consistently when given a brief model.
- At the word level, accuracy improved, with more consistent initial /s/ and fewer substitutions. Some attempts at two-word phrases ("see sun," "see sock") were made in imitation, with partial carryover of /s/.

Plan/recommendations:
Given the child’s increasing consistency with final consonants and initial /s/ and /k/ in imitation, we will continue to strengthen accuracy at the word level and gradually expand to more carrier phrases where appropriate. Parent was encouraged to model short two-word phrases highlighting final sounds (e.g., "big cup") and to praise any clear final consonants, even if the rest of the word is approximate.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
Mother reported that the child is starting to use clearer words when asking for favorite items (e.g., "cup," "pop"), and she sometimes hears a stronger last sound. She feels there has been a "nice jump" in clarity this week, especially when the child is calm and focused.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child was cheerful and highly engaged throughout the session. Participated in structured tasks with minimal redirection needed and willingly attempted multiple repetitions. Demonstrated increased confidence and pride when hearing themselves produce clearer words.

Goals/activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words and short carrier phrases (/p, m, t, k/)
- Practiced a variety of CVC words during a board game and then expanded to carrier phrases such as "I want ___" and "my ___" in imitation.
- Child produced final /p/ and /m/ with good consistency in imitation and was beginning to include these sounds spontaneously within phrases during structured play.
- Final /t/ and /k/ showed noticeable gains; there were many accurate productions in imitation and some self-corrections when the child initially omitted the final sound.

2. Initial /k/ at word and carrier phrase level
- Targeted initial /k/ words ("key, car, cow, cookie, cat") in a picture guessing game.
- Child produced /k/ more reliably in words, with fewer /t/ substitutions. Needed mainly verbal cues rather than intensive visual/tactile prompts.
- Began to use initial /k/ within short imitated phrases ("my car," "big cookie") with moderate success.

3. Initial /s/ in words and two-word combinations
- Practiced /s/ in initial position ("sun, soap, sock, soup, seat") using a turn-taking game.
- Child maintained accurate /s/ in most imitated words and was ready to trial two-word phrases (e.g., "see sun," "soap here").
- Some spontaneous use of /s/ in simple phrases was observed in play (e.g., approximated "see sun" while looking at pictures) with minimal prompting.

Plan/recommendations:
Given the meaningful gains, we will continue to build complexity by using more carrier phrases and short two-word combinations, particularly for final consonants and initial /k/ and /s/. Parent was shown how to model phrases like "my cup" and "big sock" and to emphasize the target sounds in a natural way. Next sessions will focus on consolidating phrase-level accuracy and monitoring for any carryover into spontaneous play.
notes/4/note_text:

Child was cooperative but somewhat more distracted today, frequently shifting between toys. With structured choices and short practice intervals, participation remained adequate. The child continued to enjoy listening games and was receptive to feedback.

- Child maintained similar performance to last session, with relatively strong final /p/ and /m/, and more variable /t/ and /k/.

notes/5/note_text:
Parent report:
Mother reported that the child has started to use clearer short phrases like "my cup" and "big car" at home, sometimes with good final sounds. She feels there is a "little more" clarity overall, although the child still drops sounds when excited.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child arrived in good spirits and transitioned easily. Attention to task was somewhat variable but improved when activities were highly interactive. The child showed more attempts to self-correct and appeared motivated by specific praise for "strong sounds."

Goals/activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words and carrier phrases (/p, m, t, k/)
- Practiced CVC words within carrier phrases ("I want ___," "I see ___") using picture cards.
- Child showed a slight increase in consistency of final /t/ and /k" in imitation compared to last week, with more trials where the final sound was present and clearly articulated.
- Some spontaneous phrase-level productions (e.g., "my cup," "big cap") included accurate final consonants without immediate modeling.

2. Initial /k/ in words and short phrases
- Continued work with initial /k/ words ("car, key, cookie, cat, cow") embedded in simple story play.
- Child needed fewer verbal cues to produce /k/ correctly in imitation and began to approximate /k/ more often in short, prompted phrases ("my car," "big cookie").
- Substitutions to /t/ still occurred but were less frequent.

3. Initial /s/ in words and two-word phrases
- Practiced initial /s/ during a "shopping" game ("soap, soup, socks, sun").

notes/6/note_text:
Parent report:
Mother shared that the child is using more two- and three-word phrases at home, such as "my big car" and "more juice please." She notes that some phrases sound quite clear, while others are harder to understand, particularly with new vocabulary.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child was engaged and enthusiastic, frequently initiating play and conversation. Attention to structured tasks was improved; the child tolerated slightly larger sets of practice words and was willing to repeat when prompted. Demonstrated pride when hearing their own clearer phrases.

Goals/activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words and short sentences (/p, m, t, k/)
- Practiced CVC words in short, imitated sentences ("I see a map," "I like soup," "I have a cup").
- Child demonstrated more consistent inclusion of final /p/ and /m" and improved accuracy for /t/ and /k" in these contexts.
- Occasional spontaneous sentences during play (e.g., "I want cup," "big cat jump") included clearer final consonants, though not yet across all opportunities.

2. Initial /k/ in words, phrases, and simple sentences
- Targeted initial /k/ within phrases and short sentences during a "kitchen" play theme ("cut cake," "cook corn," "my cookie").
- Child produced /k/ correctly in many imitated phrases and was beginning to use /k/ more independently in connected speech during play.
- Needed fewer verbal reminders and rarely required visual/tactile cues.

3. Initial /s/ and /s/-blends at word and phrase level
- Reviewed initial /s/ words ("sun, soup, sock, seat") and introduced a few simple /s/ blends ("spoon, stop, star") at the imitated word level.
- Child maintained good accuracy for initial /s/ in words and showed emerging ability with /s/ blends when strongly cued (e.g., separating /s/ + word).
- In two-word phrases ("see sun," "my socks"), /s/ was generally present and clearer than in earlier sessions, though blends often reduced to single consonants.

Plan/recommendations:
Given the moderate, meaningful progress, we will continue to expand to short sentences for final consonants and initial /k/, while keeping /s/ blends at the imitated word level for now. Parent was encouraged to model short, clear sentences highlighting target sounds (e.g., "I want soup," "my car is big") and to praise the child’s attempts at including final sounds and /k/ and /s/ in everyday speech.
notes/7/note_text:
Parent report:
Mother reported that the child is "talking all the time" and that some sentences are much easier to understand now, especially about familiar routines. She has noticed a few clearer /s/ words ("sock," "sun") and more consistent last sounds in words like "cup" and "pop."

Behaviour/engagement:
Child arrived eager to show toys from home and transitioned well to therapy activities. Attention was generally good; the child participated in both structured and play-based tasks with minimal prompting. Demonstrated increased self-awareness, sometimes asking "Was that good?" after saying a word.

Goals/activities:
1. Final consonants in short sentences and structured play (/p, m, t, k/)
- Continued practice of final consonants within short sentences during a picture sequencing activity ("I see a map," "The cat is on top," "I like soup").
- Child maintained strong accuracy for final /p/ and /m" and showed continued improvement for /t/ and /k" in imitation, with more spontaneous inclusion of final sounds during play.
- Some omissions still occurred when the child spoke quickly or excitedly, but overall performance was similar to last week, suggesting consolidation.

2. Initial /k/ in phrases and short sentences
- Practiced initial /k/ in phrases and sentences ("my car is fast," "I want a cookie," "the cat is big") using a storybook.
- Child continued to produce /k/ accurately in most imitated phrases and was using /k/ more independently in connected speech.
- Cueing demands were comparable to the previous session, mainly verbal reminders during more complex sentences.

- /s/-blends continued to be challenging; the child often reduced blends to a single consonant but occasionally produced clearer approximations when provided with segmented practice ("s" + word).

We will keep strengthening final consonants and initial /k/ and /s/ in short sentences and structured play, and we will continue to gently introduce /s/-blends at the imitated word level. Parent encouraged to highlight a few key /s/ words at home (e.g., "sun," "sock," "soap") and to provide slow, clear models without pressure.
notes/8/note_text:

Mother shared that the child had a "busy week" with visitors and she noticed the child was sometimes harder to understand when excited, with more dropped sounds. She also reported hearing some nice clear sentences at quieter times, similar to the last couple of weeks.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child was somewhat more active and silly at the start of the session but settled with clear routines and preferred activities. Once engaged, the child participated well in structured tasks and showed willingness to imitate and correct productions when prompted.

Goals/activities:
1. Final consonants in sentences and play (/p, m, t, k/)
- Practiced final consonants in short imitated sentences using a picture description game.
- Child’s performance was similar to prior sessions, with generally good final /p/ and /m" and variable but improving /t/ and /k".
- Occasional spontaneous final consonants were heard in conversation, though consistency fluctuated with excitement level.

2. Initial /k/ in short sentences and conversation
- Continued focus on initial /k/ within sentences ("I want a cookie," "my car is red," "the cat is up") and during free play.

notes/9/note_text:
Parent report:
Mother reported that the child continues to use many short sentences and that close family members understand more of what the child says now. She still notices sound drops, especially with longer or new words, but feels overall speech is "about the same" as last week.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child transitioned well and was cooperative throughout the session. Attention was adequate, with occasional need for movement breaks. The child remained motivated by games and story-based tasks and responded well to positive reinforcement.

Goals/activities:
1. Final consonants in structured sentences and conversation (/p, m, t, k/)
- Practiced final consonants during a story retell task and simple sentence generation ("The cat is on top," "I like soup," "I see a map").
- Accuracy was similar to previous sessions: strong for /p/ and /m", with variable but present /t/ and /k" in many imitated sentences.
- In spontaneous speech, final consonants were included in some phrases but not consistently across all opportunities.

2. Initial /k/ in sentences and spontaneous speech
- Targeted /k/ words in more open-ended sentences and play (e.g., describing pictures, talking about cars and cookies).
- Child continued to produce /k/ accurately in most imitated contexts and showed ongoing, though gradual, increase in spontaneous /k/ in conversation.

3. Initial /s/ and /s/-blends at word and phrase level
- Reviewed initial /s/ and /s/-blends in a "space" and "store" themed activity ("sun, soap, socks, star, spoon, stop").
- Initial /s/ productions remained generally strong in imitation and acceptable in two-word phrases when cued.
- /s/-blends were still frequently reduced but with occasional clearer attempts when segmented and then blended together.

Plan/recommendations:
Skills continue to appear stable at the current level, with ongoing need for practice to promote more consistent carryover into spontaneous speech. We will maintain focus on sentence-level work for final consonants and initial /k/ and /s/, while continuing supported practice of /s/-blends at the word and short phrase level. Parent encouraged to model and expand the child’s sentences at home (e.g., turning "my car" into "my car is fast") while emphasizing clear sounds.
notes/10/note_text:
Parent report:
Mother noted that the child has been very talkative and seems proud of being understood. She reported that family members are asking the child to "say it again" less often. She still hears some dropped sounds but feels things are "going pretty well" and similar to last week.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child was enthusiastic and social, frequently initiating interactions with the clinician. Attention was generally good, though the child occasionally rushed through words, leading to sound omissions. With gentle reminders to "use slow, strong sounds," the child was able to improve clarity.

Goals/activities:
1. Final consonants in sentences and structured conversation (/p, m, t, k/)
- Practiced final consonants during a picture description and simple question-answer activity.
- Performance remained similar to recent sessions: consistent final /p/ and /m" in imitation, with variable but present /t/ and /k" across many trials.
- Some spontaneous sentences during play (e.g., "I want that cup," "big cat jump down") included clearer final sounds, but omissions still occurred when speech was rapid.

2. Initial /k/ in connected speech
- Continued to embed /k/ targets in longer utterances and conversational play.
- Child maintained accurate /k/ productions in imitated sentences and was using /k/ more naturally in spontaneous phrases, particularly with familiar words ("car," "cookie," "cat").

3. Initial /s/ and /s/-blends in words and phrases
- Targeted /s/ and /s/-blends through a game involving "space" and "stop" actions.
- Initial /s/ remained strong at the word level and acceptable in two- and three-word phrases when cued.
- /s/-blends continued to be challenging but with occasional successful productions (e.g., closer approximations of "spoon" and "stop") under direct modeling and segmented practice.

Plan/recommendations:
Given the stable performance at this level, we will continue reinforcing final consonants and initial /k/ and /s/ within increasingly natural sentence and play contexts, while maintaining targeted practice of /s/-blends. Parent encouraged to keep providing clear models and to celebrate successful attempts, especially when the child includes final sounds or produces /k/ and /s/ accurately in longer sentences.
notes/11/note_text:
Parent report:
Mother reported that overall intelligibility with familiar listeners remains improved compared to the start of the block, and she feels the child is "doing about the same" as the last couple of weeks. She continues to hear nice clear words and short sentences, with occasional sound drops when the child is excited or tired.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child transitioned smoothly and engaged readily in both structured and play-based tasks. Participation was good; the child showed interest in talking about recent events and responded well to visual and verbal feedback about sound production.

Goals/activities:
1. Final consonants in short narratives and conversation (/p, m, t, k/)
- Practiced final consonants while the child described simple picture sequences and talked about familiar routines.
- The child maintained similar performance to recent sessions, with reliable final /p/ and /m" and variable but present /t/ and /k" in many imitated and some spontaneous sentences.
- Omissions still occurred in longer or less familiar words, particularly when speech rate increased.

2. Initial /k/ in connected speech
- Embedded /k/ targets within short narratives and free play about cars, cats, and cookies.
- Child continued to produce /k/ accurately in imitated sentences and used /k/ spontaneously in many familiar words, with occasional substitutions when speaking quickly or with new vocabulary.

3. Initial /s/ and /s/-blends at word and phrase level
- Reviewed initial /s/ words and /s/-blends in a final "review" game ("sun, soup, socks, star, spoon, stop, slide").
- Initial /s/ remained strong in imitation and was present in many short phrases.
- /s/-blends continued to be an area of relative difficulty, with frequent reductions but some clearer attempts when the child was cued to "start with the snake sound" and then add the rest of the word.

Plan/recommendations:
Skills this session were consistent with the recent pattern, suggesting consolidation at the current levels. Recommended continuation of speech-language services to further strengthen final consonant production, increase consistency of /k/ and /s/ in spontaneous speech, and build accuracy with /s/-blends. Parent was provided with a brief home practice handout focusing on a few target words for final consonants, /k/, and /s/ and was encouraged to integrate practice into daily routines in a playful, low-pressure way.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent reported that child is “hard to understand” for unfamiliar listeners and often leaves off sounds at the ends of words. No major changes noted since the initial evaluation. Parent interested in strategies to help at home.

Behaviour/engagement:
- 4-year-old child transitioned into the therapy room with mild hesitation but separated from caregiver with reassurance. Once seated, engaged well with toys and picture cards. Attention span was short but could be redirected with visual supports and frequent movement breaks. Followed simple play-based directions with occasional repetition.

Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: Establish production of /s/ in isolation and simple syllables
- Practiced /s/ in isolation using snake and train-themed activities. With simultaneous production (SLP and child together), child approximated /s/ in ~4/10 attempts; common substitutions included /t/ and /ʃ/ (“sh”). Required maximal verbal cues (e.g., “long, quiet snake sound”) and mirror for visual feedback. Briefly achieved clearer /s/ when reminded to keep tongue behind teeth, but not yet consistent.
- Attempted CV syllables (“see, soo, saa”) in imitation; child often reduced to vowel only or used /t/ (“tee” for “see”). Accuracy at this level was low; continued need for intensive modeling.

Goal 2: Final consonant production for /p, t, m/ in CVC words (imitated)
- Used picture cards and turn-taking game (e.g., “cup, cap, cat, map”). In imitation, child produced final consonants in ~3/10 opportunities. Frequent omissions (“cu” for “cup,” “ma” for “map”). When final consonant was produced, it was generally recognizable. Required maximal verbal cueing (“stop the word,” “put the sound at the end”) and visual cue of SLP touching chin at the last sound.

Goal 3: Initial /f/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Introduced /f/ as “lip biting windy sound.” In isolation, child was able to approximate /f/ in 5–6/10 attempts with simultaneous production, but often shifted to /p/ or omitted airflow. Needed repeated reminders to bite lower lip and blow. CV syllables (“fee, foo, faa”) were attempted; most productions were approximations with reduced airflow.

Goal 4: Functional phrase attempts in play
- During play, child produced single words and word approximations (e.g., “up,” “more,” “mine”). Two-word combinations were rare and largely unintelligible. SLP modeled simple two-word phrases (e.g., “more bubbles,” “my turn”) and child imitated ~4/10 with significant sound distortions, but intent was clear.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to target /s/ and /f/ in isolation and simple syllable shapes with heavy modeling and visual cues.
- Maintain focus on final consonant production for /p, t, m/ at the imitated word level.
- Provide parent with demonstration of “long snake sound” (/s/) and “lip biting windy sound” (/f/) to practice a few times daily in front of a mirror, focusing on fun attempts rather than accuracy. Emphasize short, positive practice (1–2 minutes).
notes/1/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent noted that child has started trying to copy some of the “snake sound” practice at home but is “hit or miss.” No big changes in intelligibility reported. Parent shared that child is motivated by stickers and pretend play.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered room more readily today and appeared comfortable with clinician. Participated well in structured play tasks for 5–7 minute stretches before needing a break. Some silliness when tasks became challenging (e.g., making animal noises instead of target sounds), but responded to gentle redirection.

Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Revisited /s/ in isolation with mirror and “quiet snake” imagery. Child produced a recognizable /s/ in isolation in approximately 7/10 imitated attempts with moderate verbal and visual cueing. Less frequent substitution with /t/ noted compared to last session.
- In CV syllables (“see, soo, saa”), child produced more consistent initial /s/ approximations in ~5/10 trials when given a clear model and reminder to “start with snake, then vowel.” Still occasionally reduced to vowel only but showed increased willingness to attempt.

Goal 2: Final consonants /p, t, m/ in CVC words (imitated)
- Used picture cards in a turn-taking game. Child produced final consonants more consistently than previous session, approximating final /p, t, m/ in ~5/10 opportunities with moderate verbal cueing (“stop the word”) and modeling. Omissions are still frequent, but occasional spontaneous self-correction noted when provided extra time.

Goal 3: Initial /f/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Practiced /f/ with mirror, focusing on biting the lower lip and blowing. Child produced /f/ in isolation in about 6/10 trials with moderate support. Still occasional stopping to /p/, but improved awareness; child sometimes adjusted lip placement after clinician reminder.
- CV syllables (“fee, foo, faa”) attempted in imitation. Accuracy ~4/10 for clear initial /f/; better control when clinician slowed rate and added tactile cue to lower lip.

Goal 4: Functional two-word phrases in imitation
- During play with bubbles and cars, SLP modeled simple two-word phrases (e.g., “more bubbles,” “go car,” “my turn”). Child imitated ~6/10 with variable sound accuracy but clearer word boundaries than last session. Intelligibility to clinician improved slightly when context was known.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue current targets with emphasis on /s/ and final consonants; child is showing emerging improvements but not yet ready to move up levels.
- Encourage parent to keep home practice short and playful, focusing on a few /s/ in isolation attempts and one simple CVC word (e.g., “cup”) per day.
- Next session: consider adding simple picture choice game to increase motivation while practicing CVC words.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent reported that child is starting to “hiss” on purpose at home and occasionally tries to say “see” and “so” after modeling. Parent feels some words are “a tiny bit clearer,” especially when they know what child is talking about. No concerns about hearing or general health.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly into the session and quickly engaged with toys. Demonstrated good joint attention during table-top tasks for longer periods (up to ~10 minutes) with brief movement breaks. Followed simple directions and enjoyed turn-taking games. Mild frustration when unable to produce a sound, but responded well to praise for effort.

Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: /s/ in isolation, CV, and emerging word level (imitation)
- In isolation, child produced /s/ accurately in ~8/10 attempts with only light verbal cues. Often able to maintain airflow when reminded “teeth together, tongue behind teeth.”
- CV syllables (“see, soo, saa, si”) produced with initial /s/ in ~7/10 imitated trials; fewer substitutions and omissions. Child began to anticipate the /s/ and occasionally initiated the sound independently before the model.
- Briefly introduced imitated word-level /s/ (e.g., “see, sun, soup”). Child produced an initial /s/ approximation in ~5/10 opportunities; errors included /t/ substitution or omission. Performance suggests readiness to continue exploring word-level /s/.

Goal 2: Final consonants /p, t, m/ in CVC words (imitation)
- Continued practice with picture cards (e.g., “cup, cat, map, mat, mop”). Child produced final consonants in ~7/10 imitated opportunities with moderate cueing, an improvement from previous session. Some spontaneous carryover during play (e.g., “up” with clear /p/ at the end when climbing toy ladder).
- When cued to “make the word stop,” child occasionally self-corrected from an open syllable (“cu”) to a more complete “cup.”

Goal 3: Initial /f/ in CV and CVC words (imitation)
- Practiced /f/ in CV syllables (“fee, foo, fa”) and simple words (“fun, four”). Child produced initial /f/ with clearer airflow in ~7/10 CV attempts and ~5/10 word-level attempts. Continued need for mild to moderate verbal cueing, but fewer /p/ substitutions compared to earlier sessions.

Goal 4: Functional two- to three-word phrases (imitation within play)
- During structured play, SLP modeled two- and simple three-word phrases (“more bubbles please,” “my turn car,” “I want that”). Child imitated ~7/10 phrases with variable speech sound accuracy but more consistent phrase length. Some spontaneous two-word combinations (e.g., “my car,” “more bubbles”) were noted, though often with reduced final consonants.

Plan/recommendations:
- Given moderate, meaningful gains in /s/ and final consonants, plan to continue strengthening these at the word level next session while still supporting isolation/CV as needed.
- Encourage parent to practice a few specific words at home (e.g., “see, sun, soup, cup, map”), focusing on slow, clear models and praising attempts rather than perfection.
- Provide parent with handout describing “stop the word” cue for final consonants.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent shared that child is “trying to copy words more” and is starting to say a clearer “up” and “pop” during play at home. They feel others can understand the child slightly more, but still need to interpret frequently. Parent has been practicing the “snake sound” and final sounds most evenings.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered room independently and quickly joined clinician at the table. Good participation in both structured and play-based tasks. Needed occasional reminders to wait for their turn but overall cooperative. Enjoyed sticker rewards and verbal praise.

Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: /s/ initial at word level (imitation and emerging spontaneous)
- Target words included “sun, soup, sock, see, sit, sand.” In imitation, child produced an initial /s/ or close approximation in ~8/10 trials with light verbal cues. Fewer substitutions with /t/ observed.
- In structured play, child produced some spontaneous /s/ initial attempts (e.g., “sun,” “sit”) with mixed accuracy; often required a quick reminder to “start with snake sound.” Overall, consistency at the imitated word level has improved.

Goal 2: Final consonants /p, t, m/ in CVC words (imitation and structured play)
- Continued practice with familiar CVC targets (“cup, cat, map, mat, mop, up”). In imitation, child produced final consonants in ~8/10 trials with minimal to moderate cues. Omissions occurred more often when child was excited or speaking quickly.
- During play, some spontaneous final consonants were noted in context (e.g., “up,” “pop”) with good clarity. Child is beginning to show awareness when a final sound is missing, especially when the SLP uses a questioning look.

Goal 3: Initial /f/ at word level (imitation)
- Focused on words such as “fun, four, food, feet.” Child imitated initial /f/ correctly in ~7/10 opportunities with mild verbal reminders to “bite lip and blow.” Occasional reversion to /p/ still present but less frequent. When slowed down and given a visual model, accuracy improved.

Goal 4: Short functional phrases (2–3 words) with targeted sounds
- SLP modeled phrases incorporating target words (e.g., “more soup,” “big sun,” “my feet,” “cup up”). Child imitated ~7/10 phrases with reasonable approximations of target sounds. Some spontaneous two-word phrases appeared in play (“my car,” “more bubbles,” “up please”), though sound production remained variable.

Plan/recommendations:
- Child is demonstrating moderate, meaningful progress and is beginning to stabilize /s/ and final consonants at the word level. Plan to continue consolidating these targets and gradually expand to simple carrier phrases for highly familiar /s/ and CVC words.
- Recommend parent continue home practice with a small set of familiar words and short phrases, emphasizing slow, clear models and celebrating attempts.
- Next session: trial simple carrier phrases (e.g., “I see ___,” “my ___”) with /s/ initial words if accuracy remains stable.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent reported that child continues to try target sounds at home but performance is “up and down.” Some days speech seems clearer, other days less so. No new concerns raised.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child was somewhat distracted at the start of the session, interested in toys outside the therapy area. After a short transition activity, attention improved and child participated adequately in structured tasks. Occasional avoidance (changing topic or making silly noises) when a task became challenging, but responded to breaks and encouragement.

Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: /s/ initial at word and emerging phrase level
- Practiced familiar /s/ initial words (“sun, soup, sit, sock, see”). In imitation, child maintained similar accuracy to last session, producing initial /s/ in ~8/10 trials with light cues. Occasional /t/ substitution persisted, especially when speaking more quickly.
- Introduced simple carrier phrases (e.g., “I see sun,” “I see soup”) with strong modeling. Child imitated these phrases with correct or near-correct initial /s/ in ~5/10 opportunities. Still required moderate support to maintain /s/ when moving from single words to phrases.

Goal 2: Final consonants /p, t, m/ in CVC words (imitation and structured play)
- Continued with familiar CVC targets. In imitation, child produced final consonants in ~8/10 attempts, similar to prior session. Errors mainly occurred when child was less attentive or rushed. In play, some spontaneous final consonants were heard (e.g., “up,” “cup”), but not consistently across words.

Goal 3: Initial /f/ at word level (imitation)
- Target words included “feet, four, fun, food.” Child imitated initial /f/ with similar accuracy to previous session (~7/10). Needed intermittent verbal reminders for lip placement. When focused, productions were clear; when distracted, accuracy decreased.

Goal 4: Short functional phrases with targeted words
- Continued to model 2–3 word phrases containing target words (e.g., “my feet,” “big sun,” “cup up”). Child imitated ~6/10 with approximate sound accuracy. Spontaneous phrase use was present but not markedly different from last week.

Plan/recommendations:
- Skills appear relatively stable this week, with consolidation of word-level /s/, final consonants, and /f/. Child is still adjusting to using targets in phrases.
- Plan to continue practicing current targets at the same levels next session, emphasizing consistency and attention to the final sounds.
- Encourage parent to keep home practice brief and positive, focusing on a few familiar /s/ words and final consonant CVC words during daily routines (e.g., snack time: “cup,” “sip,” “mop”).
notes/5/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent noted that child has been saying “see” and “sun” more clearly at home, especially when reminded to use the “snake sound.” Parent also noticed clearer “up” and “cup” during play. They feel there is a “small but noticeable” improvement in how easily family members understand the child.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned well and appeared eager to show skills. Sat for structured tasks for longer stretches with fewer redirections needed. Enjoyed turn-taking games and responded positively to specific praise (e.g., “I heard your good /s/!”).

Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: /s/ initial at word and carrier phrase level (imitation, emerging independence)
- Word level: child produced initial /s/ in familiar words (“sun, soup, sit, sock, see, soap”) accurately in ~9/10 imitated trials with minimal cueing. Occasional errors were self-corrected after a brief pause.
- Carrier phrases: practiced “I see ___” and “my ___” with /s/ initial words. Child imitated phrases with correct /s/ in ~8/10 opportunities. Began to produce short phrases with /s/ (e.g., “see sun”) more spontaneously within play when given picture choices.

Goal 2: Final consonants /p, t, m/ in CVC words (imitation and structured play)
- Child maintained good performance for familiar CVC words, producing final consonants in ~9/10 imitated trials with minimal prompts. In structured play, child produced final consonants more consistently across words, including some less practiced items. Occasional omissions still occurred, but child sometimes corrected after the SLP’s visual cue.

Goal 3: Initial /f/ at word and simple phrase level (imitation)
- Word level targets (“feet, four, fun, food, fan”). Child produced initial /f/ correctly in ~8/10 imitated attempts with light cueing. Demonstrated better control of airflow and lip placement.
- Simple phrases (“my feet,” “four fish,” “fun food”) were introduced. Child imitated with reasonably accurate /f/ in ~6/10 trials. Needed extra support to maintain /f/ when adding more words.

Goal 4: Functional 2–3 word phrases incorporating targets
- Within play-based routines (e.g., feeding toy animals, driving cars), SLP modeled and elicited short phrases such as “my cup,” “big sun,” “more soup,” “four fish.” Child imitated ~8/10 modeled phrases and produced several spontaneous two-word combinations with target words (e.g., “my cup,” “more soup”), though sound accuracy varied.

Plan/recommendations:
- Child is demonstrating emerging improvement with increased accuracy and some self-corrections. Plan to continue expanding /s/ and final consonant use in short phrases, while stabilizing /f/ at word level and beginning more phrase-level practice.
- Recommend parent practice simple carrier phrases at home (“I see sun,” “my sock”) using picture books or daily routines.
- Next session: consider trialing very simple play-based conversation prompts to encourage more spontaneous use of target sounds in short utterances.
notes/6/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent reported that child is “chatty” at home and seems more confident trying to say new words. They have noticed more final sounds in words like “up, cup, cat,” and feel that siblings are understanding the child a bit more during play.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered session with high energy but was easily engaged in therapy activities. Maintained attention for structured tasks with minimal redirection. Demonstrated pride in successful productions and frequently looked to clinician for feedback.

Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: /s/ initial at word, carrier phrase, and emerging short sentence level
- Word level: maintained high accuracy with initial /s/ in familiar words (~9/10 correct with minimal cues). Child occasionally produced accurate /s/ in unpracticed words (e.g., “sand”) after a model.
- Carrier phrases: “I see ___,” “my ___,” “more ___” with /s/ initial words. Child produced correct /s/ in ~9/10 imitated phrases, with some spontaneous use during picture description.
- Short sentences: introduced simple 3–4 word sentences (e.g., “I see the sun,” “I see soup”). Child imitated these with reasonably accurate /s/ in ~7/10 opportunities. Needed reminders to slow down and keep the “snake sound” at the start.

Goal 2: Final consonants /p, t, m/ in CVC words at phrase level
- Practiced phrases such as “my cup,” “big cat,” “mom up,” “tap it.” Child produced final consonants accurately in ~8–9/10 imitated trials. Some spontaneous phrase use with preserved final sounds occurred in play (e.g., “my cup,” “up top”).
- Fewer omissions noted overall. When final sounds were missed, child often responded well to a visual cue and attempted to repair the word.

Goal 3: Initial /f/ in words and short phrases
- Word level: “feet, four, fun, food, fish.” Child produced initial /f/ accurately in ~8–9/10 trials with light cues. Substitutions to /p/ were rare.
- Short phrases: “my feet,” “four fish,” “fun food,” “big fish.” Child imitated phrases with fairly consistent /f/ in ~7/10 attempts. Still needed occasional cueing to maintain /f/ when excited.

Goal 4: Functional spontaneous phrases with targets in play
- During play scenarios (feeding animals, building with blocks), child used more spontaneous 2–3 word combinations (e.g., “my cup,” “more food,” “big sun,” “up top”). Target sounds were present in some of these productions with varying accuracy, but overall intelligibility to clinician improved compared to earlier sessions.

Plan/recommendations:
- Child is making moderate, meaningful progress and is beginning to handle short sentences with /s/ and more consistent final consonants.
- Plan to further increase phrase/sentence-level practice next session and to introduce a few new /s/ words to support generalization.
- Encourage parent to prompt simple sentences at home using familiar routines (e.g., “I see ___” during book reading, “my ___” when dressing).
notes/7/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent stated that child is “really trying hard” with speech at home and often reminds themselves to use their “snake sound.” Family members have commented that they can understand the child better during simple conversations, though breakdowns still occur with longer sentences.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child was enthusiastic and talkative upon entering the session. Demonstrated good persistence with challenging tasks and accepted corrective feedback well. Needed occasional reminders to wait for clinician’s model before speaking but overall cooperative.

Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: /s/ initial at phrase and short sentence level (imitation and emerging spontaneous)
- Practiced structured sentences (e.g., “I see the sun,” “I see soup on top,” “my sock is wet”). Child produced accurate or close-to-accurate initial /s/ in ~8–9/10 imitated sentences with minimal cueing.
- In structured play and picture description, child produced several spontaneous phrases and short sentences with initial /s/ (e.g., “see sun,” “my sock,” “I see soup”) with variable but generally good accuracy.

Goal 2: Final consonants /p, t, m/ in short sentences
- Used short, functional sentences (e.g., “I want cup,” “cat on top,” “mom up,” “tap it”). Child produced final consonants accurately in ~8–9/10 imitated sentences. In spontaneous speech during play, final consonants were present more often than not, particularly for familiar words such as “up” and “cup.”

Goal 3: Initial /f/ in words and phrases, beginning to generalize
- Word level remained strong, with initial /f/ accurate in ~9/10 imitated trials (“feet, four, fun, food, fish”).
- Phrases and short sentences (“my feet are wet,” “four fish here”) were practiced. Child produced /f/ accurately in ~7–8/10 opportunities. Some spontaneous use of /f/ words (e.g., “my feet”) noted in play.

Goal 4: Overall intelligibility in structured play
- In a play-based routine (pretend picnic and car track), child produced many 2–4 word utterances. Intelligibility to clinician was noticeably better compared to early sessions, especially when context was known. Residual sound errors and occasional final consonant omissions persisted, but listener effort required was reduced.

Plan/recommendations:
- Skills this week appear stable to slightly improved compared to last session, with ongoing consolidation at the phrase and short sentence level.
- Plan to maintain current targets, gradually increasing complexity of sentences and introducing a small variety of new /s/ and /f/ words for generalization.
- Recommend parent continue modeling slightly longer sentences at home and encouraging child to repeat with focus on clear /s/ and final sounds (e.g., “I see the cat,” “I want cup”).
notes/8/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent reported that child’s speech has been “about the same” this week. They continue to hear clearer /s/ and final sounds in some words, but consistency varies, especially when child is excited or talking quickly.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child arrived somewhat tired today but still participated in activities. Needed a bit more prompting to stay with table-top tasks and benefitted from frequent short movement breaks. Cooperative and responsive to positive reinforcement.

Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: /s/ initial at phrase and sentence level
- Practiced previously established sentences (“I see the sun,” “I see soup,” “my sock is on”). Child produced initial /s/ accurately in ~8/10 imitated sentences, similar to prior session. Occasional /t/ substitution or omission occurred when attention waned.
- Spontaneous phrases with /s/ continued to emerge in play, though accuracy was somewhat inconsistent.

Goal 2: Final consonants /p, t, m/ in short sentences
- Used functional sentences with CVC words (“I want cup,” “put cat up,” “tap it”). Child produced final consonants in ~8/10 imitated opportunities, similar to recent performance. In spontaneous speech, final consonant presence fluctuated with energy level and task demands.

- Repeated practice with phrases/sentences involving /f/ (“my feet are cold,” “four fish here,” “fun food”). Accuracy for initial /f/ remained around ~7–8/10 in imitated sentences with light cueing, consistent with last week. Occasional /p/ substitution noted when child spoke quickly.

- During open-ended play (cars, animals), child produced multiple 2–4 word utterances. Overall intelligibility to clinician remained improved relative to early sessions, but not significantly changed from last week. Listener support (context, occasional clarification questions) still needed for some utterances.

- Plan to continue working at phrase and sentence levels for /s/, final consonants, and /f/, with an emphasis on maintaining accuracy when the child is more tired or excited.

notes/9/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent stated that there have been no major changes in speech this week. Child continues to talk a lot and occasionally asks, “Was that good?” after trying a target word, indicating increased awareness. Parent feels progress is holding steady.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned well into the session and was cooperative overall. Required occasional prompts to slow down and listen to models before speaking. Enjoyed being the “teacher” and giving the clinician a turn to say target words.

Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: /s/ initial in short sentences and structured conversation
- Continued work with short sentences and simple question/answer exchanges involving /s/ words (e.g., “I see the sun,” “Where is the sock?” “The sock is here.”). Child produced initial /s/ accurately in ~8–9/10 imitated sentences, consistent with prior performance.
- In brief structured conversation about pictures (e.g., describing what they see), child attempted /s/ in many initial positions with moderate overall accuracy, though not yet at conversational consistency.

Goal 2: Final consonants /p, t, m/ in sentences and simple dialogue
- Practiced short exchanges like “I want cup,” “Put it up,” “Tap it,” “Mom is up.” Child produced final consonants accurately in ~8/10 imitated sentences. In simple back-and-forth questions and answers, final consonant presence varied but was more frequent than in early sessions.

Goal 3: Initial /f/ in sentences and short conversational turns
- Sentences such as “My feet are wet,” “Four fish are here,” “This is fun food” were practiced. Child produced initial /f/ in ~7–8/10 imitated sentences with light cues. No major change in performance from prior week.

Goal 4: Overall intelligibility in semi-structured play
- In a semi-structured play activity (pretend grocery store), child used multiple 2–5 word utterances to request items and comment. Intelligibility to clinician remained fairly good with contextual support, though breakdowns still occurred with longer or less familiar words.

Plan/recommendations:
- Skills continue to appear stable. Child is maintaining gains but not yet showing a clear leap in independence at the conversational level.
- Plan to keep practicing /s/, /f/, and final consonants in short sentence and semi-structured conversation tasks, focusing on slowing rate and self-monitoring.
- Recommend parent reinforce child’s self-awareness by responding positively when child asks “Was that good?” and, if appropriate, briefly model a slightly clearer version without overcorrecting.
notes/10/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent reported that child’s speech was similar to last week. They continue to hear better /s/ and final sounds in practiced words, but general conversation is still sometimes difficult for unfamiliar listeners.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered session in a good mood and was eager to talk. Some impulsive responding (speaking before model was finished), but generally redirectable. Enjoyed role-play games and took turns well.

Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: /s/ initial in sentences and structured conversation
- Continued practice with sentence-level /s/ in picture description and simple question/answer tasks. Child produced initial /s/ accurately in ~8–9/10 imitated sentences, similar to recent sessions.
- In structured conversation about daily activities (e.g., “I see my socks,” “I see sun outside”), child attempted /s/ in many appropriate places with moderate accuracy. No marked change in overall performance.

Goal 2: Final consonants /p, t, m/ in connected speech
- Used short story and role-play activities to elicit CVC words in context. Child produced final consonants reliably in practiced phrases and sentences (~8/10), with some carryover into less structured utterances. Omissions still present, especially when child was more animated.

Goal 3: Initial /f/ in functional phrases and sentences
- Practiced functional phrases such as “my feet are cold,” “four fish here,” “this is fun.” Child produced /f/ correctly in ~7–8/10 imitated sentences, requiring occasional reminders for lip placement. Performance consistent with previous weeks.

Goal 4: Intelligibility and self-monitoring
- During play, child produced numerous 2–5 word utterances. Intelligibility to clinician remained improved compared to the start of the block, though still variable without context. Child occasionally paused and tried again after an unclear production, indicating growing self-monitoring.

Plan/recommendations:
- This appears to be another consolidation week with relatively stable performance.
- Plan to maintain focus on sentence-level practice and structured conversation with /s/, /f/, and final consonants, while supporting self-monitoring strategies (e.g., “Did that sound right?” checks).
- Encourage parent to provide gentle feedback at home by occasionally repeating back the child’s sentence with clear target sounds, emphasizing understanding and communication first.
notes/11/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent reported continued similar performance this week. They feel that family members generally understand the child in familiar contexts, but new listeners still have difficulty. Parent is pleased with the progress so far and is interested in next steps.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child was cheerful and cooperative throughout the session. Participated actively in all tasks and enjoyed more “grown-up” conversation-style activities. Demonstrated good rapport with clinician and responded well to feedback.

Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: /s/ initial in sentences and semi-structured conversation
- Practiced sentence-level /s/ in story-telling and picture description (e.g., “I see the sun at the park,” “My sock is on,” “She sits on the sand”). Child produced initial /s/ accurately in ~8–9/10 imitated sentences, comparable to recent sessions.
- In semi-structured conversation about favorite games and routines, child used /s/ in many appropriate words with moderate accuracy; some productions were clear, others showed residual errors.

Goal 2: Final consonants /p, t, m/ in connected speech
- Through storybook reading and role-play, child produced many CVC words with final /p, t, m/ in context. Accuracy remained strong for practiced words when given mild cueing, with omissions appearing more in longer or less familiar words. Overall performance consistent with prior weeks.

Goal 3: Initial /f/ in connected speech
- Used functional sentences involving /f/ words (e.g., “My feet are on the floor,” “Four fish swim,” “This is fun food”). Child produced initial /f/ correctly in ~7–8/10 imitated sentences, with similar accuracy in supported conversational turns when reminded to slow down.

Goal 4: Overall intelligibility and functional communication
- In a mix of structured and play-based activities, child generated multiple 3–6 word utterances. Intelligibility to clinician was generally fair to good with contextual support, though still below age expectations in more complex or less predictable contexts. Child showed good communicative intent, used gestures and repetition to repair breakdowns, and remained engaged.

Plan/recommendations:
- Skills appear stable at the current level, with maintained gains in /s/, /f/, and final consonants at the word and sentence level, and partial carryover to connected speech.
- Recommend continued therapy to support further generalization to spontaneous conversation and to increase consistency with new and longer words.
- Home practice: continue short daily practice with a mix of sentence-level /s/ and /f/ targets and CVC words in simple phrases, embedded in books, routines, and play. Encourage child’s self-monitoring by asking, “Can you try that one more time with your snake sound?” when appropriate.

notes/0/note_text:
SESSION 1
Child age: 4;0

1. Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent shared that they are most concerned about speech clarity, especially with /s/ and /f/ sounds. Reported that preschool teachers sometimes have difficulty understanding the child.
- No recent changes in health or hearing reported.

2. Behaviour/engagement observations:
- Child transitioned into the therapy room with some hesitation but separated from caregiver with reassurance.
- Became more comfortable after a few minutes of play; participated well with frequent encouragement and visual supports.
- Attention span was variable; needed redirection back to tasks when toys were highly motivating.

3. Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: Establish /f/ in isolation and simple syllables (baseline)
- Introduced /f/ as a “quiet lip-bite, windy sound.”
- In isolation, child produced /f/ accurately in approximately half of opportunities with simultaneous modeling and mirror support; frequent substitution or omission noted.
- In CV syllables (e.g., "fa, fee, foo"), accuracy was low and required maximal visual/verbal cueing and hand-over-hand support to position the lower lip.

Goal 2: Establish /s/ in isolation and initial syllables (baseline)
- Child demonstrated lateral airflow and occasional /sh/-like productions for /s/.
- With close imitation and cueing to “keep the tongue behind the teeth and blow,” child was able to approximate /s/ in isolation inconsistently.
- Attempted CV syllables ("see, soo"); productions were mostly distorted or substituted with /sh/ or /th/.

Goal 3: Probe /s/-blends at word level (baseline)
- Introduced a small set of /s/ blends (e.g., "spoon, star, slide") in play.
- Child typically omitted the /s/ or simplified the cluster (e.g., "poon" for "spoon," "tar" for "star").
- Able to repeat a few blends with heavy simultaneous modeling, but productions were not yet consistent.

Goal 4: Overall speech sound profile in play
- During free play and simple picture description, child’s speech was reduced in intelligibility, especially in multisyllabic words and words with clusters.
- Noted inconsistent final consonant productions; some final sounds were present, but deletions occurred in conversation.

4. Plan/recommendations:
- Plan to continue establishing accurate /f/ and /s/ in isolation and simple syllables before moving consistently to word level.
- Caregiver encouraged to model slow, clear speech at home and highlight /f/ and /s/ words during daily routines (e.g., “fish, food, soap, sun”) without asking for repeated practice yet.
- Will monitor tolerance for more structured drill tasks and gradually increase demands as comfort improves.
notes/1/note_text:
SESSION 2
Child age: 4;0

1. Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent reported no major changes since last session but noted they have started pointing out /f/ words at home as discussed.
- Parent feels child is enjoying “speech games” and is motivated to come.

2. Behaviour/engagement observations:
- Child separated smoothly from caregiver and entered room without difficulty.
- Highly engaged with turn-taking games and tolerated structured practice for short bursts.
- Needed occasional movement breaks but returned to task with minimal prompting.

3. Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: /f/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Reviewed /f/ in isolation with mirror. Child produced a clear /f/ in most attempts when reminded to bite the lip and blow; fewer substitutions compared to baseline.
- In CV syllables ("fee, foo, fa"), productions were more consistent with moderate verbal and visual cues. Occasional backing to /h/ or omission still present, but there was clearer airflow and better lip placement overall.

Goal 2: /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Continued work on tongue placement and airflow. Child showed more consistent ability to imitate a close approximation of /s/ when watching the clinician’s mouth.
- In CV syllables ("see, soo, saa"), accuracy remained variable but improved slightly. Needed repeated modeling and cues to keep tongue inside the mouth and teeth mostly closed.

Goal 3: /s/ blends – imitation at word level (probe/early practice)
- Targeted a small set of /s/-blends ("star, spoon, slide, spider") with picture support.
- Child continued to frequently omit /s" (e.g., "tar," "pider"), but there were a few emerging attempts where the /s/ was audible with simultaneous modeling.
- Required maximal verbal cueing ("use your snake sound first") and visual emphasis on the /s/ portion of the blend.

Goal 4: Final consonant awareness (informal)
- During play, clinician highlighted final sounds in short CVC words ("bus, cup, dog").
- Child was able to identify when the clinician left off the final sound in some cases, indicating growing awareness, though independent productions remained inconsistent.

4. Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to solidify /f/ in isolation and syllables; begin to introduce high-frequency /f/ words (e.g., "food, fish") in structured imitation next session if accuracy remains similar.
- Maintain focus on /s/ in isolation with careful modeling; continue light introduction of /s/ blends as exposure only.
- Parent encouraged to continue labeling and emphasizing /f/ words at home and to praise any clear /f/ sounds they notice without asking for repeated corrections.
notes/2/note_text:
SESSION 3
Child age: 4;1

1. Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent noted that child has started to occasionally emphasize /f/ in words like “fish” and “food” when they are reminded, and seems proud when understood.
- No new concerns from preschool; parent feels child is talking more with peers.

2. Behaviour/engagement observations:
- Child arrived in good spirits and transitioned easily.
- Demonstrated improved sustained attention to structured tasks, especially when activities were embedded in games (e.g., fishing game, picture lotto).
- Accepted corrective feedback well and sometimes attempted to fix productions without prompting.

3. Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: /f/ initial – transition to word level (imitation)
- Practiced initial /f/ words ("fish, foot, food, fan, four") in imitation.
- Child produced initial /f/ with good lip placement in most trials when given a model first; fewer substitutions to /p/ or /h/ compared to prior sessions.
- Occasional need for reminders to “bite your lip then blow.” Some spontaneous correct /f/ productions occurred during game play when labeling pictures.

Goal 2: /s/ isolation and syllables – increasing consistency
- Reviewed /s/ in isolation with visual cues. Child was more consistent in shaping a central airflow sound with tongue behind the teeth, though mild distortion persisted.
- In CV syllables ("see, soo, saa"), child achieved more frequent accurate or near-accurate productions with moderate verbal/visual cues.
- Child began to self-correct on a few trials after hearing their own error.

Goal 3: Early /s/ blends – imitated word level
- Targeted a small set of /s/-blends ("spoon, star, slide, spin") in imitation.
- Child produced blends with partially audible /s/ in several attempts when words were broken into parts (e.g., "s" + "poon") and then blended.
- Still frequently reduced clusters, but there was clearer evidence of the /s/ component than in previous sessions. Backward chaining (starting with the vowel and adding consonants) was helpful.

Goal 4: Functional carryover – intelligibility in play
- During play-based tasks, overall intelligibility to the clinician remained reduced but slightly improved for words practiced in drills (e.g., "fish," "food").
- Child was eager to be understood and repeated themselves when requested.

4. Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to strengthen initial /f/ at the word level in imitation; begin to probe for short carrier phrases (e.g., “my fish”) if consistency holds.
- Maintain focus on /s/ in isolation and syllables, gradually increasing the number of practice trials.
- Continue using backward chaining for /s/-blends; keep them at the imitated word level for now.
- Parent encouraged to practice a small set of /f/ words at home (2–3 minutes, once or twice daily) using a picture list provided, focusing on slow, clear productions with lots of praise.
notes/3/note_text:
SESSION 4
Child age: 4;1

1. Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent shared that child is now often saying “fish” and “food” clearly at home when prompted, and sometimes uses a good /f/ in other words (e.g., "five").
- Parent feels speech is “a bit clearer” with family members.

2. Behaviour/engagement observations:
- Child entered eagerly and appeared to remember the routine.
- Stayed engaged for longer stretches of structured practice and required fewer prompts to take turns.
- Showed pride after successful productions and responded positively to specific praise.

3. Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: /f/ initial – word level (imitation and early spontaneous)
- Practiced a broader set of initial /f/ words ("fish, food, foot, farm, four, fun").
- In imitation, child produced accurate /f/ in most attempts with only light verbal reminders, indicating increased independence.
- In structured play, child independently used clear /f/ in several target words without being asked, suggesting emerging generalization.

Goal 2: /f/ initial – simple carrier phrases
- Introduced short phrases ("my fish," "two feet," "big farm") using picture supports.
- Child was able to maintain an accurate initial /f/ in many phrase-level attempts when the clinician modeled the full phrase first.
- Occasional drop in accuracy when attention shifted to the rest of the phrase, but overall performance showed meaningful improvement from previous sessions.

Goal 3: /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Continued shaping /s/ in isolation with mirrors and gestures. Child produced a more consistent central /s/ with less distortion and required fewer prompts to keep the tongue in the correct position.
- In CV syllables, accuracy and consistency improved modestly; child was able to produce several clear "see" and "soo" productions in a row with visual cueing.

Goal 4: /s/-blends – imitated word level
- Revisited /s/-blends ("spoon, star, slide, spin, stop") at the imitated word level.
- Child demonstrated an audible /s/ portion more often than not when words were segmented and then blended (e.g., "s" + "top").
- Still required moderate cueing and slow rate, but there was clear progress in coordinating the cluster compared to earlier sessions.

4. Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to expand /f/ initial words and phrases, gradually reducing models and prompts as tolerated.
- Maintain structured practice for /s/ in isolation and CV syllables; begin to introduce a few simple initial /s/ words (e.g., "sun, soup") in imitation next session.
- Keep /s/-blends at imitated word level with backward chaining.
- Parent encouraged to practice short /f/ phrases at home (e.g., “my fish,” “fun food”), keeping practice brief and positive.
notes/4/note_text:

2. Behaviour/engagement observations:

- Participated well in both table-top and floor-based tasks, with minimal redirection needed.
- Some silliness emerged near the end of the session when tasks became more challenging, but child remained redirectable.

3. Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: /f/ initial – word and carrier phrase level
- Continued practice with a wider range of /f/ initial words and short phrases.
- At the single word level in imitation, child’s accuracy was high with only occasional reminders.
- In carrier phrases ("I see the fish," "my fun food"), child was able to maintain clear /f/ in many attempts, though accuracy dropped somewhat when phrases became longer or more complex.

Goal 2: Early spontaneous /f/ productions
- During play, child independently labeled pictures with /f/ (e.g., "fish, foot, five") multiple times.
- Occasional substitutions still occurred when excited or speaking quickly, but overall spontaneous use of /f/ was noticeably improved.

Goal 3: /s/ in isolation, CV syllables, and initial /s/ words (imitation)
- Reviewed /s/ in isolation. Child showed more stable tongue placement and airflow with moderate cueing.
- In CV syllables, there was slightly increased consistency, though some productions remained distorted.
- Introduced a small set of initial /s/ words ("sun, soap, sock, soup") at the imitated word level. Child was able to approximate /s/ at the start of some words with modeling, but accuracy was variable, and distortions persisted.

Goal 4: /s/-blends – imitated word level
- Continued work on /s/-blends ("spoon, star, slide, spin, stop").
- Child produced some clearer blends with segmented practice, but still needed frequent reminders and visual cues.
- Overall performance was similar to the previous session, with small, gradual gains in consistency.

4. Plan/recommendations:

- Maintain focus on shaping a clear /s/ in isolation and simple syllables; keep initial /s/ words at the imitated level.
- Continue structured /s/-blend practice using backward chaining and slow rate.
- Parent encouraged to continue praising clear /f/ at home and to begin gently drawing attention to /s/ in a few simple words (e.g., "sun, sock") by modeling, not correcting.
notes/5/note_text:

Goal 2: Functional generalization of /f/
- During unstructured play and conversation, child frequently produced clear /f/ in familiar words without prompts.

- Maintain current level for /s/ (isolation, CV syllables, and simple initial /s/ words in imitation) while continuing to refine placement and airflow.

notes/7/note_text:

Goal 1: /f/ – generalization check

- Child produced accurate /f/ across a variety of words and sentence contexts with no direct cueing.

Goal 2: /s/ in isolation and syllables – increased consistency
- Practiced /s/ in isolation using mirror, visual cues, and auditory feedback.
- Child showed noticeably more consistent, central /s/ productions and was better able to self-monitor when the sound “didn’t feel right.”
