- Child produced many accurate /s/ on familiar words with visual/verbal cues; performance remained consistent with the last couple of sessions.
- Some variability persisted for newer or more complex words.

Goal 3: /s/-blends – imitated word level
- Continued /s/-blend practice using backward chaining.
- Child was able to produce several clear blends (e.g., "star," "snow") with support, though cluster reduction still occurred on a portion of trials.
- Overall, skills appeared stable compared to the previous session, reflecting continued consolidation.

Goal 4: Functional use in connected speech
- In structured play and conversation, /f/ remained well-established.
- Occasional clearer /s/ appeared in spontaneous words, but accuracy was still higher in structured tasks than in free speech.

4. Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to reinforce consistent /s/ production at the word level and maintain practice with /s/-blends.
- Begin to plan for gradual introduction of short phrases for a small set of well-established /s/ words once accuracy is consistently high.
- Parent encouraged to continue praising clear /s/ attempts and to model slow, clear speech during daily routines.
notes/11/note_text:
SESSION 12
Child age: 4;5

1. Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent reported that overall speech clarity remains similar to recent weeks.
- They continue to practice the picture words at home and feel child is more confident trying the “snake sound,” even if it is not always perfect.

2. Behaviour/engagement observations:
- Child transitioned well and showed familiarity with therapy routines.
- Engaged cooperatively in both drill and play activities, with good turn-taking.
- Mild fatigue noted near the end of the session, but child remained participatory.

3. Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: /s/ in isolation and syllables – brief review
- Quick review of /s/ in isolation and a few CV/VC syllables.
- Productions were consistent with previous sessions: mostly clear /s/ with intermittent mild distortion.
- Only occasional cues needed to support correct placement.

Goal 2: Initial /s/ words – imitated word level
- Practiced a familiar set of initial /s/ words in a game context.
- Child produced many accurate /s/ productions when provided with a model and light verbal cues, similar to last week.
- Some inconsistency remained, particularly with newer or less familiar words, but no significant change from the prior session.

Goal 3: /s/-blends – imitated word level
- Continued work on /s/-blends ("spoon, star, slide, spin, stop, snow, sky") using backward chaining and slow rate.
- Child produced a mix of accurate blends and cluster-reduced forms, with overall performance stable compared to recent sessions.
- Strategies such as segmenting and visual emphasis on the /s/ were still required.

Goal 4: Functional communication
- During play and brief conversation, /f/ remained accurate and well-generalized.
- Some spontaneous /s/ productions were clearer than earlier in the block, but connected speech still contained a mix of accurate and distorted /s/ sounds.

4. Plan/recommendations:
- Continue therapy with a focus on increasing consistency of /s/ at the word level and supporting generalization into phrases and connected speech when ready.
- Maintain practice with /s/-blends at the imitated word level until productions are more stable.
- Parent encouraged to keep home practice short and engaging, using the provided picture cards and modeling slow, clear /s/ in everyday words.
- Will review overall progress next block and consider updating goals to reflect gains with /f/ and ongoing needs with /s/ and clusters.

notes/0/note_text:
Session 1
Child age: 4 years

Parent/Caregiver Report:
- Mother reported that the pediatrician suggested monitoring speech clarity, especially with “s” and “f” sounds. She noted that family members understand the child most of the time, but peers and unfamiliar listeners often ask for repetitions.
- No concerns about hearing or overall health. Parent shared that the child will be starting JK in the fall and she would like speech to be clearer by then.

Behaviour/Engagement:
- Child transitioned to the therapy room with some hesitation but separated from mother without tears.
- Initially quiet and observed the clinician, then warmed up with preferred toys (cars, animals). Responded well to playful, game-based tasks.
- Attention span was variable; needed frequent redirection back to the table from free play but was generally cooperative.

Therapy Goals & Observations:
Goal 1: Establish /f/ in isolation and simple syllables (imitation)
- Baseline probing revealed frequent substitutions of /p/ or /b/ for /f/ in words (e.g., “pish” for “fish”).
- In isolation, child was able to approximate /f/ with visual modeling ("bite lip, blow air") in about half of attempts; often added a voice or stopped the airflow.
- In simple CV syllables ("foo, fah, fee"), productions were mostly inaccurate without cues. With direct imitation and visual cueing (mirror and clinician model), a few clear productions were noted but not yet consistent.

Goal 2: Establish /s/ in isolation and at the imitated syllable level
- Child tended to produce /d/ or /sh/ for /s/ (e.g., “done” for “sun” in conversation).
- In isolation, with a “snake sound” cue and visual model, child approximated a prolonged /s/ a few times but had difficulty maintaining airflow and tongue position.
- At the syllable level ("see, soo, sah"), accuracy was low; most attempts were distorted or shifted to /sh/ or /d/. Required maximal verbal and visual cueing.

Goal 3: Baseline for /s/ blends at the word level
- Probed /s/ blends in initial position (e.g., "star, spoon, slide, snake") in imitation.
- Child consistently reduced blends by omitting /s/ or the second consonant (e.g., “tar” for “star,” “poon” for “spoon”).
- Even with segmented modeling ("s- tar"), child had difficulty coordinating both sounds. No independent accurate productions observed today.

Plan/Recommendations:
- Continue to focus on establishing accurate /f/ and /s/ in isolation and simple syllables with heavy visual and verbal cueing.
- Introduce parent to simple cues: "bite your lip and blow" for /f/, and "quiet snake sound" for /s/ with tongue behind teeth.
- Encourage parent to model, not drill, during play (e.g., emphasize target sounds in key words like “fish, fun, sun, sock”).
- Next session: re-check isolation for /f/ and /s/, and begin more structured CV practice if accuracy improves.
notes/1/note_text:
Session 2

Parent/Caregiver Report:
- Mother reported no major changes since last week. She has been trying the “snake sound” and “bite lip” cues at home but feels the child is mostly just watching her, not copying yet.
- She noticed that the child is starting to correct “pish” to something closer to “fish” once in a while during bath time play.

Behaviour/Engagement:
- Child separated easily from mother today and appeared more comfortable in the therapy space.
- Participated well in turn-taking games and tolerated short table-top tasks between play activities.
- Some impulsivity (grabbing toys, moving quickly between activities), but could be redirected with clear expectations and visual supports.

Therapy Goals & Observations:
Goal 1: /f/ in isolation and CV syllables (imitation)
- With mirror and visual cueing, child produced a clearer /f/ in isolation in the majority of attempts when given time to set up the mouth position.
- Still occasionally voiced or switched to /p/, but there were more clearly recognizable /f/ productions than last week.
- In CV syllables ("fee, foo, fah, fuh"), child achieved a mix of accurate and approximate productions with modeling. Needed moderate verbal cueing (“bite your lip, blow slowly”). Some emerging consistency compared to baseline.

Goal 2: /s/ in isolation and simple syllables (imitation)
- Child responded well to the “snake” metaphor. With sustained modeling, could hold a short /s/ for a second or two several times.
- Still tends to let the tongue come forward, producing a slightly slushy sound, but this was less frequent than last session.
- In CV syllables ("see, soo, sah"), accuracy remained limited; some attempts were closer to /s/, others shifted to /sh/ or /d/. Required maximal cues and slow practice.

Goal 3: Initial /f/ in single words (imitation)
- Introduced a few high-interest /f/ words ("fish, fun, five, food") in a picture game.
- Child imitated initial /f/ accurately in a small number of attempts when given clear modeling and time to prepare, though many productions were still approximations or substitutions.
- Parent coached in-session on how to provide the same cueing at home.

Plan/Recommendations:
- Continue practicing /f/ in isolation and CV syllables; maintain word-level practice only when accuracy is acceptable at the syllable level.
- Keep /s/ focus at isolation and simple CV practice; not yet ready to move consistently to word-level.
- Home program: parent to practice a few /f/ words (fish, fun) in play-based routines, praising effort and clear sounds rather than drilling.
- Next session: reassess /f/ word-level and consider adding a few more functional /f/ words if consistency continues to improve.
notes/2/note_text:
Session 3

Parent/Caregiver Report:
- Mother reported that the child is now sometimes attempting the “bite lip and blow” posture without being asked, especially when looking at books with pictures of fish or food.
- She feels that “fish” and “five” are sounding a bit clearer to her, though not all the time.

Behaviour/Engagement:
- Child entered the room eagerly and requested specific toys from last week.
- Demonstrated better sustained attention during short drill-style tasks when they were embedded in a game (e.g., feeding animals, sticker chart).
- Needed some reminders to wait for their turn but overall participation was good.

Therapy Goals & Observations:
Goal 1: /f/ in initial position at the imitated word level
- Targeted high-interest words: “fish, four, five, fun, feet.”
- Child produced initial /f/ with recognizable accuracy in more than half of imitated attempts when given a model and brief reminder cues.
- Noted fewer substitutions of /p/ for /f” compared to previous sessions. Child sometimes self-corrected when prompted (“Try your lip-biting sound again.”).

Goal 2: /f/ in short carrier phrases (imitation)
- Introduced simple two-word phrases using picture supports (e.g., “my fish,” “big fish,” “fun food”).
- With clinician modeling, child was able to maintain initial /f/ in some phrases, though accuracy decreased slightly compared to single words.
- Required visual cueing and slower rate; emerging but not yet consistent.

Goal 3: /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Continued work on sustained /s/ with “snake sound” imagery. Child produced a longer, more stable /s/ in many trials, though some lateral airflow and slight distortion persisted.
- In CV syllables ("see, soo, sah"), productions were somewhat clearer than last week. Still needed maximal verbal and visual support but produced more approximations that were clearly targeting /s/.

Plan/Recommendations:
- Maintain focus on initial /f/ at word level, gradually increasing the variety of words if accuracy continues to rise.
- Begin incorporating short, highly supported carrier phrases with /f/ when child is successful at the word level.
- Continue shaping /s/ in isolation and syllables; not yet ready to emphasize /s/ in words.
- Home practice: parent to use picture cards or books to practice a small set of /f/ words and model two-word phrases (e.g., “my fish,” “fun food”) when possible.
notes/3/note_text:
Session 4

Parent/Caregiver Report:
- Mother shared that the child is now frequently saying “fish” during bath time and is “very proud” when she notices the clear /f/ sound.
- She has also heard attempts at “five” and “funny” that sound closer to adult-like productions. She has not noticed much change yet with “s” words like “sun.”

Behaviour/Engagement:
- Child was enthusiastic and talkative today, frequently initiating interaction.
- Tolerated alternating between table tasks and play without significant resistance.
- Occasional silliness when tasks became more challenging, but responded well to structured choices and praise.

Therapy Goals & Observations:
Goal 1: Initial /f/ at word level (imitation and emerging spontaneous use)
- Used picture lotto and a fishing game with target words (fish, four, five, fun, feet, fan).
- Child produced initial /f/ accurately in the majority of imitated opportunities, often needing only a brief visual cue or no cue at all.
- Notably, child began to spontaneously say “fish” and “five” during play without prompts, often with a clear /f/.

Goal 2: Initial /f/ in short carrier phrases and 2–3 word combinations
- Practiced “my fish,” “big fish,” “five fish,” “fun food” with picture supports.
- Accuracy remained fairly high when the clinician modeled the entire phrase first, then faded to just a model of the target word.
- Child could sometimes maintain /f/ in the phrase without needing a repeat model, suggesting increasing independence at this level.

Goal 3: /s/ in isolation and CV syllables; begin probing initial /s/ in words
- Continued work on “snake sound” in isolation. Child able to produce a longer, more focused /s/ with moderate verbal and visual cues.
- CV syllables ("see, soo, suh") were somewhat more consistent than last session, though distortions persisted.
- Briefly probed a few initial /s/ words (sun, soup, sock) in imitation. Child often produced /d/ or /sh/ instead; will continue to focus on strengthening the sound in isolation/syllables before extended word-level practice.

Plan/Recommendations:
- Continue building consistency for /f/ in phrases, gradually reducing cues and increasing variety of phrases.
- Maintain /s/ at isolation and syllable levels, with occasional short probes at the word level to monitor readiness.
- Home practice: encourage daily use of a few /f/ phrases (e.g., “my fish,” “five fish”) during play, and keep modeling clear /s/ in simple “snake” games without pressuring the child to imitate every time.
notes/4/note_text:
Session 5

Parent/Caregiver Report:
- Mother reported that the child is now using “fish” and “five” spontaneously at home and sometimes reminds himself with “lip sound” when prompted.
- She has also noticed clearer /f/ in other words like “food” at mealtimes, though not every time.

Behaviour/Engagement:
- Child came in with high energy and eagerness to show the clinician a toy from home.
- Able to stay engaged in structured tasks for longer stretches today, especially when targets were embedded in games.
- Mild distractibility but responded well to brief movement breaks.

Therapy Goals & Observations:
Goal 1: Initial /f/ in words and short phrases (imitation and spontaneous)
- Targeted a wider range of /f/ words (fish, four, five, fun, feet, food, fire, fork) in board games and picture scenes.
- Child produced clear initial /f/ in most imitated productions, with occasional lapses to /p/ that he often corrected with a brief cue.
- Spontaneous use of “fish,” “five,” and “food” in play was frequent and mostly accurate, indicating growing generalization.

Goal 2: Initial /f/ in longer 3–4 word phrases (imitation)
- Practiced short sentences such as “I see a fish,” “I want five fish,” “Look at the food.”
- Child was able to maintain /f/ in many of these phrases when modeled first. Accuracy dipped slightly as phrase length increased, but overall productions were understandable.
- Required some reminders to slow down and “use the lip sound,” but less cueing than earlier sessions.

Goal 3: /s/ in isolation, CV syllables, and limited word-level practice
- Continued practicing sustained /s/ with mirror and verbal cues. Child produced several stable, recognizable /s/ sounds with moderate cueing.
- CV syllables ("see, soo, suh") showed improving control, though occasional distortions persisted.
- Brief word-level practice with high-interest words (sun, soap, sock) at the imitated level. Child continued to substitute /d/ or /sh/ in many attempts but demonstrated a few closer approximations to /s/ today, indicating emerging improvement.

Plan/Recommendations:
- Continue to strengthen /f/ in increasingly complex phrases and short sentences while monitoring for carryover into spontaneous conversation.
- Maintain systematic practice for /s/ in isolation and syllables; continue light word-level probes to track progress.
- Home program: parent to model and encourage short /f/ phrases (“I see fish,” “more food please”) during daily routines, and play “snake sound” games, praising any attempts at /s/ regardless of accuracy.
notes/5/note_text:
Session 6

Parent/Caregiver Report:
- Mother noted that the child’s “f” words are “much easier to understand” at home and that siblings are starting to imitate the cues in a playful way.
- She has noticed only occasional clearer “s” sounds and feels that those are still hard for the child.

Behaviour/Engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly and appeared very comfortable with the routine.
- Engaged well in both structured and play-based tasks; occasionally tried to rush through drills but responded to visual timers and clear expectations.
- Demonstrated increased willingness to attempt challenging sounds.

Therapy Goals & Observations:
Goal 1: Initial /f/ in short sentences and structured conversation
- Used picture sequences and storybooks to elicit sentences such as “The fish is big,” “I have five fish,” “The food is hot.”
- Child produced initial /f/ accurately in most sentence-level imitations, requiring minimal verbal cueing.
- During structured play, child spontaneously used /f/ in many familiar words (fish, five, food) with good clarity. Occasional errors occurred in less familiar /f/ words but were infrequent.

Goal 2: Maintain /f/ in mixed contexts (initial and some medial positions)
- Introduced a few medial /f/ words (sofa, coffee in pretend play) in imitation.
- Child could approximate medial /f/ with support, though this was more challenging than initial position.
- Focus remained on consolidating strong initial /f/ performance while beginning to expose the child to other word positions.

Goal 3: /s/ in isolation, CV syllables, and initial word-level (imitation)
- Continued intensive practice on /s/ isolation. Child able to produce a more stable “snake” sound when watching in the mirror and following tongue placement cues.
- CV syllables ("see, soo, say, suh") were more consistent, though still not fully accurate.
- At the word level (sun, soup, sock, soap), child continued to show a mix of /d/, /sh/, and improved /s/-like attempts. A few clearer /s/ productions were noted with maximal cueing and slow rate, suggesting slow but steady gains.

Plan/Recommendations:
- Continue consolidating /f/ at sentence level and in structured conversation; consider adding more varied vocabulary and positions once stability is confirmed.
- Maintain targeted practice for /s/ at isolation and syllable levels, with limited high-support word practice.
- Home practice: parent to encourage the child to use “f” words in short phrases during everyday activities and to play simple mirror games for “snake sounds” 2–3 times per week.
notes/6/note_text:
Session 7

Parent/Caregiver Report:
- Mother reported that the child’s “f” sound is now “pretty good most of the time,” even when he’s excited.
- She has noticed a few clearer attempts at “sun” and “sock,” but these are still inconsistent.

Behaviour/Engagement:
- Child arrived cheerful and eager to start.
- Demonstrated good participation in both structured tasks and free play. Needed minimal prompts to stay seated during short drill segments.
- Seemed proud of speech successes and responded well to specific praise.

Therapy Goals & Observations:
Goal 1: Maintain /f/ accuracy in connected speech
- Used open-ended play scenes and picture description to elicit spontaneous sentences with /f/ words.
- Child maintained clear initial /f/ in familiar words (fish, five, food, fun) in most spontaneous utterances.
- Occasional errors in new /f/ words were quickly corrected with a brief verbal reminder. Skills appear to be consolidating at this level.

Goal 2: /s/ in isolation and syllables; expand to more systematic word-level practice
- Continued “snake sound” practice with mirror. Child produced a clearer, more consistent /s/ with moderate cues.
- Practiced CV and VC syllables ("see, soo, iss, us") with improved control. Distortions were reduced but still present intermittently.
- At the word level (sun, soap, sock, soup, sad), child demonstrated a mix of approximations and substitutions. A few words were produced with a closer-to-target /s/ following detailed cueing (e.g., “teeth together, blow air”). Emerging improvement noted but not yet reliable.

Goal 3: Initial /s/ blends (baseline probe)
- Briefly probed /s/ blends (stop, spin, star, spoon) at the imitated word level.
- Child typically reduced blends (e.g., “top” for “stop,” “tar” for “star”).
- Given the ongoing work on single /s/, blends will remain a longer-term target.

Plan/Recommendations:
- Continue to monitor /f/ in conversation and maintain opportunities for practice in longer phrases and varied contexts.
- Keep primary focus on strengthening /s/ at isolation and syllable levels, with supported word practice.
- Home program: parent to continue praising correct /f/ in everyday speech and to introduce a few consistent /s/ practice words (sun, sock) using the “snake sound” cues, focusing on short, positive practice rather than long drills.
notes/7/note_text:
Session 8

Parent/Caregiver Report:
- Mother indicated that speech at home “sounds about the same” as last week. /f/ continues to be strong, while /s/ is still hit-or-miss.
- She has been using picture cards for “sun” and “sock” but feels the child sometimes gets silly when they practice.

Behaviour/Engagement:
- Child transitioned without difficulty and appeared comfortable.
- Participation was generally good, though there were a few moments of avoidance (changing the topic, joking) when tasks became more challenging.
- Responded well to breaks and visual schedules.

Therapy Goals & Observations:
Goal 1: Maintain /f/ in connected speech and mixed positions
- Used play-based tasks (picnic, grocery store) to elicit /f/ in a variety of words.
- Child continued to produce clear initial /f/ in familiar words with little to no cueing.
- Some emerging accuracy in medial /f/ (e.g., “sofa”) with modeling, though this was not a primary focus. Overall /f/ skills appear stable.

Goal 2: /s/ in isolation and syllables
- Revisited “snake sound” with mirror and tongue placement cues. Child could produce several good-quality /s/ sounds in a row with moderate support.
- Practiced CV and VC syllables ("see, soo, iss, us, es") with similar performance to last week. Some clear productions, mixed with distorted or shifted sounds.

Goal 3: Initial /s/ at the word level (imitation)
- Target words: sun, soap, sock, soup, sand.
- Child continued to show variability: some attempts were closer to target with strong cueing (e.g., “teeth together, smile, blow”), while others reverted to /d/ or /sh/.
- Overall, performance was similar to the previous session, indicating a period of consolidation rather than significant change.

Plan/Recommendations:
- Continue reinforcing /f/ in natural conversation, with periodic checks for consistency.
- Maintain structured practice on /s/ in isolation and syllables; keep using a small set of familiar /s/ words for practice.
- Home practice: encourage very short, focused practice for “sun” and “sock” once daily, keeping it playful to reduce avoidance. Emphasize quality over quantity.
notes/8/note_text:
Session 9

Parent/Caregiver Report:
- Mother reported that the child’s overall speech clarity is “better than a few months ago,” especially with “f” words.
- She has not noticed a big difference in “s” words since last week, but she does hear occasional closer attempts.

Behaviour/Engagement:
- Child entered the session cheerfully and followed the established routine.
- Attended well to games and picture tasks, though needed intermittent redirection when tasks became repetitive.
- Showed good frustration tolerance and remained willing to attempt difficult sounds.

Therapy Goals & Observations:
Goal 1: /f/ in connected speech and new vocabulary
- Used storybooks and pretend play to elicit /f/ in less familiar words (e.g., “fairy,” “forest,” “sofa”).
- Child maintained accurate /f/ in most initial positions and showed some success with medial /f/ when provided a quick model.
- Skills for /f/ remained stable and well-established across a range of contexts.

Goal 2: /s/ in isolation and syllables
- Continued “snake sound” work using mirror and tactile cues (touching at the corners of the mouth to indicate smile posture).
- Child produced a consistent /s/ in isolation in many attempts with moderate cues, similar to last week.
- CV and VC syllable practice ("see, soo, iss, us, es") yielded a combination of clear productions and mild distortions. Overall pattern was comparable to previous session.

Goal 3: Initial /s/ words (imitation)
- Practiced a set of familiar words: sun, soap, sock, soup, sand, see.
- Child produced some near-target /s/ productions, especially when slowed down and given step-by-step cues. However, substitutions (/d/, /sh/) remained frequent.
- No substantial change in overall accuracy compared to last week, but the child remained engaged and motivated.

Plan/Recommendations:
- Continue to monitor /f/ in conversation; no new /f/-specific goals needed at this time beyond maintenance.
- Maintain current level of practice for /s/ at isolation, syllable, and high-support word level, focusing on consistency and comfort with the sound.
- Home program: parent encouraged to use brief daily “snake sound” mirror play and to keep practicing a few chosen /s/ words, celebrating any close approximations.
notes/9/note_text:
Session 10

Parent/Caregiver Report:
- Mother shared that the child’s preschool teacher has commented positively on overall speech clarity, particularly noticing clearer “f” words.
- No major changes in “s” sounds reported since the last session; still variable.

Behaviour/Engagement:
- Child transitioned easily and appeared very comfortable with the clinician.
- Maintained attention well during short drill tasks when they were embedded in games.
- Occasional silliness when tasks were challenging, but quickly redirected with visual supports.

Therapy Goals & Observations:
Goal 1: Maintain /f/ skills across settings
- Used conversational activities and picture description to sample /f/ in spontaneous speech.
- Child consistently produced clear initial /f/ in familiar and less familiar words with no cueing.
- Occasional checking of medial /f/ (e.g., “sofa”) showed continued improvement with a simple model. Overall performance remained stable and strong.

Goal 2: /s/ in isolation and syllables
- Repeated “snake sound” practice with mirror, focusing on tongue placement and airflow.
- Child produced several accurate or near-accurate /s/ sounds in isolation with moderate cueing, similar to recent sessions.
- CV and VC syllables ("see, soo, ess, us, es") showed a comparable pattern of mixed clear and distorted productions.

Goal 3: Initial /s/ words (imitation)
- Targeted words: sun, soap, sock, soup, sand, see, seat.
- Child continued to require significant cueing to achieve clearer /s/, with ongoing substitutions and distortions.
- A few productions were quite close to target when the child slowed down and focused on the “snake sound,” but overall accuracy was similar to prior sessions, indicating a continued consolidation phase.

Plan/Recommendations:
- Maintain /f/ at the conversational level; consider formally documenting as an area of strength while continuing informal monitoring.
- Continue systematic work on /s/ in isolation and syllables, with consistent, high-support practice at the word level.
- Home practice: parent to keep sessions short and positive, using a visual cue (picture of a snake) to remind the child about tongue and teeth placement for /s/.
notes/10/note_text:
Session 11

Parent/Caregiver Report:
- Mother reported that the child’s speech overall is “much clearer” to family and teachers, with very few concerns about /f/ now.
- She continues to notice that “s” words are sometimes clearer and sometimes not, with no obvious pattern.

Behaviour/Engagement:
- Child entered happily and quickly engaged with the clinician.
- Participated well in both structured and play-based tasks, with minimal need for redirection.
- Demonstrated good effort and persistence with difficult sounds.

Therapy Goals & Observations:
Goal 1: /f/ maintenance in spontaneous conversation
- Throughout free play and conversation, child used many /f/ words (fish, five, food, fun, favorite) with clear initial /f/.
- No notable regression observed. Occasional errors in more complex words were self-corrected with minimal prompting.

Goal 2: /s/ in isolation and syllables
- Continued isolation practice with mirror and detailed tongue placement cues.
- Child produced a series of reasonably clear /s/ sounds in many trials, though some lateral airflow and distortions persisted.
- Syllable practice ("see, soo, ess, es, us") remained similar to previous sessions, with a mix of accurate and approximate productions.

Goal 3: Initial /s/ at the word level (imitation)
- Practiced a consistent set of words: sun, soap, sock, soup, sad, see, seat.
- Child continued to show variable performance: some improved productions with heavy cueing, alongside frequent substitutions.
- Overall, this reflected a stable pattern relative to prior weeks, with no clear jump in accuracy but ongoing willingness to attempt.

Plan/Recommendations:
- Continue reinforcing /f/ in natural conversation and document as a consolidated skill.
- Maintain current level of intervention for /s/, focusing on building consistency and confidence in isolation and syllable practice.
- Home program: parent to keep using “snake sound” activities and to highlight any good /s/ productions, providing specific praise (e.g., “I heard your good snake sound in ‘sun’!”).
notes/11/note_text:
Session 12

Parent/Caregiver Report:
- Mother stated that she feels very happy with progress on “f” sounds and overall intelligibility.
- She still notices ups and downs with “s” words, but thinks the child is trying more often.
- Family is interested in continuing services to keep working on “s” and eventually on /s/ blends.

Behaviour/Engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly, greeted the clinician spontaneously, and initiated conversation.
- Demonstrated good engagement throughout, with only brief moments of fatigue during repetitive practice.
- Responded well to visual schedules and reinforcement.

Therapy Goals & Observations:
Goal 1: /f/ maintenance in conversation and narrative
- Used story retell and open-ended questions to elicit spontaneous speech.
- Child consistently produced clear initial /f/ in a variety of words without cues.
- Occasional checks of medial and final /f/ showed generally accurate productions when modeled, indicating strong maintenance of this sound.

Goal 2: /s/ in isolation and syllables
- Revisited “snake sound” with mirror, focusing on a stable tongue posture and steady airflow.
- Child produced multiple good-quality /s/ sounds with moderate support, similar to recent weeks.
- CV and VC syllables ("see, soo, ess, es, us") remained mixed: some clearly recognizable /s/, alongside occasional distortions.

Goal 3: Initial /s/ words and brief probe of /s/ blends (imitation)
- Practiced initial /s/ words (sun, soap, sock, soup, see, seat) with picture supports.
- Performance continued to be variable: improved approximations in some trials with intensive cueing, but not yet stable across the word set.
- Briefly probed /s/ blends (stop, star, spoon, spin). Child typically reduced the blends (e.g., “top,” “tar”) and required maximal support. This confirmed that blends remain a future goal.

Plan/Recommendations:
- /f/ sound: continue to monitor informally; no further direct intervention needed for initial /f/ at this time.
- /s/ sound: continue therapy focusing on stabilizing /s/ in isolation and syllables, with gradual expansion to more consistent word-level production.
- Recommend ongoing parent involvement with short, positive home practice for /s/ using mirror play and a small, consistent set of words.
- Next block: prioritize /s/ at the word and simple phrase level once accuracy in structured word practice improves; defer intensive work on /s/ blends until single /s/ is more reliable.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that she and the daycare staff understand most of his speech, but unfamiliar listeners still ask for frequent repetitions. She noted that he often leaves off the ends of words (e.g., “ca” for “cat”). No recent changes in hearing, health, or routines.

Behaviour/engagement:
- 4-year-old boy attended with his mother. Transitioned into the therapy room with mild hesitation but joined the activities after a brief warm-up game. Sat at the table for short periods, then preferred floor-based play. Responded well to visual supports and turn-taking games. Some silliness noted when tasks became more challenging; responded to redirection and praise.

Goals / therapy activities:
1. Final consonant production (CV → CVC words: /p, m, t/)
- Targeted final consonants in simple picture naming (e.g., "map," "cap," "mom," "mat").
- In imitation, he was able to add a final sound in roughly half of opportunities; productions were often weak or omitted in spontaneous attempts (e.g., “ma” for “map”).
- Benefited from direct models, tapping the table for the final sound, and verbal cueing ("close your word").

2. Initial /k/ in isolation and simple syllables (CV)
- Practiced /k/ in isolation using a "coughing" and "back sound" cue. Accuracy was inconsistent; he often substituted /t/ (fronting).
- In CV syllables (e.g., “ku,” “ki,” “ko”), he produced a few accurate attempts with maximal verbal and visual cues (open mouth, tongue back).
- Required frequent reminders and slowed rate to achieve correct placement.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and simple CV syllables
- Introduced /s/ as a "quiet snake" sound. In isolation, he could approximate /s/ with a prolonged airflow when watching the clinician’s mouth.
- In CV syllables (e.g., “see,” “soo”), accuracy was low; he often produced /d/ or omitted the sound entirely.
- Tolerated visual cues (mirror, clinician model) and some tactile cues (pointing to teeth and lips), but needed high support.

4. Syllable structure: CV and CVCV
- Observed spontaneous use of a variety of CV and reduplicated CVCV forms in play (e.g., “mama,” “baba,” “dodo”).
- Longer words showed syllable reduction (e.g., “nana” for “banana”).
- Used modeling and clapping to highlight all the "beats" in words.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to target final consonants in simple CVC words and initial /k/ and /s/ at isolation and CV levels.
- Encourage parents to emphasize final sounds during daily routines (e.g., “cup—cuP,” “bed—beD”) with slow, clear models, without pressuring him to repeat every time.
- Plan to gather additional baseline data next session and consider introducing simple word-level tasks if consistency improves.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported no major changes since last visit. She has been trying to slow down her own speech and emphasize end sounds. She notices he sometimes adds a final sound when reminded but usually reverts to his usual pattern in conversation.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Transitioned smoothly into the room today and separated from caregiver without difficulty. Remained engaged with table-top games for slightly longer intervals than last session. Needed occasional movement breaks but returned to tasks with simple prompts. Enjoyed turn-taking and was motivated by stickers.

Goals / therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words (/p, m, t/)
- Practiced final /p, m, t/ using picture cards and a simple board game. Compared to last session, he produced more consistent final sounds in imitation, particularly for /m/ and /p/ (e.g., "map," "mom").
- Still frequently omitted final consonants in spontaneous naming, but with cues such as "say it again, close your word," he showed emerging attempts to add the ending.

2. Initial /k/ in CV and CVC syllables
- Continued work on /k/ as a "back sound" using mirror, tongue-back cues, and "cough" trick.
- Demonstrated more frequent accurate /k/ in CV syllables (e.g., "koo," "kah") in imitation compared to baseline; substitutions to /t/ still common without cues.
- Introduced a few simple CVC words ("car," "key") at the imitated level. He approximated the /k/ in some trials but needed direct modeling and slowed rate.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and CV
- Practiced /s/ in isolation using a straw and snake visuals. He was able to hold a clearer /s/ for short durations in imitation when focusing on airflow and tongue position.
- In CV syllables, he continued to show low accuracy, often producing /d/ or an indistinct sound. However, he attempted more repetitions per trial and was more willing to watch the clinician’s mouth.

4. Syllable awareness for multisyllabic words
- Used clapping and pacing boards for longer words (e.g., "banana," "butterfly"). He participated and could match the number of "claps" with clinician support.
- Production remained reduced (e.g., "nana" for "banana"), but he tolerated modeling and repetition well.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain current targets, as skills are just beginning to emerge. Continue intensive practice of final consonants and initial /k/ at syllable and simple word levels.
- Encourage home practice with 5–10 CVC words, focusing on strong final sounds, using a "tap" on the table or child’s hand to mark the ending.
- Consider adding simple carrier phrases ("my ___") with CVC words once final consonants become more consistent.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother noted that he is starting to "catch" himself on some words and occasionally repeats them with a clearer ending when she models it. She feels he is a bit easier to understand at home, though daycare staff have not reported big changes yet.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Entered the therapy room eagerly and selected preferred toys. Stayed engaged in structured activities for most of the session with minimal redirection. Responded positively to specific praise for "strong sounds" and enjoyed earning turns in a game.

Goals / therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words (/p, m, t/)
- Targeted final consonants in a picture-based board game. Compared to last week, he produced clearer final sounds in imitation and began to add them more often in prompted naming.
- For familiar words ("map," "cup," "mom," "hot"), he demonstrated emerging consistency, often achieving a noticeable final consonant after one reminder.
- Still omitted final sounds in unprompted speech, but showed more attempts to self-correct when given a visual cue (tapping for the last sound).

2. Initial /k/ in CVC words
- Focused on initial /k/ in simple words ("car," "cup," "key," "cow") in a play-based car game.
- Accuracy at the imitated word level improved relative to last session; fewer substitutions to /t/ when given a verbal cue ("tongue back").
- Still required moderate verbal and visual cueing, but there were several independent correct /k/ productions during play (e.g., spontaneous "car" while pushing the toy car).

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Continued practice of /s/ in isolation and with simple vowels. Accuracy in isolation improved; he could produce a clearer, longer "snake" sound in most imitated trials.
- In CV syllables ("see," "soo"), he produced more recognizable /s/ sounds, though consistency remained limited. Often needed a reminder to keep his tongue behind his teeth and to "blow the air." 

4. Syllable structure / multisyllabic words
- Practiced tapping and jumping for each syllable in target words ("banana," "dinosaur," "butterfly"). He participated enthusiastically.
- Still reduced syllables in speech, but attempted more full-word repetitions after models.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue at the word level for final consonants and initial /k/, with a mix of imitation and cued independent productions. Not yet ready for phrase-level work consistently but may trial short carrier phrases for highly familiar CVC words.
- Encourage caregivers to praise any attempts at final sounds and back sounds ("I heard your /k/ in 'car'!").
- Provide a short home list of 6–8 CVC /p, m, t/ words and 3–4 initial /k/ words for daily practice, focusing on quality over quantity.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that he is using clearer endings for a small set of practiced words at home (e.g., "cup," "map") when they play their "sound game." She also noticed he is starting to say "car" and "key" more clearly. She still finds longer sentences difficult to understand, but feels "single words are better."

Behaviour/engagement:
- Arrived in good spirits and transitioned easily. Demonstrated sustained attention to a picture card game and a car track activity. Followed simple directions well. Showed pride in his speech when the clinician highlighted "strong ending sounds."

Goals / therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words (/p, m, t/): imitation → cued independent
- Worked on final consonants in a fishing game and picture naming.
- At the imitated word level, he now produced final /p, m, t/ with good consistency across a set of familiar and some new words.
- With minimal cues (visual tap, brief reminder), he was increasingly able to produce final consonants independently in structured tasks. Spontaneous productions during play remained less consistent but were more frequent than in previous sessions.

2. Initial /k/ in CVC words and early carrier phrases
- Targeted initial /k/ words ("car," "cup," "cat," "cow") in a car race game.
- Accuracy for /k/ at the imitated word level improved; he produced a clear back sound in most trials with light verbal cueing.
- Introduced simple carrier phrases ("my car," "my cup") using picture supports. He could maintain an accurate /k/ in the word within the phrase with modeling and occasional repetition.

3. Initial /s/ in CV and CVC words
- Moved from isolated /s/ to simple CV and CVC words ("see," "sun," "sock," "soap").
- Accuracy in imitation was higher than in previous weeks, though he still sometimes substituted /d/ or produced a short, distorted sound.
- Benefited from a mirror, "teeth together" cues, and prolonged airflow. Showed better success when he slowed down.

4. Syllable structure: multisyllabic words
- Practiced clapping and segmenting 3-syllable words ("banana," "dinosaur").
- He was able to match the number of syllables more consistently, though his spoken productions continued to drop a syllable at times.

Plan / recommendations:
- Begin to systematically practice short carrier phrases with familiar CVC and initial /k/ words in therapy.
- At home, parents can use simple frames ("my ___," "big ___") with practiced words, modeling clearly and praising any attempt.
- Continue to build consistency with /s/ at the word level in imitation before moving to phrases.
- Monitor readiness for more independent phrase-level productions over the next few sessions.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother shared that family members are commenting that his words are "clearer" and they are asking for fewer repetitions for single words. She notices he sometimes uses his "sound words" (with good endings) even when not directly prompted. No concerns from daycare; they report he is talkative and social.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Very engaged today, quickly joined the clinician at the table. Participated actively in structured games and followed turn-taking rules with minimal reminders. Some excitement and fast talking during play, which impacted clarity; he responded well when reminded to use his "slow, strong sounds."

Goals / therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words → short phrases
- Practiced final /p, m, t/ in familiar and new CVC words ("cup," "map," "ham," "boat") within a picture sequencing and "shopping" game.
- At the single-word level, he produced final consonants with high accuracy in imitation and increasing accuracy in cued independent productions.
- Introduced short 2–3 word phrases (e.g., "my cup," "big map," "hot boat"), and he was generally able to maintain the final consonant when the clinician modeled the entire phrase first.

2. Initial /k/ in CVC words and carrier phrases
- Continued practice with initial /k/ words ("car," "cup," "cat," "cookie") in a pretend kitchen activity.
- Accuracy of /k/ at the word level was notably better than previous weeks; fewer substitutions to /t" were heard, especially when he watched the clinician’s model.
- Used carrier phrases ("I see car," "my cookie") with visual sentence strips. With modeling and a brief pause before the /k/ word, he maintained a back sound in many attempts.

3. Initial /s/ at the word level
- Targeted initial /s/ words ("sun," "soap," "sock," "sand") using a sensory bin.
- He produced more recognizable /s/ sounds in imitation, with fewer /d/ substitutions. Still needed reminders for tongue placement and airflow, but overall productions were clearer than in earlier sessions.
- Occasional spontaneous attempts at /s/ words were heard in play (e.g., "sock"), though not yet consistent.

4. Multisyllabic word production
- Practiced 3-syllable words ("banana," "dinosaur," "umbrella") with clapping and pacing.
- He showed slightly improved marking of all syllables when repeating after the clinician, but consonant clarity within the words remained variable.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue building phrase-level practice for CVC words and initial /k/ within structured tasks, gradually reducing models.
- Maintain focus on initial /s/ at the word level; not yet ready for consistent phrase-level practice.
- Home program: encourage 5–10 minutes daily of "sound sentences" using simple frames ("my ___," "I see ___") with practiced CVC and /k/ words; emphasize slow rate and clear endings.
- Plan to probe for more independent phrase productions next session.
notes/5/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that he now often says "car" and "cup" with a good /k/ without needing a reminder, especially during play. She still hears dropped endings when he talks quickly in sentences but thinks his short phrases are easier to understand.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Came in cheerful and transitioned independently. Maintained attention for the majority of structured tasks, with brief breaks built into play. Followed visual schedules well. Showed good effort and enjoyed "fixing" his words when the clinician provided feedback.

Goals / therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words and short phrases
- Practiced final /p, m, t/ in a picture lotto game and during simple role-play.
- At the word level, accuracy remained high with minimal prompting. In short 2–3 word phrases ("my cup," "big map," "hot soup"), he maintained the final consonant in many imitated and some cued independent productions.
- Still more likely to drop final sounds in longer utterances or when excited, but overall consistency at the phrase level is improving.

2. Initial /k/ in phrases and early sentences
- Targeted initial /k/ words within short phrases and simple 3–4 word sentences (e.g., "I see a car," "my cat is big") using visual sentence strips.
- He produced accurate /k/ in most imitated phrases and required fewer direct placement cues than in previous sessions.
- Some independent phrases with correct /k/ were observed during play (e.g., "my car go"), suggesting carryover.

3. Initial /s/ in CVC words
- Continued work on initial /s/ words ("sun," "sock," "soap," "sand," "soup").
- At the imitated word level, he showed gradual improvement; productions were more consistent and clearer, though still less stable than /k/ and final consonants.
- Needed moderate verbal and visual cueing (mirror, "teeth together") but was able to self-correct occasionally when given feedback.

4. Multisyllabic words
- Practiced 3-syllable words in a turn-taking game.
- Syllable marking was somewhat more consistent compared to earlier weeks, but consonant accuracy within those longer words still lagged behind his performance on shorter CVC words.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue phrase-level practice for final consonants and initial /k/, maintaining a mix of imitation and cued independence.
- Keep initial /s/ at the word level, with occasional trial of short phrases for easier /s/ words if accuracy remains stable.
- Home practice: encourage parents to pause and let him repeat if he drops final sounds in short phrases, using a positive cue ("let's say it with your strong ending").
- Plan to gather more data on spontaneous phrase use next session.
notes/6/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother shared that he is starting to use clearer sounds, especially /k/ and some final consonants, in short sentences at home (e.g., "my car here"). She still notices difficulty with /s/ and longer words. No new medical or hearing concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Entered willingly and quickly engaged with toys. Showed good participation in both structured and play-based tasks. Occasionally rushed his speech when excited; responded to cues to "slow down and use strong sounds." Overall regulation and cooperation were good.

Goals / therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in phrases and short sentences
- Practiced final /p, m, t/ within short sentences (e.g., "I see a cup," "Mom has soup") using picture scenes.
- At the phrase level, his final consonants were generally consistent when he had a model. In short sentences, he maintained many of the final sounds, particularly for familiar words, though omissions were still noted when he spoke quickly.
- Demonstrated occasional self-monitoring by repeating a word with a clearer ending after clinician feedback.

2. Initial /k/ in sentences and structured play
- Targeted /k/ in sentences ("The car is red," "The cat is big") and during a car track activity.
- Produced accurate /k/ in most imitated sentences with only light reminders. In structured play, several spontaneous sentences with correct /k/ were noted (e.g., "car go down," "my car fast").
- Continued to show reduced need for explicit placement cues compared to earlier sessions.

3. Initial /s/ in CVC words and trial phrases
- Continued initial /s/ words and introduced simple two-word phrases ("sun up," "sock on") for easier items.
- At the single-word level, accuracy was improving but still variable. In short phrases, he often maintained the /s/ when closely imitating the clinician.
- Needed frequent reminders for tongue placement and airflow; however, he tolerated cues and attempted multiple repetitions.

4. Multisyllabic words
- Practiced a few functional multisyllabic words (e.g., "spider," "banana," "dinosaur") within a storybook activity.
- He attempted all targets, with some improvement in syllable marking, but consonant clarity remained inconsistent.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain current focus on phrase and short sentence production for final consonants and /k/ while continuing to strengthen /s/ at the word and early phrase level.
- Encourage caregivers to model slow, clear sentences at home and to highlight a few "practice words" each day rather than correcting everything.
- Plan to assess readiness for more independent sentence-level targets for final consonants in upcoming sessions.
notes/7/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported noticeable improvement in his overall clarity. She said family members understand him better in short sentences, and he is starting to "fix" some words on his own. She still hears substitutions on /s/ ("dun" for "sun") and some dropped endings when he talks very quickly.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Arrived with high energy but was easily redirected into structured tasks using a visual schedule. Showed strong motivation when using games that allowed him to "earn" cars and stickers. Demonstrated good persistence with challenging words and seemed proud of his progress.

Goals / therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in short sentences and structured conversation
- Targeted final /p, m, t/ in short 3–5 word sentences and simple question/answer formats (e.g., "What is it?" "It is a cup.").
- He produced final consonants with good consistency in imitated and cued sentences. In structured conversation around picture scenes, he often maintained final sounds on familiar words even without direct prompting.
- Occasional omissions still occurred in longer or more spontaneous utterances, but overall accuracy at the sentence level was meaningfully improved.

2. Initial /k/ in sentences and semi-spontaneous speech
- Practiced /k/ in 3–5 word sentences ("The car is fast," "I like cookies") and during a car-themed pretend play.
- Accuracy for /k/ remained strong across tasks, with many spontaneous correct productions during play. Very few /t/ substitutions were noted.
- Minimal cueing was required, typically only a reminder to slow down.

3. Initial /s/ in CVC words and two-word phrases
- Focused intensively on initial /s/ words ("sun," "soap," "sock," "sand," "seat") and short phrases ("sun up," "soap on") using a sensory bin.
- At the word level, he produced a clear /s/ in a majority of imitated trials. In short phrases, he was able to carry over the /s/ in many attempts when given a strong model and visual cue.
- Still less consistent than with /k/ and final consonants, but there was a clear increase in the number of successful /s/ productions compared to earlier sessions.

4. Multisyllabic words in structured tasks
- Practiced 3-syllable words ("banana," "dinosaur," "umbrella") within a turn-taking game.
- He showed improved marking of all syllables in imitation, though consonant accuracy remained variable. He appeared more aware of the need to "say all the parts" of the word.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue reinforcing final consonants and /k/ at the sentence level and in structured conversation; begin shifting some time to focus more intensively on /s/ accuracy and generalization.
- Encourage parents to choose a few /s/ words to practice daily, using a mirror at home and emphasizing "teeth together, long air."
- Plan to maintain current level for /s/ (word and short phrase) for consolidation while monitoring for readiness to expand to more spontaneous use.
notes/8/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother stated that over the past week his speech has sounded similar to the previous week. She continues to hear good /k/ and better endings in short sentences, but /s/ is still inconsistent. She feels overall intelligibility is better than at the start of the block.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Transitioned smoothly and participated well in the planned activities. Attention and cooperation were consistent with recent sessions. Needed occasional reminders to slow his rate of speech when excited but generally responded to cues.

Goals / therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in sentences and structured play
- Practiced final /p, m, t/ in short sentences during a picture description game and pretend restaurant play.
- Performance was stable compared to last session: strong accuracy in imitated and cued sentences, with some continued omissions in more spontaneous, rapid speech.
- He remained willing to repeat and "fix" words when prompted.

2. Initial /k/ in semi-spontaneous sentences
- Targeted /k/ in sentences and in child-led play (cars, cookies, "camping").
- Accuracy of /k/ remained high, with mostly correct productions and very few substitutions.
- Required minimal support, mostly reminders to use his "slow, strong talking" in longer sentences.

3. Initial /s/ in CVC words and short phrases
- Continued focus on initial /s/ using picture cards and a sensory bin.
- Accuracy at the word and two-word phrase levels was similar to last session: several clear /s/ productions in imitation and cued contexts, but inconsistent carryover and occasional /d/ substitutions.
- He tolerated practice and repeated attempts, but no significant jump in independence was observed.

4. Multisyllabic word practice
- Practiced a small set of multisyllabic words in a storybook context.
- Syllable marking and overall clarity were comparable to last week, with some correct productions in imitation but limited generalization.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to consolidate gains at the current levels for final consonants, /k/, and /s/. Maintain structured sentence-level work and supported conversation for final consonants and /k/.
- For /s/, continue word and short phrase practice with consistent cueing; not yet ready for a higher level of complexity.
- Home practice: encourage short, frequent practice times rather than long sessions to prevent fatigue; continue praising clear sounds rather than focusing on errors.
notes/9/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that his speech over the past week has been "about the same" as the week before. She continues to appreciate the clearer /k/ and final consonants in many words, but /s/ remains variable. No new concerns were reported.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Arrived in a good mood and followed the routine without difficulty. Attention to tasks and participation were consistent with prior sessions. Needed a few prompts to stay at the table near the end of the session but re-engaged quickly with visual supports and game-based tasks.

Goals / therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in structured conversation
- Practiced final /p, m, t/ within short conversation about picture scenes and a pretend "picnic" activity.
- Accuracy remained similar to last session: good final consonant use in shorter, clinician-structured sentences and some persistence of omissions in longer or faster speech.
- Demonstrated willingness to repeat words with improved endings when cued.

2. Initial /k/ in conversation and story retell
- Targeted /k/ within a simple story retell (car trip theme) and free play.
- Continued to produce /k/ accurately in most contexts, including several spontaneous sentences. Performance was stable with minimal cueing.

3. Initial /s/ in CVC words and phrases
- Continued practice with initial /s/ words and short phrases using picture cards and a board game.
- Accuracy levels appeared similar to the prior week: some clear /s/ productions in imitation and cued phrases, with ongoing inconsistency and substitutions in less supported attempts.
- He remained cooperative and put forth effort, but no substantial change in independence was observed.

4. Multisyllabic word practice
- Revisited a limited set of multisyllabic words in a turn-taking game.
- Productions were comparable to last session, with adequate syllable marking in imitation but variable consonant precision.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain current focus on consolidating skills at the sentence and conversational levels for final consonants and /k/.
- Continue structured /s/ practice at the word and phrase level; consider introducing new cueing strategies (e.g., additional tactile cues) in upcoming sessions if variability persists.
- Home program: encourage parents to focus on a small list of functional /s/ words (e.g., "sun," "sock," "soap") each day, using slow models and positive feedback for any clear /s/ attempts.
notes/10/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother indicated that his speech this week was similar to recent weeks. She continues to notice clear /k/ and better final consonants in many short sentences, with ongoing inconsistency for /s/. She feels he is much easier to understand than when therapy started.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Cooperative and talkative throughout the session. Transitioned easily and followed the visual schedule. Needed occasional reminders to wait for his turn to speak, as he was eager to share ideas. Responded well to praise for his "strong sounds."

Goals / therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in structured conversation and play
- Practiced final /p, m, t/ during open-ended picture description and pretend play (e.g., kitchen set).
- Performance remained stable: consistent final consonants in many structured sentences, with some continued omissions in faster, more spontaneous speech.
- He was receptive to feedback and could often repair productions when errors were highlighted.

2. Initial /k/ in conversation
- Monitored /k/ use during natural play and simple question/answer exchanges.
- /k/ remained well-established, with correct productions in most contexts and minimal need for cueing.

3. Initial /s/ in CVC words and short phrases
- Continued targeted practice of initial /s/ words ("sun," "sock," "sand," "seat") and two-word phrases ("sun up," "sock on") using a game-based format.
- Accuracy and cueing needs were similar to previous sessions: several good /s/ productions in imitation and cued phrases, with variable performance in less supported contexts.
- He appeared motivated to try and tolerated multiple repetitions without frustration.

4. Multisyllabic word practice
- Briefly practiced a few multisyllabic words within a storybook.
- Productions were comparable to prior weeks, with adequate syllable marking in supported imitation and variable clarity.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue current therapy focus on consolidating final consonant and /k/ skills at the sentence/conversational level and building stability for /s/ at the word/phrase level.
- Discuss with caregiver the possibility of shifting block goals in future sessions toward more intensive /s/ work and generalization strategies.
- Home practice: continue short daily practice sessions emphasizing slow rate and clear sounds, especially for chosen /s/ words.
notes/11/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that his speech this week was similar to the last few weeks. She feels he is generally understandable in short sentences, especially to familiar listeners, and still hears inconsistent /s/ and some dropped endings when he talks very fast.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Arrived in good spirits and transitioned smoothly. Participated well in structured and play-based tasks. Showed age-appropriate energy and needed minimal redirection. Enjoyed reviewing "how much better" his words sound compared to when he started.

Goals / therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in conversation
- Focused on final /p, m, t/ within semi-structured conversation and picture description.
- Performance remained stable: frequent correct final consonants in shorter sentences and familiar words, with some omissions in longer, rapid speech.
- Demonstrated awareness of the need to "finish words" and responded well to cues to slow down and repeat.

2. Initial /k/ in natural speech
- Monitored /k/ production across activities (cars, cooking play, simple Q&A).
- /k/ continued to be produced accurately and consistently, with minimal to no cueing required.

3. Initial /s/ in words and short phrases
- Targeted initial /s/ in a small set of functional words and two-word phrases using picture cards and a game.
- Accuracy and independence were similar to prior sessions: multiple clear /s/ productions in imitation and cued phrases, with persistent variability in less structured attempts.
- He remained cooperative and willing to practice, but no significant change in level of support was observed.

4. Multisyllabic word practice
- Briefly revisited several multisyllabic words in a story context.
- Productions were in line with recent performance: he could mark most syllables in supported imitation, though consonant precision remained variable.

Plan / recommendations:
- Skills appear to be consolidating at current levels. Recommend ongoing therapy to further stabilize final consonant use in spontaneous speech, maintain /k/ gains, and intensify work on /s/ accuracy and generalization.
- For home practice, suggest continuing short, positive practice sessions focusing on a few target words/phrases, and encouraging slower, "strong sound" speech in everyday routines.
- Plan next block to prioritize /s/ at the word/phrase level and gradually build toward more independent use in conversation while monitoring maintenance of established sounds.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother reported that intelligibility is "hit or miss" with unfamiliar listeners. Family notices that child often leaves off sounds at the ends of words (e.g., "ca" for "cat") and sometimes says "t" for "k". No major changes since initial evaluation.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned with some hesitation but separated from caregiver with brief reassurance. Once in the room, engaged well in play-based tasks and responded positively to visual toys and turn-taking games. Attention was variable; needed intermittent redirection from self-directed play back to speech tasks.

Goals / observations:
1. Final consonant production (p, t, m) at CVC word level in imitation
- Target words included "cap, cat, cup, mat, map, mop, mom" in picture naming and play. Child was able to imitate final consonants inconsistently; many productions were reduced to CV forms (e.g., "ca" for "cat"). When cued to "keep the sound on," could occasionally add a quiet final consonant. Accuracy estimated around 30–40% in imitation with moderate verbal and visual cueing (touching mouth, slowing rate).

2. Initial /k/ at syllable and word level in imitation
- Practiced /k/ in isolation and in simple syllables ("kee, koo, kah") using a "back sound" cue and tongue placement demonstration. Child frequently substituted /t/ for /k/ ("tea" for "key"). With simultaneous production and mirror support, child was able to achieve a few accurate /k/ sounds, though consistency was low. At word level ("key, car, cow"), most attempts remained fronted.

3. Initial /f/ at syllable and word level in imitation
- Introduced "lip-bite" cue for /f/ and used visual model with a puppet. Practiced "fa, fee, foo" and words such as "fish, fun, feet." Child tended to substitute /p/ or omit the sound. With maximal verbal and gestural cues, achieved some approximations, but productions were often distorted or brief.

4. CV and CVCV syllable shapes in play
- During play, child produced many CV and CVCV (same consonant) shapes (e.g., "mama, dada, bubu"). Occasional CVC forms emerged spontaneously but were not consistent. No clear multisyllabic word targets today; focus was on establishing clear single syllables.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to build awareness of final consonants and back sounds (/k/) in highly structured imitation tasks.
- Encourage parent to model slow, clear CVC words in daily routines (e.g., "cup, hot, bed"), emphasizing the end sound without asking the child to repeat every time.
- Next session: continue with final /p, t, m/ at word level and explore whether /k/ is easier in play versus drill; maintain focus on imitation level for now.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother shared that child has been "trying more words" and occasionally corrected themselves (e.g., "ca...cat"). Family continues to notice dropped end sounds, but feels speech is "a bit clearer" this week. No concerns about hearing or health.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered the room readily and appeared comfortable with clinician. Demonstrated good engagement in turn-taking games and tolerated structured table-top tasks for short bursts. Needed brief movement breaks but returned to tasks with minimal prompting.

Goals / observations:
1. Final consonant production (p, t, m) in CVC words (imitation → supported spontaneous)
- Practiced minimal pairs (e.g., "ca" vs. "cat," "bee" vs. "beep") using pictures and a simple "finished sound" cue. Child showed noticeable improvement from last session: more frequent attempts to add final consonants and occasional self-corrections without prompting. Estimated accuracy ~60% in imitation, with several clear, independent productions during play (e.g., "pop," "mom"). Still required verbal reminders to "keep the last sound" but needed fewer cues overall.

2. Initial /k/ at syllable and word level in imitation
- Continued with tongue-back cue and mirror. Child produced /k/ in isolation with moderate support and was able to maintain /k/ in CV syllables ("koo, kah") in about half of opportunities. At word level ("key, car, cookie"), fronting to /t/ persisted but there were more approximations that began with a back sound. Child tolerated multiple practice trials without frustration.

3. Initial /f/ in CV and CVC words in imitation
- Reintroduced "bite your lip and blow" cue with visual model. Child demonstrated clearer /f/ in syllables ("fee, foo") with hand-over-hand cue for lip placement initially, fading to verbal reminders. At the word level ("fish, fun, food"), accuracy was emerging; many productions were approximations but more clearly fricative than last week. Accuracy still low overall but effort and awareness improved.

4. CVCV (alternating consonants) syllable shapes in play
- During play-based activities, encouraged words like "bunny, baby, cookie." Child produced several two-syllable words, though some syllables were reduced or simplified. Demonstrated increased willingness to attempt longer words, even when not fully accurate.

Plan / recommendations:
- Given improved consistency with final consonants, continue CVC practice and begin incorporating final consonants into simple carrier phrases (e.g., "my cat," "big cup") next week if accuracy remains stable.
- Encourage caregivers to highlight final sounds in key words during routines (e.g., "pop," "hot," "bed"), praising any attempts.
- Maintain focus on imitation for /k/ and /f/ while gradually fading cues as tolerated.
notes/2/note_text:

Occasional omissions persisted, but there were more accurate, natural-sounding productions overall compared to prior sessions.

Child produced initial /k/ correctly in imitation in roughly half to two-thirds of trials, especially when given a brief "tongue back" cue. Some spontaneous approximations occurred in play (e.g., "cow" during farm play) with only a visual cue. Still occasional fronting to /t/, but overall clarity improved.

Accuracy in imitation was emerging (~50–60%), with some clear /f/ productions in high-interest contexts (e.g., blowing pretend candles). Substitutions to /p/ were less frequent but still present.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother reported that she and daycare staff understand most of child's speech, but unfamiliar adults often ask for repetition. No major changes since initial evaluation.
- Parent noted child is starting to notice when others do not understand and will sometimes repeat more loudly rather than more clearly.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned to therapy room with mild hesitation but joined activities after brief warm-up with preferred toys.
- Attention was variable; needed intermittent redirection from self-directed play back to speech tasks.
- Responded well to visual supports and turn-taking games.

Goals/activities:
1. Establish /k/ in isolation
- Practiced back sound /k/ with tongue-back cues and use of mirror. Child was able to approximate /k/ in isolation with simultaneous visual and verbal cueing in a few attempts but often substituted /t/ or produced a neutral vocalization.
- Required maximal verbal models and tactile cues to jaw and throat. No consistent independent productions yet.

2. Initial /k/ in simple CV syllables (e.g., "ku, ka, ki")
- When attempting CV syllables, child continued to front to /t/ in most attempts (e.g., "ta" for "ka").
- With slow modeling and hand cue for "back sound," child produced a few approximations that were closer to /k/, but these were not yet reliable.

3. Initial /f/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Targeted /f/ using "biting lip and blowing" cue. Child could imitate /f/ in isolation in some attempts when watching the clinician's mouth but often reduced it to /p/ or omitted the airflow.
- In CV syllables ("foo, fee"), productions were mostly /p/ or /h/ plus vowel. Needed continuous visual modeling and repetition.

4. Syllable shapes (CV, CVCV)
- Observed in play: child produced a range of CV and reduplicated CVCV syllables (e.g., "mama," "baba") spontaneously.
- Less consistent with alternating CVCV (e.g., "tiger" approximated as "tata" or "tiga"). Did not directly target for drill this session but noted as baseline.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to establish consistent /k/ and /f/ in isolation and simple syllables with high levels of cueing.
- Introduce brief, play-based practice at home: parent to model "back sound" /k/ in front of mirror during bath time (e.g., "ku, ka") and "lip-bite" /f/ during toothbrushing.
- Next session: maintain focus on /k/ and /f/ at isolation and CV levels; consider using more tactile/visual supports and simple picture cards if attention allows.

1. /k/ initial at word level (imitated)
- Focused on a small set of /k/ words (car, key, cow, cookie, cat). Child imitated these with much better accuracy than previous session when given a clear model and "back sound" reminder.
- Occasional fronting to /t/ persisted ("tar" for "car"), but child was able to self-correct with prompts such as "tongue back" or visual cues in the mirror.
- Demonstrated several sequences of correct productions in a row for highly practiced words.

2. /k/ in simple carrier phrases (imitated, e.g., "my car," "big cat")
- Introduced short phrases using picture cards. With clinician modeling the full phrase, child could imitate with support, though consonant sometimes dropped in the phrase context.
- Accuracy lower than at single-word level but appropriate early attempts at moving up the hierarchy.

3. /f/ initial in isolation and CV syllables
- Child now consistently uses correct lip placement for /f/ in isolation with minimal cueing; frication is clearer and more sustained.
- In CV syllables ("fee, foo, fi"), child produced more accurate /f/ with fewer /p/ substitutions. Some blending into the following vowel but overall improved clarity.

4. Initial /f/ at word level (imitated)
- Trialed simple words ("fish, four, fan, foot"). Child could imitate several with recognizable /f/ when given slow model and visual cue.
- Occasional stopping to /p/ still present, especially when excited or speaking quickly.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue building accuracy and consistency for /k/ and /f/ at the imitated word level, with short trials of carrier phrases when child is successful.
- Home practice: parent to use picture cards or real objects to practice 5–6 /k/ and /f/ words in a playful context (e.g., "feed the cat," "find the car").
- Next session: increase practice of /k/ and /f/ words, monitor readiness for more regular phrase-level practice for highly mastered words.
notes/3/note_text:

- Child produced target /k/ words (car, key, cow, cookie, cat, coat) with good accuracy in imitation, often needing only a brief verbal reminder at the start of the set.
- Notably, during play, child spontaneously produced "car" and "cookie" with a clear /k/ in several instances without prompting.
- Occasional fronting to /t/ occurred when child was talking quickly, but they responded to gentle reminders and could adjust.

2. /k/ in short carrier phrases ("my car," "big cat")
- Child imitated 2–3-word phrases with initial /k/ words with emerging accuracy. Consonant was usually maintained in the practiced words.

3. /f/ initial words (imitation)
- Practiced a set of /f/ words (fish, four, fan, foot, fun). Child imitated with fair to good accuracy when watching clinician's mouth and using "lip-bite and blow" cue.

4. Early multisyllabic words with target sounds
- Introduced a few simple multisyllabic words containing /k/ or /f/ (e.g., "coffee," "cookie," "sofa"). Child approximated these, often preserving one target sound but simplifying other syllables.

notes/4/note_text:
