notes/0/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that she is still finding it hard to understand child’s words if she is not looking at him. She notices he leaves off “end sounds” in words (e.g., “ca” for “cat”). No major changes since initial visit.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned to the therapy room with minimal hesitation, holding mom’s hand. Once seated, he engaged well with preferred toys (cars, farm animals). Attention to structured tasks was fair; he worked in short bursts of 3–4 minutes with frequent play breaks.

Goals and therapy activities:
1. Final consonant production (CVC words: /p, t, m/)
- Targeted simple CVC words in imitation (e.g., “cup, cap, cat, map, mop, mat”).
- Child often omitted final consonants or produced a prolonged vowel (e.g., “cu” for “cup”).
- With maximal verbal cueing (“use your quiet ending sound”), visual cues (pointing to mouth), and segmentation (cu—p), he produced a few accurate final consonants, but these were inconsistent and not yet self-initiated.

2. Initial /k/ in isolation and syllables
- Practiced /k/ in isolation using “back sound” cues and mirror.
- Child could approximate the /k/ sound in isolation with simultaneous models and tongue-back cue but frequently substituted /t/ when attempting at the syllable level (e.g., “kuh” → “tuh”).
- Accuracy best in simultaneous production; reduced accuracy when imitating after a delay.

3. Initial /f/ in isolation
- Introduced “lip-bite, quiet air” cue for /f/.
- Child was interested in watching the SLP’s mouth and attempted /f/ in isolation several times.
- Productions were mostly approximations (/h/ or /p/ substitutions). Occasional clear /f/ occurred with hand-on-chin tactile cue and slowed rate, but not yet consistent.

4. Functional word attempts in play
- Encouraged use of single words to request (e.g., “more, go, up, open”).
- Child preferred pointing/gesturing and often used single-word approximations that were difficult to understand.
- With direct models and immediate imitation, he was able to repeat a small set of functional words (e.g., “go, up, in”) with variable clarity.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue targeting final consonants at the imitated word level with high support.
- Maintain focus on establishing more consistent /k/ and /f/ in isolation before expanding.
- Home practice: caregiver to model and emphasize “end sounds” in simple words (e.g., cat, cup, map) during daily routines, praising any attempts at full words rather than correcting heavily.
- Next session: re-check final consonants and initial /k/; consider adding simple CV and CVC syllable drills if attention allows.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother noted that child is starting to copy some words from his favorite TV show but they are often “mumbled.” She has not noticed major changes in clarity at home yet.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child separated from mom with some reassurance and quickly engaged with a car ramp toy. He was generally cooperative, though he needed gentle redirection when he became silly (giggling and making sound effects instead of target sounds). Able to attend to adult-directed tasks for slightly longer stretches today.

Goals and therapy activities:
1. Final consonant production (CVC words: /p, t, m/)
- Revisited same CVC targets in imitation (e.g., “cup, cap, cat, map, mat, mop”).
- Compared to last week, child showed emerging accuracy: more frequent attempts to close the word with a consonant, though often distorted or substituted (e.g., “cub” for “cup”).
- With segmented modeling (e.g., “cu—P”) and moderate verbal cueing, he produced clearer final consonants on several trials. Accuracy is still inconsistent but there were more recognizable final consonants than at the previous session.

2. Initial /k/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Continued “back sound” practice with mirror and tactile cue to the throat.
- Child produced /k/ in isolation with improved consistency when imitating the SLP’s model.
- Began trialing simple CV syllables (e.g., “ka, koo”). Many productions remained /t/-like, but there were a few clearer /k/ syllables when given slow modeling and repetition. Still relies on imitation and visual cues.

3. Initial /f/ in isolation and simple syllables
- Used “bite your lip, blow” cue with visual demonstration.
- Child showed more frequent attempts at /f/ today and appeared to enjoy blowing air.
- A small number of productions were clearly /f/, especially in isolation. When paired with a vowel (e.g., “foo, fah”), accuracy decreased and /h/ substitutions reappeared. Continued need for direct modeling and reminders.

4. Functional requesting words in play
- Practiced imitating functional words in play (e.g., “go, up, open, more”) to operate toys.
- Child imitated a few words with slightly clearer articulation than last session and occasionally attempted them spontaneously (e.g., “go” while pushing the car).
- Speech remained difficult to understand without context, but there were more attempts to use words instead of gestures alone.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue at current hierarchy level; skills are beginning to emerge but not yet consistent.
- Maintain emphasis on CVC final consonants and initial /k/ and /f/ in structured imitation.
- Home practice: caregiver to play simple “go/stop” games, encouraging child to say “go” and praising any attempts; continue to gently emphasize end sounds.
- Next session: consider adding a few new CVC words for variety while staying at the same level.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother shared that child is starting to say “cat” and “cup” more clearly at home at times, though it is not consistent. She feels she can understand him slightly better when he is calm and speaking slowly.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered the room independently and appeared comfortable with the routine. Good participation in turn-taking games. Attention was improved; he was able to remain in adult-directed tasks for 5–6 minutes with brief play breaks.

Goals and therapy activities:
1. Final consonant production (CVC words: /p, t, m/)
- Targeted a mix of familiar and new CVC words (e.g., “cup, cat, map, mat, hat, hot”) at the imitated word level.
- Notable improvement in consistency: with moderate verbal and visual cues, child frequently marked the final consonant, though some were still imprecise.
- Began to reduce cueing on familiar items; on a small set of practiced words, he sometimes produced the final consonant after just a model without segmentation.

2. Initial /k/ in CV and CVC syllables
- Continued work on /k/ using mirror and “tongue back” cue.
- Child produced /k/ in isolation with relatively good reliability during this session.
- Transitioned to CV syllables and a few simple CVC words (e.g., “key, car, cow”). There was moderate progress; he produced clearer /k/ on several imitated words, though he still frequently substituted /t/ without cues.
- Required verbal reminders (“back sound”) and slow rate to maintain accuracy.

3. Initial /f/ in isolation and simple words
- Practiced /f/ in isolation, CV syllables, and a small set of simple words (e.g., “fun, feet”).
- Child showed emerging ability to produce a recognizable /f/ at the beginning of words when given a clear model and visual cue.
- He occasionally reverted to /p/ or /h/, but the number of accurate productions increased relative to previous session.

4. Functional words and short phrases
- Encouraged child to use words to request and comment (e.g., “more car,” “my turn”).
- Child imitated several 2-word combinations with varying clarity; consonants were often reduced but vowels helped make his intentions clearer.
- No independent phrase-level speech yet in the therapy room, but he was more willing to attempt imitations.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to build consistency at the imitated CVC level for final consonants; consider trialing simple carrier phrases (e.g., “I see __”) for easier words if accuracy remains stable.
- Maintain work on initial /k/ and /f/ in words; may start to probe these sounds in short, highly familiar phrases later in the block.
- Home practice: caregiver to use picture cards or objects for 3–4 target words (e.g., “cat, cup, map”) and encourage slow, clear repetition, praising any attempts at end sounds.
- Next session: re-assess readiness for short phrases using high-probability CVC words.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly and eagerly chose a board game. He demonstrated good turn-taking and responded well to praise. Attention and participation were strong; he tolerated slightly longer structured tasks without needing as many breaks.

Goals and therapy activities:
1. Final consonant production (CVC words: /p, t, m, n/)
- Continued practice of familiar CVC words and introduced a few new ones (e.g., “pan, man, ten”).
- Accuracy at the imitated word level improved; child often produced the final consonant with only a simple verbal model and light visual cue.
- For a core set of words (e.g., “cup, cat, map”), he produced the final consonant more independently, sometimes without explicit prompting during play.
- Trialed short carrier phrases (e.g., “I see cat,” “my cup”) with these high-probability words. In phrases, he occasionally dropped the final consonant, but there were several successful productions when cued to “keep the end sound.”

2. Initial /k/ in words and short phrases
- Practiced /k/ in initial position in a set of words (e.g., “key, car, cow, coat”).
- Child demonstrated more consistent /k/ productions in imitation, needing fewer reminders to move his tongue back.
- Briefly trialed simple phrases (e.g., “my car, go car”) using picture support. At phrase level, accuracy decreased but he attempted the /k/ in many trials and responded to verbal reminders.

3. Initial /f/ in words
- Continued work on /f/ with picture cards (“fish, fan, foot, fun”).
- Child showed moderate progress: clearer /f/ productions were heard more frequently at the word level with a model.
- He still required visual cueing (lip-bite gesture) but needed less repetition than in previous sessions.

4. Functional 2-word combinations
- Targeted functional phrases such as “more please,” “my turn,” “go car.”
- Child imitated several of these with variable clarity; consonants were sometimes omitted, but his willingness to attempt longer utterances increased.
- Some spontaneous single words were noted during play (e.g., “mine,” “up”).

Plan/recommendations:
- Given the moderate, meaningful gains in final consonant production, continue to build consistency at the word level and gradually increase phrase practice for the easiest words.
- Maintain focus on initial /k/ and /f/ at the imitated word level, with occasional phrase probes.
- Home practice: encourage simple phrases using practiced CVC words (e.g., “my cat,” “big cup”) while modeling clear end sounds; keep practice playful and brief.
- Next session: monitor carryover of final consonants in short phrases; adjust hierarchy depending on stability.

1. Final consonant production (CVC words; phrase level probes)
- Practiced familiar CVC words (e.g., “cup, cat, map, man, pan, hat”).
- Accuracy at the imitated word level remained generally good with light verbal cues. Child continued to demonstrate clearer final consonants on these familiar targets.
- Phrases such as “my cat,” “big cup,” and “see pan” were probed again. Child produced some accurate final consonants within these short phrases, though he was less consistent than at the single-word level.
- Slight improvement in his ability to self-correct when the SLP modeled the phrase with emphasis on the end sound.

2. Initial /k/ in words and short phrases
- Continued practice with “key, car, cow, coat, cat.”
- Child maintained relatively good /k/ production in imitation. He required intermittent reminders to use his “back sound,” but fewer repetitions were needed.
- In simple phrases (“my car,” “go car”), he sometimes substituted /t/ but also had several accurate attempts, indicating gradual consolidation.

3. Initial /f/ in words
- Revisited “fish, fan, foot, fun” at the imitated word level.
- Child showed emerging improvement: more consistent /f/ at the beginning of words when given a model and visual cue.
- Occasional /p/ substitutions persisted but appeared less frequent than before.

4. Functional phrases in play
- Used play routines to elicit 2–3 word requests (e.g., “more bubbles,” “open door,” “my turn”).
- Child imitated multiple short phrases; speech remained somewhat reduced in consonant detail, but there were more attempts to include final sounds in familiar words.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to support final consonant production at both single-word and short-phrase levels, focusing on stability rather than rapid advancement.
- Maintain current targets for /k/ and /f/ at the imitated word level with periodic phrase probes.
- Home practice: encourage child to use “my ___” phrases with favorite toys (e.g., “my car, my cup”), and model clear final sounds; praise attempts rather than perfection.
- Next session: re-check phrase-level consistency; consider adding another final consonant (e.g., /n/) more systematically if accuracy remains steady.
notes/5/note_text:

Goals and therapy activities:
1. Final consonant production (CVC words and short phrases)

- In short phrases (e.g., “my cat,” “big pan,” “see sun”), accuracy of final consonants showed gradual improvement, though still not fully consistent.

2. Initial /k/ in words and short phrases
- Targeted “key, car, cow, coat, cat” again, plus “cup” for /k/ + final consonant.
- Child’s /k/ productions remained fairly strong in imitation; he needed only occasional cues.
- In simple phrases (“my car,” “big car,” “go cow”), he produced /k/ correctly in many attempts, though there were still intermittent /t/ substitutions.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver (mom) shared that she is mostly concerned about others not understanding child's speech, especially with longer words and when talking fast. No major changes reported since initial evaluation. Child starting to ask more questions at home and is very talkative.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned with some hesitation but joined the session with encouragement. Once in the room, she was curious and talkative. Attention to structured tasks was fair; she preferred play-based activities and needed occasional redirection from toys back to speech targets.

Goals / session targets:
1. Initial /s/ at syllable and word level (imitation)
- Practiced /s/ in isolation using "snake sound" cues and mirror. Able to hold /s/ briefly with clinician model, but productions were often too short or turned into /t/ or /sh/.
- At the CV syllable level (e.g., "see, soo, sah"), she imitated several approximations, but accuracy was inconsistent and required frequent verbal and visual cues.
- At simple word level (e.g., "sun, soup, sock"), she was often substituting /t/ or /sh/ and needed maximal modeling and reminders to keep tongue behind teeth.

2. Final /t/ at word level (imitation)
- Target words included "hat, cat, boat, feet." She often omitted the final sound or added a vowel (e.g., "ha" for "hat," "fee" for "feet").
- With simultaneous production and hand-tapping cues for the final sound, she was able to mark /t/ in some words, but consistency was low.

3. Initial /k/ at syllable and word level (imitation)
- Child tended to front /k/ to /t/ (e.g., "tar" for "car"). Practiced tongue-back placement using a "cough" and "back sound" trick.
- In isolation, a few clear /k/ productions emerged with maximal tactile/visual cues.
- At word level ("car, key, cow"), productions were mostly /t/; accurate productions occurred only with heavy support.

4. Functional communication observation
- During play, child used many 2–4 word phrases (e.g., "my turn," "I want that," "go fast") but speech clarity reduced in longer phrases. Intelligibility with familiar listener (clinician) was fair; needed repetition requests.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to focus on establishing consistent productions of /s/, /t/, and /k/ in isolation and simple syllables before moving up the hierarchy.
- Home practice: caregiver encouraged to model slow, clear "snake sound" /s/ during play and to emphasize final sounds in everyday words (e.g., "hat," "cat") using a light tap gesture on the table or child’s hand. Keep practice playful and brief (1–2 minutes, a few times per day).
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mom reported that child has started trying to copy some of the "snake sound" /s/ practice at home and enjoys making the sound in front of the mirror. No major changes in overall clarity, but mom notices child "trying harder" with certain words like "sun" and "soap."

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child separated easily from caregiver and appeared excited to show "snake sound." Engagement was good, with better tolerance for short structured tasks between play. Some silliness noted when tasks were more challenging, but she responded to gentle redirection.

Goals / session targets:
1. Initial /s/ in isolation, syllables, and words (imitation)
- Marked improvement in ability to sustain /s/ in isolation with visual cue (finger in front of mouth) and mirror. She could hold a clear /s/ for several seconds in multiple trials.
- At CV syllable level ("see, soo, sah, say"), productions were more consistent; occasional distortions but clearer airflow and less backing to /sh/.
- At word level ("sun, soup, sock, soap"), she achieved multiple accurate imitations with verbal reminders to "keep tongue behind teeth." Still required models but needed fewer repetitions to achieve a correct production.

2. Final /t/ at word level (imitation)
- Continued with "hat, cat, boat, feet" plus a few new words. She more consistently marked the final /t/ with simultaneous production and a tapping cue.
- Some spontaneous approximations (e.g., saying "hat" with a clear final sound during a game) were observed, although still inconsistent.

3. Initial /k/ at syllable and word level (imitation)
- Used "cough" trick and tongue-back placement. Child produced more recognizable /k/ in isolation than last session.
- At CV level ("kee, koo, kah"), she produced a mix of /k/ and /t/ but had more frequent correct trials compared to previous visit.
- At word level ("car, key, cow"), she needed continued maximum verbal/visual cues; still tends to front to /t/, but a few clearer /k/ productions emerged with slow modeling.

4. Functional carryover
- In play, child attempted to use /s/ words from practice (e.g., "sun," "sock") after clinician models. Intelligibility was slightly improved when she slowed down and used practiced words.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue at word level for initial /s/ and final /t/, with gradual introduction of short carrier phrases for most successful /s/ words in upcoming sessions if consistency continues.
- Maintain focus on establishing /k/ with more consistent accuracy in isolation and syllables before advancing.
- Home practice: encourage short /s/ and /t/ word games (e.g., naming pictures, simple matching). Caregiver to continue modeling slow speech and praising "clear sounds."
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mom noted that child is now sometimes correcting herself, especially on "sun" and "sock," and will say "no, sssun" after hearing a model. Family still finds her hard to understand in longer sentences.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered room independently and quickly engaged with toys. She accepted turn-taking and structured activities well today, with fewer avoidance behaviors. Needed occasional breaks but came back to tasks with minimal prompting.

Goals / session targets:
1. Initial /s/ at word and simple phrase level (imitation with emerging independence)
- Reviewed /s/ in isolation; accuracy remained strong with minimal verbal cueing.
- At word level ("sun, soup, sock, soap, seat"), child produced more consistent accurate /s/ when imitating. Needed reminders less frequently.
- Introduced simple carrier phrases ("my ___," "a ___") with target words (e.g., "my sock," "a sun"). She was able to imitate short phrases with generally clear /s/ in initial position, though accuracy dropped slightly compared to single words.

2. Final /t/ at word level and in short phrases (imitation)
- Practiced "hat, cat, boat, feet, coat" at the imitated word level. Child was more reliable in marking the final /t/, especially when the clinician used a quiet "stop" hand cue.
- Trialed short phrases ("my hat," "big cat"). She was able to approximate the final sound but sometimes reduced it or changed it to a softer sound.

3. Initial /k/ in isolation and CV syllables (imitation)
- Continued focus on tongue-back placement and "cough" strategy. Child produced multiple clear /k/ sounds in isolation with verbal and visual support.
- At CV level ("kee, koo, kah"), she showed increased accuracy compared to previous sessions, though she still occasionally reverted to /t/.
- Did not yet move consistently to word level for /k/ due to variable accuracy.

4. Functional communication / intelligibility
- During play, child used many 3–4 word phrases (e.g., "I want more bubbles," "put it in there"). Intelligibility improved slightly when she slowed down and when words with practiced sounds were used.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain focus on initial /s/ at word and short phrase level; continue to build consistency before moving to spontaneous use.
- Continue final /t/ in words and phrases, aiming for more independent productions with reduced cues.
- Keep /k/ at isolation and CV levels until accuracy is more stable, then gradually introduce simple words again.
- Home practice: caregivers to practice a small set of /s/ and /t/ words in short, fun phrases (e.g., "my sock," "big cat"), emphasizing slow, clear speech without pressuring for perfection.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mom shared that child is now using some of the practice words in everyday routines (e.g., asking for her "sock" or "seat"), and family members are noticing those words are clearer. Still difficulty with words like "car" and "key."

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child arrived in good spirits and eagerly showed clinician her "sock" to say the word. Attention to structured tasks was improved; she tolerated slightly longer drill-like practice when paired with a game. Minimal redirection needed.

Goals / session targets:
1. Initial /s/ at word and short phrase level (imitation and emerging spontaneous use)
- Child produced initial /s/ in a set of familiar words ("sun, sock, soap, seat, soup") with good consistency during imitation. Only occasional reminders for tongue placement were needed.
- In short carrier phrases ("my sock," "a sun," "soap here"), she imitated phrases with relatively stable /s/ accuracy.
- Spontaneous attempts noted during play, such as labeling "sun" and "sock" without a direct prompt. Some of these spontaneous productions maintained the clear /s/.

2. Final /t/ in words and phrases (imitation with reduced cueing)
- Practiced "hat, cat, boat, feet, coat" again and introduced "out" and "hot." She continued to mark final /t/ more consistently than previous sessions.
- Gesture cueing was faded in some trials; she could still produce a recognizable final consonant, although not always as a strong /t/.
- Phrases such as "my hat" and "get out" were imitated with mixed but improving accuracy.

3. Initial /k/ at CV and word level (imitation)
- Child continued to show improved control of /k/ in CV syllables ("kee, koo, kah").
- Re-introduced word-level targets ("car, key, cow, cookie"). She required clear models and verbal reminders to "use the back sound." Accuracy was variable but higher than at baseline; a few correct productions per word were noted with multiple attempts.

4. Early generalization
- During play, child sometimes used practiced /s/ words in simple comments ("my sock," "sun up"). Intelligibility for these specific words and phrases was noticeably better than for non-practiced words.

Plan / recommendations:
- Begin to increase opportunities for semi-spontaneous /s/ use in structured play (e.g., having child request or label items with /s/ words) while maintaining practice at phrase level.
- Continue building final /t/ in more functional phrases and short sentences as tolerated.
- Keep working on /k/ at word level with a small, consistent set of target words to support consolidation.
- Home practice: encourage child to use target /s/ and /t/ words during daily routines (e.g., getting "sock," sitting in "seat"), with caregiver modeling and praising clear productions.
notes/4/note_text:

- Tried short phrases ("my car," "big cookie") on a limited basis. She required modeling and reminders but was able to achieve some correct /k/ productions within phrases.

4. Functional communication / generalization
- During play, child independently used several practice words ("hat," "cat," "sock") with clearer consonants than previously observed. Overall intelligibility in conversation remains mixed but with more frequent clearly produced target words.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to strengthen /s/ and final /t/ in short sentences, especially with high-frequency functional phrases.
- Gradually expand /k/ practice into simple phrases while maintaining support.
- Home practice: caregivers to model and encourage short sentences with target words (e.g., "I see a cat," "My sock on"), praising any clear sound attempts rather than focusing on errors.
notes/6/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mom stated that child is now often saying "my hat" and "my sock" clearly and enjoys being praised for her "good sounds." She still struggles with "car" and "cookie" but seems to be trying more often.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly and was eager to participate. She showed good attention to clinician’s mouth and cues, and she was motivated by sticker rewards. Some fatigue noted near the end of session but she finished activities with gentle support.

Goals / session targets:
1. Initial /s/ in short sentences (imitation and semi-spontaneous)
- Practiced sentences such as "I see the sun," "My sock is on," "I sip soup." Child imitated these with generally good /s/ accuracy at the beginning of words.
- In play-based routines, she used some short sentences with /s/ words spontaneously after initial modeling, maintaining fair clarity.

2. Final /t/ in words and short sentences (imitation and emerging independence)
- Target words remained "hat, cat, feet, coat, hot, out" plus "boat." Child showed more consistent final consonant marking in imitated words; she needed only occasional reminders.
- In sentences like "Put on hat" and "I see a cat," she often included a recognizable final /t/, though still not in every trial.

3. Initial /k/ in words and short phrases (imitation)
- Continued with "car, key, cow, cookie" and introduced "coat" for initial /k/. She produced more correct /k/ sounds in words today but still showed fronting at times.
- In phrases like "my car" and "big cookie," she required clear models and slowed rate, but several accurate productions were observed.

4. Functional communication
- Child used multiple 3–5 word utterances in play. Intelligibility is gradually improving, especially for practiced words and simple phrases. Listener support is still needed for unfamiliar topics.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain current targets with focus on increasing independence and consistency at short sentence level for /s/ and final /t/.
- Continue structured practice for /k/ at word and phrase level before moving into longer sentences.
- Home practice: caregivers encouraged to use simple picture books to elicit target sentences (e.g., "I see a cat," "I see a car"), reinforcing clear sounds with specific praise.
notes/7/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mom reported noticing that child is starting to correct herself with some /k/ words (e.g., trying again when she says "tar" for "car"). Family feels she is "a bit clearer" overall, though still hard to understand when excited.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child was enthusiastic and talkative. She followed directions well and participated in both structured drill and play-based activities. She showed increased awareness of her own speech, occasionally commenting "I do it again" when she was not satisfied with a production.

Goals / session targets:
1. Initial /s/ in short sentences and early conversation (imitation and semi-spontaneous)
- Practiced sentences such as "I see the sun," "The sock is wet," and "I sit in seat." Child imitated these with good /s/ accuracy in most trials.
- During play, she spontaneously used several /s/ words ("sun," "sock," "seat," "soup") in short comments and requests, often maintaining the clear initial /s/ without prompting.

2. Final /t/ in short sentences (imitation with reduced cues)
- Continued with words and sentences including final /t/ ("hat," "cat," "feet," "boat," "put on hat," "I want feet up").
- She marked final /t/ in a majority of imitated sentences, now requiring fewer gesture cues. Some natural-sounding reductions were observed but overall clarity improved from earlier sessions.

3. Initial /k/ in words and short sentences (imitation and emerging self-correction)
- Practiced "car, key, cow, cookie, coat" at word level, then in simple sentences ("I see a car," "Big cookie," "Cow is big").
- Child showed more consistent back placement, with several instances of self-correction when she initially produced /t/ (e.g., "tar... car").
- Still needed clinician models, but the number of accurate /k/ productions increased.

4. Functional generalization
- In play, child used target words within short conversational turns, and intelligibility for these practiced words was noticeably improved. She still required repetition or clarification for longer or less familiar phrases.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to solidify /s/ and final /t/ in short sentences and encourage more spontaneous use across different activities.
- Maintain focus on /k/ at word and sentence level, supporting self-monitoring and gentle self-correction.
- Home practice: suggest brief daily routines where child describes pictures or toys using target sentences (e.g., "I see a car," "My hat on"), with caregiver providing clear models and positive feedback.
notes/8/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mom shared that this week felt "pretty similar" to last week. Child continues to say some /s/ and /t/ words clearly and is trying with /k/, but overall speech clarity in conversation seems about the same.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned without difficulty and engaged well in familiar routines. She appeared comfortable with the activities and responded to praise, though she occasionally rushed through words when excited.

Goals / session targets:
1. Initial /s/ in sentences and structured conversation
- Reviewed sentence-level practice with /s/ ("I see the sun," "The sock is wet," "I sit in the seat"). Child maintained similar accuracy to previous session, with generally clear /s/ in imitated sentences.
- In short conversational exchanges during a game, she produced several /s/ words spontaneously with good clarity, especially familiar targets. Performance was stable compared to last week.

2. Final /t/ in sentences
- Continued practice with final /t/ in words and sentences ("hat," "cat," "feet," "boat," "Put on hat," "I see a cat"). Accuracy remained comparable to prior session: she marked final /t/ in many imitated sentences but occasionally omitted or softened the sound.

3. Initial /k/ in words and short sentences
- Repeated work on "car, key, cow, cookie, coat" and associated sentences. Child continued to demonstrate awareness of the back sound and attempted self-corrections.
- Accuracy was similar to previous week; she still required consistent verbal and visual cues, with a mix of correct /k/ and fronted productions.

4. Functional communication
- Child used multiple 3–5 word phrases spontaneously. Intelligibility during play was relatively stable, with clearer productions for practiced words but persistent challenges for less familiar vocabulary.

Plan / recommendations:

- Continue supporting self-monitoring for /k/ and gently reinforcing accurate productions.
- Home practice: encourage family to keep using target sentences in natural routines, without increasing difficulty at this time, to solidify gains.
notes/9/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mom indicated that this week was "about the same." Child continues to enjoy practicing her "good sounds" but still becomes hard to understand when she talks quickly or tells longer stories.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child was cooperative and engaged in therapy activities. She followed directions well and participated in both drill and play tasks. Some mild fatigue toward the end of session but overall steady participation.

Goals / session targets:
1. Initial /s/ in sentences and structured play
- Revisited sentence-level /s/ targets ("I see the sun," "The sock is wet," "I sit in the seat," "I sip soup"). Child continued to produce /s/ accurately in most imitated sentences.
- During structured play scenarios, she used several /s/ words in short comments and requests with similar clarity to previous sessions.

2. Final /t/ in sentences and short narratives
- Practiced final /t/ in simple sentences and brief story-like sequences (e.g., "The cat is hot," "He got in the boat").
- Accuracy of final /t/ remained stable: frequently present in imitated sentences, but less consistent when she was speaking more quickly or in longer utterances.

3. Initial /k/ in words and simple sentences
- Continued practice with "car, key, cow, cookie, coat" and related sentences. Child demonstrated ongoing awareness of the back sound and attempted to correct herself in some instances.
- Overall accuracy did not show a notable change from last week; she still alternated between correct /k/ and /t/ substitutions, particularly when excited.

4. Functional communication
- Child continued to use multi-word utterances (3–6 words) to comment and request. Intelligibility remained mixed but stable, with familiar listeners understanding more when context was clear.

Plan / recommendations:

- Continue practicing a limited set of /k/ words and sentences, prioritizing accuracy over quantity.
- Home practice: recommend short, predictable sentence routines (e.g., "I see a cat," "I see a car") and encourage child to slow down when telling short stories, with caregiver gently modeling a slower rate.
notes/10/note_text:

2. Final /t/ in sentences

notes/11/note_text:
Parent report:

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned easily and was cooperative throughout the session. She showed good effort with sound practice and accepted feedback well. No significant behavior concerns.

Goals / session targets:
1. Initial /s/ in sentences and structured conversation
- Ongoing sentence-level practice with /s/ ("I see the sun," "The sock is wet," "I sit in the seat," "I sip soup"). Accuracy remained steady, with clear /s/ in most imitated sentences.
- In brief conversational exchanges, she continued to use /s/ words such as "sun," "sock," and "seat" with similar clarity to prior sessions.

2. Final /t/ in sentences and short narratives
- Practiced final /t/ in words and sentences ("hat," "cat," "feet," "boat," "Put on hat," "I see a cat," "He got in the boat").
- Child’s performance remained stable: she typically marked final /t/ during structured practice but still had variable accuracy in more spontaneous utterances.

3. Initial /k/ in words and simple sentences
- Continued with target /k/ words and sentences ("car, key, cow, cookie, coat"; "I see a car," "Big cookie").
- Accuracy and cueing needs were comparable to recent weeks. She showed ongoing awareness and occasional self-correction, but productions were not yet consistently accurate without support.

4. Functional communication / summary of block

Intelligibility has improved for specific practiced words and short sentences, particularly those with /s/ and some final /t/ sounds. General conversational clarity remains an ongoing area of need, especially for less familiar words and when speaking quickly.

Plan / recommendations:

notes/0/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent shared that child is “hard to understand” for unfamiliar listeners and often leaves off sounds at the ends of words (e.g., “ca” for “cat”). No previous speech therapy. Parent reports child is excited about starting “speech games.”

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child separated from parent with some hesitation but transitioned with visual schedule and toys.
- Engaged best with turn-taking games and pretend play. Attention fluctuated; needed frequent redirection from preferred toys back to speech tasks.
- Imitation skills emerging; child attempted to copy clinician’s models but often giggled or whispered productions.

Therapy goals and observations:
1. Initial /p, b, m/ in CV and CVC syllables (imitation)
- Targeted simple syllables (e.g., “pa, ba, me, bee, pop, mom”) using picture cards and mirror.
- Child imitated CV syllables with these early sounds with high success when given a direct model and visual cue (lip closure). CVC words were less consistent; final consonants frequently omitted (e.g., “po” for “pop”).
- Benefited from slowed rate and simultaneous productions with clinician.

2. Final consonant production in CVC words (p, m, t)
- Practiced words like “cup, cap, cat, map, mop, mat” at the imitated word level.
- Child attempted final consonants in about half of opportunities; many productions were approximations (e.g., “cu_,” “ma_”). Stopping the word and then adding the “tail sound” helped the child notice the final sound, though carryover to full words was limited.

3. Initial /k/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Child showed fronting of /k/ to /t/ in spontaneous play (e.g., “tar” for “car”).
- Introduced /k/ in isolation using “tongue up and back” cues and mirror. Child was able to produce a backed sound with maximal verbal and visual support in some trials.
- CV syllables (e.g., “koo, kah”) were attempted but often reverted to /t/; this will remain a primary target.

4. Establishing CV and CVC syllable shapes
- Observed spontaneous use of V, CV, and some CVCV (same) patterns in play (e.g., “mama, dada, baby”).
- When cued, child could imitate a variety of CV and CVCV (same) structures; CVC forms were much more difficult, particularly with final consonant closure.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to target early developing sounds (/p, b, m/) in CV and CVC forms, with emphasis on marking final consonants.
- Continue intensive work on /k/ in isolation and CV syllables with visual supports and mirror.
- Provide parent with a short list of CVC words using /p, m, t/ to practice “tail sounds” at home during play (e.g., “map, mop, cat, cap”).
- Next session: re-check final consonant production and /k/ isolation; consider adding simple two-word combinations if attention allows.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent reported no major changes since last week but noticed child occasionally trying to “fix” words when not understood (e.g., repeating more slowly). Parent has been practicing “tail sounds” on a few words during book reading.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned into therapy room more easily today and quickly joined a familiar game from last session.
- Attention to adult-directed tasks slightly improved; still required gentle reminders to stay with the activity but less resistance overall.
- Imitation was more willing; child laughed less and seemed more comfortable making “big” sounds in front of the mirror.

Therapy goals and observations:
1. Initial /p, b, m/ in CVC words (imitation → emerging spontaneous)
- Practiced with picture cards and a fishing game (e.g., “pop, map, bib, mop, mom”).
- Child produced initial consonants accurately in the majority of trials with a model. Final consonant closure remained variable but showed more consistent attempts.
- Notably, child spontaneously used clear CVC words “pop” and “mom” during play without direct prompting, suggesting early carryover for these specific items.

2. Final consonants /p, m, t/ in CVC words
- Used a “quiet tail sound” visual cue (pointing to the end of the word) and minimal pairs (e.g., “ma” vs “map”).
- Child demonstrated more frequent attempts to add the final sound after initial production, especially when clinician paused and cued, “What’s the tail?”
- Accuracy remained inconsistent but there was emerging awareness; child sometimes self-corrected when the clinician looked expectantly at the picture.

3. /k/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Continued to show fronting to /t/ in conversation.
- With maximal verbal, visual, and tactile cues (spoon under chin, mirror), child produced a backed /k/ sound in isolation in several trials. Accuracy was higher when starting from a prolonged vowel (“ah…k”).
- CV syllables were attempted; a few approximations of “koo” and “kah” were noted but often drifted back to /t/.

4. Simple two-word combinations with target CVC words
- Briefly introduced two-word combinations such as “pop it,” “my map,” “big cup” at the imitated phrase level.
- Child could imitate some two-word phrases, though the second word was often less clear. Emphasis remained on clarity of the CVC target.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue reinforcing final consonants using the “tail sound” strategy and visual cues; keep practice mostly at the word level.
- Maintain intensive /k/ work in isolation and CV syllables; continue to use mirrors and tactile cues.
- Encourage parent to highlight final sounds in a playful way at home (e.g., pausing and pointing to mouth when saying “cup,” “map”).
- Next session: re-evaluate /k/ accuracy; if stability continues with /p, b, m/ CVCs, consider expanding phrase-level work for a small set of words.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent noted child is “copying words more” at home and occasionally produces clearer CVC words (e.g., “cup,” “map”) during routines. Parent feels familiar family members understand child slightly better this week.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered room independently and quickly engaged with a car and animal play set.
- Sustained attention improved; child was able to participate in structured tasks for longer stretches with fewer redirections.
- Demonstrated good imitation willingness and appeared proud when clinician praised clear productions.

Therapy goals and observations:
1. Final consonants /p, m, t/ at imitated word level
- Focused on CVC words in a board game format (e.g., “cup, cap, cat, map, mop, mat”).
- Child marked final consonants more consistently than in previous sessions, with clearer closure on /p/ and /m/ in particular.
- Required moderate verbal cues (“close it at the end,” “tail sound”) but began to anticipate the need for the final sound, sometimes adding it without direct prompting.

2. Initial /p, b, m/ in CVC words (spontaneous use in structured play)
- During play, child produced several CVC words (e.g., “pop, mom, baby, mop”) without immediate modeling.
- Accuracy of initial consonants was high; final consonant accuracy in these spontaneous productions was variable but present more often than not.
- This suggests growing stability of these early consonants at the word level.

3. /k/ in isolation and CV syllables → trial of word level
- Continued use of mirror and “tongue back” cues.
- Child produced /k/ in isolation with improved consistency when given a vowel lead-in (“ee…k,” “ah…k”).
- Trialed simple /k/ initial words (“car, key, cow”) at the imitated word level. Productions were often approximations (e.g., “tar” for “car”), but a few clearer /k/ productions were achieved with maximal cueing and slowed rate.

4. Two-word phrases with high-probability CVC words
- Practiced short phrases such as “my cup,” “big map,” “pop bubble” using a model and gesture.
- Child imitated several two-word phrases with fair clarity on the target CVC word. The second word was sometimes reduced or less precise, which is expected at this stage.

Plan/recommendations:
- Maintain focus on final consonants, as progress is emerging; continue using consistent visual and verbal cues.
- Continue intensive work on /k/ in isolation and simple words; limit set of /k/ words to a few functional items (e.g., car, key, cow) for repetition.
- Encourage parent to use simple two-word combinations with target words at home (e.g., “my cup,” “big cat”) and to praise any attempts at final sounds.
- Next session: if consistency with final /p, m, t/ continues, consider slightly expanding final consonant set or increasing phrase-level practice.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent shared that child is using clearer words during daily routines (e.g., “cup,” “cat,” “map”) and is easier to understand at home. Parent noted child sometimes emphasizes the last sound when reminded, saying it “extra big.”

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered happily and requested specific toys verbally.
- Maintained engagement in therapist-led activities for most of the session, needing only brief breaks.
- Demonstrated good turn-taking and responded well to specific feedback about “strong sounds.”

Therapy goals and observations:
1. Final consonants /p, m, t/ at word and carrier-phrase levels
- Used picture cards and a simple “I see ___” sentence strip.
- At the imitated word level, child produced final /p, m, t/ with much improved consistency, often achieving clear closure without additional prompts.
- When moved into the carrier phrase (e.g., “I see cup,” “I see cat”), child maintained reasonably good accuracy on the target word, though final consonants occasionally dropped when attention shifted to the whole phrase.

2. Expansion of final consonants to /k/ and /n/
- Introduced a small set of new CVC targets (e.g., “book, back, can, man”).
- Child attempted these new patterns; final /k/ and /n/ were present in some imitated productions but less stable than /p, m, t/.
- Required more explicit segmentation (e.g., “ba…ck,” “ma…n”) and visual support, but showed readiness to work at this expanded level.

3. Initial /k/ in words and short phrases
- Practiced initial /k/ with a limited set of functional words (e.g., “car, key, cow, cookie”).
- With visual/tactile cues and slowed rate, child produced a recognizable /k/ at the beginning of these words more frequently than last week.
- Trialed short imitated phrases (e.g., “go car,” “my key”). /k/ sometimes reverted to /t/ in phrases, but there were several clear productions indicating a meaningful step forward.

4. Two- and three-word phrases with target CVC words
- Used a play-based routine (feeding animals, parking cars) to elicit phrases such as “my cup,” “big cat,” “more map please.”
- Child imitated these phrases with growing consistency and occasionally generated simple spontaneous phrases (e.g., “my car,” “more pop”). Target CVC words were usually the clearest element.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue practicing final /p, m, t/ in both single words and short carrier phrases to solidify accuracy.
- Keep working on initial /k/ in a small set of familiar words, with gradual introduction into two-word phrases as tolerated.
- Parent encouraged to model and gently highlight final sounds in short phrases at home (e.g., “big cat,” “hot cup”) and to praise attempts rather than perfection.
- Next session: monitor stability of phrase-level final consonants and, if maintained, slowly increase variety of words and contexts.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent reported that child’s speech “seems better” overall, and older sibling is understanding them more often. Parent noticed that /k/ in words like “car” and “cookie” is “starting to sound right” sometimes, though still inconsistent.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly and immediately requested a familiar game.
- Maintained attention to table-top tasks with minimal redirection, though needed brief movement breaks toward the end of the session.
- Enjoyed being the “teacher” and listening to their own productions on playback.

Therapy goals and observations:
1. Final consonants /p, m, t/ in carrier phrases
- Continued practice with “I see ___” and “my ___” sentence frames.
- Child maintained relatively strong accuracy on final /p, m, t/ within these short phrases. Occasional omissions occurred when child was excited or speaking quickly.
- When prompted to “slow down and close the word,” child could often self-correct.

2. Initial /k/ in familiar words (word and phrase level)
- Repeated practice with a limited set of /k/ words (e.g., “car, key, cookie, cow”) using a car track and pretend snack game.
- Child produced initial /k/ accurately in many imitated words with moderate cueing, though some productions continued to front to /t/.
- In short phrases (e.g., “my car,” “more cookie”), /k/ was less consistent but present in a subset of attempts. Slight emerging improvement from last week.

3. Expansion of final consonants /k, n/ in CVC words
- Continued with targets such as “book, back, can, man, pan.”
- Child produced more attempts at final /k/ and /n/ but accuracy remained variable. Needed segmenting and sometimes a second attempt to achieve a clear final sound.
- Performance suggested an emerging understanding, though not yet at the level of /p, m, t/.

4. Functional two- and three-word phrases in play
- Used play routines (cars, snack time) to elicit phrases like “more cookie,” “my big car,” “I want cup.”
- Child imitated many of these phrases and produced some spontaneous short phrases, with target words often intelligible and phrase structure still developing.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue consolidating final /p, m, t/ in phrases, and provide repeated opportunities for /k/ in initial position in a small, meaningful word set.
- Maintain practice with /k, n/ final in a limited group of CVC words to support gradual improvement.
- Parent encouraged to emphasize clear /k/ in a few everyday words (e.g., car, cookie) and to model short phrases containing these words.
- Next session: keep targets similar to allow consolidation; if /k/ continues to strengthen, consider adding one or two new /k/ words.
notes/5/note_text:

Behaviour/engagement:

1. Final consonants /p, m, t/ in phrases and short sentences
- Used sentence frames (e.g., “I see a ___,” “Put on the ___”) with picture scenes.

notes/6/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent reported that child is starting to be more understandable to extended family and that they heard some clearer /k/ words over the weekend (e.g., “car go,” “cookie”). Parent feels child is “trying harder” to be understood.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly and eagerly asked, “play cars?”
- Sustained attention to both table-top and floor-based activities was good, with minimal need for redirection.
- Showed increased awareness of speech clarity, sometimes asking, “that good?” after a production.

Therapy goals and observations:
1. Final consonants /p, m, t/ in short sentences
- Used picture sequences to elicit simple 3–4 word sentences (e.g., “I see a map,” “Put on hat,” “I eat soup”).
- Child generally produced final /p, m, t/ accurately within these longer utterances, with fewer omissions than in earlier sessions.
- Occasional errors persisted, but child showed more consistent self-correction when cued to listen and try again.

2. Initial /k/ in words and two-word phrases (reduced cueing)
- Continued work with “car, key, cookie, cow” and added “cup.”
- At the word level, child produced initial /k/ with improved consistency and required slightly less verbal/visual cueing.
- In two-word phrases (e.g., “my car,” “more cookie,” “green cup”), /k/ was present more often than not, representing a small but meaningful improvement.

3. Final /k, n/ in CVC words
- Practiced “book, back, can, man, pan” with a simple storybook and puzzle.
- Child’s final /n/ productions were more stable; final /k/ showed slight improvement, with a few clearer productions emerging without heavy segmentation.
- Still needed occasional modeling and slow rate but showed better carryover than in previous sessions.

4. Functional phrases using target words
- Encouraged child to use phrases during play (e.g., “my big car,” “more cookie please,” “in the cup”).
- Child imitated and spontaneously produced several such phrases; target CVC and /k/ words were often intelligible, though other parts of the phrase could be less clear.

notes/7/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Parent shared that child is talking more and family members are commenting that they “can hear more sounds” at the ends of words. Parent noted /k/ still “comes and goes” but is better in favorite words.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child arrived cheerful and quickly engaged in shared play.
- Demonstrated good persistence with practice tasks and enjoyed taking turns “being the teacher.”
- Tolerated feedback and cueing well and seemed motivated to earn chosen rewards.

Therapy goals and observations:
1. Final consonants /p, m, t, n/ in simple sentences
- Used picture scenes and short stories to elicit sentences like “I see a map,” “Mom has soup,” “I want pan.”
- Child maintained relatively strong accuracy for final /p, m, t/, with /n/ approaching similar consistency.
- Occasional drops occurred in longer or faster sentences, but overall performance was stable to slightly improved.

2. Initial /k/ in an expanded set of words and phrases
- Continued familiar /k/ words and added a few new items (e.g., “coat, cat, corn”).
- At the word level, child produced initial /k/ with good accuracy given a model and light cueing.
- In two- and three-word phrases (e.g., “my cat,” “yellow coat,” “corn in cup”), /k/ remained present more frequently than in past sessions, though not yet fully stable.

3. Final /k/ in CVC words
- Focused on “back, book, pack, duck” with a farm and backpack play set.
- Child produced clearer final /k/ in several imitated trials, particularly when given a slow, segmented model and a visual cue at the end of the word.
- Still inconsistent, but more productions were recognizable /k/ rather than omitted or replaced.

4. Spontaneous phrase production and intelligibility
- During free play, child produced multiple 2–3 word utterances incorporating target sounds (e.g., “big car,” “more cookie,” “my map”).
- Listener familiarity remained a factor, but intelligibility to the clinician appeared slightly improved compared to earlier in the block.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue focusing on /k/ in both initial and final positions with a small set of high-frequency words.
- Maintain sentence-level practice for final consonants to support generalization.
- Parent encouraged to model and emphasize /k/ in everyday routines (e.g., “coat on,” “cat sleeps”) and to prompt child to slow down and “finish the word” when communication breakdowns occur.
- Next session: keep targets similar to allow consolidation while monitoring for gradual gains in independence.
notes/8/note_text:

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver shared that child is "hard to understand" for unfamiliar listeners and often leaves off sounds at the ends of words. No major changes reported since initial evaluation.
- Parent noted child will sometimes say “top” for “stop” and “tar” for “car.”

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned to therapy room with brief hesitation but joined activities with encouragement.
- Attention was variable; child preferred free play but responded well to turn-taking games and short, play-based drills.
- Accepted clinician models and was generally cheerful and cooperative.

Goals / session targets:
1) Initial /k/ at syllable and word level (imitation)
- Practiced /k/ in isolation ("back sound") using mirror and tactile cues to keep tongue back. Child could imitate /k/ in isolation in ~4/10 trials; frequent substitution of /t/ noted.
- At CV syllable level ("ku, ka, koo"), accuracy decreased (approx. half of attempts included /t/ instead of /k/).
- In simple words ("car, key, cat"), productions were mostly /t/ ("tar, tee, tat"); needed maximal verbal and visual cues.

2) Final consonant production (p, m, t) at word level (imitation)
- Targeted CVC words (e.g., "cup, map, hat") using picture cards.
- Child often omitted final sounds ("cu_", "ma_") without cues.
- With direct imitation and cueing to "keep the sound in your mouth at the end," could produce final /p/ and /m/ in about 4/10 opportunities; final /t/ remained difficult.

3) Initial /s/ in isolation and CV syllables (imitation)
- Introduced “snake sound” /s/ in isolation with visual cue (finger along arm) and mirror.
- Child produced /s/ in isolation inconsistently (approximately 3/10 clearly accurate; other attempts were backed to /ʃ/ or sounded like /d/).
- Limited success adding vowels ("see, soo"); needed continuous modeling and segmentation.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue weekly therapy focusing on establishing /k/, initial /s/, and final consonants at isolation and simple syllable/word levels.
- Encouraged caregiver to model "back sound" /k/ in daily routines (e.g., “car, cookie”) without pressuring child to repeat.
- Next session: continue shaping /k/ and /s/ in isolation/CV and further probe final consonant production.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
- Parent reported no major changes in speech since last week. Child is "talking a lot" but still difficult for others to understand.
- Caregiver noticed child attempting “car” more often but it still sounds like “tar.”

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child separated from caregiver with minimal protest and engaged quickly with toys.
- Attention to task was slightly improved; tolerated short drill segments between play activities.
- Needed some redirection from self-directed play but responded well to visual schedules and choices.

Goals / session targets:
1) Initial /k/ at syllable and word level (imitation)
- Continued work on /k/ in isolation and CV syllables using mirror and tongue-back cues.
- Child produced /k/ in isolation more consistently (approx. 6/10 clear productions) when given a slow model and verbal cue “back sound.”
- In CV syllables ("ka, koo, kee"), accuracy increased compared to last session; still frequent /t/ substitutions but child demonstrated more successful attempts and occasional self-correction after clinician feedback.
- At word level ("car, key, cookie"), still mostly /t/ for /k/, but child produced a few approximations with partial backing when given maximal cues.

2) Final consonant production (p, m, t) at word level (imitation)
- Targeted CVC words with picture cards and minimal pair contrasts (e.g., "bee" vs. "beep").
- Child more consistently attempted final sounds; final /p/ and /m/ produced accurately in about half of imitated trials with moderate cueing (tapping cue for the last sound).
- Final /t/ remained less consistent, often omitted or replaced with a glottal stop.

3) Initial /s/ in isolation and CV syllables (imitation)
- Revisited “snake sound” /s/ using a mirror and auditory models.
- Accuracy in isolation improved slightly; about half of attempts were recognizable /s/, with fewer substitutions to /d/.
- At CV syllables ("see, soo"), child could maintain a reasonable /s/ in a few trials when provided with slow models and visual cue; still inconsistent but more frequent accurate approximations.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue current targets; child is beginning to show more consistent /k/ and /s/ in isolation and early syllable work.
- Encourage parent to highlight /k/ and final sounds during shared book reading (e.g., pausing and emphasizing end sounds) without requiring imitation.
- Next session: continue to strengthen /k/ and /s/ at syllable/word level and further shape final consonants; consider brief probes for spontaneous use in play if accuracy continues to build.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver shared that child tried to copy “cookie” and “car” at home when prompted; some attempts sounded closer but still not clear.
- No concerns with hearing or health this week.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered therapy room without difficulty and appeared eager to play.
- Good participation with turn-taking games; accepted structured practice embedded in play.
- Needed occasional reminders to look at clinician’s face/mouth but overall engagement was strong.

Goals / session targets:
1) Initial /k/ at word level (imitation; emerging spontaneous attempts)
- Focused on initial /k/ in high-interest words ("car, cat, cookie, key") using picture scenes and toy cars.
- Child produced /k/ correctly in imitation in roughly 7–8/10 opportunities when given a verbal “back sound” cue and slowed model.
- Spontaneous attempts during play (e.g., reaching for car) occasionally included a backed consonant, though often remained /t/; beginning to approximate “car” more clearly.

2) Final consonant production (p, m, t, k) at word level (imitation)
- Continued CVC word practice with visual cueing (pointing to end of word picture, tapping table for last sound).
- Final /p/ and /m/ now produced with moderate to high accuracy in imitation (around 7/10 when focused), with reduced need for repeated models.
- Final /t/ showed gradual improvement; still omitted at times but child attempted to “add a sound at the end” more frequently.
- Introduced a few final /k/ words ("back, book") in imitation with mixed success; often fronted to /t/.

3) Initial /s/ at isolation and simple word level (imitation)
- Practiced /s/ isolation and moved into a small set of words ("sun, soap, sock") using a “snake game.”
- In isolation, child produced a more consistent /s/ (approx. 7/10 clear attempts) with visual cue.
- At word level, child needed segmented modeling ("s- + un") and verbal reminders to keep tongue behind teeth; produced reasonably accurate initial /s/ on several trials but still substituted /d/ or omitted the fricative in others.

Plan / recommendations:
- Child demonstrating meaningful progress with /k/ and some final consonants at word level; continue to build accuracy and begin to trial short carrier phrases for easier /k/ words in upcoming sessions.
- For home practice, encouraged caregiver to use “car game” and model “back sound” /k/ clearly, praising any close attempts rather than correcting heavily.
- Next session: continue /k/ and /s/ at word level, expand final consonant set as tolerated, and consider early phrase-level practice for most consistent /k/ words.

Parent report:
- Caregiver shared that child is "chatty" at home and uses many of the practiced /k/ phrases spontaneously (e.g., “my car,” “more cookie”).
- Parent feels intelligibility with family is slowly improving, but unfamiliar listeners still struggle.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child separated without difficulty and was enthusiastic to start.
- Engagement and attention similar to previous sessions; participated well in turn-taking and structured play.
- Some playful sound effects during tasks, but responded to redirection.

Goals / session targets:
1) Initial /k/ at phrase and short sentence level (imitation and supported spontaneous)
- Practiced /k/ within short sentences ("I see car," "I want cookie," "my car go").
- Accuracy remained generally good in imitation, with occasional reductions when sentence length increased or when child was excited.
- During free play, child used /k/ words spontaneously; many were clear, though some reverted to /t/ when not focused.

2) Final consonants (p, m, t, k) at word level and emerging phrases (imitation)
- Continued work with CVC words and short phrases.
- Final /p/ and /m/ remained accurate in most imitated words; in phrases, occasional omissions persisted but were similar to prior weeks.
- Final /t/ productions showed no major change from last session; still needs cues to remember the final sound.
- Final /k/ remained the least consistent; a few accurate productions in drill, but not generalized.

3) Initial /s/ in words and simple phrases (imitation)
- Practiced initial /s/ in familiar word set and phrases.
- Word-level /s/ productions were consistent with prior sessions: some very clear, others distorted or replaced, depending on attention.
- In short phrases, accuracy remained variable and required multiple cues; no clear increase compared with last week.

Plan / recommendations:
- Performance continues to be stable; maintaining /k/ in short sentences in structured tasks, with ongoing variability in spontaneous speech and in final consonants.
- For home, suggested parent continue modeling slow, clear speech and emphasize endings of words by gently stretching the final sound (e.g., “cuP,” “maP”).
- Next session: maintain current targets and consider brief probe of /s/ blends at the word level to assess readiness, while keeping primary focus on consolidation.
notes/9/note_text:
Parent report:
- Parent reported that child’s speech is “about the same” this week. Child continues to use practiced phrases but still leaves off sounds sometimes.
- No new concerns reported.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly and chose familiar games.
- Attention and cooperation were consistent with recent weeks; tolerated structured drills interspersed with play.
- Needed a few prompts to use words rather than only sound effects during play.

Goals / session targets:
1) Initial /k/ at short sentence level (imitation and supported spontaneous)
- Continued work with /k/ in short sentences ("I see car," "I want cookie," "my car go fast").
- Accuracy in imitation remained good, especially for highly practiced phrases; some reduction in clarity occurred with longer or more novel sentences.
- In semi-structured play, child spontaneously used several /k/ words; many were correct, though occasional /t/ substitutions persisted.

2) Final consonants (p, m, t, k) at word level and phrases (imitation)
- Repeated CVC word practice and short phrases to maintain skills.
- Final /p/ and /m/ remained stable and generally accurate in imitated words and short phrases.
- Final /t/ continued to require visual/verbal cues to be produced consistently; performance similar to last week.
- Final /k/ accuracy remained limited, with only a few clear productions in heavily cued contexts.

3) Initial /s/ in words and simple phrases (imitation)
- Practiced initial /s/ in familiar words and short phrases.
- Word-level /s/ accuracy remained mixed; clear when child attended closely, less accurate when rushed.
- Phrase-level /s/ continued to be inconsistent, with frequent omissions and substitutions.

Plan / recommendations:
- Skills are stable at current levels; continue reinforcing accuracy for /k/ in short sentences and maintain word-level practice for /s/ and final consonants.
- For home: encouraged caregiver to model clear final sounds during routines (e.g., “brush teeth,” “wash cup”) and praise any attempts, even if imperfect.
- Next session: maintain focus on current sound targets, with potential to introduce very simple /s/ blends at the imitated word level if child is attentive and motivated.
notes/10/note_text:

- Parent noticed child sometimes attempts to add end sounds when reminded.

2) Final consonants (p, m, t, k) at word level and phrases (imitation)

- Final /p/ and /m/ continued to be produced accurately in most imitated attempts, with occasional omissions in faster speech.

notes/11/note_text:
Parent report:
- Parent reported that child’s speech “seems steady.” Family notes he continues to use practiced phrases with /k/ and that some word endings are clearer than a few months ago.
- No new concerns raised.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned well and showed familiarity with the routine.
- Engagement similar to previous sessions; participated in structured and play-based tasks with light prompting.
- Needed gentle reminders to focus on words rather than only sound effects during car play.

Goals / session targets:
1) Initial /k/ at short sentence level (imitation and supported spontaneous)
- Practiced /k/ within short sentences in structured activities and play ("I see car," "I want cookie," "my car go").
- Imitated sentences remained largely accurate for /k/, especially for well-practiced phrases.
- In spontaneous speech during play, child used several /k/ words; productions were generally consistent with recent sessions, with occasional fronting when excited.

2) Final consonants (p, m, t, k) at word level and phrases (imitation)
- Continued CVC word and short phrase practice.
- Final /p/ and /m/ remained reliable in imitation and in some simple phrases; omissions still occurred when speech rate increased.
- Final /t/ accuracy was similar to previous weeks; child needed reminders and visual cues to maintain final closure.
- Final /k/ remained the most challenging; only a few accurate productions in highly supported contexts.

3) Initial /s/ in words and simple phrases (imitation)
