notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom reported that she understands her child most of the time but unfamiliar listeners still ask for frequent repetition, especially for longer words and when he is excited. She noted that he often leaves off the ends of words and sometimes says “tup” for “cup.” No concerns about hearing or overall health this week.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned with mild hesitation but separated from caregiver with reassurance. He engaged well in play-based activities, responded to visual supports, and tolerated sitting at the table for short segments. Attention was variable but generally adequate with frequent movement breaks.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants (p, t, m) in CVC words – imitation level
- Introduced final /p, t, m/ in simple CVC words (e.g., cup, cat, mom) using picture cards and play routines. Client imitated some targets but frequently omitted the final consonant or added a vowel (e.g., “cu-” for cup, “ca-” for cat). Needed consistent visual cues (pointing to mouth, tapping for final sound) and verbal reminders to “keep the sound on.” Accuracy was low and productions were inconsistent.

2. Initial /k/ vs /t/ contrast – isolation and simple syllables
- Targeted back vs front sounds using a “back sound” vs “front sound” game. Client was able to approximate /k/ in isolation with maximal verbal and visual cueing (open mouth, “tongue in the back”) but frequently substituted /t/ in CV syllables (e.g., “too” for “koo”). Difficulty carrying over from isolation to syllables.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Practiced /s/ in isolation with a “snake sound” visual. Client could sustain a prolonged /s/ with models, but productions were sometimes distorted or stopped. In CV syllables (e.g., “see,” “soo”), accuracy decreased, with substitutions to /d/ or omission. Required direct imitation and modeling; no spontaneous use noted.

4. Two-word combinations with clear key words
- During play, encouraged two-word phrases that highlighted target sounds (e.g., “big cup,” “my cat,” “see snake”). Client produced several two-word combinations, but target sounds were often reduced or omitted. Focus was on exposure and shaping rather than accuracy.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue to target final consonants in CVC words and initial /k/ and /s/ at the isolation and simple syllable levels with high support. Encourage parents to model slow, clear speech at home and emphasize final sounds in daily routines (e.g., “cuP,” “caT,” “baTH”) without pressuring for imitation. Next session will further probe which consonants are stimulable at the syllable and word level.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom shared that she has been trying to emphasize final sounds at home and notices that he will occasionally copy her when she exaggerates them (e.g., sometimes says “cup” more clearly). She still feels others have difficulty understanding him in longer phrases. No new medical or developmental concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client entered the therapy room more easily today and quickly engaged with toys. Attention to structured tasks was improved compared to last session, with fewer redirections needed. He responded well to turn-taking games and accepted cues without frustration.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants (p, t, m) in CVC words – imitation and emerging spontaneous attempts
- Continued practice with final /p, t, m/ using picture cards and a “feeding the puppet” game. Compared with last session, client produced more consistent final consonants in imitation, especially for /p/ and /m/ (e.g., “cup,” “map,” “mom”). Still occasionally dropped final /t/, but with verbal cueing (“keep your sound at the end”) he was able to add it in a second attempt. A few spontaneous productions with clearer final sounds were observed during play (“up,” “pop”), suggesting early carryover.

2. Initial /k/ vs /t/ – CV and CVC syllables
- Practiced “back sound /k/” in CV (koo, key, kah) and simple CVC words (cup, cat, car). Client showed improved ability to produce /k/ in CV syllables with modeling and a mirror, with fewer /t/ substitutions. In CVC words, accuracy remained variable, but he was more willing to attempt correction when given feedback (e.g., “try your back sound”).

3. Initial /s/ – isolation and CV syllables
- Revisited /s/ in isolation and CV syllables using a “snake puppet” and visual cue for teeth together. Client’s isolated /s/ was more consistent today with less stopping. In CV syllables (see, soo, saa), he was able to approximate /s/ at the beginning of the syllable more frequently with verbal and visual cues, though some productions were still substituted or omitted.

4. Two-word combinations featuring target sounds
- Encouraged phrases such as “my cup,” “see car,” “big sun” during play. Client produced several two-word combinations; target sounds were more audible in imitation than in spontaneous attempts. Still relies on models for clearer productions.

Plan / recommendations:
Maintain current focus on final consonants and initial /k/ and /s/ at the word and syllable level, as skills are beginning to emerge. Provide parents with a short list of practice words for final /p/ and /m/ to use in play (e.g., pop, up, map, nap, mom). Next session will continue to strengthen consistency and begin to probe whether any targets are ready to move toward short carrier phrases in structured tasks.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom reported that he is starting to correct himself occasionally, especially on words like “cup” and “pop,” after she models them. She feels some single words are clearer, but sentences are still hard to understand. No new concerns at home or daycare.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was cheerful and eager to show toys. He maintained engagement in table-top tasks for longer stretches and followed simple directions well. Mild silliness at times, but easily redirected with visual supports and choice-making.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants (p, t, m, k) in CVC words – imitation and early carrier phrases
- Practiced final /p, t, m, k/ in CVC words using a board game and picture cards. Compared with last week, accuracy in imitation increased noticeably, with clearer final consonants on many trials (e.g., “cup, cap, cat, back, mom”). He required fewer repetitions and was able to add missing final sounds when cued. Trialed simple carrier phrases (e.g., “my cup,” “I see cat”) with moderate support; final sounds were sometimes maintained in these short phrases, though consistency decreased compared to single words.

2. Initial /k/ in CV and CVC words – imitation and minimal pairs
- Focused on distinguishing /k/ from /t/ using minimal pairs (e.g., key/tea, car/tar). Client showed improved accuracy for /k/ in initial position in imitation, especially when reminded to use his “back sound.” He could correctly identify which word the clinician said most of the time and made more accurate attempts to match it. Occasional fronting to /t/ still occurred, but he responded to cues to “make it in the back.”

3. Initial /s/ in CV and simple CVC words
- Continued practice with /s/ at the beginning of words in a “snake race” game. Client produced more consistent initial /s/ in CV syllables (see, soo, saa) with visual and verbal cues. In simple CVC words (sun, soup, sock), accuracy was emerging; he sometimes substituted /d/ or omitted the sound, but showed increasing willingness to adjust his productions when prompted.

4. Short carrier phrases with target words
- Used repetitive carrier phrases such as “I want ___,” “my ___,” and “see ___” with target words containing final consonants or initial /k/ and /s/. Client could imitate short phrases with support and occasionally produced them spontaneously in play. Target sounds were clearer in imitation than in independent use, but overall phrase length and willingness to talk were strengths.

Plan / recommendations:
Given the moderate gains in final consonant and initial /k/ production, continue to build consistency at the word level while gradually increasing use in short phrases in structured play. Provide parents with 5–6 practice words for final /p, t, m, k/ and encourage modeling within simple phrases (e.g., “big cup,” “hot cat”). Next session will further trial carrier phrases and monitor readiness to expand to more spontaneous use in play.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom noted that his words with /k/ and /p/ at the end (e.g., “back,” “cup”) are getting easier for close family to understand. She has noticed that he sometimes uses clearer speech when he slows down. Daycare staff still report reduced intelligibility in group settings.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned smoothly and participated actively in both play-based and structured tasks. He was motivated by turn-taking games and stickers and accepted feedback well. Attention was good, with only brief breaks needed.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants (p, t, m, k) in CVC words and short phrases
- Continued work on final consonants in a picture-matching and “shopping” game. Compared with last session, productions were more consistent at the single-word level, especially for /p/ and /m/. Final /t/ and /k/ were improving but still less stable. In short phrases (e.g., “my cup,” “big cat,” “in back”), he was able to maintain the final consonant more often than previously, though accuracy still dropped relative to single words.

2. Initial /k/ in CVC words and simple phrases
- Practiced initial /k/ in words like car, cat, key, cup using play with cars and pretend food. Client produced /k/ more reliably in imitation with fewer /t/ substitutions. In short phrases (“go car,” “my car,” “key please”), he was able to approximate /k/ with moderate verbal cueing. He sometimes self-corrected after hearing the clinician’s model.

3. Initial /s/ in CVC words – imitation level
- Targeted initial /s/ in words (sun, sock, soup, soap) using a “sorting” activity. Accuracy in imitation was slightly better than last session but remained inconsistent. He occasionally produced a stopped or distorted version of /s/. Visual cues (showing teeth, long air sound) were still needed, and carryover to phrases was not yet targeted.

4. Short carrier phrases to support intelligibility
- Used repetitive phrases (“I want ___,” “see my ___,” “put in ___”) with target words containing final consonants or initial /k/. Client imitated these phrases with relatively good prosody, and some spontaneous phrases emerged in play. Overall clarity of short phrases was slightly improved when he focused and accepted cues.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue strengthening final consonant production and initial /k/ in both single words and short phrases, as gains are building. Maintain initial /s/ at the word level until productions become more consistent before moving to phrases. At home, parents are encouraged to prompt slower speech and model short, clear phrases rather than long sentences. Next session will reinforce these targets and probe for any emerging spontaneous use of final consonants in unstructured play.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom reported small but noticeable changes in his clarity, especially in short, familiar phrases (e.g., “my cup,” “go car”). She mentioned that he is starting to be understood a bit more by extended family. She continues to hear him leave off some end sounds when he talks quickly.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client arrived in good spirits and transitioned independently into the room. He participated well in both floor play and table activities, with good turn-taking and responsiveness to cues. Mild fatigue noted near the end of the session, but he remained cooperative.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants (p, t, m, k) – word level and structured phrases
- Practiced final consonants in a “treasure hunt” game with picture cards and small objects. Compared to last week, final consonant productions remained fairly consistent, with slightly increased accuracy for final /t/ and /k/. He continued to do well with /p/ and /m/ in imitation and was beginning to produce some clear final sounds during less structured play (e.g., “pop,” “up,” “mom”) without direct prompting. In short phrases, accuracy was similar to previous session, with emerging self-monitoring.

2. Initial /k/ – word level and phrases
- Used toy cars and animals to practice initial /k/ words (car, cat, cow, key). Client maintained his improved performance, producing /k/ correctly in many imitated words and some short phrases (e.g., “my car,” “big cow”) when cued. He still occasionally fronted to /t/ but responded to a simple reminder (“back sound”) to adjust.

3. Initial /s/ – CVC words
- Continued focus on initial /s/ using a “sunny day” and “silly sock” game. Accuracy remained similar to last session, with intermittent correct productions in imitation and ongoing need for visual cues. He could sometimes produce a clear /s/ in isolation, but carryover into words was inconsistent. No advancement to phrases yet.

4. Short, functional phrases to support communication
- Encouraged use of short, functional phrases (e.g., “I want car,” “more pop,” “my turn”) throughout play. Client imitated phrases readily and produced some spontaneously. Intelligibility within these short, practiced phrases was slightly better, though not yet consistently clear for unfamiliar listeners.

Plan / recommendations:
Maintain current targets as skills are consolidating. Continue to emphasize final consonants and initial /k/ in home routines, praising any clear productions. Provide mom with visual cue ideas (e.g., tapping the table for the final sound). Next session will reassess initial /s/ for potential changes and continue to monitor for readiness to expand phrase-level work for /s/ once word-level accuracy improves.
notes/5/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom stated that his speech seems “a bit clearer” in everyday routines, and she is hearing more final sounds when he’s focused. She noted that he still becomes harder to understand when excited or talking quickly. No changes in health or hearing.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was calm and cooperative throughout the session. He followed routines easily, engaged in both structured tasks and pretend play, and responded positively to praise. Attention span was adequate, and he tolerated brief correction and re-dos without frustration.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants (p, t, m, k) – consolidation at word and short-phrase level
- Continued practice with final consonants using a board game and “hide and seek” with picture cards. Compared to previous sessions, accuracy remained relatively stable, with ongoing strength in final /p/ and /m/ and gradually improving /t/ and /k/. He maintained clearer final consonants in many imitated words and some short phrases (e.g., “my cup,” “big map”). Spontaneous use of final consonants appeared occasionally in play but was not yet consistent.

2. Initial /k/ – word and phrase level
- Practiced initial /k/ within themed play (cars, kitchen). Client produced /k/ accurately in many imitated words and several short phrases with minimal cueing. He still fronted /k/ in more complex or less familiar words, but he was increasingly aware of the need for a “back sound” when reminded. Performance was similar to last week, indicating consolidation.

3. Initial /s/ – CVC words, imitation
- Revisited initial /s/ words (sun, soup, sock, soap, sit) using picture cards and a simple sorting game. Accuracy showed subtle improvement but remained variable, with correct productions mixed with omissions or substitutions. Visual cues (teeth together, long air sound) were still required. Did not yet move to phrase level.

4. Functional phrase practice and intelligibility
- Used predictable routines (snack play, car race) to elicit short phrases (e.g., “my turn,” “go car,” “more please”). Client produced these with increasing ease. Intelligibility within these practiced phrases was somewhat better than in unstructured conversation but still inconsistent.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue current focus as skills are stabilizing, particularly final consonants and initial /k/. Maintain initial /s/ at the word level until accuracy is more consistent. Recommend that parents continue modeling short, clear phrases and highlight final consonants in natural contexts. Next session will aim to gently increase the complexity of phrases for final consonant targets while monitoring overall clarity.
notes/6/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom reported that family members have commented that he is easier to understand, especially when he uses short phrases they’ve practiced (e.g., “my car,” “more juice”). She still notices dropped end sounds in longer or less familiar words. No new concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned quickly and was eager to participate. He engaged well in both structured and play-based tasks, with good cooperation and response to feedback. He showed increased willingness to attempt self-corrections when given gentle prompts.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants (p, t, m, k) – expanded phrase level
- Practiced final consonants in CVC words embedded in short phrases and simple sentences using a picture story activity (e.g., “I see a cup,” “big cat on mat,” “mom has map”). Compared with last week, he showed slightly improved ability to maintain final consonants within these short phrases, particularly for /p/ and /m/. Final /t/ and /k/ were still less consistent but improving. Some self-correction emerged when he omitted a final sound and heard the clinician’s model.

2. Initial /k/ – phrases and early simple sentences
- Used play scenes (garage, farm) to elicit initial /k/ in short phrases and simple sentences (e.g., “the car goes,” “cow is big,” “I see a key”). Client produced /k/ more reliably with light verbal cueing and was able to maintain it in some short sentences with repetition. Occasional fronting to /t/ occurred but was less frequent than in earlier sessions.

3. Initial /s/ – CVC words, imitation and emerging phrases
- Continued focus on initial /s/ in words (sun, sock, soup, sand, soap). Accuracy in imitation showed small but noticeable gains, with more consistent /s/ productions when cued. Briefly trialed initial /s/ in short carrier phrases (e.g., “see sun,” “my sock”) in structured tasks; he could approximate /s/ in some of these phrases with moderate cueing but was not yet consistent.

4. Functional short sentences for communication
- Encouraged use of short, functional sentences in play (e.g., “I want car,” “I see cat,” “put in cup”). Client imitated these readily and produced some independently. Intelligibility in these short, practiced sentences was improving, especially when he slowed down.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue to expand phrase- and early sentence-level practice for final consonants and initial /k/, as performance is gradually improving. Maintain a mix of structured and play-based tasks to support carryover. For /s/, continue at the word level with brief, supported phrase trials when accuracy allows. Parents are encouraged to practice a small set of predictable phrases at home, focusing on clear final sounds and initial /k/. Next session will further strengthen these emerging skills.
notes/7/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom shared that she has noticed a “big jump” in how clearly he can say some of his practiced words and phrases, especially ones with /k/ and final /p/ or /m/. She reported that daycare staff also commented that they are understanding him more during routines.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was very engaged and talkative today. He initiated interactions, followed multi-step play routines, and responded well to feedback. He showed clear interest in trying to “fix” his words when given simple cues, indicating increased awareness of his speech.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants (p, t, m, k) – phrase and simple sentence level, increased independence
- Used a picture-sequencing and storytelling activity to embed CVC words with final consonants into short sentences (e.g., “The cat sits on the mat,” “Mom has a cup,” “Put the map in the bag”). Compared to previous sessions, he demonstrated noticeably higher consistency in producing final /p/ and /m/ and improved accuracy for /t/ and /k/ within these short sentences. He often maintained final consonants without needing direct prompts and occasionally self-corrected when he noticed an omission.

2. Initial /k/ – short sentences and early conversation
- Practiced initial /k/ in short sentences (“The car is fast,” “The cow eats,” “I can kick”) and then in more natural play-based conversation about cars and animals. He produced /k/ accurately in many imitated and some spontaneous sentences with minimal cueing. Fronting errors were less frequent and, when they occurred, he typically responded to a brief reminder (“back sound”) and corrected himself.

3. Initial /s/ – word and early phrase level
- Continued with initial /s/ in words (sun, soup, sock, sand, soap, seat) and expanded phrase-level practice (e.g., “see sun,” “soap on,” “sock on”). Accuracy at the word level was meaningfully better than earlier weeks, with more consistent /s/ productions in imitation and some clearer productions in short phrases with visual and verbal cues. Still requires support but is ready for more regular phrase practice.

4. Functional short sentences – generalization
- Encouraged the client to use short sentences to request, comment, and describe during play (e.g., “I want more,” “My car is stuck,” “Put it in cup”). Intelligibility within these short, familiar sentence frames was noticeably improved, and he seemed proud when others understood him without repetition.

Plan / recommendations:
Given the marked progress, it is appropriate to continue advancing final consonant and initial /k/ targets into short sentences and structured conversational contexts. For /s/, continue to build consistency at the word level while steadily incorporating short phrases. Parents are encouraged to prompt short sentences at home (e.g., “I want ___,” “I see ___”) and reinforce clear final sounds and initial /k/ when they naturally occur. Next sessions will focus on maintaining these gains and expanding to more spontaneous use in play and conversation.
notes/8/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom reported that his clarity continues to be better overall, and she rarely needs to ask him to repeat in routine situations. She noted that he still struggles in fast, excited storytelling, where some end sounds and certain consonants (especially /s/) are less clear. No new concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was cooperative and interactive. He engaged readily in structured tasks and pretend play, showing good attention for his age. Some increased chattiness led to reduced precision at times, but he responded well when reminded to “use his careful speech.”

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants (p, t, m, k) – short sentences and structured conversation
- Continued practicing final consonants in short sentence frames and brief, structured conversations about picture scenes (e.g., describing what people have or what animals do). His performance was similar to last week: generally good accuracy in practiced sentences, with occasional omissions when he spoke more quickly. He was often able to correct himself when cued to slow down and “say the end sound.”

2. Initial /k/ – sentence level and play-based conversation
- Used themed play (cars, cooking) to elicit sentences with initial /k/ (e.g., “The car can go,” “Cook the corn,” “The cat is on the car”). He maintained a strong pattern of accurate /k/ production in these contexts, with only occasional fronting when excited. Minimal verbal cueing was needed compared to earlier in the block.

3. Initial /s/ – word and short phrase level, consolidation
- Practiced initial /s/ in words and short phrases (sun, soap, sock, sand, “see sun,” “soap on,” “sock on foot”). Accuracy was comparable to last session, with some correct productions and some substitutions or omissions. Visual and verbal cues remained important. Did not significantly expand beyond short phrases today to allow consolidation.

4. Functional sentences – generalization across play
- Encouraged use of short, functional sentences across different play routines (e.g., “I want the blue car,” “Put it on top,” “That cat is big”). Intelligibility in these contexts was relatively stable compared to last session: improved from early in the block but still variable depending on complexity and speed.

Plan / recommendations:
Maintain current level of difficulty to allow consolidation of skills, particularly for final consonants and initial /s/ at the word and short-phrase level. Parents should continue to reinforce clear speech in everyday routines, especially by encouraging him to slow down when telling longer stories. Next session will re-evaluate /s/ accuracy to determine readiness for more consistent phrase- and sentence-level practice.
notes/9/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom indicated that his speech this week seemed similar to last week. She continues to feel that he is much easier to understand than when therapy started, but she still hears inconsistent /s/ sounds and some missing end sounds when he is tired.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client presented as friendly and cooperative. He participated well in both table and floor activities, though mild distractibility was observed near the end of the session. He accepted cues and feedback without difficulty.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants (p, t, m, k) – sentence level and simple narratives
- Used simple picture sequences to elicit sentences with final consonants (e.g., “The cat sits on the mat,” “Mom has a cup,” “He packs the bag”). His performance remained similar to the previous week: generally good accuracy for final /p/ and /m/, with some variability in /t/ and /k/ during longer or less familiar sentences. He continued to respond to prompts to slow down and add the end sound.

2. Initial /k/ – sentence level and retelling
- Practiced initial /k/ in sentences and brief retellings (e.g., describing car and kitchen scenes). He maintained a strong pattern of accurate /k/ production with minimal cueing, indicating good consolidation. Occasional fronting occurred when speech rate increased, but he was usually able to correct with a quick reminder.

3. Initial /s/ – word and short phrase level
- Continued work on initial /s/ in words and short phrases using picture cards and a simple “silly snake” game. Accuracy remained similar to last session, with a mix of correct productions and errors. He benefited from consistent visual cues and slowed speech. Phrase-level work (“see sun,” “sit down,” “soap on”) was practiced with moderate support.

4. Functional communication – short sentences and requests
- Encouraged the client to use short sentences to request and comment throughout the session. Intelligibility in these familiar sentence types was steady compared to last week, with clearer speech when he attended to cues and reduced clarity when he spoke quickly.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue at the current level to further stabilize final consonant and initial /k/ skills in sentence and narrative contexts. Maintain focused practice on initial /s/ in words and short phrases before advancing further. Parents are encouraged to continue modeling and gently prompting clearer /s/ sounds in familiar words (e.g., sun, soap, sit) without pressuring him. Next session will revisit these same targets and monitor for any emerging changes in /s/ consistency.
notes/10/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom stated that his speech sounded about the same as last week. She feels confident understanding him at home, while unfamiliar listeners still occasionally have difficulty with certain words or longer sentences. She has been practicing a few /s/ words with him during routines (e.g., soap, sit).

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was pleasant and engaged. He participated in structured drills and play-based tasks with good cooperation. Some playful silliness emerged mid-session, but he responded to clear expectations and redirection.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants (p, t, m, k) – sentence and story contexts
- Continued using picture stories and simple role-play to elicit sentences with final consonants. His production of final /p/ and /m/ in sentences remained strong and fairly automatic. Final /t/ and /k/ were generally accurate in familiar sentences, with occasional omissions in newer or more complex sentences. Performance was consistent with recent sessions.

2. Initial /k/ – conversation-level practice
- Incorporated initial /k/ into more spontaneous conversation about his favorite toys and activities (cars, cooking, camping). He produced /k/ accurately in most opportunities with little to no cueing, suggesting good generalization to less structured contexts. Occasional errors occurred when excited, but overall stability was observed.

3. Initial /s/ – word and short phrase level, continued consolidation
- Practiced initial /s/ in a limited set of high-frequency words and phrases (sun, soap, sit, sock, “see sun,” “sit down”). Accuracy was similar to prior weeks, with some clear productions and some substitutions or omissions. He continued to benefit from visual and verbal cues and did not yet demonstrate consistent accuracy without support.

4. Functional short sentences – requests and comments
- Used predictable sentence frames (e.g., “I want ___,” “Look at ___,” “Put it in ___”) throughout the session. His intelligibility within these frames was stable, with clearer output when he used his “slow, careful speech.”

Plan / recommendations:
Maintain current targets and complexity to allow ongoing consolidation of skills. Continue emphasizing clear final consonants and /k/ in everyday conversation at home, and keep practicing a small set of /s/ words with visual cues. Next session will likely use similar activities to reinforce these patterns and support generalization rather than introducing significantly new targets.
notes/11/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom reported that his overall speech clarity continues to feel similar to the last few weeks. She is pleased with how far he has come since starting therapy and would like to keep focusing on tricky sounds like /s/ and some longer sentences. No new concerns were noted.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned easily and was enthusiastic about participating. He remained engaged across the session with only brief need for redirection. He showed good tolerance for practice and enjoyed talking about his interests.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants (p, t, m, k) – maintenance in sentence and narrative contexts
- Used short stories and picture descriptions to elicit sentences with final consonants (e.g., “The cat sits on the mat,” “Mom has a cup,” “The duck is back in the pond”). His performance was in line with prior weeks: final /p/ and /m/ were consistently present and clear; final /t/ and /k/ were generally accurate in familiar sentences, with occasional omissions in newer or more complex structures. He continued to respond to reminders to slow down and “add the end sound.”

2. Initial /k/ – conversational use and carryover
- Incorporated initial /k/ words into natural conversation and open-ended questions (e.g., “What can the car do?”, “What can we cook?”). He produced /k/ accurately in most spontaneous attempts with minimal cueing, indicating stable carryover. Errors were infrequent and typically occurred when he was speaking quickly or overlapping with the clinician.

3. Initial /s/ – word and short phrase level, ongoing focus
- Practiced initial /s/ in a core set of words and phrases (sun, soap, sit, sock, soup, “see sun,” “sit down,” “soap on”). Accuracy remained variable but showed some correct productions within each activity. He continued to require visual cues (teeth together, long air) and verbal reminders. No major change from the last several sessions, suggesting this remains a developing target.

4. Functional sentences and short narratives – overall intelligibility
- Encouraged him to use short sentences to describe actions and retell simple events (e.g., what happened in the picture story, what he did over the weekend). Intelligibility in these short narratives was noticeably better than at the beginning of the therapy block, though still variable depending on length, rate, and the presence of more challenging sounds like /s/.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue to provide therapy to maintain and further refine final consonant and initial /k/ skills at the sentence and conversational level, and to support ongoing development of initial /s/. Recommend that parents keep modeling clear /s/ in daily routines and practice a small, consistent set of /s/ words and phrases. Future sessions will likely target /s/ more intensively and explore additional sounds or longer utterances as appropriate, while monitoring generalization of existing gains.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver shared that child is starting junior kindergarten in the fall and that unfamiliar listeners often have difficulty understanding him.
- No major changes since the initial assessment; family notices that he sometimes leaves sounds off the ends of words (e.g., “boo” for “book”).

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned to the therapy room with some initial hesitation but joined the clinician after a brief warm-up game.
- Engaged best with hands-on toys and short turn-taking games; needed frequent reminders to stay at the table and participate in structured tasks.
- Responded well to specific praise and visual reinforcement (stickers, picture cards).

Goals / therapy focus:
1. Establish production of /k/ and /g/ in isolation and simple syllables
- Focused on /k/ in isolation using mouth pictures and a “back sound” cue. Child could approximate /k/ with maximal verbal and visual cueing in a few attempts, but often substituted /t/.
- Introduced simple CV syllables with /k/ (e.g., “koo,” “kee”); accuracy was low, with most productions sounding more like /t/.
- Briefly trialed /g/ in isolation; child had difficulty coordinating the movement and required hand-over-hand cues for jaw stabilization.

2. Final consonant production (CVC words: /p, m/)
- Practiced final /p/ in CVC words in imitation (e.g., “up,” “cup,” “hop”). Child frequently omitted the final sound or added a vowel (e.g., “uh-puh”).
- With maximal cues (visual, tapping the table for the last sound), child produced clearer final /p/ in a few trials but was not yet consistent.
- Final /m/ in short words (“ham,” “mom”) was sometimes present in spontaneous play but often reduced or nasalized without a clear closure.

3. Initial /f/ in isolation and simple syllables
- Introduced /f/ as a “quiet lip-biting air sound.” Child was able to imitate the airflow with visual modeling but frequently added a stop (e.g., “fp”).
- Practiced CV syllables (e.g., “foo,” “fee”); productions were effortful and required continuous cues to “bite lip and blow.”

4. Early two-word combinations during play
- Encouraged simple two-word phrases (e.g., “more bubbles,” “my turn”) during familiar routines.
- Child primarily used single words or jargon; required direct models and hand-over-hand signs to attempt two-word combinations.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to build awareness of back sounds /k, g/ and final consonants at the isolation and simple word levels.
- Provide caregivers with demonstration of “back sound” cue and final consonant tapping strategy for short practice at home using 3–5 familiar words.
- Next session, plan to further stabilize /k/ in isolation/CV syllables and re-check final consonants with more structured play contexts.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver reported that child has been more chatty at home but speech is still “a bit mushy” for others.
- Family tried some “back sound” practice; caregiver noted that he can sometimes make a stronger /k/ sound when very focused.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child separated from caregiver with minimal prompting today and appeared excited to show the clinician a toy car.
- Attention to table tasks improved compared to last week; remained seated for short activities with occasional redirection.
- Responded positively to turn-taking games and verbal praise.

Goals / therapy focus:
1. /k/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Reviewed /k/ in isolation using visual models and a tongue-back cue. Child produced clearer /k/ in several attempts with moderate verbal and visual cues.
- Practiced CV syllables (“kee, koo, kah”); accuracy was higher than last session when imitating directly after the model, though still inconsistent.
- Noted a few spontaneous approximations of /k/ during play (e.g., “car”) with partial backing but reduced clarity.

2. Final consonants in CVC words (/p, m/)
- Targeted final /p/ in imitation using picture cards (“cup, up, nap”). With moderate cues (tapping for the last sound, slowed rate), child produced more audible final consonants in several trials.
- Final /m/ in words like “ham,” “mom,” “room” showed emerging accuracy; still occasional omission or vowel prolongation.
- Overall, slightly more consistent marking of final consonants compared to baseline, though still requiring high levels of cueing.

3. Initial /f/ in CV syllables and simple words
- Practiced /f/ in CV syllables (“fee, foo”) with visual mirror support. Child demonstrated more reliable airflow with lip-biting but continued to insert an extra sound at times.
- Introduced a small set of words (“fish, four, fun”) at the imitated word level. Productions were often approximations but closer to target than prior session.

4. Two-word combinations in play routines
- Modeled and elicited two-word phrases (“more bubbles,” “go car,” “my turn”) during structured play.
- Child imitated several 2-word combinations with clinician models; spontaneous use was limited but there were more attempts than last week.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue focusing on /k/ CV syllables and CVC words with final /p, m/, aiming for increased consistency with moderate cues.
- Encourage caregivers to practice 3–5 /k/ and final consonant words daily, keeping practice short and playful.
- Next session, consider trialing simple /k/ words in play if current level remains consistent or improves.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver shared that child is starting to say “cup” and “up” more clearly at home when reminded to “use the last sound.”
- Noted a few clearer /k/ words (e.g., “car”) in daily routines.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly and quickly engaged with a car and farm play set.
- Demonstrated good participation in structured tasks with brief movement breaks.
- Followed simple directions and tolerated correction and re-dos without distress.

Goals / therapy focus:
1. /k/ at CV and initial word level
- Child produced /k/ in CV syllables (“kee, koo, kah”) with improved consistency using only light verbal reminders ("tongue back").
- Advanced to initial /k/ words in imitation (e.g., “car, key, cow, cat”). Accuracy was noticeably higher than previous sessions, with several clear productions in a row.
- Occasional fronting to /t/ persisted, but child responded to visual cueing (pointing to throat/back of mouth) and could self-correct when prompted.

2. Final consonants in CVC words (/p, m/; introduction of /t/)
- Final /p/ in CVC words (“cup, up, hop”) produced more consistently with moderate cues. Child began to anticipate the last sound when clinician tapped the table.
- Final /m/ words (“ham, mom, room”) showed better closure and resonance; omissions were less frequent.
- Introduced final /t/ in a small set of words (e.g., “hat, hot”). Child often omitted /t/ but could mark it in some trials with explicit slow speech and tapping.

3. Initial /f/ in simple words
- Continued work on /f/ in words (“fish, four, fun, feet”). Child was able to produce a clearer /f/ at the beginning of some words with visual support in the mirror.
- Still showed occasional stopping or cluster-like productions (e.g., “fpish”), but overall approximations were closer to target.

4. Two- and three-word phrases in structured play
- Encouraged short phrases (e.g., “go fast car,” “more bubbles please”) with verbal models.
- Child imitated several 2–3 word phrases accurately; spontaneous combinations were emerging, though still shorter and less clear.

Plan / recommendations:
- Given progress, continue building consistency at initial /k/ word level and expand the word list.
- Maintain work on final consonants, especially /p, m, t/, using tapping cues.
- Provide caregiver with a short list of /k/ and final consonant words to practice in play (e.g., bath, snack time).
- Next session, consider probing /k/ in short carrier phrases if word-level accuracy remains strong.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver reported more frequent clear “car” and “key,” and that child sometimes corrects himself after saying “tar.”
- Family continues brief home practice; caregiver feels he is “catching on” to the back sound idea.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered the room independently and eagerly joined the clinician at the table.
- Attention was good for short, structured activities; benefitted from alternating table work with movement-based games.
- Demonstrated increased willingness to repeat words and accept feedback.

Goals / therapy focus:
1. /k/ initial words and early carrier phrases
- Practiced a wider set of /k/ initial words in imitation and then in simple carrier phrases (e.g., “my car,” “go cat”).
- At the word level, child produced /k/ with increasing consistency using intermittent verbal reminders; fewer substitutions to /t/ compared to last week.
- In short phrases, accuracy decreased slightly but remained generally intelligible, indicating readiness to continue practicing at this level.

2. Final consonants: /p, m, t/ in CVC words
- Continued work on final /p/ and /m/ using picture cards and play routines. Child more consistently marked the final consonant when given a slow rate and tapping cue.
- Final /t/ remained more challenging; omissions were common, but child produced a clearer /t/ in some high-interest words (e.g., “hat”) with maximal cueing.
- Overall, there was a modest increase in consistency compared to previous session.

3. Initial /f/ words and syllables
- Practiced /f/ in isolation and in CV syllables as a warm-up, then moved into words (“fish, feet, fun, four”).
- Child showed slightly better control over airflow and lip placement, though still required frequent reminders.
- A few spontaneous approximations of /f/ were observed in play (e.g., “food”), though not consistently accurate.

4. Two- and three-word phrases in play
- Modeled short functional phrases (“my turn,” “go fast car,” “more bubbles”) and encouraged child to imitate.
- Child imitated several phrases and occasionally combined 2–3 words independently, though speech clarity varied.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue consolidating /k/ at word level and short phrases; maintain strong emphasis on final consonants.
- Provide caregiver with simple phrase frames containing /k/ words (e.g., “my car,” “big cat”) for home practice.
- Next session, plan to further strengthen final /t/ and re-check /f/ in a slightly larger variety of words.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver noticed that child’s “cup, up, nap” sound clearer and that family members are understanding him more often.
- Reported that he will sometimes say “my car” spontaneously during play.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child arrived cheerful and transitioned without difficulty.
- Maintained attention for longer stretches at the table, especially when activities were game-based.
- Needed occasional reminders to slow down speech when excited.

Goals / therapy focus:
1. /k/ initial words and short phrases
- Reviewed /k/ words in imitation, then practiced in short phrases (e.g., “big car,” “go cow,” “my key”).
- Word-level accuracy remained strong with minimal cueing; in short phrases, /k/ was generally present but sometimes softened or fronted when speaking quickly.
- Child showed some emerging self-monitoring, pausing and attempting to fix a few productions after clinician feedback.

2. Final consonants: /p, m, t/ in CVC words
- Targeted final /p/ and /m/ within a picture-based board game. Child usually marked the final sounds when cued to “tap the last sound,” though occasional omissions still occurred.
- Final /t/ remained inconsistent. In words like “hat, hot, cat,” child sometimes produced a weak or omitted /t/ even with cues.
- Overall, accuracy was similar to last week, with small gains in consistency but still needing significant support.

3. Initial /f/ words
- Continued practice with /f/ words (“fish, feet, four, fun, food”). Child produced more recognizable /f/ at the beginning of words, but still occasionally added extra sounds or reverted to other consonants.
- Required ongoing visual modeling and reminders to “bite lip and blow.”

4. Short functional phrases
- Encouraged use of 2–3 word phrases in play (e.g., “more bubbles please,” “my big car”).
- Child imitated multiple phrases and independently produced some 2-word combinations; clarity varied depending on the sound targets included.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue focusing on consolidating /k/ in short phrases and strengthening final consonant production, particularly /t/.
- Provide caregiver with a small set of final /t/ words and a tapping strategy for home practice.
- Next session, plan to introduce simple /k/ phrases in more natural play contexts and continue monitoring /f/ progress.
notes/5/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver stated that child now often says “my car” and “big cat” at home, though “cat” sometimes still sounds like “ca.”
- Family has been practicing final /t/ words, but caregiver finds this is “the trickiest one” for him.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child separated easily and was eager to pick the first activity.
- Demonstrated good cooperation with therapist-led tasks and followed simple directions reliably.
- Enjoyed being the “teacher” and giving the clinician practice words.

Goals / therapy focus:
1. /k/ in carrier phrases and structured play
- Practiced /k/ in a variety of initial word positions within simple carrier phrases (e.g., “I see a car,” “I see a cow,” “I have a key”).
- Child produced /k/ accurately in many imitated phrases with only light reminders to keep the tongue back.
- In more spontaneous play, /k/ was present in several familiar words; occasional fronting still occurred, but less frequently.

2. Final consonants: /p, m, t/ in CVC words
- Continued practice with final consonants using picture cards and a simple board game.
- Final /p/ and /m/ were more consistently marked with moderate cues; omissions decreased compared to prior sessions.
- Final /t/ remained variable; child produced a clearer /t/ in some high-interest words but frequently omitted it in less familiar items. Accuracy showed some emerging improvement but still required substantial prompting.

3. Initial /f/ words and emerging phrases
- Reviewed /f/ in isolation and then practiced in words (“fish, feet, four, five”).
- Child showed slightly better control of lip and airflow and was able to produce several clearer /f/ words with modeling.
- Introduced short phrases with /f/ words (e.g., “big fish,” “four feet”) at the imitated level. Child needed support to maintain /f/ when adding extra words.

4. Functional 2–3 word phrases
- Encouraged phrases related to turn-taking and requests (“my turn please,” “more bubbles,” “go fast car”).
- Child imitated many phrases and independently used several 2–3 word combinations during play, though intelligibility was still impacted by sound errors.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue consolidating /k/ in carrier phrases and encourage more spontaneous use in play.
- Maintain targeted practice on final /t/ while supporting the already improving /p/ and /m/.
- Provide caregiver with phrase-level practice ideas (e.g., “I see a ___”) using familiar /k/ and /f/ words.
- Next session, plan to probe /k/ and final consonants in slightly longer phrases and monitor consistency.
notes/6/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver reported that child is using more short sentences at home (e.g., “I see car,” “I want juice”), though some words remain hard to understand.
- Continued practice of /k/ and final consonants; caregiver feels he is “getting better but still needs reminders.”

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child came into the therapy room happily and quickly chose a favorite game.
- Sustained attention well during structured tasks, especially when the activities were turned into a race or challenge.
- Accepted feedback and repeated words/phrases when prompted.

Goals / therapy focus:
1. /k/ in carrier phrases and structured play
- Practiced /k/ in initial position within short phrases (“I see a car,” “my cat,” “big cow”).
- Child produced /k/ with good consistency in imitation, and there were more spontaneous correct /k/ productions in play, especially for familiar words.
- Occasional fronting persisted but child could often self-correct when reminded to make a “back sound.”

2. Final consonants: /p, m, t/ in CVC words and short phrases
- Focused on final consonants within simple phrases (e.g., “big cup,” “red hat”).
- Final /p/ and /m/ continued to improve, with more reliable marking when slowed down.
- Final /t/ remained the most difficult; child occasionally produced a clear /t/ at the end of words when given maximal cues and extra time.

3. Initial /f/ in words and short phrases
- Worked on /f/ in words (“fish, four, fun, five”) and in 2-word phrases (e.g., “big fish,” “four cars”).
- Child produced more accurate /f/ sounds in imitation with visual support; still needed reminders to avoid adding an extra consonant.

4. Functional phrases for requests and comments
- Encouraged 2–3 word phrases for requests (e.g., “more bubbles please,” “I want car”) and comments.
- Child imitated many phrases and independently generated several short sentences; clarity varied depending on sound targets but overall length and willingness to communicate increased.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue reinforcing /k/ and final consonants in phrases and play, emphasizing self-monitoring.
- Maintain /f/ practice at word and short phrase levels.
- Provide caregiver with a list of simple request phrases to model at home, focusing on accurate /k/ and final consonants when possible.
- Next session, plan to introduce slightly longer carrier sentences with /k/ and continue shaping final /t/.
notes/7/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver shared that child is “talking up a storm” and that family is understanding more of his speech, though some words still sound unclear to unfamiliar listeners.
- Noted that he sometimes corrects himself when reminded about his “back sound.”

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly and was enthusiastic about therapy activities.
- Demonstrated good turn-taking and followed multi-step directions with visual support.
- Engaged well in both structured and play-based tasks.

Goals / therapy focus:
1. /k/ in short sentences and play-based conversation
- Practiced /k/ in slightly longer sentences (e.g., “I see a big car,” “The cat is on top”).
- In imitation, child maintained /k/ accurately in many contexts, even when additional words were added.
- In structured play, child produced multiple spontaneous /k/ words correctly; occasional fronting was noted but occurred less frequently.

2. Final consonants in CVC words and short phrases
- Continued targeting final /p, m, t/ in words and phrases (“red cup,” “mom’s hat,” “big map”).
- Final /p/ and /m/ were usually marked when child was cued to slow down; /t/ continued to require more intensive cueing but showed some clearer productions.
- Overall performance was similar to the prior session with small improvements in consistency.

3. Initial /f/ in words and short phrases
- Practiced /f/ in a variety of words and simple phrases (e.g., “fun fish,” “four big feet”).
- Child produced /f/ more reliably in imitation; still needed reminders to keep the sound continuous and avoid inserting a stop.

4. Functional 3–4 word sentences
- Encouraged child to use 3–4 word sentences for commenting and requesting during play.
- Child imitated and spontaneously used several 3–4 word sentences, though some were less intelligible due to ongoing sound errors.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue consolidating /k/ in sentences and spontaneous speech, with ongoing emphasis on self-correction.
- Maintain targeted practice of final /t/ and monitor for gradual improvement while supporting /p/ and /m/.
- Provide caregiver with sentence-level practice ideas (e.g., “I see a ___,” “I want the ___”) focusing on /k/ words.
- Next session, plan to keep working at the same levels, reinforcing stability and generalization.
notes/8/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver reported that teachers at preschool are understanding him better and commented that his words are “clearer.”
- Family still notices some dropped end sounds, especially in fast speech.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child arrived in good spirits and eagerly showed the clinician a new toy.
- Participated well in structured tasks and accepted corrections with minimal frustration.
- Needed occasional reminders to use a slower speaking rate.

Goals / therapy focus:
1. /k/ in sentences and semi-spontaneous speech
- Continued practicing /k/ in longer sentences (e.g., “I see a big red car,” “The cat is in the cup”).
- In imitation, /k/ remained consistently accurate across a range of sentence structures.
- In semi-spontaneous speech during play, /k/ was generally present in familiar words; occasional fronting occurred when child was excited or speaking quickly.

2. Final consonants: /p, m, t/ in words and short phrases
- Targeted final consonants in familiar CVC words and short phrases.
- Final /p/ and /m/ were mostly present with light cueing; /t/ continued to be less consistent but was produced clearly in some practiced words.
- Overall pattern similar to previous week, with child maintaining gains.

3. Initial /f/ in words and phrases
- Practiced /f/ in words and 2–3 word phrases.
- Child produced /f/ more clearly in imitation; still needed reminders for continuous airflow but showed stable performance compared to prior session.

4. Functional multi-word sentences
- Encouraged 3–4 word sentences in structured play and storybook activities.
- Child independently produced several short sentences; intelligibility is improving but still impacted by residual sound errors.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue at current levels to consolidate skills, especially /k/ in connected speech and final consonants.
- Encourage caregiver to model and reinforce final consonants in everyday routines.
- Next session, plan to maintain this level while monitoring for increased consistency, particularly with final /t/.
notes/9/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver shared that child is talking more with peers and that other children seem to understand him better during playdates.
- Family still hears some missing sounds at the ends of words when he is tired or rushing.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child separated easily and eagerly chose a board game for practice.
- Maintained attention throughout the session with brief movement breaks.
- Demonstrated good cooperation and enjoyed being given “challenge words.”

Goals / therapy focus:
1. /k/ in connected speech
- Practiced /k/ in longer, imitated sentences and during simple story retell tasks.
- /k/ remained accurate in imitation and was generally present in spontaneous comments involving familiar words.
- Occasional fronting persisted in less familiar words, but child responded to gentle reminders to use his “back sound.”

2. Final consonants: /p, m, t/
- Continued targeting final consonants in familiar and some new CVC words within short phrases.
- Final /p/ and /m/ were usually present, though child occasionally omitted them when speaking quickly.
- Final /t/ remained variable; some clear productions were noted in highly practiced words, with omissions in others.

3. Initial /f/ in words and short phrases
- Worked on /f/ in words and 2–3 word phrases in a picture-based game.
- Child produced /f/ accurately in many imitated productions; still needed intermittent cues to avoid adding an extra consonant.

4. Functional sentence production
- Encouraged the use of 3–5 word sentences to describe pictures and actions.
- Child produced multiple short sentences; overall intelligibility continues to improve but is not yet age-appropriate.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue reinforcing /k/ and final consonants in connected speech, with emphasis on slowing down and finishing words.
- Provide caregiver with home practice sheets for final /t/ and /f/ words.
- Next session, plan to maintain current targets while monitoring for gradual increases in consistency and independence.
notes/10/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver noted that child’s preschool staff continue to report improved clarity and participation in group activities.
- Family still hears some occasional “missing ends” but overall feels communication is much easier.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly and showed enthusiasm for both table tasks and play activities.
- Followed directions well and accepted feedback without difficulty.
- Demonstrated good stamina across the session.

Goals / therapy focus:
1. /k/ in sentences and conversation
- Continued work on /k/ in longer sentences and brief conversational exchanges.
- /k/ remained consistently accurate in imitated sentences and was generally present in spontaneous speech for familiar words.
- Occasional fronting continued to appear in less practiced words but did not significantly impact overall intelligibility.

2. Final consonants: /p, m, t/
- Targeted final consonants in practiced and some new CVC words.
- Final /p/ and /m/ were typically present; child sometimes needed a gentle reminder when speaking quickly.
- Final /t/ showed some clear productions in familiar words; overall pattern was similar to the previous session, with steady but gradual progress.

3. Initial /f/ in words and phrases
- Practiced /f/ in a mix of old and new words and short phrases.
- Child continued to produce /f/ accurately in most imitated contexts, with occasional need for cueing.

4. Functional multi-word sentences
- Encouraged 3–5 word sentences in play and picture description.
- Child produced multiple sentences independently; speech intelligibility is improved compared to earlier in the block, though still mildly reduced.

Plan / recommendations:

notes/11/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver reported that child is much easier to understand at home and in the community, though a few words remain unclear when he speaks very fast.
- Family has incorporated practice into daily routines and feels confident continuing.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered the therapy room cheerfully and engaged readily in both structured and play-based tasks.
- Maintained attention well, followed instructions, and enjoyed demonstrating his skills.

Goals / therapy focus:
1. /k/ in conversation and narrative tasks
- Practiced /k/ within brief story retell and open-ended conversation.
- /k/ was consistently present in familiar words and generally accurate in less familiar contexts, with only occasional fronting noted.
- Child appeared aware of his productions and sometimes self-corrected without prompting.

2. Final consonants: /p, m, t/
- Targeted final consonants within short sentences and simple narratives.
- Final /p/ and /m/ were typically produced; final /t/ showed clearer productions in many practiced words, though occasional omissions persisted in spontaneous speech.
- Overall, child demonstrated stable use of final consonants with mild residual variability.

3. Initial /f/ in connected speech
- Monitored /f/ in conversation and during picture description tasks.
- /f/ was produced accurately in many familiar words; occasional distortions or added sounds occurred but were less frequent.

4. Functional sentence production and intelligibility
- Encouraged 4–5 word sentences for describing pictures and events.
- Child produced multiple sentences independently; overall intelligibility is improved compared to the start of the block, with remaining difficulties primarily related to final /t/ and occasional sound substitutions.

Plan / recommendations:
- Recommend continued speech-language therapy to further refine final consonant production and support generalization of /k/ and /f/ in all contexts.
- Provide caregiver with a home practice packet including final /t/ words and simple sentence frames, emphasizing short, positive practice sessions.
- Suggest ongoing communication with preschool staff to support carryover of strategies (e.g., reminders to slow down and finish words).

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom reported that she and Dad understand most of what he says, but preschool staff have commented that peers have difficulty understanding him, especially in longer sentences. She notices he often leaves off sounds at the ends of words and says “tup” for “cup” or “doap” for “soap.” No concerns with hearing or health this week.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned to the therapy room with some hesitation but separated from Mom without tears. Needed a few minutes of free play to warm up, then responded well to structured turn-taking games. Attention was fair; he stayed with adult-directed activities for short bursts (3–4 minutes) with occasional redirection. Enjoyed cars and animal toys and was generally cheerful.

Goals / session focus:
1) Establish baseline for overall speech sound production
- During play and picture naming, frequent final consonant deletion (e.g., “bee” for “beep,” “boo” for “book”) and cluster reduction were noted. Intelligibility with the clinician was reduced in unfamiliar contexts, though Mom could often interpret his speech.

2) Final /p, t, k/ at word level (imitation)
- Introduced CVC words with final /p, t, k/ using picture cards and a simple board game (e.g., “cup, cat, cake, cap, bike, boat”). Client was able to imitate final consonants inconsistently; often added a vowel (“cup-uh”) or omitted the final sound. Rough estimate: about 40–50% accurate in direct imitation with maximal verbal and visual cues (exaggerated mouth movements, tapping the table for the last sound).

3) Initial /k/ at syllable and word level (imitation)
- In isolation, client could approximate /k/ with tongue-back cueing and using a “cough sound” trick, but often fronted to /t/ (“tar” for “car”). At the CV syllable level (e.g., “kee, koo, kah”), productions were mostly /t/ despite simultaneous modeling. Accuracy for clear /k/ in initial position was low; client required maximal cueing and slow pacing.

4) Syllable shape practice (CVCV)
- Practiced simple CVCV patterns (e.g., “mama, puppy, baby”) in play. Client enjoyed repeating these and was more successful with reduplicated syllables (e.g., “mama”) than alternating ones (e.g., “bunny”). Productions were often approximated but generally recognizable.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to target final /p, t, k/ at the imitated word level, focusing on awareness of the “last sound” in words.
- Maintain work on initial /k/ in isolation and simple syllables with heavy visual/tactile support.
- Provide Mom with a short list of final /p/ words (“cup, up, soup”) to model at home during daily routines, emphasizing the last sound without pressuring him to repeat.
- Monitor overall intelligibility and tolerance for structured practice in upcoming sessions.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom shared that he is starting to notice when others do not understand him and will sometimes repeat himself louder, but not necessarily clearer. She has been trying to emphasize the end sounds in words like “cup” and “up” during snack time; she feels he occasionally “gets it,” but it’s not consistent.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client entered the room more easily today and quickly chose preferred toys. He was playful and cooperative for most of the session, with brief periods of silliness (e.g., making silly sounds instead of target words) when tasks became more challenging. Responded well to visual supports and game-based practice.

Goals / session focus:
1) Final /p, t, k/ at word level (imitation)
- Continued CVC word practice in a turn-taking board game. Compared with last week, he showed a noticeable jump in accuracy when imitating final /p/ and /t/ with clear visual models. He produced final /p/ fairly consistently in words like “cup, up, pop,” especially when the SLP tapped for the last sound. Final /k/ remained harder, often fronted to /t/ (“back” → “bat”). Overall, accuracy and consistency were higher than baseline, with fewer “extra vowels” added at the end.

2) Initial /k/ at syllable and word level (imitation)
- Used mirror work and a “back of the mouth” cue to help him feel the /k/ sound. He began to produce a clearer /k/ in isolation several times with support. At the CV level, he approximated /k/ in “koo, kah” with moderate cues, though still often reverted to /t/ in words (“car, key”). There was a slight improvement in his awareness; he sometimes attempted to correct himself after hearing the SLP’s model.

3) CVCV and multisyllabic word practice
- Practiced CVCV words (e.g., “bunny, puppy, teddy”) in a picture book activity. Most syllables were present but some consonants were unclear. With slow modeling and clapping syllables, he was able to imitate 2–3 syllable words like “banana, dinosaur” in a simplified form.

4) Early /s/ awareness (listening)
- Briefly introduced listening discrimination for /s/ vs. no /s/ in simple words using a “sound detective” game (e.g., “sun” vs. “un”). He could usually identify when the SLP used the “snake sound,” though this was not yet targeted for production.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain focus on final /p, t, k/ at imitated word level; gradually reduce cueing when possible.
- Continue intensive work on initial /k/ in isolation and CV syllables, watching for opportunities to move into simple words.
- Encourage Mom to continue modeling final /p/ and /t/ in everyday routines, praising any attempts to include the last sound.
- Keep /s/ at the listening/awareness level for now; not yet a primary production target.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom reported that he is occasionally adding the last sound on words like “cup” when she reminds him, and she has heard a clearer /k/ in “car” a few times during play. She still feels he is hard to understand for new people. No new health concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned smoothly and appeared excited to “play the sound games.” He sustained attention well with brief movement breaks between activities. Minimal avoidance; he accepted correction and models without frustration and enjoyed choosing stickers as rewards.

Goals / session focus:
1) Final /p, t, k/ at word level (imitation and emerging cued independence)
- In a picture card race game, he produced final /p/ and /t/ with increasing consistency. With a model, he was usually accurate; with only a reminder to “remember the last sound,” he could sometimes produce the word clearly without direct imitation. Final /k/ remained more inconsistent but showed clearer productions in a few high-frequency words like “back” and “book.” Overall, performance reflected slightly increased independence compared to last session.

2) Initial /k/ at word level (imitation)
- Practiced initial /k/ words (“car, key, cow, cookie”) using a car ramp activity. With visual and verbal cues (“tongue in the back”), he produced more recognizable /k/ sounds than previously. Accuracy was still mixed, but there were more correct productions and fewer /t/ substitutions. He occasionally paused and tried again after hearing his own error, indicating emerging self-monitoring.

3) CVCV and simple multisyllabic words
- Continued CVCV practice with toys and picture books (e.g., “baby, kitty, robot”). He was able to mark most syllables when imitating longer words like “banana” and “elephant,” though some consonants were simplified. He responded well to clapping and tapping for each syllable.

4) Early /s/ production in isolation
- Briefly introduced /s/ in isolation using a “snake sound” puppet. He could imitate a prolonged /s/ sound when focused, but tended to add a vowel after it (“suh”). No structured word-level /s/ work yet.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue practicing final /p, t, k/, with a goal of increasing independent (non-imitative) productions during play.
- Maintain focus on initial /k/ words with consistent cueing; trial simple carrier phrases (“my car”) as accuracy improves.
- Encourage Mom to highlight both beginning and ending sounds in a few target words at home (e.g., “car, cup, cat”).
- Keep /s/ at isolation/imitation level only for now, focusing on sustained airflow and lip/tongue posture.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom indicated that he is “trying harder” with his sounds at home and will sometimes stop and repeat a word if she says, “I didn’t hear the last sound.” She has noticed a clearer “k” in a few words, though it is not yet consistent.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client came into the room independently and immediately joined the SLP at the table. He remained engaged for the majority of structured tasks, needing brief breaks with cars between activities. He was proud of his “good sounds” and enjoyed verbal praise and high-fives.

Goals / session focus:
1) Final /p, t, k/ at word level and emerging phrases
- Continued CVC word practice in a fishing game and then expanded to short phrases (e.g., “big cup, red hat, my book”) when accuracy in single words was good. He maintained relatively strong performance for final /p/ and /t/ in imitation and with light cues. Final /k/ continued to be more variable but showed some clearer productions in familiar words. Compared to last session, accuracy appears slightly more consistent but still requires frequent cueing.

2) Initial /k/ at word level (imitation) and early phrases
- Used a car-themed activity to practice initial /k/ in words (“car, key, coat, cookie”) and then in two-word combinations (“my car, big cookie”) when possible. He produced more accurate /k/ sounds than in earlier sessions but still fronted to /t/ intermittently. He benefited from slowing down and using a mirror. Small gains were noted in consistency, though cueing needs remain similar.

3) /s/ in isolation and simple CV syllables
- Practiced /s/ in isolation (“snake sound”) and in CV patterns (e.g., “see, soo”) using a puppet and bubbles. He was able to produce a prolonged /s/ with a closed-mouth smile posture when reminded. In CV syllables, he often added a slight “t” or “d” onset but could improve with modeling. This represents early emerging accuracy but is not yet stable.

4) Syllable structure and prosody
- Briefly targeted 2–3 syllable words during book reading. He could imitate “dinosaur, octopus” with most syllables present but with simplified consonants. Rhythm and stress patterns were generally intact.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue consolidating final /p, t, k/ at word level and begin to incorporate more short phrases when accuracy allows.
- Maintain work on initial /k/ at word and early phrase levels, focusing on consistent tongue-back placement.
- Keep /s/ practice at isolation and simple syllables, emphasizing airflow and posture.
- Mom encouraged to practice a small set of /k/ and final consonant words during play, using positive feedback rather than correction-focused language.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom reported that preschool staff have mentioned he is a bit clearer during circle time, particularly with words like “cup” and “up.” She has noticed more self-corrections when she gives a gentle reminder about “end sounds.” She is hopeful but still concerned about how well peers understand him.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was cheerful and talkative today. He followed routines easily and participated actively in both table-top and floor-based tasks. Occasional distraction by nearby toys, but he responded to simple prompts to return to the activity. No signs of frustration with correction.

Goals / session focus:
1) Final /p, t, k/ at word and short phrase level
- Practiced final consonants in a picture lotto game and then in short descriptive phrases (e.g., “big cup, hot soup, red hat, my book”). Final /p/ and /t/ were generally reliable in imitation and often maintained when the SLP faded to a verbal reminder only. Final /k/ remained less consistent but showed some clearer productions, particularly in repeated practice of “back, book, snack.” Performance is similar to last week with some emerging stability at the phrase level.

2) Initial /k/ words and two-word combinations
- Used a play kitchen set to target /k/ words (“cup, cookie, corn, cake”) and two-word combinations (“my cookie, cut cake”). He produced recognizable /k/ more frequently, especially in high-interest words like “cookie.” However, he still fronted to /t/ at times, and accuracy improved with slow modeling and mirror cues. Gains from previous sessions appear to be holding, with a small increase in consistency.

3) /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Continued work on the “snake sound” in isolation and in simple syllables (“see, soo, say”). He could produce a sustained /s/ when focused, though he occasionally added a brief vowel. CV syllables were approximated with mild distortions but generally resembled the target. Accuracy appears slightly improved but still requires frequent modeling.

4) Multisyllabic word imitation
- Brief practice with 3-syllable words (“banana, butterfly, computer”) during book reading. Most syllables were present, with some sound simplification. This remains a secondary goal.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue consolidating final consonants at both word and short phrase levels, particularly final /k/.
- Maintain initial /k/ work in functional play contexts; continue to encourage slow, careful speech.
- Keep /s/ at isolation and simple syllable practice; not yet ready for consistent word-level work.
- Mom encouraged to model and gently highlight /k/ and final sounds in daily routines, praising attempts rather than pointing out errors.
notes/5/note_text:

Final /k/ remained more variable but showed some clear productions in familiar words such as “back, book.” Compared to last session, skills appeared stable, with similar cueing needs and accuracy.

2) Initial /k/ at word and two-word level

He continued to produce /k/ correctly in many imitated trials, especially with mirror and tongue-back cues. Occasional /t/ substitutions persisted. notes/6/note_text:

Behaviour/engagement:
Client arrived in a good mood and eagerly showed the SLP a toy car he brought from home. He participated well in structured games and accepted feedback. Some rushing of speech when excited, but he responded to visual “turtle talk” cues to slow down.

Goals / session focus:
1) Final /p, t, k/ at word and short phrase level
- Practiced target words in a “hidden picture” activity and short phrases (e.g., “big cup, hot soup, my book”). Accuracy for final /p/ and /t/ remained generally good in imitation and with mild verbal prompts. Final /k/ continued to be less consistent but showed clear productions in several rehearsed words. Overall performance was similar to the previous session, suggesting consolidation of current skills.

2) Initial /k/ at word and two-word level
- Used a farm playset to practice /k/ words (“cow, corn, cat, car”) and short phrases (“my cow, big corn”). He produced /k/ more accurately in highly practiced words but still fronted to /t/ at times. Cueing level and success appeared stable relative to last week.

notes/7/note_text:
Parent report:

Behaviour/engagement:
Client separated easily from Mom and engaged quickly in a car-themed game. Attention and cooperation were good throughout the session with minimal redirection needed. He enjoyed earning small stickers for “best sounds.”

Goals / session focus:
1) Final /p, t, k/ at word and short phrase level
- Continued use of CVC words and short phrases (“big cup, hot soup, red hat, my book”) within structured games. He maintained good accuracy for final /p/ and /t/ in imitation and with light cues. Final /k/ remained somewhat inconsistent but showed several clear productions when he slowed down and focused. Performance remained similar to the past couple of sessions, with no substantial change in level of support needed.

2) Initial /k/ at word and two-word level
- Practiced /k/ words and phrases using a picture lotto game. He continued to produce /k/ correctly in many rehearsed words with visual/verbal cues, though /t/ substitutions still occurred, especially when he was excited or speaking quickly. Skills appeared stable with ongoing need for cueing.

3) /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Used a “snake train” activity to practice sustained /s/ and CV syllables (“see, soo, say”). He produced a clear /s/ sound more consistently in isolation, with some carryover to syllables. However, he still required frequent reminders about tongue placement and airflow. Overall performance was comparable to prior sessions.

4) Multisyllabic word imitation (brief)
- Short practice with 2–3 syllable words during shared reading. He continued to mark most syllables but simplified some consonant clusters.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to consolidate final consonant and initial /k/ skills at current levels.
- Maintain /s/ practice in isolation and syllables; consider probing simple word-level /s/ in upcoming sessions if accuracy becomes more consistent.
