notes/8/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom shared that he has been very talkative at home and is starting to correct himself occasionally without her prompting, especially on words like “cup” and “back.” She still notices that he drops sounds when he is excited.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client arrived energetic but cooperative. He participated well in structured games and responded positively to feedback. He sometimes rushed through words, but accepted reminders to slow down and “use his good sounds.”

Goals / session focus:
1) Final /p, t, k/ at word and short phrase level
- Practiced final consonants in a matching game and short phrases (“my cup, big hat, my book, in back”). He maintained good accuracy for final /p/ and /t/, and final /k/ continued to show clearer productions in familiar words, though not yet fully consistent. Overall, skills were comparable to recent sessions, with evidence of consolidation rather than major change.

2) Initial /k/ at word and two-word level
- Used a play garage set to target /k/ words (“car, key, coat, cookie”) and phrases (“my car, big cookie”). He demonstrated continued ability to produce /k/ correctly in many imitated trials when focused and cued. Occasional /t/ substitutions persisted, especially during more spontaneous play. Cueing demands and accuracy were similar to previous sessions.

3) /s/ in isolation and CV syllables; probe for word-level /s/
- Practiced sustained /s/ and CV syllables with the “snake sound” game. Accuracy in isolation remained good with visual support. A brief probe of simple /s/ initial words (e.g., “sun, soup”) showed emerging but inconsistent productions; he often added a slight “t” or distorted the sound. At this time, /s/ remains best targeted in isolation and syllables.

- Continued brief work on 2–3 syllable words during book reading, with similar performance to prior sessions.

- Keep /s/ primarily at isolation and syllable level, with occasional probes of simple word-level targets to monitor readiness.

notes/9/note_text:

Behaviour/engagement:

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother reported ongoing concerns about speech clarity, especially with unfamiliar listeners. She noted that he is "chatty" at home but often leaves off the ends of words and mixes up his /k/ and /t/ sounds. No recent changes in health or hearing.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client transitioned to the therapy room with some hesitation but separated from mom with verbal reassurance. Once in the room, he warmed up and participated well in play-based tasks. Attention was fair; required several redirections away from preferred toys to structured activities.

Goals/therapy activities:
1. Final consonant production (CV → CVC words)
- Targeted final /p/, /t/, and /m/ at the imitated word level (e.g., "cup, cap, cat, hat, home, ham").
- Client frequently omitted final consonants in spontaneous speech ("cu" for "cup"). With direct imitation and visual cueing (touching lips for /p/, tapping chin for final sound), he produced clearer CVC words in about half of opportunities. Final /m/ was easiest; /t/ was often reduced to a softer or omitted sound.

2. Initial /k/ vs /t/ (back vs front sounds)
- Introduced /k/ in isolation using a "back sound" cue (tongue up and back, open mouth). In isolation, he could approximate /k/ intermittently, often defaulting to /t/.
- At the syllable level ("ku, ki, ko"), accuracy was low; most productions were fronted to /t/. Needed maximal verbal models, mirror work, and tactile cues to the throat area to increase awareness of the "back" sound.

3. Early syllable shapes (CV, CVCV)
- Observed and elicited CV and reduplicated CVCV (e.g., "mama, dada, baba") in play. Client produced CV shapes easily and used them spontaneously. For alternating CVCV (e.g., "bunny, kitty"), some consonant changes or vowel distortions were noted, but overall syllable structure was present.

4. Functional words/short phrases in play
- Encouraged use of short 2–3 word phrases (e.g., "my turn," "more bubbles," "go car") with models and imitation. Client imitated several phrases but often reduced them to 1–2 words and unclear final sounds. Intelligibility was better with familiar routines and when context was clear.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue focusing on final consonant production in CVC words and establishing a more consistent /k/ in isolation and simple syllables.
- Suggested parents emphasize final sounds during daily routines (e.g., “cup-p,” “bed-d”) using slow, clear models without pressure to imitate every time.
- Next session: re-check /k/ in isolation, continue CVC with final /p, t, m/, and consider simple minimal pairs if ready.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother reported that he is “trying harder to say the ends of words” after they practiced some of the home words (cup, cap, cat). She has noticed clearer words with family but still frequent omissions with fast or excited speech.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Smooth transition into the room today. Client was curious about new toys and engaged well in structured play. Attention to task improved compared to last week; required fewer prompts to return to activities. Cooperative with mirror work.

Goals/therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words (p, t, m)
- Practiced final /p, t, m/ at the imitated word level using picture cards and a simple board game.
- Accuracy for final /m/ and /p/ noticeably improved with a model and visual cue (tapping at the end of the word). Client often self-corrected when cued to “listen for the last sound.”
- Final /t/ remained more inconsistent; he sometimes substituted a softer or distorted sound or left it off entirely. However, he produced a few clear final /t/ words (e.g., "hat," "cat") with moderate verbal cueing.

2. Initial /k/ production (isolation → syllables)
- Returned to /k/ in isolation using “cough” and “back sound” cues. Client produced a more distinct /k/ in isolation in about half of trials with strong modeling.
- At the CV syllable level ("ku, ka"), still frequently fronted to /t/, but there were a few clearer /k/ attempts today with maximal support. Able to hear the difference when SLP contrasted /k/ vs /t/ in some minimal pairs.

3. CVCV words and early multisyllabic attempts
- Practiced CVCV words ("bunny, puppy, kitty") in imitation. Most syllables were present, though some consonants were simplified (e.g., "bubby" for "bunny").
- Client showed increased willingness to repeat longer words compared to last session and enjoyed listening and repeating animal names.

4. Functional 2–3 word phrases
- Modeled and elicited short phrases ("more bubbles," "open door," "my car"). Client imitated several with clearer initial sounds; final sounds still often reduced.
- Some spontaneous 2-word combinations observed in play ("my car," "more please"), with mixed intelligibility but improved willingness to communicate.

Plan/recommendations:
- Progress since last week suggests emerging accuracy with final consonants and slightly more consistent /k/ attempts. Continue at current level to consolidate.
- Encourage parents to highlight final sounds in natural play (e.g., "You found the hat-t") and praise any attempts.
- Next session: continue CVC final /p, t, m/, maintain focus on establishing /k/ in isolation and CV, and add a few simple minimal pairs for /t/ vs /k/ if attention allows.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother shared that he now sometimes corrects himself when leaving off a sound (e.g., “cu…cup!”). She has noticed clearer words like “cup” and “ham,” though /k/ words are still sounding like /t/ ("tar" for "car"). No new concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client entered happily and quickly engaged with a car and animal play set. Good participation overall; brief episodes of silliness when tasks became more challenging, but he responded to gentle redirection. Tolerated structured turn-taking games.

Goals/therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words (p, t, m) – imitated and emerging spontaneous
- Practiced CVC words ("cup, cap, map, ham, hat, cat") at the imitated word level and then in simple carrier phrases ("my ___," "big ___") with models.
- Accuracy in imitation improved; he produced many final /p/ and /m/ sounds clearly with only light reminders. Final /t/ is emerging; produced several accurate examples today, though still omitted /t/ when speaking quickly.
- Beginning to carry over some final consonants in short spontaneous comments during play, especially for /m/ (e.g., “yum,” “ham”).

2. Initial /k/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Continued with “back sound” teaching using mirror and visual of a mouth. In isolation, /k/ was produced more reliably with less effort than in previous sessions.
- At the CV level ("ka, ku"), there were a few more accurate /k/ productions, but fronting to /t/ remains common. Client could sometimes distinguish the SLP’s /k/ vs /t/ when listening.

3. Early minimal pairs /t/ vs /k/ (listening and imitation)
- Introduced simple minimal pairs ("tea/key," "toe/coat") at a listening level first. Client did fairly well identifying which word the clinician said when paired with visuals.
- When attempting to imitate, he often produced both as /t/, but there were a few approximations of /k/ with maximal cueing.

4. Short functional phrases
- Used play routines (feeding animals, parking cars) to encourage 2–3 word phrases ("more food," "my car," "big cat"). Client imitated many phrases and initiated several 2-word combinations spontaneously.
- Intelligibility within the session was slightly improved, especially when he slowed down and watched the clinician’s mouth.

Plan/recommendations:
- Noted small but consistent gains in final consonant production and early self-monitoring. Continue at current level while building consistency.
- Home practice: families to continue emphasizing final sounds in a few target words and model clear /k/ vs /t/ contrasts during play, without pressuring for perfection.
- Next session: maintain work on CVC final /p, t, m/, continue /k/ isolation and CV, and expand minimal pair listening games as tolerated.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother reported that family members are understanding him a bit more when he talks about food and toys. She notices clearer endings on words like “cup” and “ham” more consistently. /k/ is “still tricky,” but she occasionally hears something closer to /k/ when he’s really focused.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client transitioned easily and appeared eager to show the SLP his toy car from home. He remained engaged in tabletop and floor activities with minimal redirection. Tolerated repeated practice well and responded positively to verbal praise.

Goals/therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words – increased independence
- Practiced final /p, t, m/ in CVC words through a picture lotto game ("cup, cap, map, ham, hat, cat, mat").
- At the imitated word level, accuracy was generally good, with fewer cues needed than previous sessions.
- Began to elicit some CVC words in short carrier phrases ("I see ___," "my ___"). Client produced final /p/ and /m/ fairly consistently in these phrases with only occasional reminders; final /t/ still less consistent but showed more clear productions than earlier in the block.

2. Initial /k/ – isolation to simple syllables
- In isolation, /k/ was produced correctly in the majority of attempts when he used the “back sound” strategy.
- In CV syllables ("ka, ko, ku"), he produced a mix of /k/ and /t/, with a slight increase in accurate /k/ productions compared to last session. Continued to require maximal verbal and visual cueing, but he appeared more aware of the difference.

3. /t/ vs /k/ minimal pairs – listening and production
- Revisited minimal pairs ("tea/key," "toe/coat," "two/cow"). Client showed good listening discrimination with visual supports, correctly identifying the clinician’s target most of the time.
- In imitation, he occasionally produced a clearer /k/ in the "key" and "coat" words when reminded to "pull tongue back," though many attempts were still fronted.

4. Functional phrases with target words
- Incorporated target CVC words into short phrases ("more ham," "big cup," "my cap"). Client imitated many phrases with improved final consonant clarity.
- A few spontaneous short phrases were noted during play ("my cup," "big cat"), with final consonants present in some instances.

Plan/recommendations:
- Client is demonstrating emerging independence with final consonants and slightly more consistent /k/ attempts. Continue building stability at this level before moving to more complex contexts.
- Home practice: encourage use of a small set of “practice words” (cup, cap, cat, ham, map) in daily routines, praising any clear final sounds.
- Next session: continue CVC work, begin trialing a few simple phrases with consistent final /p, m/, and maintain intensive support for /k/ in CV syllables and minimal pairs.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother shared that he is “much clearer” on certain words they practice regularly (cup, cat, map) and that he enjoys showing off his “strong sounds.” She has heard a few better attempts at /k/ in words like “car” when prompted.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client entered the room independently and appeared enthusiastic. Maintained attention well across several structured games. Showed pride when he produced a clear word and responded well to visual feedback in the mirror.

Goals/therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words – word to phrase level
- Practiced final /p, t, m/ across a larger set of words using a fishing game and picture cards.
- At the imitated word level, accuracy for final /p/ and /m/ was high with minimal cueing; /t/ remained slightly less consistent but improved from prior sessions.
- Transitioned some targets into short carrier phrases ("I see the cup," "big cat," "my map"). Client produced many final /p/ and /m/ sounds in phrases with light verbal reminders; /t/ was present in some phrase-level attempts but still dropped in faster speech.

2. Initial /k/ – CV and simple word level trials
- Continued /k/ work in CV syllables and introduced a few simple initial /k/ words ("car, cow, key") at the imitated level.
- He produced more accurate /k/ sounds today than previously, especially in isolation and CV. At the word level, productions were mixed: several clear /k/ attempts and some fronted /t/ productions.
- Client benefited from a visual cue of “coughing” to get the /k/ sound started and then blending it into the word.

3. /t/ vs /k/ minimal pairs – increasing production attempts
- Used minimal pair picture sets ("tea/key," "toe/coat," "tar/car"). Client continued to do well in listening discrimination.
- In production, he achieved a few clear contrasts (producing /k/ for the /k/ words) with heavy cueing and slowing down, suggesting moderate progress compared to earlier sessions.

4. Functional phrases and short sentences
- Encouraged 2–3 word phrases incorporating target words ("my cup please," "big cat there"). Client imitated several with improved clarity, particularly for final /p/ and /m/.
- Some spontaneous 2–3 word comments were noted ("more bubbles," "my car now"), with increasing intelligibility in familiar contexts.

Plan/recommendations:
- Notable progress in final consonant accuracy and /k/ production suggests readiness to continue expanding phrase-level practice for stable targets.
- Home practice: parents to continue practicing 5–7 target CVC words in short phrases, and to model slow, exaggerated /k/ in words like car, key, cow.
- Next session: maintain phrase-level work for final /p, m/, continue building /t/ stability, and expand initial /k/ word practice as tolerated.
notes/5/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother reported that he is now often understood by close family when talking about his toys and snacks. She hears clearer endings on many practice words, though /t/ endings still “come and go.” She has noticed more attempts at /k/ in “car” and “cookie” with reminders.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client arrived in a cheerful mood and transitioned quickly. Sustained attention to adult-directed tasks was good today, with only brief periods of distraction. He was motivated by sticker rewards and verbal praise.

Goals/therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words – consolidation at phrase level
- Continued practice with final /p, t, m/ in CVC words and short phrases using a board game format.
- At the word level, /p/ and /m/ remained highly accurate with minimal cues; /t/ showed gradual improvement but was still inconsistently marked, especially in less structured speech.
- In simple carrier phrases ("I see the cup," "my hat," "big map"), client produced many final consonants correctly when reminded to “use the last sound,” indicating emerging stability at this level.

2. Initial /k/ – imitated word level
- Practiced initial /k/ in words ("car, cow, key, cat"), starting with isolation and CV warm-ups.
- Client produced more accurate /k/ in isolation and CV, with several successful word-level attempts when he slowed down and used the back-of-tongue cue.
- Still required frequent modeling and visual reminders, but the number of clear /k/ productions increased relative to previous sessions.

3. /t/ vs /k/ minimal pairs – continued support
- Used familiar minimal pairs ("tea/key," "tar/car"). Listening discrimination remained strong.
- During production, he continued to mix /t/ and /k/, though a few more correct /k/ productions were observed with maximal cueing. This remains an area of ongoing practice.

4. Functional phrases with target sounds
- Integrated target words into short, functional phrases during play ("my car," "big cup," "more ham").
- Client imitated many phrases and produced some spontaneously, with clearer final consonants than earlier in the block.

Plan/recommendations:
- Progress this week reflects continued strengthening of previously targeted skills rather than a big jump. Maintain current goals to consolidate gains, especially for final /t/ and initial /k/.
- Home practice: encourage slow, clear productions of practice words in simple phrases and continue contrasting /t/ vs /k/ in play.
- Next session: re-assess consistency of final /p, t, m/ at phrase level and consider introducing a small set of new /k/ words if current targets remain stable.
notes/6/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother noted that his speech has been “about the same” as last week. Family understands him well in routines, but new listeners still have difficulty. She continues to hear clearer endings on some practice words, with mixed success for /t/.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client transitioned easily but was somewhat more active today, preferring movement-based games. Required occasional breaks and redirection but remained cooperative and willing to participate in practice.

Goals/therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words – maintenance
- Revisited CVC words with final /p, t, m/ using a race car game.
- Word-level accuracy for /p/ and /m/ remained strong; /t/ continued to be variable, with a mix of clear and omitted productions.
- Phrase-level practice ("my cup," "big cat," "more ham") showed similar performance to prior session. He benefitted from consistent reminders to “finish the word.”

2. Initial /k/ – imitated word level
- Practiced initial /k/ in familiar words ("car, cow, key, cat") and a few new ones ("cake").
- Client produced several accurate /k/ sounds with modeling and visual cues. Still fronted to /t/ in many attempts, particularly when excited or speaking quickly.

3. /t/ vs /k/ minimal pairs – continued drills
- Used minimal pairs ("tea/key," "tar/car"). Listening remained strong; he could identify the clinician’s word with visual support.
- Production contrasted /t/ and /k/ inconsistently, similar to previous week; a few correct /k/ productions were achieved with maximal cueing.

4. Functional phrases
- Continued encouraging 2–3 word phrases with target words during play ("my car please," "big cup there").
- Phrase structure is stable, and he is using short phrases spontaneously; sound clarity within those phrases remains similar to last session.

Plan/recommendations:
- Skills this week appeared stable, with no major change in performance. Continue at current level to reinforce consistency, particularly for final /t/ and initial /k/.
- Home practice: maintain daily practice of a small set of CVC and /k/ words, focusing on slow, clear speech rather than speed.
- Next session: repeat similar tasks and monitor for emerging changes that might indicate readiness to expand /k/ targets or move more phrases toward greater independence.
notes/7/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother reported no major changes in speech clarity since last week. She feels he is “holding steady” with the words they practice and continues to struggle more when talking fast or when excited.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client came in cheerful and engaged, though attention fluctuated somewhat. He responded well to using short, timed turns and visual countdowns. Enjoyed choice-making within activities.

Goals/therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words – ongoing practice
- Practiced final /p, t, m/ in a picture-matching game and simple barrier activity.
- Performance remained similar to prior session: /p/ and /m/ were usually present and clear in imitation, while /t/ continued to be less consistent, especially outside of direct modeling.
- In brief conversational exchanges during play, some final consonant marking carried over, particularly for familiar words.

2. Initial /k/ – imitated word level
- Continued work on /k/ words ("car, cow, key, cat, cake").
- Client produced a number of accurate /k/ sounds with support, but fronting to /t/ was still frequent. No notable change in cueing needs compared to last week.

3. /t/ vs /k/ minimal pairs
- Repeated minimal pair listening and production practice. Listening remained a relative strength; he could usually point to the correct picture.
- Production of distinct /k/ sounds was similar in quantity to last session, with some success under maximal cueing.

4. Functional phrases and simple sentences
- Encouraged 2–3 word phrases and emerging 3–4 word attempts ("I want my car," "more bubbles please").
- Phrase length and structure were age-appropriate, but articulation clarity within these phrases remained comparable to previous sessions.

Plan/recommendations:
- This week reflected a consolidation period with stable performance. Continued practice is warranted to build consistency and set the stage for future gains.
- Home practice: maintain existing word list; encourage slow, clear practice once or twice a day in short, positive play routines.
- Next session: re-assess /k/ accuracy and final /t/ in both words and short phrases, and consider gradually introducing another sound class if stability continues.
notes/8/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother indicated that his speech has been “about the same” this week. She continues to hear good endings on some practiced words, with more difficulty in longer or less familiar words. No new concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client separated easily and participated in both tabletop and floor play. Attention was fair-to-good; he occasionally sought to change activities quickly but responded to clear expectations and visual schedules.

Goals/therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words – practice and monitoring
- Targeted final /p, t, m/ in CVC words with a memory game.
- Accuracy remained similar: strong for /p/ and /m/ in imitation and emerging in short phrases; /t/ continued to be less consistent, though a few clear productions were noted.
- No significant shift in independence compared to prior sessions.

2. Initial /k/ – structured practice
- Practiced /k/ in isolation, CV syllables, and a familiar set of words ("car, cow, key, cat").
- He produced a number of accurate /k/ sounds with cues, consistent with prior performance. Still required frequent verbal and visual support to avoid fronting.

3. /t/ vs /k/ minimal pairs – ongoing work
- Continued using minimal pairs for listening and production. Listening discrimination remained strong.
- Production of clear contrasts remained limited but present with maximal supports, with no marked change from the last few sessions.

4. Functional communication in play
- Encouraged short phrases in functional contexts (requesting items, commenting). Client used 2–3 word phrases spontaneously and imitated slightly longer ones.
- Intelligibility during play remained similar to recent sessions, with context supporting understanding.

Plan/recommendations:
- Skills appear stable this week, representing continued consolidation. Ongoing direct intervention remains appropriate to support generalization and increased independence.
- Home practice: keep using the same practice words and phrases; continue to emphasize final sounds and “back sound” /k/ during daily routines.
- Next session: maintain current targets, monitor for any emerging changes that might indicate readiness to expand to new word sets or contexts.
notes/9/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother reported that his speech continues to be fairly consistent with recent weeks. She feels comfortable with the practice routine at home and notices that he is proud of his “strong sounds,” even if they are not always accurate.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client transitioned smoothly and chose between two preferred games to start. He remained cooperative throughout the session, with occasional brief breaks. Responded well to positive reinforcement and visual cues.

Goals/therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words – continued consolidation
- Reviewed final /p, t, m/ in CVC words using a simple board game and picture cards.
- /p/ and /m/ remained reliable in imitated words and were present in many short phrases with light reminders. /t/ persisted as more variable, though some clear productions were observed when he slowed his speech.
- Overall performance was consistent with prior sessions.

2. Initial /k/ – word-level practice
- Continued practice with /k/ in initial position ("car, cow, key, cat, cookie").
- Client produced several accurate /k/ sounds with cueing similar to previous weeks. Fronting to /t/ remained common without support.

3. /t/ vs /k/ minimal pairs
- Used familiar minimal pair sets. Listening discrimination remained a strength.
- Production accuracy for /k/ within pairs did not show a clear change this week; he continued to need maximal verbal and visual cues.

4. Functional phrases/sentences
- Encouraged 2–3 word phrases and some 3–4 word attempts, integrating target words when possible ("I want my car," "big cup on table").
- Phrase length and use were stable, with articulation errors similar to earlier sessions.

Plan/recommendations:
- This session reflected continued stability. Maintaining consistent practice is important to support eventual generalization and possible next-step gains.
- Home practice: continue the established routine; consider recording short video clips of practice to share successes with family.
- Next session: repeat core targets, and if performance remains steady, consider trialing a small expansion of /k/ words or introducing another sound for variety while still monitoring main goals.
notes/10/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother reported that his speech this week was similar to the last few weeks. She continues to understand him well at home, but others may still need repetition. She feels the home practice is manageable and is open to ideas for keeping it engaging.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client came in cheerful and readily joined the clinician at the table. Attention was adequate; he participated in all activities with minimal resistance and enjoyed earning small rewards.

Goals/therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words – monitoring and reinforcement
- Practiced final /p, t, m/ in familiar CVC words through a spinner game.
- /p/ and /m/ continued to be strong in imitation and in many short phrases. /t/ remained less consistent but showed some correct productions, especially when he was reminded to “tap the last sound.”
- Overall performance aligned with previous sessions.

2. Initial /k/ – familiar word set
- Revisited initial /k/ words ("car, cow, key, cat, cookie").
- Client produced accurate /k/ sounds in some trials with visual and verbal support. Fronting persisted when supports were reduced, similar to prior weeks.

3. /t/ vs /k/ minimal pairs – continued focus
- Minimal pair practice again emphasized listening and production. Listening discrimination remained good.
- Production attempts yielded a few successful /k/ contrasts but with cueing needs consistent with recent sessions.

4. Functional phrases
- Encouraged use of short phrases in play ("my car please," "more bubbles," "I want that").
- He used these phrases spontaneously and imitatively, with articulation clarity comparable to prior weeks.

Plan/recommendations:
- Skills appear stable and consolidated at the current level. Continued therapy is warranted to support gradual gains in independence and generalization.
- Home practice: maintain the current routine, rotating in a few new pictures or toys to keep practice interesting while using the same target sounds.
- Next session: maintain goals, review overall progress across the block, and discuss with caregiver potential next steps or focus areas for the upcoming therapy block.
notes/11/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother reported that his speech this week was consistent with recent patterns. She feels more confident supporting his practice at home and would like guidance on what to focus on next.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client transitioned smoothly and was eager to play. Participation was good; he responded well to structured routines and clear expectations. Appeared comfortable and familiar with therapy activities.

Goals/therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words – review
- Reviewed final /p, t, m/ across familiar CVC words and simple phrases.
- /p/ and /m/ remained strong at the word and short-phrase levels with minimal prompting. /t/ continued to be variable but showed some correct productions when he slowed and attended to the clinician’s model.
- Performance was stable compared to previous sessions, suggesting consolidation of gains.

2. Initial /k/ – review of current word set
- Practiced initial /k/ in the established word set ("car, cow, key, cat, cookie").
- Client produced several accurate /k/ sounds with visual and verbal cueing, similar to recent sessions. Fronting to /t/ persisted when cueing was reduced.

3. /t/ vs /k/ minimal pairs – wrap-up
- Briefly revisited minimal pair activities to check listening and production.
- Listening discrimination remained a strength. Production accuracy for /k/ within pairs was comparable to previous sessions, with some correct productions under high cueing.

4. Functional communication in play
- Used play-based routines to encourage 2–4 word phrases ("I want my car," "more bubbles please," "big cat there").
- Phrase use and structure were appropriate for age; articulation patterns within phrases remained consistent with his known sound errors.

Plan/recommendations:
- This session reflected stable performance, with skills appearing consolidated at the current level. Ongoing therapy is recommended to continue building independence with final consonants and to further develop accurate /k/ production.
- Home practice: continue daily short practice with a small set of CVC words and /k/ words, emphasizing slow, clear speech and praising effort.
- For the next therapy block, consider: (1) continued focus on final /t/ consistency, (2) further development of initial /k/ from isolation to word level with reduced cueing, and (3) gradual introduction of additional sound targets as appropriate while monitoring functional intelligibility in everyday contexts.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver shared that they understand today is the start of a new block and would like to focus on making speech clearer for unfamiliar listeners. Reported that child is sometimes hard to understand at daycare, especially with longer words and when talking fast.
- No recent health concerns reported.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned to the therapy room with some hesitation but separated from caregiver with reassurance.
- Once engaged in preferred toys (cars, farm set), child was generally cooperative, with short attention span typical for age. Needed occasional redirection back to tasks.

Goals and session activities:
1. Establish baseline for overall speech sound production
- Collected an informal speech sample in play and structured naming tasks.
- Noted reduced intelligibility in connected speech, with patterns including: cluster reduction (e.g., "top" for "stop"), occasional final consonant deletion, and difficulty with /k/ and /g/ (fronting to /t/ and /d/).
- Child was willing to repeat single words when prompted but often required a model and visual cueing.

2. Initial /s/ at word level (imitation)
- Introduced initial /s/ in simple words (sun, sock, soup, soap, see).
- With direct imitation and visual cue (watching clinician’s mouth), child approximated /s/ in about half of attempts; frequent substitutions to /d/ or omitted /s/ ("un" for "sun").
- Able to hear the difference when clinician contrasted correct vs. incorrect productions, but not yet consistently self-correcting.

3. Initial /f/ at syllable and word level (imitation)
- Practiced /f/ in syllables ("faa," "fee," "foo") and simple words (fish, foot, fun).
- Tended to stop /f/ to /p/ in words ("pish" for "fish").
- With verbal cue to “bite your lip and blow,” child could briefly shape an accurate /f/ in isolation; carryover into words was inconsistent.

4. CV and CVC syllable shapes
- Practiced consonant-vowel (CV) and consonant-vowel-consonant (CVC) structures using picture cards and play.
- CV shapes generally intact (e.g., "bee," "moo"); CVC words often missing final consonant ("bee" for "bed," "pi" for "pig").
- With immediate imitation and clinician emphasizing the final sound, child occasionally added the final consonant but did not yet do this independently.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to focus on initial /s/ and /f/ at the imitated word level, and on marking final consonants in CVC words.
- Provided caregiver with simple practice words for /s/ (sun, sock, soup) and /f/ (fish, foot, fun) to model once or twice daily in play, emphasizing slow, clear speech and praising all attempts.
- Next session: further probe /k/ and /g/ and continue building consistency for /s/ and /f/ in imitation.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver reported trying some of the /s/ and /f/ words at home; child was “silly” and not always interested, but did say "sun" and "fish" a few times with prompting.
- No major changes in overall speech reported; daycare staff still occasionally ask for clarification.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned more easily into the room today, joining activities with less prompting.
- Engaged well with turn-taking games and tolerated structured practice interspersed with play. Short breaks helped maintain attention.

Goals and session activities:
1. Initial /s/ at word level (imitation → emerging spontaneous)
- Target words: sun, sock, soup, soap, seat, sad.
- With direct imitation and visual cue to keep tongue behind teeth, child produced clearer /s/ in the majority of attempts today, with fewer substitutions to /d/.
- In structured play, began to use some target words more independently (e.g., "sun" while placing picture) but still omitted /s/ at times.
- Noted early self-monitoring: child occasionally paused and attempted to fix "dun" to "sun" when cued to “try again with your snake sound.”

2. Initial /f/ at syllable and word level (imitation)
- Practiced /f/ in isolation and in words (fish, foot, four, fun).
- Child more readily shaped an accurate /f/ in isolation using the “bite lip and blow” cue.
- In words, accuracy improved slightly; still frequent /p/ substitutions, but child required fewer repetitions to achieve a correct model than last week.
- Needed consistent verbal and visual cueing; not yet independent.

3. Final consonants in CVC words
- Target words: bed, bus, cup, dog, cat.
- With imitation and emphasis on the “end sound,” child marked final consonants more often today, especially /p/ and /t/.
- Still omitted some final sounds in spontaneous speech but showed increased willingness to repeat and try again.

4. Probe of initial /k/
- Briefly probed /k/ in isolation and initial position (car, key, cow).
- Continued to front to /t/ in words ("tar" for "car").
- With tongue-back cue ("back sound"), child could approximate /k/ in isolation a few times, but carryover to words was limited.

Plan/recommendations:
- Maintain focus on initial /s/ and /f/ at imitated word level, with opportunities for simple spontaneous use during play.
- Continue practice of final consonants in a small set of familiar CVC words.
- Home: encourage short, playful practice of 3–5 /s/ words daily, praising effort and any clear “snake sounds.”
- Next session: continue building consistency and consider expanding /s/ practice to short carrier phrases if accuracy remains improved.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver noted that child has been saying "sun" and "sock" more clearly and will sometimes correct when prompted with "snake sound." Still feels overall speech is hard to understand when child talks quickly.
- No new concerns reported.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered the room willingly and quickly sought out familiar toys.
- Participated well in structured tasks when activities were kept brief and play-based. Occasional silliness but easily redirected.

Goals and session activities:
1. Initial /s/ at word and carrier phrase level (imitation → structured phrases)
- Reviewed word-level targets (sun, sock, soup, soap, seat, sad). Child produced initial /s/ with fairly consistent accuracy in imitation.
- Introduced simple carrier phrases ("my ___," "see ___"). With clinician modeling, child was able to insert target words into phrases, often maintaining the initial /s/.
- Some phrase-level reductions noted when excited (e.g., "ee un" for "see sun"), but with a gentle reminder, child could slow down and correct.

2. Initial /f/ at word level (imitation)
- Continued practice with fish, foot, four, fun, fan.
- Slight increase in accurate productions with modeling and visual cue; fewer /p/ substitutions compared to last week.
- Still requires consistent cueing and slow rate; independent use not yet observed.

3. Final consonants in CVC words (imitation)
- Target words: bus, bed, dog, cat, cup, hat.
- Child marked final /t/ and /p/ more reliably in imitation; final /g/ and /d/ remained more challenging.
- When clinician highlighted the “end sound,” child attempted to add it in a greater number of trials, though carryover into spontaneous speech was limited.

4. Initial /k/ in isolation and syllables (early shaping)
- Practiced back-of-tongue placement for /k/ in isolation and simple syllables ("kaa," "koo").
- With mirror and tongue-back cue, child produced several approximations of /k/ in isolation.
- Accuracy decreased when moving into words; still primarily fronting in spontaneous attempts.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to solidify initial /s/ at word level and begin more consistent work at carrier phrase level, as tolerated.
- Maintain focus on initial /f/ in imitation and final consonant marking in high-frequency CVC words.
- Home: caregiver encouraged to model short phrases with /s/ words ("see sun," "my sock") and gently cue child to slow down and use “snake sound” when interested.
- Next session: continue same targets, monitoring readiness to expand /s/ to more varied phrases.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver reported that child is now using "sun" and "sock" clearly in short phrases at home (e.g., "my sock") when reminded. Daycare staff have commented that some words are easier to understand.
- Caregiver feels child is proud when praised for clear speech.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly and appeared eager to show toys and talk about recent events.
- Maintained attention well with a mix of structured tasks and play; responded positively to visual supports and verbal praise.

Goals and session activities:
1. Initial /s/ at carrier phrase and short sentence level
- Practiced phrases such as "see sun," "my sock," "I see soup," and short sentences in play ("sun is hot," "sock is red").
- Child produced initial /s/ with good consistency in imitation and with faded models (clinician starting phrase, child completing it).
- In structured play, child began to use some /s/ words independently within short phrases, occasionally omitting /s/ when excited but responding well to a brief reminder.

2. Initial /f/ at word and emerging phrase level
- Target words: fish, foot, four, fun, farm.
- Child showed improved accuracy for /f/ in imitation; fewer /p/ substitutions and quicker success with cueing.
- Introduced simple phrases ("my fish," "four fish"). With modeling, child could maintain /f/ in many trials, though still needed reminders for lip placement.

3. Final consonants in CVC words (imitation → early spontaneous)
- Continued practice with bus, bed, dog, cat, cup, hat.
- Child marked final consonants more reliably during structured tasks, particularly /t/ and /p/.
- In play-based contexts, occasional independent productions with final consonants were observed (e.g., clear "cup" while pouring).

4. Initial /k/ in isolation, syllables, and limited word practice
- Reviewed /k/ in isolation and syllables with mirror; child produced a number of accurate back sounds.
- Attempted initial /k/ in key, car, cow within a picture game.
- Still frequently fronted to /t/ at the word level, but a few correct productions were achieved with maximal verbal and visual cues.

Plan/recommendations:
- Given increased consistency, continue to strengthen /s/ in carrier phrases and short sentences, encouraging more spontaneous use in play.
- Maintain work on /f/ at the word level and expand phrase-level practice as tolerated.
- Continue supporting final consonant production in familiar CVC words.
- Home: caregiver encouraged to incorporate /s/ and /f/ words into simple daily routines (getting dressed, snack time), praising clear productions and keeping practice brief and fun.
- Next session: monitor generalization of /s/ phrases and continue gradual shaping of /k/ at the word level.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver shared that child is using more clear /s/ words at home, especially in routines ("sock on," "see sun"). Still needs reminders for /f/ words.
- No new concerns; caregiver pleased with progress.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered the room happily and initiated conversation about home activities.
- Participated well overall; brief periods of silliness were redirected with visual supports and choices of activities.

Goals and session activities:
1. Initial /s/ in phrases and short sentences (imitation and spontaneous)
- Practiced structured phrases ("see sun," "my sock," "soap is wet") and short sentences during play.
- Child maintained good accuracy in imitation and with faded models.
- Spontaneous use of /s/ words in phrases occurred intermittently (e.g., "my sock" without prompting); occasional omissions persisted but were less frequent.

2. Initial /f/ at word and phrase level (imitation)
- Target words: fish, foot, four, fun, farm, fan.
- Accuracy for /f/ in imitation continued to improve slightly; child often needed one or two cues to achieve a correct production.
- Phrase-level practice ("my fish," "four fish") was introduced again; child could produce accurate /f/ in some trials with close support but did not yet maintain it independently.

3. Final consonants in CVC words (structured play)
- Used a board game to practice words such as bus, bed, dog, cat, cup, hat.
- Child marked many final consonants in imitation and began to do so occasionally in more natural play.
- Final /g/ remained more challenging; frequent omission or substitution was noted.

4. Initial /k/ at isolation and word level (continued shaping)
- Reviewed back-of-tongue placement for /k/ using mirror and tactile cues.
- Practiced key, car, cow, cat. Child continued to front /k/ to /t/ in many spontaneous attempts.
- A few accurate /k/ productions at the word level were observed with maximal cueing and slowed rate.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to consolidate gains for initial /s/ in phrases/sentences and build toward more consistent spontaneous use.
- Maintain focus on /f/ at word and emerging phrase level, with emphasis on lip placement and airflow.
- Ongoing practice of final consonants in high-frequency CVC words.
- Home: caregiver encouraged to pick 3–4 target words (/s/ and /f/) and practice them briefly during play, modeling clear sounds and avoiding pressure if child resists.
- Next session: similar focus, monitoring for readiness to gradually increase independence for /f/ and continued shaping of /k/.
notes/5/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver stated that child continues to use clearer /s/ words in familiar routines. Still hears "pish" for "fish" most of the time, though child will sometimes fix it when reminded.
- No concerns with hearing, feeding, or overall health.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned easily and appeared comfortable in the therapy space.
- Attention to tasks was variable but adequate when sessions alternated between structured drills and preferred play.

Goals and session activities:
1. Initial /s/ in structured phrases and early conversation
- Continued practice of /s/ words in carrier phrases and short sentences during play.
- Child demonstrated stable performance, with good accuracy in imitation and with minimal cues.
- In simple conversational exchanges about pictures, child occasionally maintained clear /s/ without prompting but still showed some omissions when speaking quickly.

2. Initial /f/ at word and short phrase level (imitation)
- Target words: fish, foot, four, fun, farm, food.
- Child showed ongoing, gradual improvement; more frequent correct productions in imitation with standard verbal/visual cues.
- Phrase practice ("my fish," "four fish") continued; child produced some accurate /f/ in these contexts but remained cue-dependent.

3. Final consonants in CVC words (imitation and play)
- Practiced bed, bus, dog, cat, cup, hat, pig.
- Child continued to mark many final consonants when repeating after the clinician and during structured games.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver (mom) shared that she is most concerned about how hard it is for unfamiliar adults to understand him. She notices he often leaves off the ends of words and says “t” or “d” for many other sounds. No major changes since the initial assessment.

Behaviour/engagement:
- 4-year-old boy, transitioned to therapy room with some hesitation but separated from mom with reassurance. Once engaged with toys, he was cheerful and talkative. Attention was fair; he needed intermittent redirection back to tasks but generally participated.

Goals / session focus:
1) Establish production of early consonants in isolation (p, b, m, t, d, k, g)
- Probed a range of consonants in isolation and in simple CV syllables during play. He could imitate /p, b, m/ in isolation with good accuracy when given a clear model and visual cueing (watching clinician’s mouth). /t, d/ were often produced with an added vowel ("tuh, duh"). /k, g/ were fronted to /t, d/ in most attempts despite maximal verbal, visual, and some tactile cues (e.g., touching throat, cueing tongue "at the back").

2) Final consonant production at word level (CVC words)
- Baseline probes for final /p, m, t/ in single words (e.g., “cup, nap, map, hat, sit”) in imitation. He frequently omitted the final consonant or replaced it with a neutral vowel (e.g., “cu_,” “si_”). Occasional accurate final /p/ with intensive modeling and segmenting ("cu—P"). Overall low accuracy; remained at highly supported, imitated level.

3) Initial /k/ and /g/ at syllable and word level
- Targeted initial /k/ and /g/ in CV syllables ("ku, ki, go, ga") using mirror, tongue-back visual cue, and play-based activities (feeding toy animals). Consistent fronting to /t, d/ despite maximal cueing; only rare approximations of /k/ in isolation. Not yet ready to move beyond imitation.

4) Overall intelligibility in simple play
- During unstructured play, speech was often difficult to understand without contextual support. Frequent final consonant deletion and fronting patterns noted. He was talkative and attempted to communicate, but required frequent clarification requests.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue weekly therapy focusing on establishing accurate production of final consonants (especially /p, m, t/) in imitation and increasing awareness of "back" sounds /k, g/ in isolation.
- Home practice: caregiver to practice simple CVC words with final /p/ (e.g., "cup, up, nap") using slow, exaggerated models and pausing for his attempt. Encourage praise for any attempt at a final sound, not just perfect productions.
- Will monitor tolerance for tactile cues and consider additional visual supports (picture cards, gesture cues) next session.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mom reported that he is “trying to say more” and is eager to talk, but others still have trouble understanding him. She has been practicing "up" and "cup" at home; she hears the "p" sometimes but says it’s “hit or miss.”

Behaviour/engagement:
- Smooth transition into the room today, no hesitation. Highly motivated by cars and animal toys. Attention was good for short structured tasks when activities were rotated frequently. Followed simple directions and responded well to praise and visual reinforcement.

Goals / session focus:
1) Final /p/ in CVC words (imitation)
- Practiced CVC words with final /p/ ("up, cup, nap, map, hop, top") in imitation with visual cueing (touching lips together, slow models). Accuracy improved across the session: started with frequent omissions, but by mid-session he produced a clear final /p/ on many trials, especially "up" and "cup." Still inconsistent for longer or less familiar words (e.g., "nap, hop"). Needed verbal reminders to "close your lips at the end" but responded well.

2) Final /m/ in CVC words (imitation)
- Introduced final /m/ in words such as "ham, mom, gum, jam" at imitated word level. Initially omitted the /m/ or replaced with a prolonged vowel, but with modeling and tactile cue to feel vibration on lips, he produced more accurate final /m/ in several attempts. Still required significant support, but a few clear productions were noted in structured drill.

3) Initial /k/ in CV and CVC words (imitation)
- Continued work on initial /k/ in syllables and simple words ("key, car, cow, cookie"). Used mirror, "tongue up and back" cue, and play with a toy car garage. He produced a few closer approximations to /k/ in isolation with maximal verbal and visual cues. In words, productions remained mostly fronted to /t/, though he occasionally paused and attempted to adjust his tongue after cues.

4) Awareness of final consonants in play
- During play, the clinician highlighted final sounds (e.g., “Look, cuP!”) and had him identify if he heard the "end sound." He showed emerging awareness by sometimes nodding or repeating the clinician’s model with more emphasis on the final consonant, though spontaneous use of final sounds remained rare.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to target final /p/ and /m/ in CVC words at the imitated level, reinforcing his emerging correct productions.
- Home practice: caregiver to focus on 3–4 familiar /p/ final words ("up, cup, nap, hop"), using a short game (e.g., feeding items into a box) and praising any attempts at closing the lips at the end.
- Maintain work on establishing /k/ in isolation and simple syllables; not yet ready to move to phrases.
- Next session will re-check consistency of final /p/ and consider adding a few more CVC targets if accuracy continues to improve.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:

Behaviour/engagement:
- Entered the therapy room willingly and quickly engaged with a car racing game. Attention and participation were strong today; he accepted turn-taking and followed structured tasks well with minimal redirection.

Goals / session focus:
1) Final /p/ in CVC words – imitation and early spontaneous use
- Practiced a set of CVC words with final /p/ ("up, cup, nap, hop, tap, pop"). In imitation, accuracy was consistently high across the session with minimal cueing (occasional reminder to "pop your lips" at the end). During structured play, he began to use some of these words spontaneously (e.g., saying "up" and "pop" during bubble and car play) with a clearly audible final /p" in many attempts. A few omissions still occurred, but overall there was a noticeable increase in independence and consistency.

2) Final /m/ in CVC words – imitation
- Targeted final /m/ in words such as "ham, mom, gum, jam, home." Compared to last week, he produced more accurate final /m/ in imitation with moderate cueing (tactile cue to lips and verbal reminder to "hum at the end"). Still less consistent than final /p/, but more frequent correct productions were observed, especially with "mom" and "gum."

3) Initial /k/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Continued to focus on establishing /k/ in isolation and simple syllables ("ku, ki, ka"). He showed better control today, producing several clear /k/ sounds in isolation with intensive verbal and visual cues (mirror, "tongue back" cue). In CV syllables, approximations were closer to /k/ but still inconsistent; he occasionally self-corrected after hearing the clinician’s model.

4) Carryover of final consonants in short phrases
- Briefly explored final /p/ in short 2-word combinations (e.g., "up car," "pop bubbles") in structured play. With a model, he could imitate these short phrases, generally maintaining the final /p/ in the target word. This was trial-level work only but suggests readiness to begin expanding beyond single words for final /p/.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain focus on final /p/ at the word level while gradually increasing practice in simple 2-word phrases when accuracy is high.
- Continue building consistency with final /m/ in imitation; keep practice to a small set of familiar words.
- Ongoing work on /k/ in isolation and CV syllables; will not yet push to word-level spontaneously.

notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mom shared that he now often says "pop" and "up" with a clear ending when excited during play. She feels close family members are understanding him a bit more, but strangers still have difficulty.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Very eager to come into the therapy room and immediately requested favorite toys. He was highly engaged with structured games today and tolerated brief drill activities between play turns. Mild silliness at times, but easily redirected.

Goals / session focus:
1) Final /p/ in words and short phrases
- Practiced CVC words with final /p/ ("up, cup, nap, pop, top, tap") and then embedded them into short carrier phrases ("my cup," "pop it," "go up"). At the single-word level, he produced final /p/ accurately in most imitated attempts with only light verbal reminders. In short phrases, he generally maintained the final consonant when the target word was in a salient position (e.g., last in the phrase), though occasional omissions occurred when speech rate increased.

2) Final /m/ in CVC words
- Continued final /m/ targets ("mom, gum, home, jam, ham"). He showed improved consistency in imitation compared to previous sessions, especially for "mom" and "gum." Still needed moderate verbal cues ("close lips and hum") and benefited from slower pacing. Accuracy was variable in longer or less familiar words.

3) Initial /k/ in CV/CVC words (imitation)
- Focused on initial /k/ in simple words ("key, car, cow, cookie") using mirror and tactile cues (touching under chin, cueing tongue back). He produced some clearer /k/ sounds in isolation and a few accurate or near-accurate productions in words like "car" with maximal support. Fronting to /t/ is still his default, but there were more frequent approximations and attempts to adjust his tongue placement.

Spontaneous use of final consonants outside of practice words is still limited but starting to appear occasionally.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue strengthening final /p/ at the phrase level while monitoring consistency and readiness to introduce a few simple sentences.
- Maintain final /m/ practice at the imitated word level, focusing on a limited core set of words.
- Keep building foundational control for /k/ in isolation and simple CV structures; not yet ready to move to spontaneous /k/ in words.

Goals / session focus:
1) Final /p/ in short sentences and semi-spontaneous play
- Continued 3–4 word sentences including final /p/ ("I go up," "I pop it," "Put it up"). At the imitated sentence level, he maintained high accuracy for final /p" with light cueing. During semi-structured play (bubbles, blocks), he produced some of these sentences spontaneously, often preserving the final /p/ without prompting. A few omissions occurred when he was speaking quickly, but overall he showed increasing independence.

2) Final /m/ in words and 2-word phrases
- Revisited final /m/ targets ("mom, gum, home, game") and short 2-word phrases ("mom here," "home now"). Compared to last week, his accuracy was slightly higher, with more consistent productions of final /m/ in familiar words. Still benefited from verbal cues and slower rate but required somewhat fewer prompts to achieve a correct production.

3) Initial /k/ in CVC words and simple phrases (imitation)
- Focused on initial /k/ words ("car, cat, key, cookie") and briefly trialed 2-word phrases ("car go," "key here") in imitation. He produced more accurate /k/ sounds today, especially in "car" and "cat," requiring moderate but not maximal cueing. In short phrases, he sometimes maintained the /k/ but would occasionally revert to /t/. This reflects moderate, ongoing progress with this sound.

4) Overall intelligibility in structured play
- In a structured play routine with familiar vocabulary, the clinician understood him more easily than at the start of the block, particularly when he used practiced words and phrases with final /p/ and /m/. Intelligibility remained reduced for less familiar words or when he spoke rapidly, but there were clear gains in clarity within practiced contexts.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to practice final /p/ in short sentences and begin to incorporate more varied sentence structures.
- Maintain focus on final /m/ in a small set of words and phrases to further solidify accuracy.
- Gradually increase the complexity of /k/ practice at the imitated word and short-phrase level, while ensuring he can still achieve accurate productions.
- Home practice: encourage use of target sentences during daily routines (e.g., “I go up” on stairs, “pop it” with bubbles). Mom to keep modeling clear /k/ in words like "car" and praising any attempts that sound closer.
notes/7/note_text:
Parent report:

Behaviour/engagement:
- Transitioned easily; appeared comfortable and confident in the therapy setting. Stayed engaged with activities and accepted feedback. Mild silliness at the end of the session when he was getting tired, but overall participation was good.

Goals / session focus:
1) Final /p/ in short sentences and early conversation
- Continued 3–4 word sentences with final /p/ words ("I go up," "I pop it," "Put it up here"). In imitation, he maintained a high level of accuracy for final /p/ with very minimal cueing. During more natural play-based conversation, he used several of these sentences spontaneously and typically preserved the final /p/, with only occasional omissions when excited. This suggests stable, strong performance at this level.

2) Final /m/ in CVC words and short phrases
- Practiced final /m/ in words ("mom, home, game, gum") and 2–3 word combinations ("mom home," "my game"). There was a slight increase in consistency: he produced clear final /m/ more often, particularly for "mom" and "home," though he still required moderate prompting for newer or less familiar words. Accuracy is improving gradually but is not yet as strong as final /p/.

3) Initial /k/ in CVC words and 2-word phrases
- Continued work on initial /k/ words ("car, cat, key, cookie") and short phrases ("car go," "cat eat"). He demonstrated more frequent accurate /k/ productions today with moderate cueing and occasionally produced a clearer /k/ in "car" without explicit reminders. In short phrases, he sometimes maintained the /k/, though there were still reversions to /t" when his attention shifted. Overall, this reflects emerging improvement in consistency.

4) Self-monitoring and correction
- The clinician occasionally asked him to "check" his word when the final sound was omitted. He was sometimes able to repeat the word with a clearer final consonant or a better /k/, indicating that early self-monitoring skills are beginning to develop.

- Maintain final /m/ work at the word and short-phrase level to support increasing consistency.
- Keep building accuracy of /k/ at the imitated word and 2-word phrase level, with an emphasis on consistent tongue-back placement.

notes/9/note_text:

notes/10/note_text:
Parent report:

Behaviour/engagement:

Goals / session focus:
1) Final /p/ in sentences and conversational play
- Practiced target sentences with final /p/ words in structured games and then observed use during play. His performance remained strong and stable: he consistently produced final /p/ in imitated sentences and frequently maintained it in semi-spontaneous use. Occasional omissions still occurred but were not increased compared to prior sessions.

2) Final /m/ in words and short phrases
- Continued with final /m/ words ("mom, home, game") and short phrases ("mom here," "my home"). Accuracy was similar to previous weeks, with a clear /m/ more often in familiar items and variable performance for newer words. He continued to benefit from moderate cueing and slowed speech.

3) Initial /k/ in CVC words and 2-word phrases
- Focused on initial /k/ in "car, cat, key, cookie" and short phrases ("car go," "key here"). His productions were mixed, with both accurate and fronted attempts, comparable to earlier sessions. He responded to visual cues but did not yet show consistent independent control.

notes/11/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mom stated that this week felt similar to the past few weeks. She continues to hear clearer final sounds in certain words, especially "pop," "up," and "mom," but has not noticed any big changes.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Transitioned smoothly and appeared comfortable in the therapy room. Participated in structured activities and play with adequate attention. Needed brief breaks but returned to tasks without difficulty.

Goals / session focus:
1) Final /p/ in sentences and naturalistic play
- Practiced short sentences containing final /p/ words ("I go up," "I pop it," "Put it up there") and monitored use during play. He continued to produce final /p/ accurately in imitated sentences and frequently maintained the sound in semi-spontaneous phrases. Performance was stable compared to recent sessions, indicating consolidation at this level.

2) Final /m/ in words and 2–3 word phrases
- Targeted final /m/ in familiar words ("mom, home, game") and short phrases ("my mom," "home now"). Accuracy remained similar to previous weeks: he produced a clear /m/ reliably in the most familiar items with support, and more variably in newer words. Moderate cueing and slower speech rate were still helpful.

3) Initial /k/ in CVC words and short phrases
- Continued practice with initial /k/ in words ("car, cat, key, cookie") and short phrases ("car go," "cat eat"). He produced a mix of accurate /k/ and fronted /t/ productions, comparable to prior sessions. Visual and verbal cues remained necessary to elicit his best productions.

4) Overall intelligibility and generalization
- In structured play, his intelligibility for practiced target words and phrases (especially those with final /p/ and /m/) remained improved relative to the beginning of the therapy block. notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom shared that she and Dad notice that speech is still "a bit hard to understand" for unfamiliar listeners. She reported that he often leaves off sounds at the ends of words (e.g., "boo" for "book") and sometimes says "t" instead of "k". No major changes since initial evaluation. Family is motivated to work on home practice once they know what to focus on.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned with some hesitation but separated from Mom with reassurance. He warmed up with toys and became more talkative in play. Attention was fair; he preferred self-directed play and needed frequent redirection back to sound practice. He responded well to visual supports and silly games.

Goals / session focus:
1) Establish /k/ in isolation and simple syllables
- Practiced /k/ in isolation using mirror, tongue depressor for awareness (no intraoral stimulation), and “cough” trick. Able to approximate /k/ in isolation in about half of opportunities with maximal verbal and visual cueing.
- In CV syllables ("koo, kee, kah"), productions often shifted to /t/ ("too, tee, tah"). When cued to “make the sound in the back,” he could occasionally correct with hand cue to throat.

2) Final consonant production (CVC words: /p, m, t/)
- Targeted high-frequency words in play (e.g., "cup, up, map, mat, pop, mop"). At the imitated word level, he produced the final consonant clearly in some trials but frequently omitted it or added a schwa ("muh" for "m").
- Needed models plus visual cue (pointing to a “sound dot” at the end of the word). Accuracy was higher when words were broken into two parts (e.g., "cu—p").

3) Initial /f/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Introduced /f/ as “lip-bite windy sound.” With mirror and clinician model, he could sometimes place his teeth on his lip but often produced /p/ or /h/ instead.
- In CV syllables ("foo, fee, fah"), productions were mostly /pV/ or /hV/. He responded best when reminded to “keep lips together and blow slowly.”

4) Syllable shapes (CV and CVCV)
- Observed spontaneous use of CV and reduplicated CVCV ("mama, dada, baba") in play. Attempted alternating CVCV words (e.g., "bunny, teddy"), with some simplification ("buh-ee" for "bunny").
- Used clapping to mark each syllable; he joined in and appeared to enjoy the activity, though speech productions were often reduced.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue targeting /k/ in isolation and simple CV syllables next session; maintain focus at sound and syllable level until productions are more consistent.
- For home practice, encouraged Mom to model and emphasize final sounds in simple words during play (e.g., "cup-P," "up-P"), without pressuring for imitation.
- Plan to introduce simple picture cards for CVC words next week to increase structure while keeping activities play-based.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom reported no big changes since last visit but feels she is “hearing the ends of words a tiny bit more” when he is really focused. She has been trying the strategy of emphasizing final sounds at home. She noted he still often says "t" for "k" in words like "cat" and "car."

Behaviour/engagement:
Client separated from Mom more easily today and entered the room willingly. He engaged well with cars and animal toys and was generally cooperative. Attention to adult-directed tasks was improved; he could participate in short drill games between play turns. Occasional silliness but easily redirected.

Goals / session focus:
1) /k/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Continued work on /k/ using mirror and “cough” strategy. Accuracy in isolation was noticeably improved compared to last week; he produced a clear /k/ in most attempts with moderate verbal and visual cues.
- In CV syllables ("koo, kah, kee"), he produced a correct /k/ onset more often, though /t/ substitutions still occurred. Back-of-tongue cue and hand-to-throat gesture supported more consistent productions.

2) /k/ in initial word position (CVC words)
- Trialed a small set of words ("car, key, cow, cat") at the imitated level. With segmented modeling (breaking the word into initial sound + rest of word), he was able to produce an accurate /k/ at the start of several words, though not yet consistent.
- When given only a full word model, he tended to revert to /t/ ("tar" for "car").

3) Final consonant production in CVC words (/p, m, t/)
- Practiced with picture cards and a “feed the animal” game. At the imitated level, final consonants were present more frequently than previous session, especially for /p/ and /m/.
- He still occasionally omitted the final sound or added a vowel (“cu-puh”). Visual cue (tapping a dot after the word) and slowing the word helped increase accuracy.

4) Initial /f/ in isolation and simple syllables
- Reviewed /f/ with mirror and “bite lip, blow” description. Able to achieve the correct posture more frequently today, though he needed frequent reminders to keep lips together and blow.
- In CV syllables ("foo, fee"), approximations were closer to /f/ than last week, with fewer /p/ substitutions. Still required models and moderate cueing.

Plan / recommendations:
- Given improved accuracy with /k/ in isolation, will continue to strengthen CV syllables and gradually increase practice at the word level in upcoming sessions.
- For home, suggested Mom pick 3–4 “final sound” words (e.g., up, pop, map) and play quick turn-taking games, modeling clear final sounds and praising any attempts.
- Plan to continue shaping /f/ and monitor readiness to move /k/ into more consistent word-level practice.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom reported that over the past week he started saying "car" and "cow" with a clearer "back sound" at times, especially when reminded. She noted that some words are much clearer with family members, but unfamiliar listeners still ask for repetition. She feels he is more aware of when he is "not quite right" and sometimes tries again on his own.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client came into the therapy room independently and chose activities quickly. He was highly engaged in turn-taking games and tolerated short drill periods between play turns. Attention and cooperation were good, with minimal need for redirection. He responded well to specific praise.

Goals / session focus:
1) /k/ initial at word level (CVC words)
- Focused on initial /k/ words ("car, key, cow, cat, coat, cup") at the imitated word level. Accuracy was much stronger this week, with many clear /k/ productions when given a model and brief cue to “lift the back of the tongue.”
- Introduced simple carrier phrase "my ___" ("my car," "my cow") with supported imitation. He was able to maintain /k/ in some phrase attempts, though accuracy decreased compared to single words.

2) /k/ in isolation and syllables (maintenance)
- Briefly revisited /k/ in isolation and CV syllables to warm up. He produced consistent /k/ in isolation and in simple syllables with minimal cueing, suggesting this level is becoming solid.

3) Final consonant production in CVC words (/p, m, t/)
- Continued with picture-based drill in a game format. At the imitated word level, he included final consonants more consistently than in prior weeks, particularly for /p/ and /m/ (e.g., "map, mop, pop"), with occasional omissions.
- For final /t/ ("hat, mat, boat"), there were more variable productions, with some omissions or added vowels (“ha-tuh”). Using a slow model and tapping for the last sound helped him approximate final /t/ more clearly.

4) Initial /f/ in CV syllables and simple words
- Practiced /f/ in CV syllables ("fee, foo, fah") and introduced a small set of words ("fish, fun, four") in imitation. When cued to "bite your lip and blow," he produced a recognizable /f/ much of the time, though occasional /p/ substitutions persisted.
- He benefited from watching the clinician’s mouth in the mirror and seemed to enjoy “blowing the windy sound” to move small paper pieces.

Plan / recommendations:
- Given the noticeable jump in /k/ accuracy at the word level, plan to continue /k/ initial words and gradually increase short carrier phrases next session.
- For home practice, encouraged Mom to use a few /k/ words (car, cow, key) during daily routines, modeling clearly and inviting him to “try the back sound,” but without pressure.
- Continue work on final consonants, especially final /t/, and support emerging /f/ accuracy.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom shared that he is now saying "car" and "cup" more clearly in play and sometimes uses them in short phrases like "my car" at home with her model. She still hears many dropped final sounds but feels they are “popping in” more often. No concerns with hearing or overall health this week.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned smoothly and was eager to show the clinician his toy car. He remained engaged in structured tasks for longer stretches and responded well to visual schedules. Some mild distractibility toward the end of the session but generally cooperative and motivated.

Goals / session focus:
1) /k/ initial at word and carrier phrase level
- Continued practice of initial /k/ words ("car, cup, cat, coat, key, cow") in imitation. Accuracy at the word level remained strong overall, with mostly clear /k/ productions when given a model and light cueing.
- Expanded practice in short carrier phrases ("my car," "big cat," "go car") at the imitated phrase level. He was able to maintain the /k/ in many phrase attempts, although accuracy decreased when the phrase length increased or when attention waned.

2) /k/ in simple two-word combinations in play
- During structured play, the clinician modeled and elicited short two-word combinations (e.g., "push car," "blue cup") with picture support. With modeling, he produced several accurate attempts, though some reverted to /t/ (“push tar”).

3) Final consonant production (CVC: /p, m, t/)
- Continued work with picture cards in a turn-taking game. Final /p/ and /m/ were present in many imitated trials; final /t/ remained more inconsistent, with a mix of clear productions, omissions, and added vowels.
- He showed increased awareness, sometimes pausing and attempting to “add the end” after a reminder.

4) Initial /f/ in words
- Practiced initial /f/ words ("fish, four, fun, food") in imitation. When visually cued to bite his lower lip and blow, he produced several clear /f/ sounds, but still needed consistent reminders.
- In spontaneous play language, /f/ was not yet observed without cueing.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue consolidating /k/ at the phrase level next session, with a mix of high-success words and some new items.
- Maintain focus on final consonants, particularly final /t/, using simple games at home (e.g., emphasizing "hat," "feet" in books).
- Encourage parents to model short two-word phrases with /k/ words and praise any attempts to copy, without correcting every error.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom noted that he is using more short phrases overall (e.g., "my car," "more juice") and that some /k/ words sound clearer even when she does not remind him. She still hears many missing final sounds but feels he is trying more. No new concerns reported.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client entered the room happily and quickly engaged with the therapy materials. He demonstrated good participation in both play-based and more structured activities. He tolerated brief drill tasks and seemed proud when praised for “strong sounds.” Mild fatigue at the very end of session.

Goals / session focus:
1) /k/ initial in carrier phrases and short sentences
- Practiced /k/ words in simple carrier phrases ("I see ___," "my ___," "go ___") at the imitated phrase level. He was able to maintain /k/ in many phrases with a model, although accuracy still dropped when phrases were longer or when he spoke quickly.
- Trialed short, repetitive sentences ("I see a cat," "I see a car") in a picture book. He produced some accurate /k/ sounds in this context but required models and occasional segmenting.

2) /k/ in structured play, emerging spontaneous use
- During play with cars and animals, the clinician modeled /k/ words and encouraged him to request items (e.g., "car please," "cow please"). He produced several imitated phrases with correct /k/ and a few more independent attempts with variable accuracy.

3) Final consonants in CVC words
- Continued practice with final /p, m, t/ in a picture game. Accuracy was similar to last week: final /p/ and /m/ were often present in imitation; final /t/ remained more variable.
- He benefited from a visual of three “sound dots” to highlight beginning, middle, and end sounds and responded well to reminders to “tap the last dot.”

4) Initial /f/ in words and CV syllables
- Reviewed /f/ in CV syllables and practiced initial /f/ words ("fish, four, fun, feet"). Accuracy remained at a similar level to last session: he could produce clear /f/ with visual and verbal cues but rarely did so without support.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to work on stabilizing /k/ in short phrases and simple sentences next session, aiming for more consistent accuracy.
- Maintain current final consonant and /f/ targets, as skills are emerging but not yet ready for the next level.
- For home, encouraged Mom to highlight /k/ words during shared book reading and to pause for him to try, praising effort and any clear productions.
notes/5/note_text:
Parent report:

Goals / session focus:
1) /k/ initial in carrier phrases and short sentences
- Continued practice of /k/ words in carrier phrases ("I see ___," "my ___," "go ___") at the imitated level. Performance was similar to last week: good accuracy for /k/ in short, familiar phrases with a model; reduced accuracy in longer or less familiar phrases.
- Some spontaneous phrase attempts with /k/ (e.g., "my car") occurred during play, with mixed accuracy.

notes/6/note_text:
Parent report:
