Behaviour/engagement:
Client came in cheerfully and quickly engaged with the clinician. He participated in planned activities and tolerated short drill segments interspersed with play. He occasionally attempted to be silly to avoid harder tasks, but responded to firm, supportive redirection.

Goals / session focus:
1) /k/ initial in carrier phrases and short sentences
- Continued practice using /k/ words in familiar carrier phrases ("I see a ___," "my ___," "big ___"). His accuracy with /k/ in these imitated phrases remained stable relative to prior sessions, with generally good productions when he attended to the model.
- Accuracy still decreased when phrases lengthened or when he spoke spontaneously.

2) /k/ in structured play, emerging independence
- In play-based tasks, the clinician modeled /k/ words and encouraged him to request items. He produced multiple imitated phrases with /k/ and a small number of independent attempts. Spontaneous /k/ productions were still inconsistent but are beginning to appear more often.

3) Final consonants (CVC)
- Practiced final /p, m, t/ in a picture matching game. Performance was similar to last session: more consistent for /p/ and /m/, more variable for /t/. When cued to “use your last sound,” he could correct some omissions.

4) Initial /f/ in words
- Continued work on /f/ at the imitated word level. He still required visual and verbal cues to achieve the correct articulatory posture. Clear /f/ productions were noted in many cued trials, but the skill has not yet carried over into spontaneous speech.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue consolidating /k/ in short phrases and sentences; monitor for increased spontaneous use before moving up to more complex sentence work.
- Maintain current final consonant and /f/ practice, emphasizing consistency and awareness rather than speed.
- Reinforced with Mom the value of short, fun practice moments rather than long drill sessions at home.
notes/7/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom reported that she is still hearing clear /k/ in words like "car" and "cup" fairly often, especially when he slows down. She has not noticed major changes in final consonants or /f/ this week. She feels comfortable continuing with the home strategies provided.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned smoothly and appeared relaxed. He engaged well with structured tasks, especially when they involved turn-taking or earning small rewards. Attention remained adequate across the session with occasional brief breaks.

Goals / session focus:
1) /k/ initial in carrier phrases and short sentences
- Repeated practice of /k/ words in structured carrier phrases ("I see a cat," "my car is big," "go to the cup"). His performance remained consistent with prior sessions: good accuracy in short, familiar phrases when imitating, with some drop in accuracy for longer utterances.
- A few spontaneous phrases containing /k/ were noted in play, with variable clarity.

2) /k/ in structured play

3) Final consonants in CVC words

4) Initial /f/ in words
- Practiced initial /f/ in words ("fish, four, fun, feet") with mirror support. He continued to need explicit cues to bite his lip and blow, but once set up, could produce several accurate /f/ productions in a row.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue current level of practice for /k/ in phrases and simple sentences, as skills are stable and consolidating.

notes/8/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom stated that his speech sounds "pretty similar" to the past few weeks. She hears some nice clear /k/ words and short phrases, but it is still inconsistent in everyday conversation. Final sounds and /f/ remain challenging. She is continuing to practice briefly at home.

Occasional giggles and silliness, but he responded to gentle reminders about “strong talking time.”

Goals / session focus:

2) /k/ in semi-structured play
- The clinician set up play routines that encouraged repeated use of /k/ words (e.g., racing cars, feeding a cow). He produced several accurate imitated phrases and some less accurate independent attempts, consistent with recent sessions.

notes/9/note_text:

- Used a car track game to elicit repeated /k/ words and phrases. He imitated multiple clear /k/ productions and attempted a few independent phrases. Accuracy for independent productions was mixed.

notes/10/note_text:

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver shared that they are most concerned about how hard it is for unfamiliar people to understand the child, especially with longer words and when excited.
- Parent notices frequent omissions of sounds at the ends of words and difficulty with /s/ and /f/ in everyday speech. No major changes since initial evaluation.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned to the therapy room with brief hesitation, then engaged well with toys and activities.
- Attention was variable; needed occasional redirection from self-directed play back to structured tasks but responded well to visual choices and play-based routines.

Therapy goals and observations:
1) Initial /f/ at syllable and word level (imitation)
- Practiced /f/ in isolation using a “bite your lip, blow” cue; child produced an approximated /f/ in about half of attempts with maximal verbal and visual cueing.
- At CV syllable level ("foo, fah, fee"), productions were often stopped to /p/; a few clear /f/ productions achieved with slowed rate and mirror support.
- At initial word level in imitation (e.g., "fish, fun, five"), accuracy was low and child frequently substituted /p/; needed frequent reminders to use teeth-on-lip.

2) Initial /s/ in isolation and simple syllables (imitation)
- Introduced /s/ as a “quiet snake sound.” In isolation, child could approximate a prolonged /s/ with jaw stability and tongue behind teeth in about 3/10 attempts; other attempts were distorted or substituted with /t/.
- At CV syllables ("see, soo"), accuracy decreased; child tended to insert a vowel or stop consonant before the /s/.
- Benefited from auditory bombardment and watching clinician’s mouth; tolerates visual cueing well.

3) Final consonants in CVC words (primarily /p, m, t/ in imitation)
- Targeted simple CVC words in play (e.g., "map, mop, mat, mom, top, tap"). Child consistently produced the initial consonant and vowel but omitted final consonants in most attempts.
- With simultaneous productions and hand-tapping cues for “end sounds,” child produced a few clearer final /p/ and /m/ endings.
- Marking of final consonants remains emerging; still largely absent in spontaneous play.

4) Syllable shapes (CV, CVCV) in play
- Observed frequent use of CV and CVCV (same) patterns (e.g., "mama, dada, baby"), with reduced consonant accuracy but overall syllable structure present.
- Longer multisyllabic words were reduced (e.g., "nana" for "banana").

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to establish /f/ and /s/ in isolation and simple syllables with high levels of support.
- Maintain focus on marking final consonants in simple CVC words using visual hand-tapping cues.
- Home practice: caregiver to model slow, clear /f/ and /s/ sounds in front of a mirror during daily routines (e.g., "fish, soap"), emphasizing “end sounds” in a few familiar words without pressuring the child to repeat.
- Next session: recheck /f/ and /s/ in isolation; consider adding more movement-based cues for final consonants.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
- Parent reported no major changes at home since last week.
- Noted that the child sometimes tries to copy /f/ in words like "fish" when reminded, but it is still inconsistent.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child separated from caregiver easily and joined the session with enthusiasm.
- Good participation in turn-taking games; needed some breaks between structured trials but returned to tasks with simple prompts.

Therapy goals and observations:
1) Initial /f/ at syllable and word level (imitation)
- Reviewed /f/ in isolation with mirror and visual cue "bite and blow." Child produced a recognizable /f/ in most attempts when given a model and cues.
- At CV syllables ("fee, foo, fa"), child showed improved consistency compared to baseline, with fewer substitutions of /p/.
- At initial word level ("fish, four, fun"), child imitated several accurate /f/ productions when slowed down and reminded to use the correct lip posture. Accuracy remains variable but clearly improved relative to last session.

2) Initial /s/ in isolation and CV syllables (imitation)
- Child demonstrated better control for /s/ in isolation when given a mirror and “teeth together, tongue behind teeth” cues, holding the sound briefly in several trials.
- At CV syllables, there were more approximations of "see" and "soo" today, although distortions and /t/ substitutions still occurred frequently.
- Child attended well to auditory bombardment and enjoyed the “snake sound” game, indicating increased awareness of the target.

3) Final consonants in CVC words (imitation with cues)
- Focused on final /p/ and /m/ in high-interest words (e.g., "map, mop, pop, mom").
- With simultaneous productions and hand-tapping cues, child marked final consonants more often than last week; still omits many in less-supported attempts.
- When the clinician exaggerated the final sound and paused, the child occasionally attempted to add the ending independently.

4) Syllable shapes / multisyllabic words in play
- Continued to hear consistent CV and CVCV patterns.
- In imitation, child attempted "banana" and "butterfly" with 2–3 syllables marked, though consonant clarity was reduced.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue at current levels, as gains are emerging but not yet solid.
- Keep practicing /f/ and /s/ in isolation and CV syllables, gradually increasing word-level trials where accuracy is higher.
- Home practice: caregiver to play a short “snake sound” game (long /s/) and “bite and blow” game (/f/) once per day, praising any close approximations.
- Next session: consider adding a few initial /f/ and /s/ words into simple play routines while maintaining high cueing support.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
- Parent shared that the child is starting to attempt the “snake sound” at home during play and will sometimes correct themselves when reminded.
- Caregiver noted that final sounds are still hard to hear in everyday words.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered the room readily and was eager to choose toys.
- Attention to adult-led tasks was improved; the child remained engaged in structured activities for longer stretches with minimal redirection.

Therapy goals and observations:
1) Initial /f/ at word level (imitation and emerging spontaneous use)
- Child produced /f/ in isolation consistently with visual and verbal cues.
- At initial word level in imitation ("fish, five, fun, food"), accuracy was noticeably higher, with many clear /f/ productions when given a model.
- During play, the child spontaneously attempted a few /f/ words (e.g., “fish”) without being asked, though accuracy was mixed. This indicates early carryover from imitation to more independent use.

2) Initial /s/ in syllables and simple words (imitation)
- Child produced /s/ in isolation more reliably and was able to hold the sound for several seconds with support.
- At CV syllables ("see, soo, sah"), child produced more consistent /s/ on the first attempt, with fewer /t/ substitutions.
- Introduced a small set of initial /s/ words ("sun, sock, soap, soup") at the imitated word level. Child produced several close approximations; occasional distortions and substitutions persisted but overall accuracy was improved compared to prior sessions.

3) Final consonants in CVC words (imitation, emerging independence)
- Continued work on final /p, m, t/ using picture cards and hand-tapping cues.
- Child is beginning to mark final consonants more frequently when cued and sometimes attempted to add the ending after the clinician paused.
- A few independent productions with audible final /p/ and /m/ were observed in play, though not yet consistent.

4) Multisyllabic words in imitation
- Practiced 2–3 syllable words (e.g., "banana, bunny, pizza"). Child marked most syllables but sometimes reduced consonants.
- With clapping and segmenting, child could approximate full word shapes more clearly.

Plan / recommendations:
- Expand practice of initial /f/ and /s/ at the imitated word level, while continuing to reinforce accurate isolation and syllable-level productions.
- Maintain focus on final consonants in CVC words, aiming for more consistent marking with cues.
- Home practice: caregiver to pick 3–4 favorite /f/ and /s/ words (e.g., fish, fun, sun, soap) and model them in short phrases (“fish in water,” “soap on hands”), praising any attempts at beginning or end sounds.
- Next session: consider adding very simple carrier phrases (e.g., “my ___”) for high-accuracy /f/ or /s/ words if consistency remains strong.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
- Parent reported that the child is saying "fish" and "funny" more clearly at home and enjoys showing off their “snake sound.”
- Caregiver has noticed occasional final sounds in words like “pop” during play.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly and demonstrated good participation in both structured tasks and play-based activities.
- Accepted clinician cues and corrections well; no significant avoidance behaviors noted.

Therapy goals and observations:
1) Initial /f/ at word and carrier phrase level (imitation and emerging spontaneous)
- At the imitated word level ("fish, four, fun, feet"), child produced initial /f/ with high accuracy and needed fewer visual reminders.
- Introduced simple carrier phrases ("my fish," "two feet") in imitation. Child was able to maintain the /f/ in these short phrases with moderate success, indicating readiness to work at the phrase level.
- Spontaneous use: produced several /f/ words in play without a model; accuracy was generally good, with occasional reversion to /p/ when excited.

2) Initial /s/ at word level (imitation)
- Child continues to produce /s/ in isolation reliably with cueing.
- At the imitated word level ("sun, soup, sock, soap"), accuracy improved compared to last session; more clear /s/ productions on the first attempt.
- Began incorporating minimal visual cues only (less mirror use) in some trials, with the child still able to produce adequate /s/.

3) Final consonants in CVC words (imitation, increasing consistency)
- Focused on final /p, m, t/ using picture cards and a turn-taking board game.
- Child is now marking final consonants more frequently when given verbal reminders and hand-tapping; omissions still occur but are less frequent.
- A few spontaneous productions with clear final /p/ were heard in play (e.g., “pop”), suggesting early generalization.

4) Multisyllabic words (2–3 syllables) in imitation and structured play
- Practiced words such as "banana, pizza, robot." Child marked most syllables and is better able to maintain the overall word shape with clapping and segmenting cues.
- Consonant clarity remains an area to monitor, but syllable marking is more consistent.

Plan / recommendations:
- Increase use of carrier phrases for high-accuracy /f/ words and begin to trial simple phrases for /s/ if accuracy continues to improve.
- Continue to reinforce final consonant production in CVC words, especially /p, m, t/ in functional vocabulary.
- Home practice: caregiver to use short, repetitive phrases with /f/ (e.g., “fun food,” “fast fish”) and to gently emphasize final sounds in a few play words (e.g., “pop, map”).
- Next session: further expand phrase-level practice for /f/ and monitor readiness to introduce /s/-blend targets at the imitated word level.
notes/4/note_text:

notes/5/note_text:

notes/6/note_text:
Parent report:
- Parent reported that the child is talking more in longer phrases at home and that /f/ words are often easier to understand now.
- Caregiver has not yet noticed many changes with /s/ blends but feels overall speech is a bit clearer.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered the room independently and quickly engaged with the planned activities.
- Good eye contact and turn-taking noted; child responded positively to feedback and enjoyed “being the teacher” for some practice rounds.

Therapy goals and observations:
1) Initial /f/ in short sentences (imitation and increasing independence)
- Child produced initial /f/ in short imitated sentences (e.g., “I see fish,” “I found food,” “four funny feet”) with good accuracy.
- During play, the child spontaneously used several /f/ words within longer utterances with generally clear /f/ productions.
- Compared with prior weeks, sentence-level /f/ is more consistent, though occasional substitutions still appear when the child is excited or speaking quickly.

2) Initial /s/ in phrases and trial sentences (imitation)
- Child maintained good accuracy for /s/ at the word level.
- Practiced short phrases and simple sentences ("see sun," "I see soap," "my sock on") with moderate success. Some productions were clear; others showed /t/ substitutions or reduced precision.
- Cueing needs are similar to last session, though there are slightly more correct productions on the first attempt.

3) Initial /s/ blends at imitated word level (early introduction)
- Introduced a small set of /s/ blend words ("spoon, star, stop, swim") at the imitated word level.
- Child often omitted the /s/ or simplified the cluster (e.g., “poon” for “spoon”), requiring maximal verbal and visual cues.
- A few approximations with both consonants present were achieved when words were broken down (e.g., "s" + "poon"). This target is just beginning.

4) Final consonants in CVC words (imitation)
- Continued to practice final /p, m, t/ with picture cards.
- Child’s accuracy remains similar to last week, with more consistent marking when explicitly prompted, but frequent omissions when supports are reduced.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain focus on sentence-level /f/ and phrase/sentence-level /s/, consolidating gains before further increasing complexity.
- Keep /s/ blends at the imitated word level with strong support, using backward chaining.
- Home practice: caregiver to model and practice a few simple sentences with /f/ and /s/ (e.g., “I see fish,” “I see sun”), and to play simple games with /s/ blends like saying “stop/go” during movement play.
- Next session: continue building consistency at current levels; no major changes in hierarchy planned unless accuracy significantly increases.
notes/7/note_text:

notes/8/note_text:
Parent report:
- Parent reported that the child surprised them by clearly saying “stop” during a game this week, which was new.
- Caregiver feels overall speech clarity is improving but still notices dropped sounds in longer or new words.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child arrived in a cheerful mood and engaged quickly with clinician-directed activities.
- Sustained attention was good; only occasional prompts needed to stay on task.

Therapy goals and observations:
1) Initial /f/ in sentences and conversation
- Child maintained strong performance for /f/ in short imitated sentences and carried this over into semi-structured conversation.
- Clear /f/ productions were heard in many spontaneous words (e.g., “funny, food, fish”) with minimal cueing.
- Skills appear stable compared to recent sessions; /f/ at this level is approaching a functional strength, though continued monitoring is appropriate.

2) Initial /s/ in sentences (imitation and emerging independence)
- Child produced /s/ accurately in many imitated sentences (e.g., “I see sun,” “I see soap,” “Sally sits”).
- Some spontaneous sentences included initial /s/ with clear productions, though occasional /t/ substitutions still occurred.
- Compared to earlier in the block, /s/ is more consistent, and the child is beginning to self-correct in a few instances.

3) Initial /s/ blends at imitated word and emerging phrase level
- Continued work on /s/ blends ("stop, star, spoon, slide"). Child produced more accurate blends in imitation with backward chaining than in previous weeks.
- Introduced simple phrases (e.g., “stop car,” “big star”) in imitation. Child was able to keep both consonants in some trials, though cluster reduction still occurred frequently.
- Overall, skills show meaningful improvement from the initial introduction of blends, with reduced cueing needed on familiar words.

4) Final consonants in CVC words (imitation and prompted)
- Practiced final /p, m, t/ in a picture-matching game.
- Child continues to need reminders to add end sounds, but there were more instances of clearly audible final consonants when prompted.
- This remains an active goal area, with gradual, steady progress.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue reinforcing /s/ in sentences and expand /s/ blend practice at the word and simple phrase levels.
- Maintain monitoring of /f/ in conversation; consider shifting some focus toward other error patterns as /f/ stabilizes.
- Home practice: encourage use of target /s/ blend words in daily routines (e.g., saying “stop” during movement games) and emphasize final sounds gently in a few key CVC words.
- Next session: keep current hierarchy, focusing on consistency and carryover of /s/ blends and final consonants.
notes/9/note_text:

- Parent feels that people outside the family are starting to understand the child a bit better, though they still occasionally ask for repeats.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned easily and showed good motivation to participate.
- Enjoyed being given choices of activities and remained engaged throughout the session with minimal redirection.

Therapy goals and observations:
1) Initial /f/ in conversation
- /f/ remained strong in both structured and unstructured tasks.
- Clear /f/ productions were observed in many spontaneous utterances, and the child rarely substituted /p/ today.
- This goal is largely consolidated; the clinician will continue to monitor but may shift direct instruction toward other targets.

2) Initial /s/ in sentences and conversation
- Child continues to produce /s/ accurately in most imitated sentences and is increasingly carrying this over into natural speech.
- Occasional /t/ substitutions remain, but the child is often responsive to a brief cue and can adjust their production.
- Performance is similar to last week, representing ongoing consolidation at this level.

3) Initial /s/ blends at word and phrase level (imitation and emerging independence)
- Practiced a set of /s/ blend words ("stop, star, spoon, slide, swim") and related short phrases ("stop car," "big star," "slide down").

4) Final consonants in CVC words (imitation and prompted independent)

notes/10/note_text:
Parent report:
- Parent reported that the child is using clearer /s/ blends like “stop” and “slide” more often without being reminded.
- Caregiver still notices some dropped end sounds but feels overall intelligibility has improved at home and in the community.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child came into the session with high energy but was easily guided into activities.
- Demonstrated good perseverance with more challenging sound tasks and enjoyed earning small rewards.

Therapy goals and observations:
1) Initial /s/ in sentences and conversation
- Child continues to show good control of /s/ in imitated sentences and is using clear /s/ in many spontaneous phrases.
- Occasional errors persist, but the child is often able to correct themselves with minimal prompting.
- Skill level is comparable to recent weeks; /s/ at this level is becoming more functional and stable.

2) Initial /s/ blends at word and phrase level (imitation and spontaneous)
- Practiced a variety of /s/ blends ("spoon, star, slide, swim, stop, snow") in words and short phrases ("stop car," "slide down," "snow fall").
- Child produced many accurate blends in imitation with less need for segmented practice, especially on familiar words like “stop” and “slide.”
- Several spontaneous uses of /s/ blends with reasonably clear clusters were observed during play, indicating meaningful generalization.

3) Final consonants in CVC words (imitation, prompted, and emerging independent)
- Targeted final /p, m, t/ and introduced some final /k/ in simple CVC words.
- Child is now more consistently marking final consonants when reminded and occasionally does so independently, particularly in high-frequency words.
- While not yet fully consistent, there is a noticeable increase in awareness and accuracy compared to earlier in the block.

4) Multisyllabic words and overall intelligibility
- Practiced 2–3 syllable words ("banana, robot, dinosaur, spider").
- Child marked most syllables with support and is beginning to produce clearer consonants within these words.
- In conversation, overall intelligibility within the therapy context is improved, especially with familiar topics.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to strengthen /s/ blends and final consonants, aiming for increased independence and carryover to spontaneous speech.
- Begin to incorporate more multisyllabic words into functional phrases to support generalization.
- Home practice: caregiver to encourage use of /s/ blends in daily routines (e.g., “stop, go, slide”) and to play simple listening games to notice end sounds in words.
- Next session: maintain current targets and begin planning for next block, focusing on generalization and maintenance.
notes/11/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver shared that the child’s preschool staff have commented on improved clarity, especially for words with /s/ and /f/.
- Parent still notices some sound errors in new or longer words but feels the child is easier to understand overall.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly and was very cooperative throughout the session.
- Demonstrated good attention to both structured practice and play-based conversation, and responded well to feedback.

Therapy goals and observations:
1) Initial /s/ in sentences and conversation
- Child maintained good accuracy with /s/ in imitated sentences and frequently used clear /s/ in spontaneous speech.
- Occasional substitutions were present but less frequent, and the child often corrected themselves when given a brief cue.
- Performance is stable relative to recent sessions, suggesting consolidation at this level.

2) Initial /s/ blends at word, phrase, and emerging sentence level
- Continued practice with a range of /s/ blends in words, phrases, and short sentences (e.g., “The star is big,” “I slide down,” “I stop car”).
- Child produced many accurate blends in imitation and showed several spontaneous uses with both consonants present.
- Accuracy and independence are similar to last week, reflecting ongoing strengthening and generalization rather than a large jump.

3) Final consonants in CVC words and simple phrases
- Targeted final /p, m, t, k/ in CVC words and began to place some of these words in short imitated phrases (e.g., “big map,” “hot soup”).

Plan / recommendations:

- Focus next block on generalization of skills to conversation across settings and continued work with multisyllabic words.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom shared that she and daycare staff notice that he is “hard to understand” when he’s excited. She reported frequent omissions of sounds at the ends of words and difficulty with /k/ and /s/ sounds. No major changes since the evaluation.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned into the room with some hesitation but warmed up quickly with preferred toys. He was generally cheerful and cooperative, with short attention to tabletop tasks; needed occasional redirection from self-directed play back to structured practice.

Goals and session observations:
1. Final consonant production (CVC words: /p, m, t/)
- Targeted final consonants in simple CVC words (e.g., “cup, map, cat”) at the imitated word level.
- Client frequently omitted final consonants or replaced them with a neutral vowel (e.g., “cu_,” “ma_”).
- With maximal verbal cueing (e.g., “stop your sound,” “close your lips at the end”) and visual support, he produced clearer final consonants in a small number of trials, but consistency was low.

2. Initial /k/ in syllables and simple words
- Practiced /k/ in isolation and CV syllables (“koo, kah, kee”) using a “back sound” cue and mirror.
- Client tended to substitute /t/ for /k/ (“tea” for “key”).
- With hand-under-chin tactile cue and slowed rate, he achieved a few accurate /k/ sounds in isolation and in CV syllables; accuracy dropped when moving to CVC words (e.g., “cat, car”).

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Introduced /s/ as a “long, quiet snake sound.”
- In isolation, client could approximate /s/ intermittently but often added a vowel (“suh”) or changed to /ʃ/.
- In CV syllables (e.g., “see, soo”), he required maximal verbal and visual cueing; productions were mostly distorted or stopped.

4. Syllable shapes (CV, CVCV)
- During play, modeled and elicited CV and CVCV (reduplicated) forms (e.g., “moo, baa, baba, mama”).
- Client produced many CV and CVCV reduplicated forms spontaneously; accuracy decreased in alternating CVCV (e.g., “nana” for “banana”).

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue weekly therapy focusing on final consonants in CVC words and establishing /k/ and /s/ in isolation and simple syllables.
- Home practice: encourage “stop your sound” at the end of words like “up, in, out,” praising any attempts to mark final sounds; model slow, clear speech during play. Provide caregiver with picture list of 5 CVC words to model, not to drill.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom reported that he is starting to notice when people don’t understand him and will repeat himself, but usually says it louder rather than changing his sounds. She has been trying to remind him to “stop his sounds” at the end of words a few times a day.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client entered the room more easily today and separated from mom without difficulty. He was engaged with turn-taking games and tolerated short periods of structured drill between play activities. Needed light reminders to stay at the table but responded well to visual schedules.

Goals and session observations:
1. Final consonant production in CVC words (/p, m, t/)
- Practiced final /p, m, t/ at imitated word level (e.g., “cup, map, mat, ham”).
- Compared to last session, he marked final consonants more often when provided with a model plus verbal cue (“close your lips,” “tap your tongue”).
- Still omitted final sounds in spontaneous speech, but during practice trials he produced clearer final consonants in roughly half of the opportunities with moderate cueing.

2. Initial /k/ in isolation and CV/CVC
- Continued to work on /k/ in isolation using tongue-back imagery and mirror.
- Client produced more accurate /k/ sounds in isolation with moderate cueing and less need for hand-under-chin support.
- At CV level (“koo, key, kah”), he was able to imitate more consistent /k/ productions, though he often reverted to /t/ at the CVC word level (e.g., “tat” for “cat”).

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and CV
- Reintroduced /s/ with “snake sound” visuals.
- Client produced several accurate /s/ sounds in isolation when given clear modeling and time to shape the sound.
- In CV syllables (“see, so, sue”), productions were slightly clearer than last week but remained inconsistent; he required maximal cueing to avoid adding a vowel (“suh”).

4. Syllable shapes (CVCV alternating)
- Targeted alternating CVCV words (e.g., “nana/banana, baby, bunny”) in play.
- Client attempted these words more frequently and showed increased willingness to imitate longer words.
- Accuracy of all syllables remained variable; he often reduced to CVCV reduplicated forms (“nana” for “banana”).

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue focusing on final consonants and /k/ at syllable and simple word levels; maintain early work on /s/ in isolation.
- Home practice: 5–10 short practice trials of final /p/ and /m/ during daily routines (e.g., “up, nap”), using gentle reminders and lots of praise for any final sound attempts. Caregiver encouraged to notice and comment positively when he marks a final sound, even if it is not perfect.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom shared that he is starting to copy her when she emphasizes ending sounds (e.g., “cuP,” “maP”), and she feels his speech is “a tiny bit clearer” to family members. Daycare has not reported any major changes yet.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was eager to show the clinician a toy he brought from home and transitioned smoothly. Attention to tasks improved; he was able to participate in slightly longer practice blocks when activities were embedded in games. Mild silliness at times but easily redirected with clear expectations.

Goals and session observations:
1. Final consonant production in CVC words (/p, m, t, n/)
- Expanded CVC targets to include final /n/ (e.g., “sun, man”).
- Client showed increased consistency in marking final /p/ and /m/ at the imitated word level with moderate verbal and visual cues.
- For /t/ and /n/, accuracy was emerging but less stable; he sometimes substituted or omitted these sounds.
- Noted occasional independent final consonant use during play (e.g., “up!” “in!”) without direct prompting.

2. Initial /k/ in CV and CVC words
- Practiced /k/ in CV syllables and moved into a small set of CVC words (“car, cat, key, cow”).
- Client produced more accurate /k/ in CV combinations with light cueing, needing fewer reminders to use his “back sound.”
- At the CVC level, he produced several accurate imitations (e.g., “car”) but still reverted to /t/ in spontaneous attempts; overall, fewer substitutions than in previous sessions.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and CV
- Continued shaping /s/ in isolation, then CV syllables.
- Client was able to hold a clearer /s/ in isolation for longer durations with clinician model.
- CV productions (“see, so, sue”) were somewhat more intelligible; still a tendency to add a vowel (“suh”) or distort, but he needed slightly fewer cues for correct tongue placement.

4. Early /s/ blends (awareness only)
- Introduced listening task with /s/ blends (e.g., “stop” vs. “top”) using minimal pairs.
- Client demonstrated emerging awareness by pointing to the correct picture more often when the clinician emphasized the /s/.
- No expectation for accurate production at this time; focus was auditory discrimination.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue strengthening final consonant production and /k/ in simple words; maintain work on /s/ in isolation and CV. Continue auditory discrimination for /s/ blends.
- Home practice: model and practice a short list of final /p, m/ words during daily routines; introduce a simple listening game at home (“Did I say top or stop?”) to build awareness of /s/ blends without pressure to say them correctly.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom reported that he is now sometimes correcting himself on final sounds when she reminds him (e.g., “cu_…cup!”). She feels more people in the family are understanding him in short phrases. No new concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client arrived in a good mood and participated readily in structured tasks. He tolerated more repetitions before requesting a break and responded well to visual timers. Occasional frustration when a word was difficult, but he accepted reassurance and kept trying.

Goals and session observations:
1. Final consonant production in CVC words (/p, m, t, n/)
- Practiced a larger set of CVC words in a picture game.
- Client showed more consistent marking of final /p/ and /m/ in imitation and began to produce some accurate final /t/ and /n/ with moderate cues.
- During play-based conversation, he spontaneously used final consonants on a few familiar words (e.g., “up,” “out,” “map”), indicating emerging carryover.

2. Initial /k/ at word and early phrase level
- Targeted /k/ in CVC words (e.g., “key, car, cat, coat”) and short carrier phrases (“my key,” “go car”).
- In imitated single words, he produced initial /k/ with improved consistency and needed fewer tactile cues.
- At the phrase level, accuracy decreased but several clear productions were noted with a model and light verbal prompting.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation, CV, and simple words
- Continued /s/ shaping and introduced a limited set of initial /s/ words (e.g., “sun, sock, soup”) at the imitated word level.
- In isolation and CV (“see, so”), his productions were more consistently recognizable as /s/ with less distortion.
- At the word level, accuracy was variable; he often approximated /s/ but needed frequent reminders for tongue placement and airflow.

4. Syllable shapes (CVCV alternating and early multisyllabic words)
- Practiced alternating CVCV and early multisyllabic words in play (e.g., “banana, baby, bunny, robot”).
- Client showed slightly clearer marking of multiple syllables, though still reduced some words to shorter forms.
- He was motivated to repeat longer words when embedded in a story.

Plan/recommendations:
- Maintain current focus: final consonants and /k/ at word and short-phrase levels; expand initial /s/ at the word level as tolerated.
- Home practice: encourage use of carrier phrases with /k/ words (e.g., “my car, big cat”) during play. Continue highlighting final sounds in everyday words and praising self-corrections.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom stated that he is using more complete words at home, especially with final /p/ and /m/, and that she doesn’t have to remind him as often. She has noticed a few clearer /k/ words like “car” and “cookie.” She continues to feel /s/ is “tricky” for him.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned independently and was highly engaged with a car and animal theme. He demonstrated improved stamina for structured trials when these were embedded in games. Mild distractibility with new toys but generally responsive to cues to return to task.

Goals and session observations:
1. Final consonant production in CVC words (/p, m, t, n/)
- Continued practice with a larger variety of CVC words at the imitated word level and short carrier phrases (“my cup,” “big map”).
- Client produced final /p/ and /m/ with good consistency in imitation and occasionally in short phrases with minimal cueing.
- Final /t/ and /n/ showed gradual improvement; he marked them more often, though some omissions and substitutions persisted.
- Overall, accuracy is slightly better than last session, with more frequent self-correction attempts.

2. Initial /k/ at word and carrier phrase level
- Practiced /k/ words in a board game and in short phrases (“my car,” “go cat,” “big cow”).
- In single-word imitation, /k/ was generally accurate with light verbal cues and occasional visual support.
- At the phrase level, he produced several clear /k/ productions with a model; he still reverted to /t/ in spontaneous speech but corrected himself when prompted.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and word level
- Focused on consolidating /s/ in isolation and a small set of /s/ words (e.g., “sun, soup, sock”).
- He maintained a clearer /s/ in isolation, often needing only a brief reminder to “keep teeth together and blow.”
- At the word level, productions were more intelligible but still inconsistent; he required moderate cueing and frequent practice.

4. Early /s/ blend awareness
- Continued auditory discrimination tasks with /s/ blends (“stop/top,” “snow/no”).
- Client correctly identified the clinician’s target more often and appeared to enjoy the “silly vs. correct” game.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue working at the current levels, consolidating final consonants and /k/ in words and phrases; maintain focused practice on /s/ at isolation and word level.
- Home practice: brief daily practice of 5–6 /k/ words in simple phrases (e.g., “my car, big cookie”) and 3–4 /s/ words with slow, clear modeling. Caregiver encouraged to continue praising his attempts and self-corrections.
notes/5/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom reported a noticeable jump in how clearly he is saying some words, especially those with final sounds and /k/. She shared that grandparents commented that they could understand him better this week. She still hears many errors on /s/ and longer words.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client entered the session excited and eager to talk. He stayed engaged in both structured drill and play-based tasks for longer periods with fewer prompts. He responded well to feedback and appeared proud of his successes, frequently asking, “Was that good?”

Goals and session observations:
1. Final consonant production in CVC words and short phrases (/p, m, t, n/)
- Targeted final consonants in CVC words and simple carrier phrases (“I see cup,” “big map,” “in bed”).
- Client demonstrated markedly improved consistency with final /p/ and /m/ in both imitated and short-phrase contexts, often needing only minimal cues.
- Final /t/ and /n/ showed clear gains; he marked these more reliably and occasionally produced them independently in play.
- Overall, final consonant production appears much more stable compared to earlier sessions.

2. Initial /k/ in words and 2–3-word phrases
- Practiced initial /k/ words in short phrases and simple sentences (“my car go,” “I see cat,” “big cookie”).
- Client produced /k/ accurately in most imitated words and showed improved carryover into short phrases, sometimes using correct /k/ spontaneously.
- Fewer /t/ substitutions were observed; when they did occur, he responded quickly to a single cue to correct.

3. Initial /s/ at word and emerging phrase level
- Focused on a small set of /s/ words (“sun, soup, sock, sit”) and introduced short phrases (“see sun,” “my sock”).
- In imitated words, /s/ was more consistently recognizable with moderate cues; less distortion than in previous weeks.
- At the phrase level, accuracy decreased, but he was able to produce some clear /s/ productions when given a model and visual support.

4. Early /s/ blends (imitated word level)
- Moved from listening-only to imitated production for a limited set of /s/ blends (e.g., “stop, snow, spoon”).
- Client attempted blends with enthusiasm; he often omitted the /s/ but produced a few emerging accurate or close approximations with maximal cueing and slow rate.

Plan/recommendations:
- Given the noticeable improvement, continue to advance final consonants and /k/ within short phrases and begin to incorporate these into simple play-based sentences. Keep building consistency for initial /s/ at word and phrase level and gently introduce a few high-interest /s/ blends.
- Home practice: encourage simple 2–3-word phrases using /k/ and final consonant words during daily routines (e.g., “big cup,” “my cat”). For /s/, practice a short set of familiar /s/ words with visual support (e.g., mirror or picture of “snake sound”).
notes/6/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom shared that he continues to be easier to understand, especially when he slows down. She has noticed some clearer /s/ words like “sun” and “sock,” though it is still not consistent. No new concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned smoothly and engaged readily in turn-taking games. He tolerated repetitive practice without protest and responded well to specific praise. Mild fatigue noted toward the end of the session but he completed all planned activities.

Goals and session observations:
1. Final consonant production in short phrases and simple sentences
- Practiced final consonants in short structured phrases and early sentences (“I see cup,” “put it in,” “big cat”).
- Client maintained good accuracy for final /p/ and /m/ with minimal cueing and was increasingly consistent with /t/ and /n/ in imitated short sentences.
- Occasional omissions still occurred in spontaneous conversation, but he often accepted a quick reminder and attempted a clearer final sound.

2. Initial /k/ in 2–3-word phrases and short sentences
- Targeted /k/ in short phrases and early sentences (e.g., “my car go,” “I want cookie,” “look at cat”).
- Client produced initial /k/ accurately in most imitated phrases with only brief verbal cues.
- Some spontaneous phrases with correct /k/ were observed during play (e.g., “my car,” “big cat”), showing emerging generalization.

3. Initial /s/ at word and carrier phrase level
- Continued practice with initial /s/ words (“sun, sock, soup, sit”) and added simple carrier phrases (“see sun,” “my sock,” “I see soup”).
- Word-level accuracy remained better than previous weeks, with more consistent, less distorted /s/ productions in imitation.
- At the phrase level, he produced several clear /s/ targets with moderate cueing, although consistency was still variable.

4. /s/ blends at imitated word level
- Continued targeting a small set of /s/ blends (e.g., “stop, snow, spoon, star”) in imitation.
- Client remained motivated to try blends; he produced a few closer approximations, occasionally including both consonants with heavy cueing and slow rate.
- Most attempts still involved omission of /s/ or simplification, but he showed increased awareness of the need for two sounds.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue consolidating final consonant and /k/ use in short sentences during structured tasks and play. Maintain focus on initial /s/ at word and carrier phrase levels, while gradually strengthening /s/ blend production.
- Home practice: encourage 2–3-word phrases using /k/ and final consonant words in daily routines, and practice a small set of /s/ words in simple phrases (e.g., “see sun,” “my sock”), emphasizing slow, clear speech.
notes/7/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom noted that he is starting to correct himself more often when she gives a small reminder like “end sound” or “snake sound.” She reported that daycare staff have commented positively on his clearer speech during circle time, though they still have difficulty with some longer words and /s/ blends.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was cooperative and talkative throughout the session. He participated well in both table tasks and floor play, with minimal redirection needed. He responded positively to visual feedback (mirror, pictures) and seemed motivated by sticker rewards.

Goals and session observations:
1. Final consonant production in structured sentences
- Practiced final consonants in simple structured sentences using picture scenes (e.g., “I see a cup,” “The cat is on the bed”).
- Client maintained good accuracy for final /p/ and /m/ and showed more consistent marking of /t/ and /n/ in imitated sentences.
- In less structured play conversation, he produced more final consonants than earlier in the block, though not yet fully consistent.

2. Initial /k/ in short sentences and play-based conversation
- Targeted /k/ in short story descriptions and play (“The car is fast,” “The cat is big,” “I want cookie now”).
- In structured sentences, /k/ was generally accurate with minimal verbal cueing.
- During play, he produced multiple spontaneous phrases and short sentences with correct /k/ (e.g., “my car go fast”), indicating growing generalization.

3. Initial /s/ in words and short phrases
- Continued work on initial /s/ words and short phrases.
- Word-level productions were clearer overall, though still not at a mastery level; he required moderate cues for consistent tongue placement.
- In phrases (“see sun,” “my sock is on”), he produced some accurate /s/ productions with a model; other attempts remained distorted or reduced.

4. /s/ blends at imitated word level and minimal pair listening
- Practiced a small set of /s/ blends in imitation and continued minimal pair listening tasks (e.g., “stop/top,” “snow/no”).
- He successfully identified the correct word in listening tasks more often than not, suggesting improved perception.
- Production attempts still frequently omitted the /s/, but a few closer approximations were heard when provided with maximal cues and slowed rate.

Plan/recommendations:
- Maintain focus on consolidating final consonants and /k/ within short sentences and play. Continue building consistency for initial /s/ at word and phrase levels and keep gently practicing /s/ blends in structured contexts.
- Home practice: encourage simple sentence models with final consonant words (e.g., “I see cup,” “big cat is here”) and reinforce his self-corrections. For /s/, continue practicing familiar /s/ words in short phrases, using a mirror to show the “snake sound.”
notes/8/note_text:

Mom reported that this week felt more like a “holding steady” week. She continues to hear clearer /k/ and final consonants, but /s/ and /s/ blends remain challenging. No concerns about behavior or participation.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was cooperative but slightly more tired today, possibly due to a busy morning per mom. He still participated in planned activities but requested brief breaks more often. With short movement breaks, he was able to return to tasks and complete them.

Goals and session observations:
1. Final consonant production in structured sentences and play
- Continued practice with final consonants in simple sentences and play-based tasks.
- Accuracy for final /p, m, t, n/ in imitated sentences remained similar to last week, with generally good consistency when cued.
- In spontaneous play, he continued to use more final consonants than at the start of the block, but performance did not noticeably increase relative to the previous session.

- Targeted /k/ within short story descriptions and free play.
- He maintained good accuracy for /k/ in structured sentences with minimal cueing.
- Spontaneous use of /k/ in phrases (e.g., “my car go,” “look at cat”) was observed at a level similar to last session, showing stable but not newly expanded performance.

- Continued practicing the same set of /s/ words and short phrases.
- Word-level productions remained variably accurate; some were clear with moderate cues, while others were distorted.
- Phrase-level performance was also similar to last week, with some accurate productions and some reduced or unclear /s/ sounds.

4. /s/ blends at imitated word level
- Practiced a few familiar /s/ blends in imitation.
- Client continued to show interest but still mostly omitted /s/ or simplified the blends. No major change in accuracy compared to prior session.
