- Caregiver to keep emphasizing final sounds in a few key words and to model clear /s/ in slow speech, without pressuring him to repeat every time.
notes/7/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother stated that he is "talking a lot more" and is eager to tell stories about preschool. She continues to hear clear /k/ in many words, but final sounds and /s/ are "hit or miss." No major changes reported.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Transitioned readily from the waiting room. Very talkative and enjoyed sharing about his week. Required gentle redirection at times to stay on topic but was cooperative with all tasks.

Goals and observations:
1. Initial /k/ in sentences and conversation
- Maintained strong /k/ accuracy in imitated sentences (~9/10) with minimal cueing. In semi-structured conversation about picture scenes and personal experiences, he consistently used accurate /k/ in high-frequency words (car, cookie, cow, key). Occasional fronting appeared in less familiar words, but he was usually able to repeat with a clearer /k/ when prompted.

2. Final consonant production (p, t, m) in CVC words and short phrases
- Word-level accuracy remained similar to previous session: final /m/ ~8/10, final /p/ ~7/10, final /t/ ~5/10 with visual and tapping cues. Trialed simple phrases for final /m/ and /p/ ("my cup," "big ham"); he was able to maintain the final sound in about half of these phrase attempts. Still tends to drop final /t" in phrases.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- /s/ productions at isolation and syllable level remained stable. Isolation: ~8/10 clear attempts with minimal cueing. CV syllables: ~6/10 with reduced /th/ substitutions but occasional slushy quality. Needed reminders to keep tongue behind teeth and blow gently.

4. Functional communication
- Produced longer phrases and simple sentences to comment and ask questions. Intelligibility to clinician was fair to good in context; unfamiliar listeners would likely continue to have difficulty due to ongoing final consonant omissions and fricative distortions.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue current level for /k/ while monitoring for spontaneous generalization across a wider range of vocabulary.
- Maintain focus on final /p, t, m" at the word and emerging phrase level, particularly building consistency for final /t/.
- Continue stabilizing /s/ in syllables; plan to introduce a small set of /s/ words in imitation once accuracy is consistently higher.
- Encourage caregiver to continue brief, playful practice sessions at home and to provide specific praise for marked final sounds ("I heard your /p/ in cup!").
notes/8/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that preschool staff have commented that he is "more understandable" during circle time, especially when talking about cars and animals. She still notices many words without final sounds at home, but she feels he is "trying more" when reminded.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Arrived with good energy and transitioned easily. Very social and wanted to show the clinician how fast his toy cars could go. Sustained attention to structured speech activities when they were embedded in play.

Goals and observations:
1. Initial /k/ in conversation
- /k/ remained strong in both structured and unstructured conversation. In talking about pictures and personal experiences, he used accurate /k/ in familiar words with high consistency. Occasional errors persisted in unfamiliar multi-syllabic words, but he was responsive to prompts to "use your back sound." No change in overall level of support required compared to prior session.

2. Final consonant production (p, t, m) in CVC words and short phrases
- Performance similar to last week. At the imitated word level, final /m/ ~8/10, final /p/ ~7/10, final /t/ ~5/10. In simple phrases for /m/ and /p/ ("my cup," "big ham"), he maintained the final consonant in about half of attempts. Still tended to omit final /t/ in both words and phrases. Required consistent cues to slow down and tap the last sound.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- /s/ accuracy remained stable: isolation ~8/10 with light cueing, syllables ~6/10. Distortions were somewhat reduced but still present. No significant change in cueing level or consistency compared to previous sessions.

4. Functional communication
- Continued to use longer utterances with varied vocabulary. Intelligibility within context was fair; errors on final consonants and /s/ continued to affect clarity, particularly in less familiar topics.

Plan/recommendations:
- Maintain current goals, with continued emphasis on final consonant practice and monitoring of /k/ generalization.
- Continue repeated, high-frequency practice of a small set of final /t/ words and consider adding visual supports (e.g., picture of a "stop sign" for the last sound).
- Continue /s/ work at syllable level, focusing on consistency before moving to words.
- Encourage caregiver to model clear final consonants and to use short, focused practice times rather than long drills.
notes/9/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother stated that he continues to enjoy practicing his "back sounds" and will sometimes show off his clear "car" and "cookie" to family members. She feels final sounds are "about the same" and is hoping to see more change with his "snake sound" in words.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Transitioned well. Cooperative and engaged, though somewhat tired near the end of the session. Responded to visual schedule and short movement breaks.

Goals and observations:
1. Initial /k/ in conversation and narratives
- Maintained high accuracy with /k/ in conversation and simple narratives about his week. Very few instances of fronting were noted, primarily in longer or unfamiliar words. He required only occasional prompts and demonstrated consistent generalization in a variety of contexts.

2. Final consonant production (p, t, m) in CVC words and short phrases
- Accuracy levels were similar to prior sessions: final /m/ ~8/10, final /p/ ~7/10, final /t/ ~5/10 in imitation with cues. In phrases, he continued to mark final /m/ and /p/ in about half of attempts and often omitted final /t/. He benefited from slowed rate and segmentation but did not yet carry this over independently.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- /s/ performance remained stable. Isolation accuracy around 8/10 with minimal cueing; syllable-level around 6/10 with some persistent distortions. He continued to enjoy "snake sound" activities but required similar levels of support as previous weeks.

4. Functional communication
- Used multi-word sentences and basic story-telling about preschool. Intelligibility improved for topics with familiar vocabulary but remained reduced overall due to ongoing phonological patterns.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to monitor /k/ in conversation but shift primary treatment focus toward final consonants and /s/ as /k/ is nearing generalization.
- Maintain targeted practice for final /p, t, m" and consider adding simple visual cues for final /t/ (e.g., drawing a dot for the last sound).
- Continue /s/ syllable practice; re-assess readiness for word-level targets in upcoming sessions.
- Encourage caregiver to focus on a few "power words" at home for final consonants and to keep practices positive and brief.
notes/10/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that family continues to notice clearer "car" and "cookie" productions. She has been working on words like "cat" and "cup" at home and feels he can do them when they practice slowly, but he "forgets" in regular talking.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Entered willingly and checked in with the clinician verbally. Participated well in both table-top and play-based tasks. Needed occasional prompts to stay seated during structured activities.

Goals and observations:
1. Initial /k/ in conversation
- /k/ remained consistently accurate in conversation across familiar topics. Only rare fronting errors occurred, and he typically accepted prompts to repeat. This skill appears stable at the conversational level.

2. Final consonant production (p, t, m) in CVC words and short phrases
- Accuracy remained similar to last several sessions. In imitation, final /m/ ~8/10, final /p/ ~7/10, final /t/ ~5/10 with visual and tactile cues. In short phrases, he continued to mark final /m/ and /p/ in roughly half of attempts and tended to omit final /t/. No notable change in independence or generalization.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- /s/ production continued at a similar level: isolation ~8/10, CV syllables ~6/10 with mild distortions. He was able to adjust slightly with reminders to keep his tongue behind his teeth but still needed consistent cueing.

4. Functional communication
- Used complex sentences and expanded comments during play. Intelligibility to the clinician was fair to good in context; unfamiliar listeners would likely still need repetition and context support.

Plan/recommendations:
- Maintain monitoring of /k/ while focusing direct treatment time on final consonants and /s/.
- Continue practice with a limited set of high-value final /p, t, m" words and phrases, emphasizing slow rate and tapping cues.
- Continue /s/ syllable work; consider trialing a small number of simple /s/ words in imitation to probe readiness, while recognizing that syllable-level stability is still developing.
- Encourage caregiver to keep home practice short and consistent, praising effort and approximations rather than only perfect productions.
notes/11/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that he remains enthusiastic about "practicing his sounds" and will occasionally remind her, "I did my back sound!" She continues to notice missing final sounds in many words but is pleased with his increased talking and confidence.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Transitioned easily and was eager to begin. Stayed engaged throughout the session with minimal redirection. Responded well to visual supports and positive feedback.

Goals and observations:
1. Initial /k/ in conversation
- /k/ productions remained consistently accurate in conversational speech across a variety of topics and activities. Rare errors were observed and were easily corrected when prompted. This target appears well-established at this level.

2. Final consonant production (p, t, m) in CVC words and short phrases
- Performance remained in the same range as recent sessions. In imitation, final /m/ ~8/10, final /p/ ~7/10, final /t/ ~5/10 with cues. In short phrases, he continued to show better maintenance of final /m/ and /p" than /t" and still relied on clinician support to slow down and mark the last sound. No clear shift in independence yet.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and CV syllables; probe for word level
- Isolation and CV syllable accuracy remained stable (isolation ~8/10, syllables ~6/10). Briefly probed simple /s/ words in imitation (e.g., "sun," "sock"). He produced approximations with mixed accuracy and required substantial cueing; not yet ready for consistent word-level practice.

4. Functional communication
- Continued to use multi-word sentences to share stories and participate in play. Intelligibility was improved for familiar topics but still reduced overall due to ongoing final consonant and /s/ difficulties.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to monitor /k/ in conversation but shift primary treatment focus toward improving final consonant accuracy and advancing /s/ when ready.
- Maintain intensive practice on a small, functional set of final /p, t, m" words and phrases, gradually encouraging more independent productions.
- Continue shaping /s/ in syllables and revisit word-level practice as accuracy and consistency improve.
- Caregiver to continue short daily practice with chosen target words, emphasizing slow, clear speech and specific praise for marked final sounds and successful "snake sounds."

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
Mother reported that she is understanding most of his speech at home, but other family members still ask for repetition. She noted he often leaves off the last sound in words (e.g., “ca” for “cat”) and sometimes says “tup” for “cup.” No major changes since the initial evaluation.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned into the therapy room with minimal hesitation, holding mom’s hand. He engaged well with toys and responded positively to praise and visual supports. Attention was best in short, play-based activities (3–4 minutes) with frequent changes in materials.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonant production (p, t, k) in CVC words – imitation level
- Targeted words such as “cup, cap, cat, hat, hot, hop, back, bike.”
- With simultaneous production and maximal verbal/visual cueing (e.g., “say it with your quiet stop sound at the end”), client produced final consonants accurately in some trials but frequently omitted or neutralized them (e.g., “cu” for “cup”).
- Accuracy was inconsistent and strongly cue-dependent; he did not yet carry over to independent productions.

2. Initial /k/ in isolation and simple syllables (CV)
- Practiced “k” in isolation using “tongue back” cues and a mirror.
- Able to imitate /k/ in isolation intermittently but often substituted /t/ despite cues.
- In CV syllables (e.g., “koo, kah”), production was mostly fronted to /t/; successful productions occurred only with slow pacing and maximal verbal models.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation
- Introduced “long, quiet snake sound” /s/ in isolation.
- Client could approximate a prolonged /s/ with strong visual models; intermittent nasal emission and stopping observed.
- Not yet ready to move to syllables; focus remained on establishing a consistent airflow and tongue placement.

4. Functional phrases for requesting
- During play, modeled short, functional phrases (e.g., “more cars,” “my turn”).
- Client primarily used single words or gestures to request; imitated some two-word combinations with direct models.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to establish final consonants and initial /k/ and /s/ at the isolation and simple word level with heavy support.
- Encourage parents to model clear final sounds in daily routines (e.g., emphasizing end sounds in simple words like “up, out, in, hot”) without pressuring him to repeat.
- Next session: maintain current targets; consider adding simple minimal pairs (e.g., “key/tea”) if /k/ in isolation stabilizes.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
Mother reported that she has been trying to slow down her speech and emphasize end sounds. She notices he occasionally says “up” more clearly now, but most of the time final sounds are still missing. No concerns with hearing or health this week.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client separated from mom with reassurance and joined the session willingly. He was playful and cooperative, though he needed frequent redirection from self-directed play back to speech tasks. Responded well to turn-taking games and visual token reinforcement.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonant production (p, t, k) in CVC words – imitation level
- Practiced “cup, cap, cat, coat, bike, back, eat, out.”
- Compared to last week, he produced more audible final /p/ and /t/ when given a clear model and verbal cue (e.g., “stop your sound at the end”).
- Still inconsistent, but showed a slight increase in attempts to add the end sound, even when not fully accurate.

2. Initial /k/ in CV and CVC words – imitation
- Continued with tongue-back cues and mirror.
- Client produced /k/ in isolation more consistently, though still not yet automatic.
- At the word level (e.g., “key, car, cat”), he continued to substitute /t/ frequently, but there were a few clear /k/ productions with maximal cueing. Emerging improvement noted, but still at the imitated word level.

3. Initial /s/ isolation and CV syllables
- Revisited /s/ as a “long, quiet snake sound.”
- Able to hold /s/ for several seconds with visual cue to keep tongue behind teeth.
- Introduced simple syllables (“see, soo”); accuracy was variable, with some productions distorted or stopped, but overall more consistent than at baseline.

4. Two-word functional phrases in play
- Modeled and elicited two-word requests (e.g., “more bubbles,” “big car”).
- Client imitated several two-word phrases and independently produced a few (e.g., “my car,” “more please”) with light cueing.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain focus on final consonants and initial /k/ and /s/ at the word and syllable levels, emphasizing accuracy with strong models.
- Encourage caregivers to continue modeling end sounds and to praise any attempts where he adds a final sound, even if not perfect.
- Next session: consider beginning simple minimal pairs for final consonants (e.g., “bee/beep”) to heighten his awareness if consistency continues to improve.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
Mother shared that he is starting to copy her when she exaggerates end sounds, especially with “up” and “out.” She feels he is “trying harder” to fix his words when she repeats them. No new concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client entered the room independently and appeared eager to play. Attention was improved; he remained engaged in structured tasks for longer intervals when activities were presented as games. Accepted verbal feedback and enjoyed earning stickers.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants (p, t, k) in CVC words – imitation and emerging spontaneous
- Practiced a variety of CVC words (e.g., “cup, cap, cat, coat, cut, bike, back, book”).
- Notable increase in accuracy with final /p/ and /t/ in imitation; fewer omissions compared to prior sessions.
- Began to produce some final consonants spontaneously in play (e.g., “up” and “out” during a ball game) after prior modeling, indicating early carryover.

2. Initial /k/ in CV and CVC words – word level, mostly imitation
- Targets included “key, car, cow, cat, cup, cookie.”
- Marked breakthrough in his ability to produce /k/ with reduced cueing; he often achieved a correct /k/ after only a verbal reminder (“tongue in the back”) rather than full visual/tactile support.
- Fewer substitutions to /t/ and more consistent accuracy across different words.

3. Initial /s/ in CV and CVC words – imitation level
- Practiced “see, sun, soup, sock, sit.”
- Client produced a clearer, sustained /s/ at the beginning of words with moderate cues.
- Accuracy was higher than last session, with fewer stops and distortions. Some spontaneous “sss” sounds during play when prompted to make the “snake sound.”

4. Two- and three-word phrases in structured play
- Encouraged short phrases in routines (e.g., “more blue bubbles,” “I want cars”).
- Client imitated 2–3 word phrases well and occasionally initiated his own short combinations with intelligible key words.

Plan / recommendations:
- Given the improvement, begin to probe final consonants and initial /k/ in short carrier phrases next session (e.g., “I see __,” “my __”).
- Continue to support /s/ at the word level; once more stable, will trial simple phrases.
- At home, encourage slow, clear models and praise for any attempts with clearer end sounds or back sounds (/k/).
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
Mother reported that his “k” sound is noticeably clearer this week in words like “car” and “cookie.” She still notices missing end sounds in longer words but feels familiar listeners are understanding him better.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned smoothly and quickly chose a preferred toy. He was very engaged and talkative, often commenting on the activities. Required occasional reminders to wait for his turn to speak but remained cooperative and motivated.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants (p, t, k) in CVC words and short phrases
- Continued with CVC words (e.g., “cup, cat, coat, bike, back”) and introduced carrier phrases such as “my cat,” “big cup,” “in back.”
- At the single-word level, final /p/ and /t/ were often correct with only light verbal cues; /k/ final was slightly less consistent but improving.
- In short phrases, he maintained some final consonants independently, though accuracy dipped compared to single words. Demonstrated a clear jump from last week in his ability to attempt end sounds in connected speech.

2. Initial /k/ in words and two-word phrases
- Targets included “key, car, cat, cookie, cow, cup” in contexts like “my car,” “big cookie.”
- Accuracy at the word level was high with minimal cueing; he often corrected himself if he started to say /t/.
- In two-word phrases, /k/ was largely maintained, suggesting generalization beyond isolated words.

3. Initial /s/ in CVC words and emerging phrases
- Practiced “sun, soup, sock, seat, sand” and phrases such as “my sock,” “see sun.”
- He produced initial /s/ more consistently in imitation, requiring moderate cues for tongue placement.
- Some spontaneous uses of /s/ in play (e.g., “snake,” “see”) were noted with reasonably clear production.

4. Early /s/ blends – imitation
- Briefly introduced /s/ blends at the word level (e.g., “spoon, stop”) using slow, segmented models.
- Client attempted blends but often omitted the /s/ or simplified the cluster (e.g., “poon” for “spoon”).
- This was framed as an exploratory task; not yet ready for higher-level practice.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue working final consonants and initial /k/ in short phrases to solidify carryover.
- Maintain focus on initial /s/ at the word and simple phrase level; revisit /s/ blends in a more structured way once single /s/ is consistently accurate.
- Parents encouraged to model short phrases with strong final consonants at home (e.g., “big cup,” “hot soup”) during daily routines.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent report:
Mother noted that he is putting more words together at home, often using 3–4 word phrases. She feels his “k” sound is usually there now, but she still hears dropped end sounds, especially when he talks fast.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was cheerful and talkative on arrival. He was eager to show the clinician a toy car he brought from home. Participation remained good, though he occasionally rushed through words when excited. Gentle reminders to use his “slow, careful talking” were effective.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants (p, t, k) in phrases and simple sentences
- Practiced short phrases and emerging simple sentences (e.g., “I want cup,” “put it back,” “I see cat”).
- Accuracy of final /p/ and /t/ remained similar to last session: generally good with verbal reminders, more variable when speaking quickly.
- Final /k/ in phrases showed small, steady gains; he is increasingly aware when the end sound is missing but still needs prompts to correct.

2. Initial /k/ in phrases and short sentences
- Used picture description tasks with phrases like “big car,” “my cookie,” “I see a cow.”
- Performance was largely stable compared to last week: /k/ remained well-established in most contexts with minimal cueing.
- Occasional fronting to /t/ was noted in longer or less familiar phrases, but he often accepted correction and tried again.

3. Initial /s/ in CVC words and short phrases
- Targets included “sun, soup, sock, seat, sad,” with phrases such as “see sun,” “my sock.”
- Accuracy showed a slight increase; he more consistently produced a clear /s/ at the start of words with moderate cueing.
- Spontaneous /s/ in play was present but still less consistent than in structured tasks.

4. Brief probe of /s/ blends
- Rechecked /s/ blends (“spoon, star, stop”) at the imitated word level.
- Similar pattern to previous trial: frequent reduction of the blend to a single consonant, with some emerging correct attempts when words were heavily segmented.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue consolidating final consonants and /k/ in phrases; maintain current level of complexity.
- Ongoing work on initial /s/ with a focus on consistency; blends to remain a lower priority until single /s/ is more stable.
- At home, encourage “slow talking” during key routines (e.g., mealtime, bath) and reinforce clear final sounds without overcorrecting.
notes/5/note_text:
Parent report:
Mother reported that his speech seemed “about the same” this week. She continues to hear clearer /k/ words but still notices that he drops end sounds when he’s excited or talking to cousins.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client entered the room easily and participated well in familiar routines. Attention and cooperation were similar to prior sessions. He responded best to short, game-like tasks and occasional movement breaks.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants (p, t, k) in phrases
- Continued practice with phrases such as “my cup,” “on top,” “put it back,” “big cat.”
- Overall performance was stable relative to last week: final /p/ and /t/ generally correct with verbal reminders; final /k/ somewhat less consistent.
- Accuracy dropped in longer utterances, but he remained willing to repeat and try again when cued.

2. Initial /k/ in phrases and short sentences
- Used picture scenes to elicit sentences like “The car is red,” “I see a cow.”
- Accuracy was similar to prior session: /k/ was usually correct at the beginning of words, with occasional fronting in more complex sentences.
- He accepted feedback well and showed good effort to repair errors when pointed out.

3. Initial /s/ in CVC words and short phrases
- Revisited words “sun, soup, sock, seat, sad,” plus “see snake,” “sit down.”
- Performance appeared stable: he continued to need moderate cues for tongue placement, with a mix of accurate and distorted productions.
- No major change in spontaneous /s/ use; he did produce some clearer /s/ when prompted to use his “snake sound.”

4. /s/ blends – brief maintenance probe
- Checked “spoon, stop, star” in imitation.
- Similar to last week: blends often reduced to a single consonant, though he occasionally produced both sounds when the word was broken into parts.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain current focus to consolidate gains, particularly for final consonants and /k/ in phrases.
- Continue practicing initial /s/ with visual supports (mirror, picture of tongue placement).
- Encourage caregivers to keep modeling clear end sounds and to highlight his successful productions with specific praise (e.g., “I heard your /p/ in ‘cup’—nice job!”).
notes/6/note_text:
Parent report:
Mother shared that she is hearing him correct himself occasionally, especially with “up” and “back.” She noted a few clearer /s/ words (e.g., “sun”) at home when they look at picture books together.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client arrived in good spirits and transitioned smoothly. He showed strong engagement with a new board game activity and was motivated by taking turns and earning small rewards. Needed only minimal redirection to stay on task.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants (p, t, k) in phrases and simple sentences
- Practiced phrases and short sentences such as “I want cup,” “put it back,” “cat on top,” “I see a bike.”
- Compared to recent sessions, there was a modest increase in consistency: he more frequently added final /p/ and /t/ without needing as many direct prompts.
- Final /k/ remained more variable but showed some clearer productions in structured phrases.

2. Initial /k/ in short sentences
- Used picture description and simple story sequences (e.g., “The car goes,” “The cow eats grass”).
- Accuracy stayed generally high, with fewer fronting errors even as sentence length increased slightly.
- He occasionally self-corrected when he started with /t/ instead of /k/, indicating growing awareness.

3. Initial /s/ in CVC words and phrases
- Targets included “sun, soup, seat, sad, soap,” with phrases like “see sun,” “my soap.”
- Slight improvement noted: he required somewhat fewer cues to achieve a clear /s/ at the beginning of words.
- Some spontaneous /s/ productions in play (e.g., “snake,” “see”) were clearer than in prior weeks, though still inconsistent.

4. /s/ blends – structured imitation
- Practiced /s/ blends in a more structured way (“spoon, star, stop, sky”) with segmented models and visual cueing.
- He continued to reduce blends in many attempts, but there were a few more correct productions when words were broken into slow parts, reflecting early emerging skill.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue building consistency for final consonants and /s/ in words and short phrases.
- Maintain /k/ work at the sentence level to support generalization.
- At home, suggest brief daily practice with picture cards for “sun, soup, seat” and simple phrases (“see sun”), emphasizing the “snake sound” at the start.
notes/7/note_text:
Parent report:
Mother reported that he is “much clearer” when talking about his favorite toys and books. She specifically noticed better /s/ in words like “sun” and “soap,” and that his cousins are asking for repetition less often.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was enthusiastic and talkative throughout the session. He attended well to both table-top and floor-based activities and enjoyed being the “teacher” to correct the clinician’s silly errors. Very responsive to visual feedback using a mirror.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants (p, t, k) in sentences and structured play
- Used short sentences during play (e.g., “I put it back,” “The cat is on top,” “I like soup”).
- Noticeable improvement: he maintained final /p/ and /t/ in many sentences with minimal cueing and showed better consistency with final /k/ than in previous weeks.
- When excited, some omissions still occurred, but he often attempted to fix them when prompted (“Say it with your end sound”).

2. Initial /k/ in varied sentence positions
- Practiced sentences such as “The car is fast,” “The cookie is big,” “The cow is sleeping.”
- /k/ was produced accurately in initial positions with little to no cueing.
- Accuracy remained strong even as sentence structures varied, suggesting good generalization.

3. Initial /s/ in CVC words and short sentences
- Targets included “sun, soup, soap, seat, sad,” with sentences like “The sun is hot,” “I see soap.”
- Marked improvement: he produced initial /s/ more consistently with reduced cueing and clearer airflow.
- Several spontaneous uses of /s/ in conversation were intelligible (e.g., “so big,” “see it”), showing carryover beyond drill tasks.

4. /s/ blends at the imitated word level
- Focused on /st/ and /sp/ blends (e.g., “stop, star, spoon, spot”) using slow, segmented models.
- He successfully produced some accurate blends, particularly /st/, when given clear visual and verbal cues.
- While not yet fully consistent, this represented a meaningful step up from earlier attempts.

Plan / recommendations:
- Begin to incorporate more sentence-level practice for final consonants and initial /s/ to support generalization.
- Continue structured practice of /s/ blends, starting with easier clusters (/st/) before expanding.
- Caregivers encouraged to notice and praise clear /s/ and final consonant productions in everyday conversation and to model short sentences with those sounds.
notes/8/note_text:
Parent report:
Mother stated that his speech continues to be “pretty clear” for family members. She notices that /s/ blends like “stop” are still tricky, but she is hearing more final sounds in his sentences overall.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned easily and participated well in familiar games and picture description tasks. His attention and cooperation were similar to last week. He enjoyed correcting the clinician’s “silly” mispronunciations.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants (p, t, k) in sentences
- Practiced a variety of short sentences (e.g., “I like soup,” “Put it back,” “The cat is on top,” “I see a bike”).
- Performance remained generally stable: final consonants were present in many sentences with light prompting, with some omissions when he spoke quickly.
- He continued to respond well to cues to “add your end sound” and showed good effort to repair.

2. Initial /k/ in connected speech
- Used simple storybooks and picture scenes to elicit sentences including /k/ words (e.g., “The car is blue,” “The cow is big,” “I want a cookie”).
- /k/ remained well-established with minimal support. Occasional fronting was observed in longer, less familiar sentences but was infrequent.

3. Initial /s/ in sentences
- Practiced sentences like “The sun is hot,” “I see soap,” “The soup is hot,” “Sit on the seat.”
- Accuracy and consistency were similar to last week: he produced initial /s/ clearly much of the time with light to moderate cues.
- Some spontaneous /s/ words in conversation (e.g., “so big,” “see car”) were noted and were generally intelligible.

4. /s/ blends – word level
- Continued work with /st/ and /sp/ words (“stop, star, spoon, spider, spot”).
- Blends were still challenging; he often reduced them to a single consonant, though some correct productions occurred with slow, segmented modeling.
- Overall, skill level appeared stable compared to the previous session.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain current goals to consolidate gains at the sentence level for final consonants and /s/.
- Continue practicing /s/ blends at the imitated word level, focusing on accuracy before moving to phrases.
- At home, encourage slow, clear speech during key routines and continue to highlight correct productions (e.g., “I heard your /st/ in ‘stop’!”).
notes/9/note_text:

notes/10/note_text:
Parent report:

1. Final consonants (p, t, k) in sentences and structured conversation

2. Initial /k/ in connected speech
- Used conversational topics about his toys and daily routines to elicit /k/ words in sentences.
- /k/ remained well-established with minimal cueing; accuracy level appeared stable.

3. Initial /s/ in sentences and short narratives

notes/11/note_text:
Parent report:
Mother reported that overall intelligibility remains good for familiar listeners. She continues to notice that blends like “spider” and “spoon” are hard for him, but is pleased with how often he is using full sentences.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned into the room easily and seemed comfortable with the therapy routine. He participated well in both structured tasks and play-based activities, with attention and cooperation similar to recent sessions.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants (p, t, k) in sentences and conversation

2. Initial /k/ in connected speech
- Used conversational topics (e.g., favorite foods, animals, vehicles) to elicit /k/ words.

notes/0/note_text:
Session 1
Child age: 4;0

Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother shared that she and daycare staff notice that others have difficulty understanding him, especially with new people.
- No recent changes in health or hearing reported.
- Parent is most concerned about his “funny” /s/ and that he leaves off sounds at the ends of words.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Transitioned to therapy room with some hesitation but separated from mother with reassurance.
- Once engaged in play-based tasks, he was cheerful and cooperative.
- Attention span was appropriate for age with occasional redirection back to the task.

Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: Establish accurate production of /s/ in isolation and simple syllables (imitation)
- Baseline probing revealed difficulty sustaining a clear /s/; frequent lateral airflow and occasional /t/ substitutions.
- With visual model and verbal cue to “keep your tongue behind your teeth and blow,” he produced a recognizable /s/ in isolation in about half of attempts.
- In simple CV syllables (e.g., “see,” “suh”), accuracy decreased; required frequent repetition and close visual model.

Goal 2: Explore initial /f/ production in isolation and CV syllables (imitation)
- Initial attempt at /f/ showed labiodental placement emerging but inconsistent airflow; often substituted /p/.
- With cueing to “bite your lip and blow,” he achieved accurate /f/ in isolation in several trials but was not yet consistent.
- In syllables (e.g., “fee,” “fo”), productions were mostly approximations; needed maximal verbal and gestural support.

Goal 3: Assess final consonant production at single word level
- Probed CVC words with final /p, t, m/ in play (e.g., “cup, cat, mom”).
- Frequently omitted final consonants or added a neutral vowel (“cu,” “ca-uh”).
- When cued to “keep your sound on” or given a simultaneous model, he could mark some final consonants, but accuracy was low and not yet reliable.

Goal 4: Obtain general speech/language sample
- During free play, used many 2–3 word phrases (e.g., “my car,” “want that one”).
- Intelligibility to clinician unfamiliar with him was reduced, particularly in longer utterances and with less contextual support.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to build /s/ and /f/ in isolation and simple syllables with strong visual and verbal cues.
- Begin systematic work on final consonant marking with high-frequency CVC words.
- Parent educated on using a mirror at home to practice “snake sound /s/” and “lip-bite sound /f/” for a few minutes daily in a playful way.
- Plan to introduce simple picture-based word practice for /s/ and /f/ once isolation becomes more stable.
notes/1/note_text:
Session 2

Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported practicing the “snake sound” in the bathroom mirror a few times, though not daily.
- She feels he is starting to notice when words “sound funny” but is not yet correcting himself.
- No new concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Entered room easily and appeared comfortable with clinician.
- Very engaged in car and animal play; responded well to turn-taking games.
- Needed occasional breaks from direct drill; responded well to integrating targets into play.

Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: /s/ in isolation and CV syllables (imitation)
- Compared to last week, /s/ in isolation was more consistent with visual cueing; able to produce a sustained /s/ in most attempts when given time and reminder to keep tongue back.
- In CV syllables ("see, soo, sah"), accuracy improved; several clear productions with immediate imitation.
- Began to reduce cueing slightly on some trials (only visual model, no verbal cue) with fair success.

Goal 2: Initial /f/ in isolation and CV syllables (imitation)
- Demonstrated clearer awareness of lip and teeth placement.
- With a verbal cue (“bite your lip, blow”), he produced accurate /f/ in isolation in the majority of attempts.
- In simple syllables ("fee, foo, fa"), approximations improved; fewer substitutions to /p/. Still needed frequent models and reminders to slow down.

Goal 3: Marking final consonants in CVC words (imitation)
- Targeted high-interest CVC words (e.g., “cup, map, hat”) in a picture game.
- Continued to omit many final consonants independently.
- With simultaneous production and emphasis on the end sound, he was able to mark final consonants more often than last session, but accuracy remained inconsistent.

Goal 4: Functional phrases in play
- Modeled short carrier phrases (e.g., “my ___,” “I want ___”) and encouraged imitation.
- He readily imitated many phrases; speech remained reduced in clarity but willingness to attempt longer utterances was strong.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue focusing on /s/ and /f/ in isolation and syllables, gradually reducing cueing where possible.
- Maintain work on final consonant marking with a small core set of CVC words.
- Encourage parent to integrate quick “sound practice” into daily routines (e.g., brushing teeth, bath time) with 5–10 repetitions of /s/ and /f/.
- Anticipate trialing initial /s/ and /f/ at word level next session if current gains are maintained.
notes/2/note_text:
Session 3

Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that he is starting to copy her when she exaggerates /s/ in words like “sun” and “sit.”
- She notices a few clearer /f/ words at home (e.g., “fish”) when she reminds him.
- No concerns with hearing or health.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Transitioned smoothly and appeared eager to show toys.
- Maintained attention well during short drill segments when paired with a reward game.
- Responded positively to specific praise about “strong sounds.”

Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: /s/ in isolation, CV syllables, and initial word level (imitation)
- Isolation: Produced a consistent, recognizable /s/ with minimal verbal cueing; often only needed visual model.
- CV syllables: Accuracy and stability improved; several sequences of correct productions.
- Trialed initial /s/ at imitated word level (e.g., “sun, soup, sock, see”). Accuracy was variable but better than baseline, with some clearly correct productions when given a slow model and visual cue.

Goal 2: Initial /f/ in CV syllables and simple words (imitation)
- CV syllables ("fee, fo, fa"): Notably more consistent; fewer substitutions to /p/ and better control of airflow.
- Introduced a small set of initial /f/ words ("fish, fun, four, fan") at the imitated word level.
- With a model and lip-bite cue, he produced several accurate tokens; occasional weak airflow or omission of /f/ still noted.

Goal 3: Final consonant production in CVC words (imitation with cueing)
- Continued targeting final /p, t, m/ in familiar words.
- With a clapping or tapping cue on the final sound, he was able to mark more final consonants compared to previous sessions.
- Still frequently omitted endings without support; not yet generalizing to spontaneous speech.

Goal 4: Functional short phrases using target sounds (imitation)
- Incorporated target sounds into simple phrases (e.g., “my fish,” “sun up,” “fun game”) within structured play.
- He enjoyed these phrases and imitated many successfully, though individual sound accuracy remained variable.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to build consistency for /s/ and /f/ at the imitated word level while maintaining practice in isolation/syllables.
- Maintain work on final consonants with strong visual and tactile cues (e.g., tapping for the last sound).
- Encourage parent to practice a small list of /s/ and /f/ words with him at home using slow, clear models.
- Consider introducing very simple /s/ blends in isolation once initial /s/ at word level is more stable.
notes/3/note_text:

- No new concerns or changes.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Arrived in good mood and transitioned independently.
- Participated well in turn-taking board game; some silly behavior when tasks became more challenging, but redirected with gentle prompts.
- Tolerated brief structured drill mixed with play.

Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: Initial /s/ at imitated word level
- Targeted a small set of initial /s/ words ("sun, soap, sock, soup, seat") using picture cards.
- Accuracy similar to last session: some clear productions with visual/verbal cues, but inconsistent.
- Continued to need reminders about tongue placement and airflow; not yet ready to move to phrases without substantial support.

Goal 2: Initial /f/ at imitated word level
- Practiced words such as “fish, four, fun, feet.”
- Maintained previous gains; still produced several accurate /f/ words with direct models and cues.
- Occasional reversion to /p/ or omission when excited or speaking quickly.

Goal 3: Final consonant production in CVC words
- Focused on final /p, t, m/ within a matching game.
- Performance generally stable compared to last week; he continued to require simultaneous production and emphasis on the end sound to mark final consonants.
- Without support, he often dropped the final sound, especially in fast, playful contexts.

Goal 4: Short carrier phrases with targets (imitation)
- Used simple frames such as “my ___” and “I see ___” with target words (e.g., “my sock,” “I see sun”).

Plan/recommendations:

notes/4/note_text:

- No new medical or developmental concerns.

- Introduced short imitated phrases ("sun up," “soap here,” “my sock”). He was able to maintain a reasonably accurate /s/ in some phrases with slowed rate and visual cueing.

Goal 2: Initial /f/ at word and short phrase level (imitation)
- Targeted words such as “fish, four, fun, feet, food” within a fishing game.
- Showed more reliable /f/ production at the word level; required fewer reminders to “bite your lip.”
- Successfully imitated short phrases (“my fish,” “four fish,” “feet up”) with generally good /f/ when cued to go slowly.

Goal 3: Final consonant marking in CVC words (imitation)

notes/8/note_text:

notes/9/note_text:

- Still reliant on visual/verbal prompts for best performance.

Goal 2: Initial /f/ at word and short phrase level (imitation)
- Continued practice with initial /f/ words and short phrases in a “feeding game.”
- Performance stable: good accuracy in structured tasks with moderate cueing, less consistent in spontaneous conversation.
- Occasional self-corrections noted when given time.

Goal 3: Final consonant production in CVC words (imitation)
- Focused again on final /p, t, m/ using a picture lotto game.
- Maintained similar level of accuracy as previous sessions; still needed tapping and simultaneous models to reliably mark final consonants.
- Few spontaneous final consonants were noted in play but not consistently.

Goal 4: Functional phrases with targets (imitation)
- Used short phrases incorporating targets (e.g., “I want soup,” “big cup,” “my fish”).
- He imitated many of these successfully, though target sound accuracy remained variable.
- Intelligibility was somewhat better within these rehearsed phrases.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue with current plan, as skills appear to be consolidating with gradual increases in self-awareness.
- Home: parent encouraged to use the same carrier phrases from sessions and to gently prompt for “beginning and end sounds” in a playful way.
- Plan to reassess overall progress across the block and consider minor goal adjustments in upcoming sessions.
notes/10/note_text:
Session 11

Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother shared that a family member recently commented that he was “easier to understand” than a few months ago.
- She notices he sometimes slows down on his own and tries again when he is not understood.
- No new concerns reported.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Arrived cheerful and transitioned independently.
- Attended well to clinician models and was more reflective about his speech, asking “was that good?” at times.
- Enjoyed getting feedback and earning small rewards.

Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: Initial /s/ at word and short phrase level (imitation and emerging independence)
- Practiced initial /s/ words and short phrases as before.
- Slight improvement in consistency compared to last session; more frequent accurate /s/ productions with the same level of cueing.
- A few independent correct /s/ words were heard in play (e.g., “sun,” “sock”) without direct prompting, suggesting emerging carryover.

Goal 2: Initial /f/ at word and short phrase level (imitation and emerging independence)
- Continued with familiar /f/ words and phrases.
- Accuracy remained good in structured tasks; required somewhat fewer verbal reminders for lip placement on well-practiced words.
- Several spontaneous, clearer /f/ productions were noted in connected speech, and he occasionally self-corrected after an error.

Goal 3: Final consonant production in CVC words (imitation)
- Practiced final /p, t, m/ again with visual and tapping cues.
- Accuracy showed small gains: he marked final consonants more consistently within structured tasks and needed slightly less intensive cueing on some items.
- Still not yet generalized across all words or in conversation, but progress is emerging.

Goal 4: Functional phrases and intelligibility
- Used structured play to elicit short phrases (e.g., “I want fish,” “big soup,” “my cup”).
- With a reminder to “use your strong sounds,” he produced several phrases that were easier for an unfamiliar listener to understand.
- Demonstrated increased willingness to repeat and repair when not understood.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to support emerging independence for /s/ and /f/ at the word and phrase levels, gradually reducing cueing where successful.
- Maintain focused work on final consonant marking, with attention to self-monitoring.
- Home: encourage parent to praise self-corrections and to model slow, clear speech rather than repeatedly correcting.
- Plan to summarize progress across this therapy block at the next session and discuss next steps with caregiver.
notes/11/note_text:
Session 12

Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that family and daycare staff are starting to understand him more often, especially when he uses shorter, slower phrases.
- She notices he is proud of his “good sounds” and sometimes reminds her, “I did my snake sound!”
- She is interested in continuing therapy to build on these gains.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Transitioned without difficulty and appeared motivated to work.
- Demonstrated improved ability to attend to clinician feedback and to attempt self-correction.
- Engaged well in both structured and play-based tasks.

Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: Initial /s/ at word and phrase level (imitation and independent use)
- Practiced initial /s/ words and short phrases in a picture scene activity.
- Compared to prior weeks, he produced more consistent accurate /s/ in imitated words and phrases, with reduced cueing needed on familiar items.
- Several spontaneous correct /s/ productions were heard in functional phrases (e.g., “my sock,” “sun up”) without direct prompting, indicating emerging generalization.

Goal 2: Initial /f/ at word and phrase level (imitation and independent use)
- Targeted /f/ words and phrases within a “picnic” game (e.g., “four fish,” “food in”).
- Maintained strong accuracy in imitation and showed more independent correct /f/ productions during play.
- Self-correction was observed (e.g., “pish…fish”) with minimal clinician prompting, reflecting increased awareness.

Goal 3: Final consonant production in CVC words (imitation and emerging independence)
- Worked on final /p, t, m/ in a simple story-telling task.
- Demonstrated better consistency in marking final consonants in structured contexts, with some correct final sounds produced independently in familiar words.
- Still needs reminders, but overall accuracy is improved relative to earlier in the block.

Goal 4: Functional communication and intelligibility
- During free play, used many 3–4 word utterances.
- When cued to “use your strong sounds,” he was able to produce several phrases that were noticeably clearer and easier to understand.
- Showed good tolerance for clarification requests and willingness to repeat or slow down.

Plan/recommendations:
- Recommend continuation of speech therapy to further increase consistency of /s/ and /f/ production, expand to more complex word shapes (including /s/ blends), and strengthen final consonant marking.
- Home: provide caregiver with updated word and phrase lists for /s/, /f/, and final consonants, emphasizing brief, positive practice sessions and praise for self-correction.
- Next block will focus on consolidating gains at the phrase level and carefully introducing new targets (e.g., simple /s/ blends) as readiness allows.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother shared that she is most concerned about how hard it is for unfamiliar listeners to understand him. She reports that he is talking in short sentences at home but often leaves off the ends of words. No recent changes in health or hearing.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client transitioned to the therapy room with some hesitation but separated from mother with encouragement. Once engaged in play, he was cheerful and cooperative. Attention to adult-directed tasks was fair; needed occasional redirection from self-directed play back to speech tasks.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonant production in CVC words (p, t, m)
- Targeted final /p, t, m/ in simple words (e.g., pop, hat, mom) at the imitated word level. Client frequently omitted the final consonant or added a neutral vowel ("pah" for "pop"). With maximal verbal models and visual cues (tapping for the last sound, hand-to-mouth cue), he produced clearer final consonants in a small number of trials, but accuracy was inconsistent and required significant support.

2. Initial /s/ in single words
- Introduced initial /s/ in isolation and at the imitated word level (sun, soup, sock). In isolation, he approximated /s/ with a more /sh/ or /th/-like sound. At the word level, /s/ was often substituted or omitted. Required frequent cueing to keep tongue behind teeth and to use a “quiet snake sound.” Limited success with independent productions.

3. Syllable shapes (CV, CVCV)
- Observed and elicited a range of syllable shapes in play (e.g., "mama," "baba," "tata" for car sounds). CV and reduplicated CVCV forms were easier. When cued to attempt alternating CVCV (e.g., "bunny," "tiger"), he tended to reduce them to shorter forms ("bu," "tie").

4. Functional phrases for requesting
- During play, modeled simple 2–3 word phrases ("my turn," "more cars," "open please"). Client mostly used single words or jargon. He imitated short phrases in a few opportunities when given a direct model and enthusiastic reinforcement.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to target final consonants at the imitated word level with heavy visual and verbal cueing.
- Maintain focus on initial /s/ in isolation and simple words; consider adding tactile cues as tolerated.
- Encourage parents to model clear final sounds in everyday words (e.g., "up," "cup," "bed") without pressuring him to repeat.
- Next session: re-check final consonants and initial /s/; consider introducing simple /s/-blend perception activities if appropriate.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother reported no major changes since last week. She notices he is trying to say longer sentences but they "come out a bit mushy." Family has started emphasizing final sounds as discussed but is unsure if he is picking it up yet.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client entered the room more easily this week and appeared comfortable with the clinician. He engaged readily in toy-based activities and followed simple routines with visual supports. Attention was good for short, structured activities; brief breaks were helpful between tasks.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonant production in CVC words (p, t, m)
- Revisited final /p, t, m/ in imitated CVC words (pop, hat, mom, map, mat). Compared to last session, he produced the final consonant more frequently when provided with a slow model and visual cue (tapping the table for the last sound). Continued to add a slight vowel after some consonants but showed more attempts to “stop” his voice at the end of the word. Accuracy remained variable but there were more clear, successful productions.

2. Initial /s/ in isolation and single words
- Practiced /s/ in isolation using a mirror and “snake sound” imagery. Client was able to approximate a more consistent /s/ when focusing on teeth together and airflow. At the imitated word level (sun, soup, sock, sand), he still showed frequent substitutions and omissions, but there were a few clearer /s/ productions with direct modeling and repeated practice.

3. Syllable shapes (CVCV alternating)
- Targeted alternating CVCV forms (e.g., "bunny," "tiger," "happy") in imitation. He continued to reduce many to CV or CVC forms ("bu," "happi" without final vowel). With segmented modeling (clapping each part), he began to mark more of the syllables, though individual sounds within syllables were often imprecise.

4. Functional 2–3 word phrases
- Modeled and elicited simple phrases for requesting and commenting ("more bubbles," "my car," "go fast"). Client imitated several 2-word combinations when given a clear model and immediate access to a preferred item. Spontaneous phrases were mostly single words, but he showed increased willingness to repeat longer phrases.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue with final consonant practice at the imitated CVC level, reinforcing any clear final sound.
- Maintain focus on /s/ in isolation and a small set of high-frequency /s/ words.
- Encourage caregivers to use short, clear 2–3 word models during play (e.g., "big ball," "more blocks").
- Next session: consider beginning simple minimal pair activities for final consonants if consistency continues to improve.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother shared that she has noticed he is starting to copy her when she exaggerates final sounds ("cuP," "haT"). She reports a few clearer words at home, especially with /p/ at the end ("up," "cup"). She has not yet noticed changes with /s/.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client was eager to come into the room and quickly engaged with the toys offered. Participation in structured tasks was stronger today; he responded well to visual schedules and brief, game-like drills. Minimal redirection was needed.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonant production in CVC words (p, t, m)
- Targeted final /p, t, m/ using picture cards and a simple game. Accuracy in imitated CVC words was noticeably improved compared to previous sessions. He produced a clear final consonant in many trials when given a model and light verbal reminders ("don’t forget the last sound"). Able to contrast pairs such as "bee" vs. "beep" with increasing awareness. Occasional neutral vowel addition persisted but was less frequent.

2. Final consonant minimal pairs (presence vs. omission)
- Introduced basic auditory discrimination for final consonants (e.g., "bow" vs. "boat"). Client was generally able to point to the picture that matched the clinician’s production, suggesting emerging awareness of the difference. When attempting to produce the pairs himself, he was more successful with /p/ and /m/ than /t/.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and simple words
- Continued work on /s/ in isolation and a small set of words (sun, soup, sock, sad). With mirror support, he produced a clearer /s/ sound in isolation more consistently, though it sometimes drifted toward /sh/. At the word level, accuracy showed small gains with immediate imitation, but he still needed close modeling and verbal cueing for tongue placement.

4. Two- and three-word phrases in play
- During play-based activities, modeled and elicited short phrases ("more bubbles please," "I want car," "big truck"). Client imitated many 2-word phrases and a few 3-word combinations with good effort. Spontaneous phrases remained mostly 1–2 words but were more frequent overall.

Plan / recommendations:
- Begin to increase complexity for final consonants by incorporating more minimal pair practice while still supporting accuracy at the word level.
- Continue shaping /s/ in isolation and a limited number of functional /s/ words.
- Caregiver to keep highlighting final sounds at home and praise any attempts.
- Next session: consider trialing final consonants in short carrier phrases ("my ___," "big ___") if accuracy remains stable.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother reported that he is using more words overall and is “chattier,” but still difficult to understand when he is excited. She notices clearer endings on some words like "cup" and "map" but says it is not yet consistent.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client arrived in good spirits and transitioned smoothly. He remained engaged across the session with brief, play-based breaks. Occasional silliness emerged during drill tasks but was easily redirected with structured choices and visual supports.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonant production in CVC words and minimal pairs
- Continued practice with final /p, t, m/ in CVC words and simple minimal pairs (e.g., "bee"/"beep," "see"/"seat"). Performance was similar to last week: he produced final consonants correctly in many imitated trials when cued to “tap” the last sound, though some omissions and vowel additions remained. Discrimination of minimal pairs was generally accurate with picture choices.

2. Final consonants in short carrier phrases
- Trialed final consonants within simple carrier phrases ("my cup," "big hat") in imitation. Client was able to maintain the final consonant on some words when the phrase was slowed down and segmented. However, accuracy dropped when phrases were spoken more naturally, and he required frequent reminders.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and limited word set
- Practiced /s/ in isolation and in a few key words (sun, sock, soup). His ability to approximate /s/ in isolation remained similar to last session, with a need for visual and verbal cues. At the word level, there were some clearer productions but also many substitutions. No major change in independence noted.

4. Two- and three-word phrases
- Continued to model functional 2–3 word phrases for commenting and requesting ("more bubbles," "my turn," "go fast"). Client imitated several phrases accurately. Spontaneous speech during play included frequent 2-word combinations and occasional 3-word utterances, though articulation remained reduced in clarity.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain focus on consolidating final consonants in CVC words and short carrier phrases; aim for greater consistency before increasing complexity.
- Continue work on /s/ in isolation and a small word set, prioritizing accuracy over quantity.

notes/4/note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother noted that his preschool teacher mentioned he is a bit easier to understand during structured activities, especially when labeling pictures. At home, she hears more clear "up," "cup," and "hot" productions. She has not noticed much change with his "s" words yet.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client appeared well-rested and cooperative. He quickly joined in the planned games and followed simple directions with minimal prompting. Attention span for table-top tasks was slightly improved; he tolerated short drill segments followed by play.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants in CVC words and short phrases
- Targeted final /p, t, m/ and introduced a few /n/ and /k/ words in CVC form. At the imitated word level, he demonstrated increased consistency, producing many final consonants accurately with only light verbal cues ("remember the last sound"). In simple carrier phrases ("my cup," "big hat," "mom’s map"), he was able to maintain some final consonants, though accuracy decreased as phrase length increased. Notably fewer vowel additions at the end of words today.

2. Final consonant minimal pairs
- Continued work with minimal pairs to highlight presence vs. absence of final consonants (e.g., "bee"/"beep," "see"/"seat," "no"/"nose"). Client showed strong listening skills and was quick to identify the word spoken by the clinician. When producing the pairs, he showed emerging ability to contrast them himself, particularly with /p/ and /m/.

3. Initial /s/ at syllable and word level
- Practiced /s/ in isolation and moved into CV syllables ("see," "soo") and a small set of words (sun, soup, sock). Compared to prior sessions, there was a slight increase in accurate /s/ productions when given a clear model and visual cue for tongue placement. He occasionally self-corrected after an inaccurate attempt when reminded of the “snake sound.”

4. Functional 3-word phrases
- Modeled short 3-word phrases in play ("I want bubbles," "I see car," "more big blocks"). Client imitated many of these with fair intelligibility and showed more frequent spontaneous 2–3 word combinations, though speech sound errors persisted.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to strengthen final consonants at the word and short phrase level, including /n/ and /k/ as tolerated.
- Maintain practice of initial /s/ in CV syllables and high-frequency words.
- Caregiver to keep playing listening games at home ("do you hear the last sound?") and reinforce clear productions.
- Next session: if consistency with final consonants remains strong, consider expanding phrase work and monitoring for carryover into more spontaneous speech.
notes/5/note_text:

- Continued practice with final /p, t, m, n, k/ in CVC words and simple phrases ("my cup," "big pan," "mom’s book"). His performance remained consistent with prior sessions: many accurate final consonant productions in imitation with light cueing, with more difficulty maintaining accuracy in longer or faster phrases.

- Used minimal pair picture sets to highlight meaning differences (e.g., "bow"/"boat," "bee"/"beep"). Client continued to show good auditory discrimination and was beginning to comment when he "forgot" the last sound after clinician feedback, suggesting growing awareness.

Performance remained stable compared to recent sessions: he produced many accurate final consonants in imitation with light cueing, with more variability in longer or faster utterances. Some spontaneous uses of clear final consonants were observed in play.

2. Final consonant generalization in play
- Embedded final consonant practice in child-led play (e.g., cars, animals). When cued to repeat key words, he was often able to add or clarify final consonants. In spontaneous speech, omissions were still present but did not appear to limit his willingness to communicate.

3. Initial /s/ in a small functional set
- Practiced initial /s/ in a limited set of words and short phrases (sun, sock, soup, sit; "sit down," "sock on"). Accuracy and cueing needs were similar to previous weeks. He produced a handful of clearer /s/ sounds in imitation with visual support.

4. Multiword phrases and intelligibility supports
- Modeled and elicited 3–4 word phrases during routines. Client imitated these readily and used many spontaneous 2–3 word utterances. Introduced simple strategies such as slowing down and looking at the listener when repeating.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue weekly therapy focusing on consolidation and gradual generalization of final consonant skills into more natural contexts.
- Maintain focused practice on a small, functional set of /s/ words and phrases with strong visual cues.
- Caregiver to keep modeling clear final sounds and support communication repair by encouraging slower, repeated attempts when needed.
- Next block: consider formal re-evaluation of overall intelligibility and potential adjustment of targets (e.g., expanding to additional sound classes) depending on progress and functional needs.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that she is still finding it hard to understand him if she doesn’t already know what he is talking about. She notices he leaves off the ends of a lot of words and often says “doo” or “guh” for many things.
- No major changes since the evaluation; family has started trying to slow him down and ask him to "say it again" at home.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client transitioned with some hesitation but entered the room with encouragement and a preferred toy.
- Once seated, he engaged well in play-based tasks and responded to visual supports. Attention was intermittent; needed occasional redirection back to the task when toys became too exciting.

Therapy goals and observations:
1. Final consonant production (CVC words: /p, t, m/)
- Targeted at the imitated word level using picture cards (e.g., "cap, cup, map, mat, mop, boat, feet").
- Frequently omitted final consonants or replaced them with a neutral vowel (e.g., "ca" for "cap").
- With simultaneous productions and maximal verbal cueing ("keep your sound at the end"), produced final consonants in ~3–4 clear trials across the session, but accuracy was inconsistent.
- Requires continued practice in imitation; not yet ready to move to phrases.

2. Initial /k/ in isolation and syllables
- Practiced "back sound" /k/ in isolation with mirror and tongue-back visual.
- Able to approximate /k/ in isolation in about half of attempts when given strong verbal and visual cues ("tongue up and back"). Many productions drifted to /t/.
- Added simple CV syllables ("ku, ki, ko"); accuracy low, with frequent fronting to /t/.

3. Early syllable shapes (CV, CVCV)
- Practiced simple CV ("moo, bee, key") and CVCV ("mama, dada, baby") structures in play.
- CV shapes were generally clear and easy for him. CVCV words were produced but often with reduced clarity or merged syllables.
- Benefited from clapping and tapping each syllable.

4. Spontaneous functional words in play
- Encouraged use of functional words ("more, go, up, open, mine") during play-based routines.
- Used a small set of single words spontaneously ("go, up, mine, ball"), though many were distorted.
- Needed direct models to expand beyond single-word level.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue focusing on final consonant production in CVC words at the imitated level with high support.
- Maintain work on establishing /k/ in isolation before expanding to more syllables.
- Encourage parents to model slow, clear CVC words in daily routines (e.g., "cup, bed, cat") and praise any attempts at final sounds without demanding perfect accuracy.
- Next session: repeat similar targets to establish a consistent baseline and build comfort with therapy routines.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother shared that he has started to repeat some words more clearly when she asks, "say it slowly," especially at bedtime reading.
- She still notices that word endings are missing most of the time.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Smooth transition into therapy room today; greeted clinician with eye contact and a quiet "hi" after a model.
- Stayed with structured activities for longer periods than last session, though still needed prompts when switching games.

Therapy goals and observations:
1. Final consonant production (CVC words: /p, t, m/)
- Practiced minimal pairs contrasting presence/absence of final consonant (e.g., "bee" vs. "beep," "see" vs. "seat").
- At the imitated word level with moderate to maximal verbal/visual cues, he produced final consonants more consistently than last week. Several short runs of 4–5 correct productions in a row were observed.
- Still omits endings spontaneously, but there is emerging awareness; occasionally paused and attempted a self-correction when cued ("Oh! Add your sound at the end").

2. Initial /k/ in isolation and simple syllables
- Continued mirror work and "back sound" explanation.
- Accuracy of /k/ in isolation improved compared to last session; several clear /k/ sounds produced in a row when given direct models and gestural cues.
- In CV syllables ("ku, ki, ko"), he approximated /k/ in about half of attempts, with fewer substitutions to /t/.

3. Initial /k/ at word level (emerging)
- Briefly introduced simple words ("car, key, cow") in imitation.
- Needed maximal modeling and simultaneous production; still primarily at the emerging level with frequent fronting ("tar" for "car").
- A few close approximations noted (e.g., "kar" after a slowed model and tactile cue).

4. Functional words and short phrases
- Modeled simple two-word combinations in play ("more bubbles," "my car," "go fast").
- He imitated several two-word phrases with variable clarity but increasing willingness to try.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue intensive practice on final consonants at the imitated CVC level; consider adding a small set of highly familiar words for home practice.
- Maintain focus on /k/ in isolation and CV syllables; only brief, supported exposure to word-level /k/ at this time.
- Home: parent to practice 5–10 CVC words daily using a slow model and visual cue (e.g., tapping for final sound). Emphasize effort over accuracy.

notes/2/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:

- Very engaged with turn-taking activities; some silliness and sound play, but this was used productively to practice targets.

Therapy goals and observations:
1. Final consonant production (CVC words: /p, t, m, n/)
- Practiced at the imitated word level using picture cards and a simple board game.
- Accuracy for final consonants showed meaningful improvement; with moderate verbal and visual cues, he produced many CVC words with clear endings.
- Demonstrated short phrases like "my cap," "big cat" with the final consonant maintained in imitation.
- Required less repetition per word to achieve a correct production compared to previous sessions, suggesting increasing stability.

2. Initial /k/ at word level
- Focused on a small set of words ("car, key, cow, cookie") at the imitated word level.
- With mirror, tongue-back cue, and slowed rate, he produced recognizable /k/ in initial position for several trials, though still inconsistent.
- Fewer substitutions to /t/ were observed; he often attempted to fix it when clinician modeled the difference.
- Appears ready to continue structured practice at the word level.

3. Syllable shapes: CVCV and multisyllabic play words
- Practiced CVCV ("mama, dada, bunny") and some early 3-syllable words ("banana," "elephant") through songs and play.
- Most syllables were present, though some consonants remained imprecise.
- Benefited from tapping or clapping each beat; responded well to rhythmic cues.

4. Two-word combinations
- Modeled and elicited two-word phrases related to play ("my turn," "more cars," "big truck").
- He imitated two-word phrases frequently and produced a few spontaneously, though speech clarity varied.

- Maintain /k/ at word level with intensive cueing; begin tracking a small core set of /k/ words for home practice.

notes/3/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother shared that his endings are "popping out" more often, especially on words like "cup" and "bed" when he's excited.
- She also reported hearing a clearer "k" in a few words such as "car" and "cookie" when practicing together.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Cheerful on arrival; walked to the room independently.
- Maintained attention well in turn-taking games, and responded positively to praise for "strong ending sounds" and "back sounds."

Therapy goals and observations:
1. Final consonant production (CVC words; expanding consonant set)
- Targeted CVC words with /p, t, m, n, k/ in final position during a fishing game.
- At the imitated level with primarily verbal cues and occasional visual prompts, he produced final consonants with noticeably higher consistency than last week.
- Able to maintain final consonants in short imitated phrases ("big cup," "my cat," "in bed").
- Began to require fewer reminders to "keep the sound at the end," indicating increased independence.

2. Initial /k/ at word and carrier phrase level
- Practiced a core set of /k/ words ("car, key, cow, cookie, cat") in imitation.
- Demonstrated a marked breakthrough in producing initial /k/ correctly with moderate verbal cues and visual modeling; accuracy clearly higher than in previous sessions.
- Briefly introduced simple carrier phrases ("my car," "big cow," "cookie please") in imitation. He was able to keep the /k/ sound in some short phrases when cued to slow down.

3. Syllable shapes and multisyllabic words
- Practiced 2–3 syllable words ("banana, spider, dinosaur") within play.
- Most syllables were present; some consonant distortions remained, but overall word shapes were more consistent.
- He enjoyed tapping each syllable and often requested to "do it again."

4. Two- to three-word combinations
- Expanded work on two-word phrases and trialed some three-word models ("more blue car," "I want cookie").
- He imitated many of these combinations, with clearer productions when speech rate was slowed.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to build on gains with initial /k/ at the word and short phrase level; gradually reduce cueing as tolerated.
- Maintain practice of final consonants in familiar CVC words and short phrases to support generalization.
- Home: parents to practice 5–10 /k/ words and a few CVC words daily, using simple phrases ("my car," "big cat"). Praise effort and any clear endings or /k/ sounds.
- Next session: consider adding another target sound class if consistency with /k/ and final consonants remains strong.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:

- She has noticed more attempts at /k/ in words like "car" and "cookie," though it is still not always clear.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Transitioned smoothly; briefly checked in with mother at the door, then moved into therapy room.
- Participated well in structured games; needed some breaks between tasks but returned to work with simple prompts.

Therapy goals and observations:
1. Final consonant production (CVC words and short phrases)
- Continued practice with CVC words including /p, t, m, n, k/ in final position.
- At the imitated word level, he maintained a generally good level of accuracy, similar to last session. Some variability in final /k/ remained.
- In short imitated phrases ("red cup," "big cat"), he sometimes dropped the final consonant when speech rate increased.
- Overall performance suggests consolidation of gains rather than a large new change this week.

2. Initial /k/ in words and short phrases
- Repeated core /k/ word set ("car, key, cow, cookie, cat") using picture cards and a matching game.
- Accuracy remained fairly strong with verbal and visual cues, consistent with previous session.
- In short carrier phrases ("my car," "more cookie"), he often kept the /k/ when given a slow model; some phrases still showed fronting or sound omission.

3. Introduction of /s/ in isolation and simple syllables
- Introduced "snake sound" /s/ in isolation using mirror and visual cue (teeth together, long airflow).
- With maximal cueing and simultaneous production, he approximated /s/ in several trials, though productions were inconsistent and sometimes sounded more like /sh/ or /t/.
- Began simple CV/VC combinations ("see, so, us"), with emerging accuracy.

4. Short functional phrases
- Encouraged use of simple requests and comments ("my turn," "I want ___," "more please") within play routines.
- He imitated several functional phrases and produced a few independently; clarity varied but overall length of utterances is increasing.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue consolidating final consonant and initial /k/ gains at word and short phrase level; maintain similar level of support.
- Gradually increase practice with /s/ in isolation and simple syllables, keeping expectations low while the sound is being established.
- Home: practice "slow talking" with CVC and /k/ words during daily routines; introduce a "snake sound" game with /s/ in front of a mirror a few times per week.
- Next session: repeat similar targets and reassess readiness to expand /s/ work depending on stability of productions.
notes/5/note_text:

notes/6/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
