notes/0/note_text:
Session 1

Parent report:
- Caregiver shared that they are most concerned about how hard it is for others to understand Child in conversation. Noted that Child often leaves off ending sounds and has trouble with “s” sounds in words like “sun” and “spoon.” No changes in health or hearing reported.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child separated from caregiver with brief hesitation, then transitioned into the therapy room with encouragement.
- Engaged well with toys and books, preferred to lead play but responded to structured tasks with gentle redirection.
- Attention to task was fair; needed frequent reminders to look at the clinician’s mouth when practicing sounds.

Goals / therapy activities:
1. Establish baseline for final consonant production (CVC words)
- Probed final /p, t, k, m, n/ in simple words (e.g., “cup, cat, cake, mom, sun”) in imitation.
- Child frequently omitted final consonants or replaced them with a neutral vowel (e.g., “cu-,” “ca-”).
- Occasional accurate final /m/ and /n/ in familiar words with maximal verbal and visual cueing (e.g., “keep your sound on at the end,” pointing to mouth).

2. Initial /s/ at word level (imitation)
- Introduced “snake sound” /s/ in isolation using mirror and visual cue (smile, teeth together, long airflow).
- Child approximated /s/ in isolation inconsistently; productions were often closer to /d/ or a distorted /s/ with tongue too far forward.
- In simple words (e.g., “sun, soup, sock”), accuracy was low; required maximal modeling and tactile cue (finger along forearm for “long sound”).

3. Initial /f/ at syllable and word level (imitation)
- Practiced /f/ in isolation using “lip-bite, quiet air” cue.
- Child produced /f/ in isolation in a few trials with direct imitation; often substituted /p/ or omitted airflow.
- In simple words (“fish, four, fun”), Child needed continuous modeling, visual cue in mirror, and slowed rate.

4. Syllable shapes (CV, CVCV)
- Observed spontaneous productions during play (e.g., “mama,” “nana,” “baba,” “uh-oh”).
- CV and reduplicated CVCV shapes (e.g., “dada, baba”) were frequent; limited use of alternating CVCV shapes (e.g., “baby”) without model.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to build awareness of final sounds and “long” sounds (/s/, /f/) at the imitated word level.
- Encourage caregiver to model clear final sounds in daily routines (e.g., emphasize “cup,” “bed,” “up”) without asking Child to repeat every time.
- Next session: further probe final consonants and initial /s/ and /f/; consider using picture cards and simple games to increase motivation and consistency.
notes/1/note_text:
Session 2

Parent report:
- Caregiver reported no major changes since the initial visit. Noted that Child has been “copying” some words more at home, especially during book reading, but still often drops the ends of words.
- No new medical or hearing concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned into the room more smoothly today, separating from caregiver with minimal prompting.
- Remained engaged in turn-taking games and tolerated brief structured practice interspersed with play.
- Required occasional redirection when tasks became more challenging (tended to switch topics or move to another toy).

Goals / therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words (imitation)
- Targeted final /m, n, p/ in picture naming and play (e.g., “gum, moon, cup, map”).
- With maximal verbal cueing (“keep your sound on at the end”) and visual cue (tapping a dot for the final sound), Child produced some accurate final /m/ and /n/ in imitation.
- Final /p/ remained more difficult; often omitted or replaced with a glottal stop.

2. Initial /s/ in isolation and simple syllables
- Reviewed “snake sound” with mirror. Child showed slightly more consistent airflow but continued to place tongue too far forward at times.
- In CV syllables (e.g., “see, soo, sah”), Child achieved a few clear /s/ productions with maximal modeling and slowing.
- Still not yet ready to move to phrase level; accuracy remains variable.

3. Initial /f/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Continued “lip-bite, quiet air” practice. Child produced /f/ in isolation more reliably when given a visual model and reminded to “bite lip, blow.”
- In CV syllables (“fee, foo, fah”), Child had some emerging accurate productions with imitation, though still frequently backed to /p/.

4. Syllable shapes (CVCV alternating)
- Practiced words like “baby, bunny, puppy” in play.
- Child imitated these words with partial accuracy; often reduced to a simpler form (e.g., “bibi,” “pup”).
- Needed models and slowing to include both syllables.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to focus on final consonant production and initial /s/ and /f/ at the imitated word and syllable level.
- Caregiver encouraged to highlight final sounds in key words during routines (e.g., “up, in, on”) and to play simple “snake sound” games in front of a mirror.
- Next session: maintain current targets; monitor for any spontaneous use of final consonants or clearer /s/ and /f/ sounds.
notes/2/note_text:
Session 3

Parent report:
- Caregiver noted that Child has started to say “up” and “in” more clearly at times, especially when asking for help. Reported that endings are still often missing in longer words.
- No concerns regarding hearing, sleep, or appetite.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered the therapy room willingly and appeared eager to show toys.
- Demonstrated good participation in turn-based activities (e.g., puzzle, car ramp) and accepted structured practice when embedded in play.
- Attention was improved; Child was able to remain on a single task for several minutes with minimal redirection.

Goals / therapy activities:
1. Final consonants in CVC words (imitation, emerging spontaneous use)
- Targeted final /m, n, p, t/ in words such as “sun, moon, cup, map, hat, cat.”
- With moderate verbal and visual cues (tapping for final sound, slow rate), Child produced clearer final /m/ and /n/ in most imitated trials.
- Some spontaneous attempts at final consonants were heard in play (e.g., “up,” “in,” “on”), though not consistently.
- Final /p/ and /t/ remained more variable; Child occasionally added a vowel after the consonant (e.g., “cup-uh”).

2. Initial /f/ in syllables and single words (imitation)
- Practiced CV syllables (“fee, foo, fi”) and simple words (“fish, four, fun”) with mirror.
- Child demonstrated more consistent /f/ in imitation when reminded to bite lower lip and blow gently.
- Accuracy was noticeably higher than previous sessions at the syllable level; still occasional substitution with /p/ in words without visual cue.

3. Initial /s/ in syllables and simple words (imitation)
- Continued “snake sound” work in CV syllables and high-interest words (“sun, soap, sock”).
- Child showed slightly increased accuracy in CV syllables, especially when watching the clinician’s mouth.
- In words, Child often approximated /s/ but sometimes used /t/ or a distorted /s/; needed frequent verbal and gestural cueing.

4. CVCV alternating syllable words
- Practiced words like “bunny, baby, cookie” during play.
- Child imitated these with greater success when the clinician tapped out each syllable; still occasional syllable reduction.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain focus on final consonants and initial /f/ and /s/ at the imitated word level, as emerging improvements are observed.
- Encourage caregiver to reinforce final consonants in daily phrases (e.g., “put it in,” “pick up”) and to praise Child’s attempts at complete words.
- Next session: consider gradually increasing complexity (e.g., short carrier phrases for words with more reliable final consonants) if consistency continues to improve.

- Final /p/ and /t/ showed improved accuracy in single words when given a visual cue (touching chin for “stop” sound); still some omissions in phrases.

2. Initial /f/ at word level (imitation, beginning independence)

1. Final consonants in CVC words and short sentences

- Child demonstrated noticeable improvement in maintaining final /m/ and /n/ across words and into simple sentences with only light reminders.
- Final /p/ and /t/ were more accurate in structured tasks, with fewer omissions; occasional drops still occurred in faster or more spontaneous speech.

2. Initial /f/ in sentences and semi-spontaneous speech
- Practiced /f/-initial words in short sentences (“The fish is fun,” “Four frogs jump”) and during play-based conversation.
- Child produced initial /f/ accurately in most imitated sentences and was often able to use clear /f/ in more spontaneous comments during play (e.g., “fun food,” “my foot”).
- Required minimal cueing; independence with this sound is increasing.

3. Initial /s/ in words, phrases, and early sentences
- Targeted /s/ in words (“sun, soup, sock, sit, sad”) and in short phrases and sentences (“I see sun,” “sad face,” “sit down”).
- Child showed moderate, meaningful progress with /s/ accuracy in imitation, especially when using mirror and “smile, teeth together” cues.
- Began to maintain /s/ in short sentences in structured tasks, though spontaneous carryover remains emerging.

4. Multisyllabic words in short phrases
- Practiced “banana, teddy bear, cookie, pizza, umbrella” in phrases (“yellow banana,” “big cookie,” “open umbrella”).
- Child was able to mark most syllables when given a model and tapping; syllable reduction decreased compared to earlier sessions.

Plan / recommendations:
- Given the progress, appropriate to expand sentence-level work for final consonants and /f/ and to continue building /s/ from words into simple sentences.
- Caregiver encouraged to model short, clear sentences featuring target sounds (e.g., “I see sun,” “four fish swim”) and to reinforce Child’s self-corrections.
- Next session: continue sentence-level practice for /f/ and final /m, n/; maintain phrase/sentence practice for /s/ with ongoing cueing.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported ongoing concerns about intelligibility, especially with unfamiliar listeners. She noted that he is "chatty" at home but can be hard to understand, particularly for words with /s/ and /f/. No new medical or developmental concerns reported.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client transitioned to the therapy room with brief hesitation but separated from caregiver with reassurance. He was curious and engaged in play-based activities, needing occasional redirection from preferred toys back to speech tasks. Tolerated structured turn-taking games.

Therapy goals and observations:
1. Establish /f/ in isolation and simple syllables
- Targeted /f/ sound in isolation using mirror and visual cueing for "bite lip, blow air." Client was able to approximate /f/ with clear airflow in about half of opportunities, but often substituted /p/ or omitted airflow. In CV syllables ("fo," "fee"), productions were largely inaccurate without direct imitation. He responded best to slow models and exaggerated lip positioning.

2. Initial /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Practiced /s/ in isolation with visual and auditory models. Client frequently produced /t/ or /d/ in place of /s/. With maximal verbal cueing ("long, quiet snake sound") and some tactile cues to keep tongue behind teeth, he produced a recognizable /s/ intermittently. In simple CV syllables ("see," "soo"), accuracy was low; he tended to stop the sound or shorten it.

3. Simple CVC words with final consonants (general consonant marking)
- Used picture cards and play-based routines (e.g., "pop," "bed," "map") to probe final consonant production. Client frequently omitted final consonants, especially in spontaneous attempts. With direct imitation and segmenting ("ma-p"), he marked some final sounds but was inconsistent and required significant support.

4. Baseline probe of multisyllabic word production
- Briefly sampled 2–3 syllable words during play ("banana," "dinosaur," "potato"). Client generally produced a reduced syllable shape (e.g., "nana" for "banana") and did not yet respond to syllable-tapping cues.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to build awareness and accuracy of /f/ and /s/ at the isolation and simple syllable level with high support.
- Maintain focus on marking final consonants in high-interest CVC words.
- Provide parent with simple modeling strategies (slow speech, emphasizing final sounds) during shared book reading.
- Next session: re-check /f/ and /s/ in isolation, consider introducing a small set of high-frequency CVC words for more structured practice.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother noted no major changes since last week. She reported that he is "trying hard" to copy her when she stretches sounds but that it's still difficult for others to understand him. No concerns with hearing or health this week.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client entered the room more easily today and appeared comfortable with the routine. He was playful and occasionally silly, which sometimes interfered with accurate imitation. With gentle prompts and visual timers, he returned to tasks and participated well in short, structured activities.

Therapy goals and observations:
1. /f/ in isolation and CV/CVC syllables
- Reviewed /f/ in isolation using mirror and visual cues. Client produced a clearer /f/ more consistently when reminded to "bite lip and blow," showing better control of airflow compared to last session. In CV combinations ("fee," "foo"), accuracy improved with imitation. He was able to produce a few CVC words ("feet," "fun") with partial accuracy, though he often reduced to CV or substituted /p/ initially.

2. Initial /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Continued work on /s/ in isolation. Client demonstrated more frequent recognizable /s/ sounds when given a slow model and cue to keep tongue behind teeth. Still tended to shorten the sound or shift to /t/ without cues, but there were more correct attempts than last week. In simple CV syllables ("see," "sue"), he had a few accurate productions with maximal modeling.

3. CVC words with final consonants
- Practiced high-interest CVC words ("pop," "bus," "cup") in a turn-taking game. Client continued to omit final consonants in spontaneous attempts but showed emerging ability to mark some final sounds during imitation. Needed segmented models ("cu-p") and visual cueing (pointing to end of word) to support final consonant production.

4. Early multisyllabic words (2-syllable focus)
- Briefly revisited 2-syllable words ("baby," "nana," "spider") with clapping for each beat. Client began to tolerate clapping and could sometimes produce both syllables when strongly cued, though consonant clarity remained limited.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue current targets; emerging improvement noted in /f/ and /s/ accuracy with high levels of cueing.
- Encourage caregiver to model slow, stretched productions of target words during play, praising any attempts.
- Next session: maintain focus on /f/ and /s/ in CV/CVC contexts and continue practice with a small set of CVC words emphasizing final consonants.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that he is starting to "copy" her snake sound at home when she reminds him, particularly during book reading. She feels he is a little easier to understand for family members but still unclear in longer words. No new concerns reported.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client transitioned smoothly and appeared eager to show toys. He engaged readily in structured play and accepted therapist-led activities with minimal protest. Attention to task was better sustained today, with fewer off-topic behaviors and good response to visual supports.

Therapy goals and observations:
1. /f/ in initial position at word level
- Targeted initial /f/ in a small set of words ("fish," "foot," "fun," "four") at the imitated word level. Client produced a clear /f/ at the beginning of these words in many attempts when given a single model and brief verbal cue ("bite lip, blow"). Fewer substitutions to /p/ were observed compared to previous sessions. Accuracy was noticeably higher, and he began to self-correct on a few trials when he heard himself say /p/.

2. Initial /s/ in CV and CVC words
- Practiced /s/ in CV and early CVC words ("see," "so," "sun," "soap") using picture cards and a simple board game. Client showed increased consistency in producing a sustained /s/ at the start of words with visual cueing. Still needed frequent reminders to keep tongue behind teeth, but he required fewer repetitions of the model to achieve a recognizable /s/.

3. CVC words with final consonants (high-frequency set)
- Continued work on final consonants using a limited set of functional words ("pop," "bus," "cup," "bed"). Client marked final consonants more often in imitation today and occasionally attempted to mark them spontaneously during the game. Errors included sound omissions and devoicing. He benefited from slow, segmented models and pointing to the end of the picture.

4. Two-syllable words with movement cues
- Introduced a short list of 2-syllable words ("spider," "happy," "daddy") using tapping on the table for each syllable. Client was able to produce both syllables in several trials with strong modeling, though consonant accuracy remained inconsistent. He seemed to enjoy the tapping routine and followed along.

Plan/recommendations:
- Given moderate gains, continue to strengthen /f/ initial at the word level and expand the word list.
- Maintain work on initial /s/ in CV/CVC words with consistent cues, and continue practicing final consonants in a small set of functional words.
- Encourage parent to use the "tap and say" strategy for some 2-syllable words at home.
- Next session: consider trialing simple carrier phrases for /f/ words if accuracy remains strong.

notes/8/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that his /f/ words remain clear and that she is hearing more attempts at /s/ in words like "sun" and "sock" at home, though they are not always accurate. She feels his endings are still hard to hear. No changes in health or routines.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client arrived calm and cooperative. He was engaged with the materials and followed the visual schedule with minimal prompting. Attention was adequate throughout, and he responded positively to verbal praise for specific sound productions.

Therapy goals and observations:
1. /f/ initial in words and short phrases (maintenance)
- Practiced familiar /f/ words and phrases ("funny fish," "four feet," "my food") during structured play. Client maintained good accuracy for /f/ at the word level and continued to produce /f/ correctly in many short phrases with minimal cueing. Occasional errors were present when he spoke quickly, but overall performance was similar to last week.

2. Initial /s/ in CVC words and short phrases
- Continued work on /s/ in CVC words ("sun," "soap," "sock," "sand," "seat") and short phrases ("my sock," "hot sun"). Client's performance was consistent with the previous session: he produced /s/ accurately in a fair number of imitated words and required fewer repetitions of the model than earlier in the block. However, he still needed regular verbal reminders for tongue placement and to stretch the sound, particularly as tasks became more complex.

3. Final consonant production in CVC words
- Revisited final consonants in familiar words ("bus," "cup," "bed," "map," "cat"). Accuracy was largely stable. Client continued to omit final consonants in many spontaneous productions but was able to mark them more reliably with segmented models and slow speech. There was no clear shift in independence compared to the prior session.

4. Two-syllable and early 3-syllable words (imitation)
- Practiced 2-syllable words ("spider," "happy," "daddy") and again attempted "banana" with tapping. Client consistently produced both syllables in 2-syllable words when imitating the clinician and showed partial success with 3-syllable productions. Consonant clarity remained variable, but syllable structure awareness appeared stable.

Plan/recommendations:
- Skills this week appear similar to last session, suggesting ongoing consolidation. Continue current targets and cueing levels for /f/, /s/, and final consonants.
- Encourage caregiver to maintain home practice with a small set of /s/ words and to highlight end sounds in daily routines.
- Next session: consider varying activities (e.g., more movement-based tasks) while keeping speech targets the same to support generalization.
notes/9/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother shared that he continues to use his practiced /f/ and some /s/ words at home, but overall intelligibility is still variable in conversation. She has not noticed big changes in the past week but feels he is trying to use his "good sounds." No new concerns reported.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client transitioned easily and engaged with a movement-based obstacle course activity. He was generally cooperative, though he occasionally rushed through his turns, which affected clarity. With reminders to "slow down and use your good sound," he was able to participate well.

Therapy goals and observations:
1. /f/ initial in words and short phrases (maintenance/generalization)
- Practiced /f/ words and short phrases within the obstacle course (e.g., say a target phrase before moving to the next station). Client maintained consistent accuracy with /f/ at the word level and in many phrases, even with the added motor component. Occasional reductions to /p/ still occurred when he was excited, but he responded well to prompts to repeat more slowly.

2. Initial /s/ in CVC words and short phrases
- Targeted /s/ in CVC words ("sun," "soap," "sock," "sand," "seat") and short phrases ("see sun," "my sock") embedded in the course. Performance was similar to prior sessions: he produced some accurate /s/ sounds in imitation and with a single model, but continued to require regular cueing for tongue placement and sustained airflow. Consistency decreased slightly when movement demands were added.

3. Final consonant production in CVC words
- Practiced final consonants in familiar words ("bus," "cup," "bed," "cat") using a picture-matching game at the table. Accuracy remained comparable to previous weeks. Client marked some final consonants with segmented models but often omitted them in more spontaneous speech. He tolerated cues but did not yet show clear independent carryover.

4. Two-syllable and 3-syllable words
- Revisited 2-syllable words ("spider," "happy") and "banana" with tapping. Client continued to produce both syllables in 2-syllable words with modeling and partial productions of 3-syllable words. No notable change from prior sessions.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to consolidate gains with /f/ and /s/ at the word and phrase levels, particularly focusing on accuracy when he is excited or moving.
- Maintain targeted practice on final consonants, emphasizing slow speech and clear end sounds.
- Encourage caregiver to incorporate short practice into movement games at home (e.g., say a target word before jumping).
- Next session: keep current targets and monitor for any emerging changes in independence or consistency.
notes/10/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that he is still using clear /f/ words and is starting to occasionally correct his /s/ when she reminds him (e.g., trying again for "sun"). She continues to feel that word endings are difficult to hear. No new concerns or changes reported.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client entered willingly and appeared familiar with the routine. He engaged in a mix of table-top and floor play tasks, with good attention when activities were brief and varied. He responded to specific feedback and seemed proud when his productions were praised.

Therapy goals and observations:
1. /f/ initial in words and short phrases (maintenance)
- Practiced /f/ words and short phrases ("funny fish," "four feet," "my food") within a picture sequencing activity. Client continued to demonstrate consistent accuracy for /f/ in initial position, both in single words and short phrases, with minimal cueing. Occasional errors were present but were infrequent and easily corrected.

2. Initial /s/ in CVC words and short phrases
- Continued /s/ work in CVC words ("sun," "soap," "sock," "seat," "sand") and short phrases ("big sun," "my sock") using a board game. Performance remained similar to recent sessions: he produced many accurate /s/ sounds in imitation and with a single verbal or gestural cue, but still required consistent support to avoid /t/ substitutions and to maintain airflow. A few spontaneous self-corrections were noted.

3. Final consonant production in CVC words
- Targeted final consonants in familiar words ("bus," "cup," "bed," "cat") during a turn-taking game. Accuracy was stable. Client marked some final sounds when the clinician modeled and segmented the word but continued to omit them in more independent attempts. No substantial change in cueing needs was observed.

4. Two-syllable and early 3-syllable words
- Practiced 2-syllable words ("spider," "happy," "daddy") and "banana" with tapping. Client maintained his ability to produce both syllables with modeling, with similar patterns of simplification. He enjoyed the rhythmic aspect and participated consistently.

Plan/recommendations:
- Skills appear to be holding steady. Continue current focus on /f/, /s/, and final consonants with an emphasis on building independence and consistency.
- Encourage caregiver to continue highlighting /s/ and final sounds at home and to praise any self-corrections.
- Next session (end of block): review overall progress and discuss potential next steps and updated goals with caregiver.
notes/11/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that overall, she feels his speech is clearer than when therapy began, especially for /f/ and some /s/ words. She continues to notice difficulty with certain sounds and endings, particularly in longer sentences, but feels he is making steady progress. No new health concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client transitioned easily and appeared comfortable and familiar with therapy routines. He participated well in structured and play-based tasks and was receptive to feedback. He demonstrated good stamina for the full session with brief movement breaks.

Therapy goals and observations (end-of-block summary session):
1. /f/ initial in words and short phrases
- Reviewed a variety of /f/ initial words ("fish," "food," "four," "fun," "farm") and short phrases ("my fish," "four feet," "fun food") across games and picture description. Client consistently produced /f/ accurately at the word level and in short phrases with minimal to no cueing. Occasional errors were self-corrected. Overall, /f/ in initial position is emerging as a relative strength and is approaching readiness for more spontaneous use in conversation.

2. Initial /s/ in CVC words and short phrases
- Probed /s/ in a broader set of CVC words ("sun," "soap," "sock," "sand," "seat," "soup") and short phrases ("big sun," "my sock," "see soap") using a board game and picture scenes. Client demonstrated improved consistency compared to the beginning of the block, with many accurate /s/ productions in imitation and with reduced cueing. He still needed reminders about tongue placement and airflow, especially as phrases became longer, but he showed more frequent correct productions and some independent attempts.

3. Final consonant production in CVC words
- Reassessed final consonants using familiar words ("bus," "cup," "bed," "map," "cat") and a few new items. Client continued to omit final consonants in a significant portion of spontaneous productions, but could mark them more reliably when the clinician provided segmented models and slowed speech. Independence remains limited at this level, and this area will likely remain a key target moving forward.

4. Two- and three-syllable word structure
- Reviewed 2-syllable words ("spider," "happy," "daddy") and 3-syllable words ("banana," "umbrella") with tapping and visual supports. Client was generally able to mark both syllables in 2-syllable words with imitation and showed partial success with 3-syllable words, though consonant clarity varied. Awareness of syllable structure has improved since baseline, but productions remain at the imitated level.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue speech-language therapy to support ongoing development of speech sound production.
- Next block of therapy should focus on: (1) maintaining and generalizing accurate /f/ in conversation; (2) increasing independence and consistency for initial /s/ at the phrase and early sentence levels; (3) more intensive work on final consonant production in functional CVC words; and (4) continued support for multi-syllable word production.
- Recommend caregiver continue home practice with a small set of /f/ and /s/ words and phrases, emphasizing slow, clear models and praising attempts and self-corrections. Provide caregiver with a brief written summary of current targets and suggested home activities.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver shared that child is “hard to understand” for unfamiliar listeners and often leaves off sounds at the ends of words. No major changes since initial evaluation.
- Family is most concerned about clarity of speech as child prepares for starting kindergarten next year.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned to therapy room with brief hesitation, then engaged well once preferred toys were available.
- Attended to adult-directed tasks for short bursts (3–4 minutes) with visual supports and frequent play breaks.
- Imitation was better during table-top games than during free play.

Goals / session targets:
1) Establish /k/ and /g/ in isolation and simple syllables
- Initial probing revealed consistent fronting of /k, g/ to /t, d/ (e.g., “tea” for “key”).
- With direct instruction and use of a tongue depressor for awareness only (no pressure), child was able to approximate /k/ in isolation in ~3/10 attempts; productions were often backed but still somewhat distorted.
- Imitated simple CV syllables (e.g., “koo,” “kee”) with maximal verbal and visual cues; accuracy was low and required repeated modeling.

2) Final consonant production (CVC words) in imitation
- Child frequently omitted final consonants in conversation and play (e.g., “boo” for “book,” “ca” for “cat”).
- At the imitated word level with picture cards, child produced final /p, m, t/ in CVC words with frequent omission; about half of attempts were approximated when given direct modeling plus a visual cue (pointing to the “end” of the word).
- Needed segmented practice (saying the final sound alone, then blending) and frequent repetition.

3) Initial /s/ in isolation and syllables
- Initial probe showed /s/ was often substituted with /d/ or a lateralized fricative.
- With mirror work and “snake sound” imagery, child approximated /s/ in isolation in a small number of trials, requiring maximal cueing for tongue placement and airflow.
- Limited success with CV syllables (e.g., “see,” “soo”); productions were often distorted or stopped.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to establish /k/ and /s/ in isolation and simple syllables before moving consistently to word level.
- Introduce parent to simple “end sound” tapping strategy for final consonants in short practice bursts at home (1–2 minutes, a few times per day) using familiar words like “cup,” “mom,” “hat.”
- Next session: re-probe /k/ and /s/ in isolation; continue CVC practice with high-interest pictures or toys.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver reported practicing “end sounds” a few times but found it hard to keep child interested. Noted that child sometimes says “up” more clearly now when asking to be picked up.
- No other significant changes reported.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child separated more easily from caregiver today and joined clinician at the table.
- Remained engaged with turn-taking games; occasional silliness but redirected with visual schedules and choice-making.
- Responded well to praise and small rewards (stickers).

Goals / session targets:
1) /k/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Marked improvement in ability to produce a backed sound for /k/ with verbal cues to “keep your tongue in the back.”
- In isolation, child produced a recognizable /k/ in the majority of attempts when watching in the mirror and imitating the clinician.
- Able to imitate CV syllables (“koo, kah, kee”) with moderate cueing; productions were more consistent, though still occasionally fronted.

2) Final consonant production in CVC words (imitation)
- Practiced final /p, t, m/ in familiar words (e.g., “cup, hat, mom, map”).
- Accuracy improved compared to last week; child more consistently attempted the final consonant even when not fully accurate.
- Visual “sound dots” and tapping at the end of the word were helpful; child began to anticipate the final sound with less prompting.

3) Initial /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Continued mirror practice with “snake sound.”
- Child produced clearer /s/ in isolation in more trials than previous session, especially when reminded to keep tongue behind teeth and use a “soft” air stream.
- CV syllables remained challenging but showed some emerging accuracy (occasional correct “see/soo” with heavy modeling).

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to reinforce /k/ in isolation and CVs; begin to introduce a small set of /k/ initial words in imitation next session if accuracy remains similar.
- Maintain practice on CVC words with final /p, t, m/ at home using the tapping strategy; encourage very short, positive practice periods.
- Continue to build consistency for /s/ in isolation before moving more fully to word-level tasks.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver noted child is starting to say “cat” and “cup” with more complete endings during play, though not every time.
- Family reports child likes the “sound dots” activity and asks to do it occasionally.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered room happily and quickly chose preferred toys.
- Maintained attention for slightly longer structured activities today (4–5 minutes) with minimal redirection.
- Imitation was strong when tasks were embedded in play (e.g., feeding animals, car races).

Goals / session targets:
1) /k/ initial at word level (imitation)
- Transitioned from CV syllables to a small set of /k/ initial words (e.g., “key, car, cow, cat, cup”).
- Child imitated these with moderate cueing for tongue placement; accuracy was noticeably better than last week, with most attempts recognizable as /k/.
- Occasional fronting to /t/ persisted but child began to self-correct after a model.

2) Final consonants in CVC words (imitation and emerging spontaneous)
- Practiced final /p, t, m/ with picture cards and simple games.
- In imitation, child produced final consonants more consistently; there were only occasional omissions.
- During play-based conversation, child spontaneously produced some final consonants (e.g., “cup,” “up,” “hat”) without prompting, though not yet reliable.

3) Initial /s/ in isolation and syllables
- Continued work on /s/ in isolation with mirror and “snake sound” cue; child produced a clearer /s/ more frequently with moderate cueing.
- CV syllables (“see, soo, sah”) were attempted with mixed success; some accurate productions emerged when the clinician slowed the rate and emphasized airflow.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue /k/ initial at the imitated word level; consider adding a few more /k/ words as accuracy improves.
- Maintain final consonant practice and begin to highlight when child uses them spontaneously in play to build awareness.
- Keep /s/ practice at isolation and simple syllables; not yet ready to move to consistent word-level work.
- Home: caregiver to practice a few /k/ words in a game format (e.g., hiding “cup, cat, car” pictures and saying the words together).
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver shared that child now often says “cat” and “cup” with clear /k/ and final sounds when reminded.
- Noted that child is starting to notice when others don’t understand and will repeat or try again.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly and appeared excited to show off speech sounds.
- Demonstrated improved persistence with challenging tasks and responded well to verbal praise.
- Occasional fidgeting, but accepted brief movement breaks and returned to the table without difficulty.

Goals / session targets:
1) /k/ initial at word and emerging phrase level
- At the imitated word level, child produced initial /k/ in a variety of words (e.g., “key, car, cow, cookie, cat, cup”) with fairly high consistency.
- Needed fewer verbal cues for tongue backing; occasional fronting occurred but child often corrected after a reminder.
- Trialed simple carrier phrases (“my cat,” “go car”) in imitation; child was able to maintain /k/ in some two-word combinations with moderate support.

2) Final consonants in CVC words (imitation and structured play)
- Practiced final /p, t, m, k/ in picture naming and in a board game.
- In imitation, child produced final consonants reliably across most trials, with only a few omissions.
- During structured play, child spontaneously included final consonants in several target words; accuracy was not perfect but clearly improved.

3) Initial /s/ in isolation and early word attempts
- Continued isolation work; /s/ was more consistent with reduced cueing.
- Introduced a very small set of /s/ initial words (e.g., “sun, soup, sock”) in imitation.
- Child produced a mix of correct and distorted /s/; benefitted from reminders to keep tongue behind teeth and use “gentle air.”

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to strengthen /k/ at the word level and gradually increase use in short phrases as tolerated.
- Maintain final consonant production in both structured tasks and natural play; begin to listen for carryover at home and provide positive feedback when heard.
- Keep initial /s/ at a limited set of words while solidifying accuracy; continue mirror work.
- Home: encourage caregiver to use simple two-word phrases with /k/ (e.g., “go car,” “big cup”) and praise clear productions rather than correcting every error.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver reported that child is now using “car,” “cup,” and “cookie” clearly in daily routines, often without reminders.
- Noted that grandparents commented that child’s words are “a bit clearer” on the phone.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child was cheerful and talkative on arrival.
- Participated well in both table-top and floor-based activities, with good turn-taking.
- Showed increased willingness to try again when a production was difficult.

Goals / session targets:
1) /k/ initial at word and short phrase level
- Child continued to produce initial /k/ in a range of words with good consistency during imitation.
- Expanded to more two- and three-word phrases (e.g., “my car,” “big cat,” “go in car”).
- Needed occasional verbal prompts but was often able to maintain /k/ independently in short phrases.

2) Final consonants in CVC words (structured play and emerging conversation)
- Practiced final /p, t, m, k, n/ within a fishing game and picture naming.
- In structured tasks, child produced final consonants in most attempts; rare omissions were noted.
- In more spontaneous play, child showed increasing use of final consonants in familiar words; intelligibility was noticeably better.

3) Initial /s/ in isolation and limited word set
- Continued with isolation and a small group of /s/ words (“sun, soup, sock, sit”).
- Accuracy of /s/ in isolation was higher and required less cueing; child sometimes produced a clear /s/ when prompted only by a visual cue.
- Word-level productions showed gradual improvement but remained less consistent than /k/ and final consonants.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to consolidate /k/ at phrase level and begin to look for more spontaneous use in play.
- Maintain final consonant practice with a variety of words and encourage caregiver to notice and praise clear endings at home.
- Keep working on initial /s/ at isolation and word level; not yet ready to move into phrases for /s/.
- Home: suggest quick daily practice with 3–5 /k/ words in short phrases and a simple picture game for final consonants.
notes/5/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver shared that child’s teachers at preschool have mentioned improved clarity, especially for words with /k/ and some final sounds.
- Caregiver still notices difficulty with “s” words and some longer words.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned easily and appeared confident.
- Sustained attention to adult-led activities was strong today, with minimal need for redirection.
- Enjoyed being the “teacher” and correcting the clinician’s silly productions.

Goals / session targets:
1) /k/ initial at phrase and early sentence level
- Child produced /k/ initial words accurately in imitation and many spontaneous attempts during play.
- Practiced short sentences with embedded /k/ words (e.g., “The cat is big,” “I see a car”).
- Needed only occasional light verbal reminders; overall, /k/ production appeared stable at this level.

2) Final consonants in CVC words and short phrases
- Continued practice of final /p, t, m, k, n/ in CVC words within a storybook activity.
- Child demonstrated high accuracy for final consonants during imitation and improved consistency in short phrases (e.g., “big cup,” “red hat”).
- In play-based conversation, final consonants were present more often than not, though still variable with new vocabulary.

3) Initial /s/ in isolation and word level
- Revisited /s/ in isolation; child produced a clearer, more consistent /s/ with reduced cueing.
- Practiced an expanded set of /s/ initial words (e.g., “sun, soup, sock, seal, sit, soap”).
- Accuracy improved compared to prior sessions, though distortions and substitutions (e.g., /d/ for /s/) were still noted in less familiar words.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to support /k/ in simple sentences and monitor for generalization in spontaneous language; may consider shifting primary focus away from /k/ if stability is maintained.
- Maintain final consonant practice and encourage caregiver to model clear final sounds in everyday routines.
- Keep working on initial /s/ at the word level; introduce a few two-word combinations with /s/ if accuracy continues to build.
- Home: caregiver to read short books emphasizing /k/ and final consonants, pausing for child to name familiar pictures.
notes/6/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver reported that child continues to use /k/ clearly in many words and short sentences at home.
- No major new changes noticed this week; caregiver feels “things seem about the same” as last session.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child arrived in good spirits and followed the visual schedule.
- Participation was consistent, though child occasionally tried to skip to preferred games; responded to gentle redirection.
- Imitation remained strong when tasks were clearly explained.

Goals / session targets:
1) /k/ initial in sentences and spontaneous play
- Practiced short sentences with /k/ words (e.g., “The cat is on the car,” “I like cookies”).
- Accuracy for /k/ remained high in imitation, similar to last week.
- In spontaneous play, child continued to use /k/ correctly in many familiar words; no major changes from prior session.

2) Final consonants in CVC words and short phrases
- Continued CVC practice with picture cards and a simple board game.
- Child maintained good accuracy for final /p, t, m, k, n/ in structured tasks.
- In less structured play, final consonants were present in several target words but still occasionally omitted, consistent with last week’s performance.

3) Initial /s/ in word level
- Targeted the same set of /s/ initial words as last session.
- Accuracy was comparable to previous week: some clear productions with cues, interspersed with distortions or substitutions.
- Child remained motivated to practice but still required moderate cueing for tongue placement and airflow.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to reinforce stable /k/ production while shifting more attention to /s/ and final consonants, as /k/ appears relatively well established.
- Maintain current level of difficulty for /s/ (word level) and final consonants (CVC and short phrases) to consolidate gains.
- Home: caregiver encouraged to keep routines for practice the same this week, focusing on consistency rather than adding new targets.
notes/7/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver reported noticing a few clearer “s” words at home, such as “sun” and “sock,” especially when child is reminded to “do the snake sound.”
- Family continues to feel overall speech is easier to understand than a few months ago.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child separated from caregiver without difficulty and quickly engaged in a game.
- Showed slightly longer attention span for drill-based tasks when these were embedded in play.
- Demonstrated occasional self-correction attempts, especially with /s/.

Goals / session targets:
1) Initial /s/ at word and early phrase level
- Revisited established /s/ initial words and added a few new ones (e.g., “sand, soap, seal, soup, sun, sit”).
- Accuracy at the imitated word level was modestly improved; child needed fewer cues to achieve a clear /s/ on familiar items.
- Trialed simple two-word combinations (e.g., “sun up,” “soap here”) in imitation; child was able to maintain /s/ in some phrases with moderate support.

2) Final consonants in CVC words and phrases
- Practiced final /p, t, m, k, n/ in a picture bingo activity and in short phrases.
- Child continued to produce final consonants reliably in imitation, with slightly increased consistency in short phrases compared to previous weeks.
- During semi-structured play, child showed more frequent attempts to include final consonants, sometimes self-correcting after an omission.

3) /k/ in spontaneous speech
- /k/ initial words were monitored rather than explicitly drilled today.
- In conversation and play, child produced /k/ accurately in most familiar words (e.g., “car, cat, cookie, cup”).
- Occasional fronting occurred on less familiar words, but overall pattern suggested stable generalization.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to build /s/ accuracy at word and short phrase level; maintain focus on consistent tongue placement and airflow.
- Support final consonant use in more naturalistic contexts (play and simple storytelling) while keeping some structured practice.
- Monitor /k/ and provide brief refreshers as needed but prioritize /s/ and final consonants.
- Home: suggest caregiver use a mirror at home for “snake sound” practice with 3–4 favorite /s/ words, focusing on quality rather than quantity of trials.
notes/8/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver noted that child is starting to correct their own “s” sounds occasionally, saying “no, sssun” after an unclear attempt.
- Reported that preschool staff continue to notice improved clarity but still have difficulty understanding longer sentences.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child was energetic but cooperative; benefitted from a clear structure and frequent movement breaks.
- Showed pride in “fixing” sounds and asked to hear recordings of their speech.

Goals / session targets:
1) Initial /s/ at word and short phrase level
- Practiced both familiar and new /s/ initial words in a turn-taking game.
- Accuracy remained similar to last session overall, with some good productions and some distortions.
- Child demonstrated more frequent attempts to self-correct after an inaccurate /s/ when cued to “try the snake sound again.”

2) Final consonants in CVC words and short phrases
- Continued CVC practice using a picture lotto game and simple role-play.
- Accuracy for final consonants in structured tasks was stable compared to last week, with high success on known words.
- In phrases and brief narratives, final consonants were present in many target words but still inconsistent, particularly in longer utterances.

3) /k/ monitoring in spontaneous language
- No direct /k/ drills today; clinician monitored productions during play and conversation.
- /k/ remained accurate in most contexts, with only rare fronting noted on unfamiliar words.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain current level for /s/ and final consonants to allow consolidation, while continuing to encourage self-monitoring.
- Begin to incorporate more connected speech tasks (e.g., short stories, describing pictures) to support carryover of final consonants and /s/.
- Home: caregiver encouraged to gently prompt child to “check your snake sound” when motivated, but avoid over-correcting to maintain confidence.
notes/9/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver reported that child had a busy week and there was less time for structured practice.
- Noted that overall speech clarity seems similar to last week, with some good “s” words and some that are still hard to understand.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child appeared slightly tired today but was still willing to participate.
- Needed a bit more encouragement to complete drill tasks, but engagement improved when activities were tied to pretend play.

Goals / session targets:
1) Initial /s/ at word and short phrase level
- Repeated practice with established /s/ word set within a pretend store game.
- Performance was comparable to recent sessions: some clear, accurate /s/ productions with cues, interspersed with distorted or substituted sounds.
- Child responded well to visual cues (e.g., picture of a snake) but did not show a clear jump in independence.

2) Final consonants in CVC words and phrases
- Practiced final /p, t, m, k, n/ within a simple story retell.
- Accuracy in structured imitation remained strong and similar to previous weeks.
- In longer phrases and short story sentences, final consonants were sometimes omitted, particularly when child was excited or speaking quickly.

3) /k/ in spontaneous speech
- Continued monitoring without direct drill.
- /k/ productions stayed consistent and accurate in most spontaneous utterances.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue current goals and difficulty levels; today appeared to be a consolidation week without major changes.
- Emphasize quality over quantity in home practice, especially for /s/ words, to avoid fatigue.
- Home: encourage caregiver to embed practice into daily routines (e.g., saying “soap, sink, socks” during bath/bedtime) rather than adding extra practice sessions.
notes/10/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver stated that child has been using clearer “s” in a few new words (e.g., “sand,” “soap”) during play.
- Reported that child is starting to be more understandable to unfamiliar adults in short sentences.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly and was eager to show “new words.”
- Maintained attention well during structured tasks and accepted feedback with good humor.

Goals / session targets:
1) Initial /s/ at word and short phrase level
- Practiced a mix of familiar and new /s/ words in a picture card guessing game.
- Accuracy showed small, steady improvement; child required slightly less cueing for correct tongue placement on familiar items.
- In short two- to three-word phrases (e.g., “see sun,” “soap on,” “silly sock”), child was able to maintain /s/ in more trials than in prior weeks with moderate cues.

2) Final consonants in CVC words and short phrases
- Continued CVC and short phrase practice using a puppet activity.
- Child maintained good accuracy for final consonants in imitation and showed slightly more consistent use in short phrases.
- Some omissions persisted in longer utterances, but child occasionally attempted to repair when prompted.

3) /k/ in spontaneous speech
- /k/ remained stable and accurate across spontaneous speech; no direct drills were required.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to support /s/ at word and phrase level, with gradual increase in complexity of phrases as accuracy allows.
- Maintain final consonant practice and begin to incorporate more naturalistic conversation tasks (e.g., talking about a favorite show) to encourage carryover.
- Home: caregiver encouraged to play simple “I spy” or picture naming games focusing on /s/ words and CVC words with final consonants.
notes/11/note_text:

notes/0/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that she understands most of what child says, but unfamiliar listeners have difficulty, especially with longer words and when child talks quickly.
- No concerns about hearing or health this week.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned to the therapy room with minimal support, held mom's hand but separated without distress.
- Engaged well with toys and books; attention was best in short, play-based activities (3–5 minutes) before needing a change.
- Responded well to visual supports and clear routines.

Goals and observations:
1. Establish baseline for target sounds (/k/, /g/, /s/, /f/)
- During play and picture naming, child frequently fronted /k, g/ to /t, d/ (e.g., "tat" for "cat," "doe" for "go").
- /s/ was often reduced or changed to /d/ or /t/ in initial position (e.g., "doap" for "soap").
- /f/ in initial position was usually replaced with /p/ (e.g., "pish" for "fish").
- Overall intelligibility in connected speech estimated to be reduced, especially for unfamiliar listeners.

2. /k/ in isolation and simple syllables (imitation)
- Introduced "back sound" concept with mirror and tongue depressor for visual reference.
- With maximal verbal and gestural cueing (touching throat, "back sound" gesture), child produced /k/ in isolation inconsistently; able to approximate 3–4 times but often reverted to /t/.
- Attempted CV syllables ("koo," "kay"), but productions were mainly /too/, /tay/.

3. /f/ initial in isolation and CV syllables (imitation)
- Modeled "lip-bite and blow" strategy using mirror and picture of a face.
- With direct imitation and visual cueing, child was able to approximate /f/ in isolation in about half of attempts; often produced /h/ or /p/ instead.
- Able to imitate simple CV syllables ("fee," "foo") with heavy modeling and slow rate; accuracy inconsistent.

4. Early syllable shapes and clarity in CVCV words
- Observed use of CV, VC, and CVCV (same consonant) patterns during play (e.g., "mama," "dada," "nana").
- In longer CVCV words (e.g., "bunny," "teddy"), some consonants were omitted or simplified, impacting clarity.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to focus on establishing /k/ and /f/ in isolation and simple syllables with strong visual supports.
- Introduce a small set of functional words with /f/ (e.g., "fish," "fun") once isolation is more consistent.
- Home practice: encouraged parent to play in front of a mirror for short periods, practicing "lip-bite and blow" for /f/ with lots of praise for attempts, not perfection.
- Plan to gather more detailed baseline next session for /s/ and final consonants.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported child is enjoying "sound games" in the mirror at home and will sometimes ask to "do funny lips." She has not noticed big changes in clarity yet.
- No new concerns since last visit.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Smooth transition into room; briefly checked that mom could see him, then joined therapist at table.
- Participated well in turn-taking games; occasional silliness but easily redirected with visual timer and choices.

Goals and observations:
1. /f/ in isolation and CV syllables (imitation)
- With mirror and "lip-bite and blow" cue, child produced a clearer /f/ in isolation in most attempts; fewer /p/ substitutions noted compared to baseline.
- In CV syllables ("fee, foo, faa"), accuracy improved with slowed rate and simultaneous production with SLP; occasional voicing noted but overall more consistent.
- Able to imitate a small set of /f/-initial words ("fish, food, fan") with moderate verbal and visual cues; several productions were recognizable to unfamiliar listeners.

2. /k/ in isolation and CV syllables (imitation)
- Continued to emphasize "back sound" using gesture and tactile cues along the jawline.
- Child produced more distinct /k/ sounds in isolation than last week, though still mixed with /t/; needed frequent reminders to "make it in the back."
- CV syllables ("koo, key") remained challenging; approximations often closer to /too, tee/, but a few clearer /k/ productions were observed when the SLP exaggerated the back-of-tongue movement.

3. Initial /s/ awareness and production attempts
- Introduced /s/ as a "snake sound" with picture support and sound-only practice.
- Child attempted /s/ in isolation with variable success; many attempts sounded more like /t/ or /sh/.
- Able to discriminate /s/ vs. /t/ in a listening game with picture cards in most trials, indicating growing awareness even though production is not yet consistent.

4. Functional word attempts in play
- During structured play (farm and bubbles), child imitated a few target words containing /f/ and /k/ when provided with a direct model (e.g., "fish," "cow"), but spontaneous productions still reflected previous error patterns.
- Some increased willingness to try new words after specific praise for effort.

Plan/recommendations:
- Given the improvement, continue to strengthen /f/ at the CV and simple word level in imitation, and keep /k/ at isolation/CV level.
- Maintain /s/ at awareness and isolation attempts only for now.
- Home practice: encourage short, fun practice of "snake sound" /s/ in front of mirror, focusing on listening and trying, not accuracy. Continue daily brief practice of /f/ using picture cards or toys (e.g., fish, food).
notes/2/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother shared that child is now sometimes saying a clearer "fff" when blowing bubbles or pretending to be a dragon, especially when reminded.
- She has noticed "cat" still sounds like "tat" most of the time.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered room independently and showed interest in new toy set.
- Maintained attention for longer periods today (up to ~7–8 minutes) when tasks were embedded in play.
- Enjoyed being the "teacher" and giving the SLP a turn, which increased participation.

Goals and observations:
1. /f/ initial at word level (imitation, emerging spontaneous)
- Focused on a small set of /f/ words (fish, food, fun, four, feet) using picture cards and play.
- In imitation, child produced initial /f/ clearly in the majority of trials; occasional /p/ substitutions still occurred, particularly when speaking quickly.
- During play, a few spontaneous uses of /f/ were heard (e.g., "fish" and "four") with recognizable /f/ without a direct model, though not yet consistent.

2. /k/ in isolation and CV syllables; probing initial word level
- With continued "back sound" cue and mirror, isolation /k/ was more consistent than previous sessions, though still not fully stable.
- CV syllables ("koo, kay, kee") produced with mixed accuracy; some clear /k/ productions emerged when paired with a visual cue of a car going "back."
- Briefly probed initial /k/ in simple words ("key, car, cow"); most productions remained fronted ("tee, tar, tow"), but a few approximations with partial back placement were observed.

3. /s/ isolation and simple CV syllables (imitation)
- Continued to practice "snake sound" /s/ with visual of a snake and long airflow.
- Child produced a more distinct /s/ in isolation in several trials, with fewer /t/ intrusions when reminded to keep tongue behind teeth.
- CV syllables ("see, soo") were attempted; many were closer to /dee, doo/, but some improved approximations were noted with slow rate and mirror support.

4. Overall intelligibility in short phrases
- During structured play, child produced some 2–3 word combinations ("my turn," "more bubbles," "big one").
- Speech remained somewhat difficult to understand without context due to sound substitutions, but there were more self-initiated attempts to repeat when not understood.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to strengthen /f/ at the word level and begin introducing very simple carrier phrases (e.g., "my ___," "I see ___") with support.
- Maintain /k/ focus at isolation and CV level with brief probes at word level.
- Keep /s/ at isolation/CV level, emphasizing airflow and tongue placement.
- Home practice: encourage use of /f/ words during daily routines (feet, food, fish crackers), praising any clear attempts and gentle reminders to "bite your lip and blow."
notes/3/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported child is now sometimes correcting herself when saying /f/ words (e.g., "pish...fish").
- She has not noticed much change yet with "k" words.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child was cheerful and eager to show SLP a toy from home.
- Needed some prompting to shift from free play to structured tasks but responded well to visual schedule.
- Tolerated brief sitting tasks at the table with intermittent movement breaks.

Goals and observations:
1. /f/ initial at word and simple phrase level (imitation and supported spontaneous)
- Practiced a set of familiar /f/ words (fish, four, fun, feet, food) in a picture game.
- In imitation, productions were mostly accurate; clear /f/ in initial position in the majority of attempts.
- Began using short carrier phrases ("my fish," "I see four") with model; child imitated these phrases with generally good maintenance of /f/.
- Some spontaneous phrases with /f/ ("more fish," "my feet") were heard in play, with emerging consistency.

2. /k/ in isolation, CV, and limited word-level trials (imitation)
- Continued to use "back sound" cues, including visual of tongue position and hand gesture.
- Isolation /k/ produced more reliably, though occasional /t/ persisted, particularly when child was distracted.
- CV syllables ("koo, kee, kay") showed improving accuracy with slower rate and simultaneous productions.
- Probed initial /k/ in a small set of words ("key, car, cow"); several approximations showed clearer back placement but were not yet consistent across trials.

3. /s/ isolation and CV syllables
- Practiced "snake sound" using a story and toy snake.
- Child able to hold a sustained /s/ in isolation in many attempts, though some productions were short or blended with /t/.
- CV syllables ("see, soo") remained variable; improvements were seen when the SLP used a visual cue for long air and "quiet tongue."

4. Intelligibility in short, structured phrases
- In play-based routines (feeding animals, building a tower), child used 2–3 word phrases.
- When phrases contained newly practiced /f/ words, intelligibility was noticeably better.
- Outside of target words, sound substitutions (especially for /k/) continued to impact clarity.

Plan/recommendations:
- Maintain /f/ at word and short-phrase level, with continued support to encourage spontaneous use during play.
- Continue /k/ work at isolation and CV level; briefly practice high-interest /k/ words with strong modeling.
- Keep /s/ at isolation and simple syllable level; focus on building consistent airflow.
- Home practice: encourage parent to model short phrases with /f/ ("fun food," "four fish") and invite child to repeat in a playful way. Reinforce any self-corrections warmly.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:

2. /k/ in CV syllables and initial word level (imitation)

- CV syllables ("koo, kay, key, kah") produced with improved accuracy; more consistent back placement, though occasional fronting to /t/ still occurred.
- Initial /k/ words ("key, car, cookie, cat") practiced in imitation; child produced clearer /k/ in some trials (especially "key" and "cookie") but remained inconsistent overall.

3. /s/ isolation and emerging word-level attempts

Plan/recommendations:

notes/5/note_text:

notes/6/note_text:

notes/8/note_text:

- Occasional fronting occurred when child was very excited but was infrequent and easily corrected.

3. /s/ isolation and initial word level (continued focus)
- Practiced sustained /s/ and initial /s/ in familiar word set.
- Isolation /s/ was stable and clear across trials.
- At word level, accuracy remained similar to prior sessions, with a combination of accurate /s/ productions and approximations; child responded well to cues but had difficulty maintaining accuracy independently.

4. Intelligibility overview
- In structured play and short conversational exchanges, child used multiple 4–6 word sentences.
- Intelligibility was generally good for the clinician, particularly when target /f/ and /k/ sounds were present; residual /s/ difficulties and other minor patterns still affected some words.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue with current plan: maintain /f/ and /k/ at the conversational level, providing occasional reminders as needed.
- Ongoing focus on /s/ at isolation and word level until consistency improves; consider future progression to phrase level once accuracy is more reliable.
- Home practice: encourage parent to continue brief, playful /s/ practice, and to keep reinforcing clear /f/ and /k/ in everyday conversation. Discussed carrying over strategies to preschool and providing teachers with a brief explanation of child’s target sounds.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver shared that they understand today's focus will be on speech sounds and reported that the child is "hard to understand" for unfamiliar listeners. No formal home practice completed yet as this was the first session.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned with some hesitation but joined play with moderate support. Once engaged, they were cheerful and talkative, though speech was often unintelligible in connected speech. Attended to adult-led tasks for short bursts (2–3 minutes) with frequent redirection back to the table.

Goals / therapy focus:
1. Baseline of overall speech sound production
- Informal play-based sample taken. Frequent omissions of final consonants (e.g., "ca" for "cat," "pi" for "pig"), and reduced consonant blends (e.g., "pane" for "plane"). Vowel productions generally intact. Intelligibility to clinician estimated as low-to-moderate in conversation.

2. Final consonant production (p, m, t) in CVC words – imitated level
- Targeted words such as "map, mop, mat, cat, cup." Child was often able to add a final consonant when given a clear model and visual cue (pointing to the end of the word picture), but productions were inconsistent. Tended to add an extra vowel (e.g., "muh-puh") or omit the final sound entirely without cues.

3. Initial /f/ in syllables and simple words – imitated level
- Practiced /f/ in isolation ("ffff") and in CV syllables ("fa, fee, foo") with visual cue of "bitting lip and blowing air." Child needed frequent reminders and tended to substitute /p/ or /h/ for /f/. A few clear productions were heard with simultaneous clinician modeling.

4. Initial /s/ in isolation and simple CV syllables – imitated level
- Introduced "snake sound" /s/ in isolation. Child attempted to copy the clinician but often produced /t/ or a short, stopped sound. Could briefly hold /s/ with maximal verbal and visual cues ("keep teeth together, blow air"). Not yet ready to move to words.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to build awareness of final consonants and introduce consistent practice for /f/ and /s/ in isolation and syllables. Caregiver encouraged to model "quiet snake sound" /s/ and "lip-bite" /f/ during daily routines (e.g., "feet," "food," "soap") without pressuring the child to repeat. Next session will further probe which sounds are most stimulable for goal setting.

Goals / therapy focus:

With a clear model and visual cue (tapping the table for the final sound), child produced final consonants more frequently than last session, though accuracy remained variable. In structured imitation, there were a few more accurate CV productions ("fee, foo") than previously, with less need for hand-over-hand prompting. Word-level attempts ("fish, fun, four") were mostly approximations.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and CV syllables – imitated level
- Revisited "snake sound" /s/. Child was more willing to watch the clinician's mouth and listen to the long sound. Still required maximal verbal and visual cues to achieve a continuous /s/. notes/2/note_text:

2. Initial /f/ in CV and CVC words – imitated level
- Used mirror work and a "blowing" theme ("fan, fish, food"). Child demonstrated improved placement for /f/ in isolation and could produce a short continuous /f/ when prompted. Accuracy for CV syllables increased, and a few clear productions of "fish" and "feet" were heard with direct imitation and visual cues. Still not yet independent but requiring slightly fewer prompts.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and simple CV syllables – imitated level
- Continued with "snake sound" using a toy snake and visual of air flow. Child was able to sustain /s/ for a brief moment with the clinician's model and verbal cue to "keep teeth together." Attempts at "see" and "sun" remained inconsistent, with frequent substitutions or distortions, but there was more willingness to practice multiple trials.

notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child was enthusiastic and talkative upon arrival. Participated in table tasks for several short rounds and accepted clinician-led choices. Mild silliness at times, but easily redirected with visual schedules and concrete expectations.

Goals / therapy focus:
1. Final /p/ and /m/ in CVC words – imitated and emerging spontaneous level
- Used a matching game with target words ("cap, cup, map, mop, mom, ham, gum"). Child produced final /p/ and /m/ more consistently than previous sessions when given a model and a brief cue. In familiar words like "cup" and "mom," child occasionally produced the final sound independently during play. When prompted to "say it one more time," accuracy often improved without the need for a full model.

2. Final /t/ in CVC words – imitated level
- Focused on words such as "cat, hat, hot, net." With a slow model and gesture cue (tapping the end of the picture), child was able to add a soft /t/ in several attempts. Production was not yet consistent across the set, but this represents a step forward from last session's brief probing.

3. Initial /f/ in CVC words – imitated level, expanding set
- Continued mirror practice, then moved into a small group of words ("feet, fun, fish, four"). Child showed more reliable placement and was able to produce /f/ at the beginning of words when directly imitating. Fewer substitutions to /p/ were heard, though some productions remained unclear in connected speech.

4. Initial /s/ in isolation and CV – imitated level
- Shorter practice segment on /s/ to maintain familiarity. Child could produce a brief continuous /s/ with model and cueing. Attempts in CV syllables ("see, soo") showed some improvement in airflow control but still required maximal support.

Plan / recommendations:
- Begin to build more opportunities for spontaneous final consonant use in structured play, while maintaining direct practice for final /p, m, t/ at the word level. Continue strengthening initial /f/ at word level and keep /s/ in the routine as a shorter, high-support target. Caregiver encouraged to notice and praise any clear final sounds at home and to model slightly slowed speech when emphasizing the last sound in key words.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver reported that the child is more understandable during routines like snack and bath time when they are using shorter phrases. They notice clearer "feet" and "food" occasionally, especially when they remind the child of the "lip-bite" sound. They requested ideas for making practice feel more like a game.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child arrived in good spirits and transitioned independently. Attentive for short, repeated practice bursts embedded in play (e.g., racing cars, feeding animals). Enjoyed choosing between two target pictures. Needed occasional prompts to stay seated but returned easily.

Goals / therapy focus:
1. Final /p, m, t/ in CVC words – imitated and cued spontaneous level
- Continued work with picture cards and a simple board game. Child demonstrated more consistent accuracy for final /p/ and /m/ in high-frequency words and improved performance with final /t/. With a single reminder ("remember the last sound"), the child often produced the full CVC word without needing the clinician to say it first. Some spontaneous final consonants were heard during game play when the child named familiar pictures.

2. Initial /f/ in CVC words – imitated and cued level; early carrier phrase attempts
- Practiced words such as "fish, feet, fun, food" and began to introduce short carrier phrases ("my fish," "more feet") with heavy support. Child showed better control over the /f/ placement and produced several clear initial /f/ words in direct imitation. When phrases were attempted, the child tended to maintain the /f/ but dropped some final consonants.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and simple words – imitated level
- Spent a brief portion of the session on /s/ using a toy snake and visual cue for airflow. Child could produce a short /s/ on request with full support and attempted a few /s/ initial words ("sun, soup, sock") in imitation. Accuracy remained variable, but there was slightly improved control of the continuous airflow.

4. Intelligibility in structured play
- In a simple play routine (feeding toy animals), the child was more easily understood when using words that had been practiced earlier in the session, especially those with improved final consonants. Longer utterances remained more challenging to understand.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue consolidating final /p, m, t/ at word level and begin to create more opportunities for short phrases with at least one clear final consonant. Maintain and gradually expand initial /f/ word set, keeping carrier phrases short and highly supported. Provide caregiver with a simple "picture race" game to practice 3–4 words at home, focusing on one sound at a time (e.g., final /p/ words) for brief, fun practice.
notes/5/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver shared that the home game was used a few times, mostly with final /p/ words. They feel the child is "trying harder" to add the last sound when reminded. Some frustration was noted when the child is not understood by others in the community.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child was initially quiet but warmed up quickly. Participated in structured tasks with good effort and responded well to specific feedback ("I heard your last sound!"). Some impulsive responding, but generally cooperative and engaged.

Goals / therapy focus:
1. Final /p, m, t/ in CVC words – imitated, cued, and emerging independent level
- Used a spinner game with mixed final consonant targets ("cup, cap, map, mop, mom, ham, cat, hat, hot"). Child continued to show solid performance when given a model or brief reminder. There were more instances of accurate final consonants without the clinician saying the word first, particularly for well-practiced items. Occasional omissions and distortions persisted, but overall consistency is trending upward.

2. Initial /f/ in CVC words and short phrases – imitated and cued level
- Practiced "fish, feet, fun, food, fire" within a simple play scenario (feeding animals, pretend picnic). Child produced initial /f/ more reliably in imitation, and a few clear productions were heard within short phrases ("my fish," "more food") when the clinician provided a model and gestural cue. Final consonant accuracy decreased somewhat in phrases, which is expected at this stage.

- Maintained /s/ practice using a brief drill segment. Child could produce a more sustained /s/ with clinician modeling and visual cue. Maintain and expand initial /f/ practice in both single words and highly supported two-word phrases. Keep /s/ as a shorter, high-support target until accuracy becomes more consistent. notes/6/note_text:

- Targeted familiar words in simple two-word phrases ("big cup," "my hat," "mom up"). At the single-word level, the child demonstrated fairly consistent final consonants for practiced items with minimal cueing. In short phrases, accuracy decreased but remained better than previous weeks, especially when the clinician highlighted the final sound in the last word of the phrase.

- Used a picture scene to practice words ("fish, feet, food, fan, five") and short phrases ("my fish," "more food," "five feet").
