notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom reported that she and Dad understand most of what he says, but grandparents and preschool staff are still asking for frequent repetitions. She notices he often leaves off sounds at the ends of words and sometimes says “doo” for “shoe.” No concerns with hearing or health since the evaluation.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned to the therapy room with some hesitation but joined preferred toy play after a brief warm-up. He was generally cooperative and responded well to visual supports. Attention to task was variable; he needed redirection when activities were less preferred but returned to work with encouragement and short movement breaks.

Goals / session focus:
1. Establish /sh/ in isolation
- Targeted /sh/ in isolation using mirror work, auditory bombardment, and “quiet sound” cueing. Client produced an approximation of /sh/ with clinician modeling and hand cue at about half of opportunities. He tended to substitute /s/ or /t/ when the tongue did not retract. Able to hold a prolonged “shhh” for 1–2 seconds with direct imitation.

2. Initial /sh/ in simple syllables (CV)
- Practiced CV syllables (e.g., “she,” “shoo,” “sha”) in imitation. Accuracy was inconsistent; he was most successful on “she” and “shoo” when paired with picture cues and slowed rate. Required frequent verbal reminders to “lip out, tongue back.”

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and CV
- Elicited /s/ in isolation with snake visual. Client could imitate a sustained /s/ with intermittent stopping to /t/. With verbal cue to “keep teeth together” and model, he produced clearer /s/ about half the time. CV syllables (e.g., “see,” “soo”) were attempted; these were more challenging, with frequent omission or substitution.

4. Final consonant awareness
- In play, SLP modeled final consonants (e.g., “bus,” “dog,” “cup”) with visual emphasis and minimal pairs (e.g., “bee” vs “beep”). Client was able to notice a difference when the final sound was exaggerated but did not yet consistently add final sounds in imitation.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue to build consistency of /sh/ and /s/ in isolation and simple syllables before moving to word level. Provide parents with simple “quiet sound” (/sh/) and “snake sound” (/s/) practice sheets for short, play-based practice at home (5 minutes, 3–4 times/week), focusing on listening and a few imitated sounds rather than accuracy. Monitor final consonant production in familiar words in upcoming sessions.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
Mom shared that he enjoys practicing his “quiet sound” at home and will sometimes shush his stuffed animals. She has not noticed big changes in word clarity yet but feels he is trying to copy her more often. No new medical concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client entered the room more readily today and quickly engaged in play-based activities. He was cheerful and talkative, with occasional silliness that required gentle redirection. He responded well to visual schedules and short, varied tasks.

Goals / session focus:
1. /sh/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Reviewed /sh/ in isolation; client produced a clear, sustained /sh/ in most imitated attempts when using the visual cue (finger to lips). Accuracy was higher than last week, with fewer /s/ substitutions. Transitioned to CV syllables (e.g., “she,” “shy,” “shoe”); he imitated these with emerging consistency, especially “she” and “shoe.” Needed moderate verbal cueing to keep his tongue back and lips rounded.

2. Initial /sh/ at single-word level (imitation)
- Introduced simple /sh/-initial words (e.g., shoe, sheep, shark, shop) in picture naming with direct imitation. Client achieved several accurate productions, particularly with high-interest items (shark, shoe). Errors included reducing to /s/ or omitting the initial sound. With repetition and slowed rate, he could often correct with support.

3. /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Practiced sustained /s/ with snake visual. Client demonstrated slightly improved control, with longer and clearer productions when watching the clinician’s mouth. In CV syllables, he produced approximations (e.g., “dee” for “see”) but was able to move toward /s/ with repeated modeling and tactile cue to keep tongue behind teeth.

4. Awareness of final consonants in play
- Continued minimal pair listening tasks (e.g., “bee/beep,” “boo/boot”) using toy-based games. Client was engaged in pointing to the picture he heard and showed good listening discrimination for some pairs. Still rarely added final sounds in his own spontaneous words.

Plan / recommendations:
Maintain focus on /sh/ in isolation and CV, and continue early work on /sh/-initial words in imitation, as he is showing a noticeable increase in accuracy. Keep /s/ work at isolation/CV level for now. Home practice: short daily “quiet sound” games and a few /sh/ words (shoe, shark) in imitation, emphasizing fun and not correcting every error.

He remained seated for structured tasks for longer periods than in previous sessions and responded positively to praise and token rewards. Brief fatigue was noted near the end of session, but he was able to re-engage with a movement break.

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /sh/ at word level (imitation → cued spontaneous)
- Targeted /sh/-initial words (shoe, sheep, shark, shop, shell) using picture cards and play. Client produced these with improved accuracy in imitation, often correct on the first or second attempt. Began to reduce the amount of cueing; he could sometimes say “shoe” or “shark” in a short phrase (e.g., “my shoe”) after a model. Occasional carryover into spontaneous play (e.g., labeling a toy shark) when visually prompted.

2. /sh/ in simple carrier phrases (imitation)
- Introduced carrier phrase “my ___” and “I see ___” with /sh/-initial targets. With direct modeling, he produced short phrases such as “my shoe” and “I see shark” with emerging accuracy. Needed frequent reminders to keep the /sh/ sound, but was able to self-correct a few times when he heard himself say /s/.

3. /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Continued practice of sustained /s/ with mirror and snake cue. Client showed better consistency in holding the sound without stopping to /t/. In CV syllables (see, soo, say), he produced closer approximations, with some clearly recognizable /s/ on initial attempts. Still required moderate verbal and visual cues.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that child is starting junior kindergarten in the fall. She noted that family members understand most of what the child says, but peers and unfamiliar listeners still have difficulty, especially with words that have "s" or longer words.
- No major changes since the initial assessment. Mom would like to focus on making the child easier to understand with friends.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned to the therapy room with brief hesitation, then engaged well with toys and books.
- Attention to adult-directed tasks was variable; needed frequent reminders to stay at the table but responded to visual supports and short, play-based activities.

Therapy goals and observations:
1) Establish /s/ in isolation and simple syllables
- Introduced /s/ as a "snake sound." Child was able to imitate a prolonged /s/ in isolation in about half of opportunities with visual cueing (watching clinician’s mouth) and a tactile cue on the arm to indicate "long sound."
- Productions were often stopped to /t/ or omitted. When successful, child could hold the sound briefly but had difficulty maintaining consistent airflow.
- At CV syllable level ("see, soo"), accuracy was low; child frequently produced "tee, doo" despite models.

2) Initial /s/ at imitated word level
- Targeted initial /s/ in simple words (sun, soup, sock, soap, sad) using picture cards.
- With direct imitation and maximal verbal and visual cues, child produced approximations in some trials but often substituted /t/ ("tun" for sun) or deleted the initial consonant.
- Accuracy was inconsistent; child required repeated models and slow pacing.

3) Early /s/ blends awareness
- Introduced /s/ blends (e.g., stop, spin, star) only for auditory discrimination and awareness.
- Child enjoyed listening for the "snake sound" at the beginning of words and could point to pictures when the clinician emphasized /s/ about half the time.
- No expectation for independent production at this time; productions were largely reduced to the second consonant or simplified (e.g., "top" for stop).

4) Baseline for final consonants in simple CVC words
- Probed final /t/ and /p/ in play (hat, hot, up, cup). Child frequently omitted final consonants in spontaneous speech ("ha" for hat, "uh" for up).
- With simultaneous production and gesture cue (tapping the table for the final sound), child was able to mark some final consonants but not consistently.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to establish a consistent /s/ sound in isolation and simple CV syllables before increasing word-level demands.
- Maintain short, play-based activities with frequent movement breaks to support attention.
- Home practice: caregiver to model long "snake sound" /s/ during daily routines (e.g., "sssssock," "ssssoup"), encouraging the child to listen and occasionally try the sound, without pressure for accuracy.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported no major changes in speech clarity over the week. She notices the child trying to copy "snake sounds" at home but is not always successful.
- Family has been using the home practice ideas during dressing and mealtimes.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child separated from caregiver more easily today and entered room with minimal prompting.
- Remained engaged for slightly longer stretches at the table with visual schedule support; still required redirection when tasks became more challenging.

Therapy goals and observations:
1) /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Child showed meaningful improvement in producing a sustained /s/ in isolation with visual and verbal cues. Able to imitate a clear /s/ in most attempts when given time to prepare ("teeth together, air through the middle").
- Introduced more CV syllables ("see, soo, sah"). Accuracy improved compared to last session; child produced a recognizable /s/ at the beginning of about half of trials with moderate cueing.
- Child began to self-correct occasionally after hearing their own production (e.g., trying again after producing "tee" for "see").

2) Initial /s/ at imitated word level
- Practiced initial /s/ words (sun, soap, sock, soup, sad) with picture support and a pacing board.
- With imitation and moderate verbal/visual cues, child produced closer approximations than last week, with fewer /t/ substitutions.
- Accuracy was still inconsistent but overall productions were clearer, and child accepted feedback well.

3) Auditory discrimination for /s/ vs no /s/
- Used minimal pairs (sun/un, sip/ip, seat/eat) at a listening level. Child was asked to point to the picture that matched the clinician’s word.
- Child correctly identified the target in more than half of opportunities, indicating emerging awareness of the presence/absence of /s/.
- Required repetition of instructions and additional modeling of listening behavior, but showed good interest in the "listening game."

4) Final consonant awareness (CVC words)
- Briefly revisited final /t/ and /p/ in simple words (hat, hot, up, cup) using simultaneous production.
- Child was more willing to attempt full CVC words, though final consonants were still often omitted without cues.
- With tapping cue, some accurate final consonant productions were observed, but this remains an emerging skill.

Plan/recommendations:
- Given the notable improvement with /s/ in isolation and syllables, begin to systematically build consistency at the imitated word level for initial /s/.
- Continue brief listening tasks for /s/ vs no /s/ to support phonological awareness.
- Home practice: caregivers to continue modeling /s/ and can now encourage the child to say 3–5 simple /s/ syllables ("see, soo, sah") once or twice daily, praising effort rather than perfection.

- Required continued verbal reminders ("long snake sound at the start") but needed fewer simultaneous productions.

3) /s/ vs no /s/ discrimination and minimal pairs
- Revisited auditory discrimination tasks with minimal pairs (sun/un, seat/eat, sip/ip).
- Child correctly identified the clinician’s target word in a higher proportion of trials than earlier in the block.
- Began to verbalize the difference (e.g., pointing and saying "sss" for the /s/ word), suggesting improved phonological awareness.

4) Final consonant practice – stabilizing CVC words
- Continued work on final /t/ and /p/ in a simple board game context (hat, hot, cup, up, sit).
- Child produced some accurate final consonants with imitation and tapping cues, but this remained less consistent than initial /s/ progress.
- Skills are slowly emerging; performance similar to last week with small gains in willingness to attempt full words.

Plan/recommendations:
- Given meaningful improvement with initial /s/ at the word level, continue to strengthen this and gradually increase the proportion of phrase-level practice.
- Maintain discrimination tasks and occasional final consonant practice.
- Home practice: caregivers to use simple two-word phrases with /s/ words ("my sock," "see sun") and encourage the child to copy once or twice daily, keeping practice short and positive.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that the child is now using clearer /s/ in a few familiar words at home ("sun" and "sock") when reminded.
- She also noted that the child seems proud when others understand them and will repeat words if asked.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered session in a cheerful mood and quickly engaged with both play and structured tasks.
- Demonstrated improved ability to follow simple directions and remained at the table for longer intervals before seeking other toys.

Therapy goals and observations:
1) Initial /s/ at imitated word level and emerging spontaneous use
- Practiced an expanded set of initial /s/ words (sun, soap, sock, soup, sit, sad, seal, sand, sail) using a picture matching game.
- Child showed stable, improved accuracy compared to previous sessions. With imitation and moderate cues, many productions included a clear initial /s/.
- In structured play, child spontaneously produced a few target words ("sock," "sun") with recognizable /s/ without direct prompting.

2) Initial /s/ in short carrier phrases
- Continued work with carrier phrases ("my sock," "see sun," "sip soup") using model + imitation.
- Child produced target phrases with improved consistency, maintaining /s/ in several trials when given visual and verbal cues.
- Still needed support for longer or less familiar phrases; occasional reductions to "my ock" or "tee sun" were observed.

3) Introduction of simple /s/ blends at listening and imitation level
- Introduced a small set of /s/ blends (stop, star, spoon) first at a listening level, then at imitation.
- Child could identify the "snake sound" at the beginning when listening, but productions were mostly reduced to single consonant (e.g., "top" for stop).
- This was framed as early exposure rather than a main accuracy goal.

4) Final consonants – continued practice
- Used a turn-taking game to practice CVC words with final /t/ and /p/ (hat, hot, cup, up, sit, nap).
- Child attempted more final consonants with imitation and tapping cues; however, accuracy remained variable.
- Performance suggests small but steady improvement, with more frequent marking of a final sound even if not always precise.

Plan/recommendations:
- Maintain focus on strengthening consistency for initial /s/ at word and short phrase level, as skills are progressing.
- Continue gentle exposure to /s/ blends and ongoing practice for final consonants.
- Home practice: encourage the child to use 3–5 practiced /s/ words during daily routines and to try a short phrase such as "my sock" or "see sun" once per day.
notes/5/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother shared that the child is now sometimes using clear /s/ in short phrases at home ("my sock," "see sun") when prompted.
- She notices that familiar listeners are understanding the child a bit more easily in these practiced contexts.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned without difficulty and eagerly participated in choosing activities from a visual choice board.
- Demonstrated good joint attention and turn-taking, with only occasional need for redirection during more repetitive drill tasks.

Therapy goals and observations:
1) Initial /s/ at word and phrase levels
- Practiced initial /s/ words (sun, soap, sock, soup, sit, seal, sand, sail, soup) in a picture bingo game.
- Child continued to produce many initial /s/ words clearly in imitation with moderate cues. Accuracy has remained relatively stable compared to last week, with slightly smoother transitions into phrases.
- Some spontaneous use of target words occurred during play (e.g., "sock," "sun"), typically when the child was drawing attention to items.

2) Short carrier phrases and emerging two-word combinations
- Continued phrases such as "my sock," "see sun," "sip soup," and added "see seal," "silly sock."
- With model + imitation, child produced these phrases with generally recognizable /s/ in the target words, though accuracy declined as phrase length increased.
- The child benefited from slowed rate and visual cueing but required similar levels of support as in the previous session.

3) Early /s/ blends – imitation level
- Practiced a limited set of /s/ blends (stop, star, spoon, slide) with picture supports.
- Child frequently reduced blends to a single consonant ("top," "tar," "poon") even with maximal cues; a few approximate productions with both sounds were heard with simultaneous production.
- This area is still emerging; focus remains on exposure and shaping, not mastery.

4) Final consonants – CVC words in play
- Used a pretend picnic activity to practice final /t/ and /p/ (hat, hot, cup, up, nap, soup).
- Child continued to omit final consonants in spontaneous speech but was more willing to repeat words with final sounds when cued.
- Overall performance similar to prior session, suggesting skills are consolidating.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue consolidating gains in initial /s/ at the word and short phrase levels, aiming for more consistent accuracy before advancing demands.
- Maintain light practice of /s/ blends and final consonants.
- Home practice: caregivers to continue practicing 3–5 target phrases (e.g., "my sock," "see sun," "sip soup") once daily, using slow, clear models and praising all attempts.
notes/6/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that the child has started using clearer /s/ in more everyday words, sometimes without being reminded, especially when talking about clothes and outdoor play ("sun," "sand," "sock").
- She feels others at home are noticing that the child sounds a bit clearer.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child came into the session with good energy and participated readily in both structured and play-based activities.
- Tolerated slightly longer practice sets and showed increased willingness to repeat words to improve clarity.

Therapy goals and observations:
1) Initial /s/ – increasing independence at word and phrase levels
- Practiced a mix of high-frequency /s/ words (sun, sock, sand, soap, soup, sit, seal) in a turn-taking game.
- Child demonstrated more consistent accurate /s/ productions in imitation and, notably, produced several clear /s/ words spontaneously during play without prompts.
- Needed somewhat fewer cues to achieve an accurate /s/, and occasionally initiated self-correction (e.g., "tun...sun").

2) Short phrases and early sentences with /s/
- Expanded from two-word phrases to short, simple sentences such as "I see sun," "I see sock," and "my sock on."
- With model + imitation, child produced these with recognizable /s/ in many attempts, though accuracy remained best in shorter phrases.
- The child showed good motivation, often requesting another turn to "try again" when prompted.

3) /s/ blends – continued shaping at imitation level
- Targeted /s/ blends (stop, star, spoon, slide, snow) with visual cues and slow segmentation.
- Child was able to produce more approximate blends using backward chaining (e.g., "top" → "stop"). Some productions included both consonants, though /s/ was often reduced in connected speech.
- Still required maximal support, but the number of attempts and partial successes increased from earlier sessions.

4) Final consonants – CVC practice
- Continued CVC practice with final /t/ and /p/ in a game format (hat, hot, cup, up, sit, nap).
- Child produced more final consonant attempts with imitation and cues but still omitted them in most spontaneous productions.
- Progress in this area remains gradual but moving in a positive direction.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to strengthen independent initial /s/ at the word level and build phrase and short sentence use, as the child is showing increasing readiness.
- Maintain focused practice for /s/ blends using backward chaining and slow rate.
- Home practice: caregivers to encourage the child to use short sentences with /s/ (e.g., "I see sun," "my sock on") in natural routines, praising effort and gentle self-corrections.
notes/7/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother stated that the child is now often using clear /s/ in several familiar words without prompting and occasionally in short phrases.
- She feels the child is easier to understand during routine activities, though longer sentences are still sometimes unclear.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned quickly and was eager to show the clinician how they could say "sun" and "sock."
- Maintained good engagement in both drill and play activities, with minimal need for redirection.

Therapy goals and observations:
1) Initial /s/ – word and short sentence levels
- Practiced a broad set of initial /s/ words (sun, sock, sand, soap, soup, sit, seal, silly, sick) within a picture scene activity.
- Child continued to produce many accurate /s/ words in imitation and demonstrated increasing spontaneous use during play.
- At short sentence level ("I see sun," "The sock is silly"), child generally maintained /s/ in the target words with verbal and visual cues. Performance was similar to last session, indicating consolidation of new skills.

2) /s/ blends – imitation and early phrase level
- Focused on /st/ and /sp/ blends (stop, star, spoon, spill, spin) using backward chaining and visual cueing.
- Child produced several approximations containing both consonants with strong support (e.g., simultaneous production, slowed rate). Some phrases like "stop it" were attempted with partial success.
- Compared to last session, performance was similar overall, with a stable number of successful approximations.

3) Final consonants – CVC words in structured play
- Used a car race game to practice CVC words with final /t/ and /p/ (hat, hot, cup, up, seat, nap).
- Child continued to benefit from tapping cues and simultaneous productions. Final consonants were present more often in structured practice than in spontaneous speech but remained inconsistent.
- Skills appear stable with gradual improvement in the child’s willingness to attempt full words.

4) Generalization to spontaneous play
- Observed the child during free play with toys and pretend scenarios.
- Child used some spontaneous initial /s/ words ("sun," "sock," "sand") with recognizable /s/, especially when labeling or requesting.
- Longer utterances were less clear, but the presence of clearer target words indicates positive carryover.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue consolidating gains in initial /s/ at word and short sentence levels.
- Maintain focused, high-support practice for /s/ blends and final consonants.
- Home practice: caregivers to continue encouraging use of practiced /s/ words and short sentences in natural contexts, such as getting dressed or playing outside (e.g., "I see sun," "My sock on").
notes/8/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that the child’s "s" words sound much clearer to the family, and the child is now initiating some of these words independently.
- She is still concerned about speech clarity in longer sentences and with less familiar listeners.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child arrived in good spirits and transitioned smoothly into the therapy space.
- Demonstrated strong participation in both structured and play-based tasks, with good tolerance for repetition.

Therapy goals and observations:
1) Initial /s/ – word, phrase, and short sentence levels
- Practiced initial /s/ words and short sentences within a storybook activity (e.g., "sun," "sand," "sock," "soap," "I see sun," "The sock is wet").
- Child consistently produced initial /s/ accurately in many familiar words during imitation and often in spontaneous comments related to the story.
- At short sentence level, child maintained /s/ in target words with light to moderate verbal cues. Overall performance represents a marked improvement from earlier in the block, and skills are now relatively stable.

2) /s/ blends – focused practice with /st/ and /sp/
- Targeted /st/ and /sp/ blends (stop, star, stairs, spoon, spill, spin) using backward chaining, visual cueing, and minimal pair contrasts (e.g., "top" vs "stop").
- Child achieved several clear approximations of /st/ blends in imitation with strong support and began to produce more consistent two-consonant clusters at the beginning of words.
- Some short phrases including blends ("stop it," "spill it") were attempted with partial success. This reflects meaningful progress compared to initial blend attempts earlier in the block.

3) Final consonants – CVC words and short phrases
- Continued CVC practice for final /t/ and /p/ within a picture-based game (hat, hot, cup, up, seat, nap) and short phrases ("my hat," "big cup").
- Child produced final consonants more reliably in imitation with tapping cues; however, these sounds were still often omitted in spontaneous phrases.
- Accuracy is gradually improving, but this area remains less advanced than initial /s/.

4) Generalization and intelligibility in play
- In spontaneous play, child used several clear initial /s/ words and some short phrases (e.g., "see sun," "my sock") without direct prompting.
- Overall intelligibility to the clinician was noticeably better for practiced /s/ words and phrases, though longer, unstructured sentences remained less intelligible.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue practicing initial /s/ at sentence level and begin incorporating targets into simple, semi-structured conversations.
- Maintain ongoing work on /s/ blends with backward chaining and clear visual cues.
- Home practice: caregivers to encourage the child to use short sentences with /s/ ("I see sun," "My sock is on") during everyday routines and to practice a few /st/ words ("stop," "stairs") with slow, supported models.
notes/9/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother shared that the child is using more sentences at home with clearer "s" sounds, especially in familiar routines.
- She still notes that when the child talks fast or is excited, some sounds and endings are harder to understand.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned easily, appeared comfortable with the clinician, and eagerly engaged in choosing activities.
- Maintained attention well across the session with minimal prompts; responded positively to praise and was willing to repeat to improve clarity.

Therapy goals and observations:
1) Initial /s/ – sentence and early conversational levels
- Practiced initial /s/ within structured sentences during a picture description task (e.g., "The sun is big," "The sock is wet," "I see the sand").
- Child produced accurate initial /s/ in most target words with light verbal cues and sometimes without cues.
- In brief semi-structured conversation about pictures ("What do you see?"), child spontaneously used several clear /s/ words and short sentences, indicating good carryover from drill tasks.

2) /s/ blends – /st/ and /sp/ at word and short phrase levels
- Focused on /st/ and /sp/ blends (stop, stairs, star, spoon, spill, spin) in a board game format.
- With imitation and backward chaining, child produced several accurate or close approximations of these blends. Accuracy was better for /st/ ("stop," "stairs") than for /sp/.
- Short phrases like "stop it" and "spill it" were practiced with model + imitation; child required moderate cues but demonstrated clearer blends than in earlier sessions.

3) Final consonants – CVC words and phrases
- Continued practicing CVC words with final /t/ and /p/ (hat, hot, cup, up, seat, nap) within short phrases ("my hat," "big cup," "hot soup").
- Child produced more final consonants accurately in imitation but continued to omit them frequently in spontaneous speech.
- Compared to the last session, performance was similar, suggesting a period of consolidation rather than large changes.

4) Intelligibility in spontaneous play
- During free play, child used a number of spontaneous /s/ words and short sentences with recognizable /s/ sounds, particularly when labeling or requesting.
- Intelligibility to the clinician was higher for practiced contexts; less familiar words and longer utterances remained more challenging.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to strengthen initial /s/ at sentence and early conversational levels, focusing on naturalistic contexts.
- Maintain focused practice for /s/ blends and final consonants; consider adding visual reminders for final sounds at home.
- Home practice: caregivers to encourage the child to use sentences with /s/ during daily routines (e.g., "I see the sun," "My sock is on the bed") and to practice a few /st/ words ("stop," "stairs") slowly with clear models.
notes/10/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother reported that the child’s speech is noticeably clearer to family members, and the child is more confident when speaking.
- She still observes dropped sounds at the ends of words, especially when the child is excited.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered the session cheerfully and easily separated from caregiver.
- Showed sustained participation in both structured and play tasks, and responded well to feedback about "using your best sounds."

Therapy goals and observations:
1) Initial /s/ – sentences and semi-structured conversation
- Practiced initial /s/ within longer sentences during picture description and simple story retell (e.g., "The sun is shining on the sand," "The silly sock is on the bed").
- Child maintained accurate initial /s/ in most target words with minimal verbal cueing, sometimes producing correct /s/ independently.
- In semi-structured conversation about the story, child used multiple spontaneous /s/ words and short sentences with good clarity, reflecting stable gains.

2) /s/ blends – expansion of blend set
- Targeted a slightly larger set of /s/ blends (/st/, /sp/, /sk/; stop, stairs, star, spoon, spill, spin, sky, skate) in a game format.
- Child produced several accurate or near-accurate blends in imitation, with ongoing need for slowed rate and backward chaining. /st/ remained the strongest, with /sp/ and /sk/ more variable.
- Some short phrases ("stop it," "spill it," "sky is blue") were attempted with partially correct blends. Overall performance was consistent with last session, with gradual refinement.

3) Final consonants – CVC words and phrases
- Continued practice of CVC words with final /t/ and /p/ within phrases and short sentences ("my hat," "hot soup," "big cup," "I sit down").
- Child produced more final consonant attempts in imitation and occasionally in short prompted phrases, but spontaneous use of final sounds remained inconsistent.
- Performance was similar to previous weeks, indicating an ongoing focus area.

4) Intelligibility and functional communication
- In free play and conversation about favorite activities, child’s speech was generally more intelligible to the clinician, particularly for practiced /s/ words and blends.
- Longer, less structured utterances still contained some sound omissions and cluster reductions, but overall the child was easier to understand than at the start of the block.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue reinforcing accurate initial /s/ and /s/ blends in more naturalistic sentence and conversation contexts.
- Maintain targeted work on final consonants and consider increasing parent coaching strategies for this area.
- Home practice: caregivers to highlight and praise clear /s/ words and blends during daily interactions and to practice a few CVC words with final /t/ and /p/ (e.g., "hat," "hot," "cup," "up") using slow models and a tapping cue for the last sound.
notes/11/note_text:
Parent/caregiver report:
- Mother stated that she is pleased with the child’s progress, especially with "s" sounds, and feels the child is easier to understand overall.
- She continues to notice occasional dropped sounds at the ends of words but reports that the child is starting to try to "fix" some words independently.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly, appeared comfortable and confident, and requested favorite therapy games.
- Demonstrated strong participation, good turn-taking, and openness to feedback about speech sounds.

Therapy goals and observations:
1) Initial /s/ – conversational use
- Practiced initial /s/ within structured conversation about pictures and daily routines (e.g., talking about the sun, socks, sand, soup).
- Child consistently produced accurate initial /s/ in familiar words with minimal to no cueing. Occasional self-corrections were observed when productions were less clear.
- In conversational exchanges, the child used multiple /s/ words and short sentences with good intelligibility, indicating functional carryover of this skill.

2) /s/ blends – word and short sentence levels
- Continued targeting /s/ blends (/st/, /sp/, /sk/) within a story-based game (stop, stairs, star, spoon, spill, spin, sky, skate).
- Child produced several accurate or close approximations of /st/ blends and some improved productions of /sp/ and /sk/ in imitation with cues for slow rate and segmenting.
- Short sentences such as "The star is big," "I go up the stairs," and "The spoon will spill" were attempted with variable but generally improving blend accuracy.

3) Final consonants – CVC words and short sentences
- Practiced final /t/ and /p/ in CVC words (hat, hot, seat, cup, up, nap) embedded in short sentences ("My hat is on," "The soup is hot," "Big cup").
- Child produced more accurate final consonants in imitation and some prompted phrases, though spontaneous final consonant use remained inconsistent.
- This area continues to show emerging improvement and remains a target for ongoing intervention.

4) Overall intelligibility and next steps
- In spontaneous play and conversation, the child’s overall intelligibility to the clinician was noticeably better than at the beginning of the block, especially for practiced /s/ targets.
- Residual difficulties include inconsistent final consonants and some cluster reductions in less familiar words, but the child is demonstrating increased self-monitoring and willingness to repair communication breakdowns.

Plan/recommendations:
- Recommend continued speech-language therapy to further support /s/ blends and final consonant production, and to promote generalization of skills to all conversation contexts.
- Continue home practice with short sentences featuring initial /s/ and selected /s/ blends, along with focused practice for CVC words with final /t/ and /p/ using tapping cues.
- Encourage caregivers to provide specific praise for clear sounds and gentle reminders to "finish the word" when final consonants are omitted.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
- Parent shared that they are most concerned about child's "mumbled" words and that unfamiliar listeners have difficulty understanding them.
- No formal home practice yet; parent notices child often leaves off the ends of words (e.g., "ca" for car, "do" for dog).

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned to the therapy room with some hesitation but separated from parent with reassurance.
- Engaged well with toys and books once settled; required gentle redirection at times to attend to speech tasks.
- Responded positively to praise and stickers.

Goals / session focus:
1. Baseline for speech sound production
- Informal play-based sample collected. Child produced mostly single words and short 2–3 word phrases.
- Frequent final consonant deletion noted (e.g., "boo" for book, "pi" for pig).
- Inconsistent production of /k/ and /g/ (often fronted to /t/ and /d/, e.g., "tar" for car).
- Occasional difficulty with initial /s/ and /f/ (substitutions and omissions), though not formally targeted.

2. Final consonant production (CVC words) – exploration
- Introduced final /p, t, m/ in simple CVC words at the imitation level (e.g., "map," "cup," "ham").
- Child was able to imitate final consonants in isolation with strong visual cues (tapping the last sound) in a few trials but tended to drop them when attempting the whole word.
- Accuracy at the word level was low and inconsistent; heavy modeling and segmentation were needed.

3. Initial /k/ – exploration in isolation and simple syllables
- Practiced “back sound” /k/ in isolation with mirror and verbal cues (“tongue up and back”).
- Child could approximate /k/ in isolation in some trials but often reverted to /t/.
- When attempting CV syllables ("koo," "key"), productions frequently sounded closer to /t/.

4. Functional phrase use
- Encouraged child to use short functional phrases ("more please," "my turn," "help me") during play.
- Child often relied on single words or pointing; with modeling, was able to imitate 2–3 word phrases in most opportunities.

Plan / recommendations:
- Begin structured work on final consonants and initial /k/ at the imitation level.
- Parent encouraged to model slow, clear CVC words in play (e.g., "cup," "map," "bed"), emphasizing the last sound with light tapping.
- Continue to support short functional phrases at home (e.g., “more bubbles,” “my turn”) with models and gentle prompts.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
- Parent reported no major changes since last week but is noticing more attempts at longer phrases at home.
- Still concerned about words being “chopped off” at the end.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Smooth transition into the room today; greeted clinician with a quiet "hi" when prompted.
- Sat at the table for short periods and then preferred floor play; responded well to alternating between play and structured tasks.
- Attention to tasks was adequate with occasional reminders.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants in CVC words (imitation)
- Targeted final /p, t, m/ in picture naming and play (e.g., "cup," "cat," "ham," "map").
- With direct imitation and visual cueing (pointing to the final sound), child produced final consonants more consistently than last session.
- Still omitted final sounds in many spontaneous attempts, but was able to add them when reminded in several trials.

2. Initial /k/ at isolation and syllable level
- Practiced /k/ in isolation using mirror and verbal cues about “back sound.”
- Child produced clearer /k/ in isolation today with moderate verbal and visual cues.
- Attempted CV syllables ("key," "cow," "car") in imitation; accuracy remained inconsistent, with frequent /t/ substitutions, but there were several recognizable /k/ productions across the session.

3. Early functional phrases (2–3 words)
- Continued to encourage use of short phrases such as "more bubbles," "my turn," "help me." 
- Child imitated 2-word phrases in many opportunities when given a direct model; occasionally produced 2-word combinations spontaneously during play (e.g., "my car," "more bubbles").

4. Observational monitoring of /s/ and /f/
- During play, child sometimes substituted or omitted /s/ and /f/ in initial position (e.g., "un" for sun, "ish" for fish), but these were not formally drilled.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue working on final consonants and initial /k/ at the imitation level, as child is beginning to show more consistent approximations.
- Parent encouraged to emphasize final sounds in everyday words and to praise any attempts.
- Continue modeling short functional phrases and give child time to imitate before giving the item.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
- Parent noted that child is starting to copy some of the CVC words practiced last week (e.g., "cup," "cat") but often drops the last sound when speaking quickly.
- Parent has been using hand-tapping cues for the end of the word as suggested.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered the therapy room more confidently and quickly engaged with preferred toys.
- Good participation in both table-top and floor-based activities; needed only minimal redirection.
- Appeared motivated by turn-taking games and verbal praise.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants in CVC words (imitation → emerging cued independence)
- Targeted final /p, t, m/ in CVC words using picture cards and play routines.
- Compared to last week, child produced final consonants with higher consistency when provided with light visual or gestural cues (pointing to mouth, tapping last sound).
- In some trials, child independently produced the final consonant without a full model after an initial cue.
- Still tends to omit final sounds in spontaneous speech, but is beginning to self-correct occasionally when reminded.

2. Initial /k/ at syllable and word level (imitation)
- Practiced /k/ in CV syllables and initial position of simple words ("key," "car," "cow," "cup").
- With moderate visual and verbal cues, child was able to produce initial /k/ accurately in many imitated trials today.
- Fewer /t/ substitutions were noted compared to previous session; child occasionally produced /k/ in play when naming familiar items after a model.

3. Early functional phrases (2–3 words) – increasing independence
- Encouraged use of 2–3 word phrases to request and comment (e.g., "more bubbles," "big car," "my turn").
- Child imitated these phrases in most opportunities and produced a few short phrases independently during structured play.
- Speech clarity in phrases was variable, with continued final consonant omissions.

4. Informal monitoring of /f/ initial
- During play, child attempted a few /f/-initial words ("fish," "funny"). Productions were sometimes approximated (e.g., using /p/ or omitting the sound), but there were occasional clearer /f/ productions with clinician modeling.

Plan / recommendations:
- Given improved consistency, continue with final consonants in CVC words and initial /k/ at the imitated word level; begin to fade cues when possible.
- Encourage parent to pause and let child try to add the "end sound" after a gentle cue rather than repeating the whole word.
- Continue supporting short phrases in daily routines, praising any clearer productions.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
- Parent shared that child said "cup" and "bed" more clearly this week when reminded to "say the end." 
- Reports that some family members have commented that a few words are easier to understand, though overall intelligibility is still reduced.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child separated from parent without difficulty and joined the session with interest.
- Stayed engaged for longer stretches in structured tasks, especially when activities were game-like.
- Some silliness noted but easily redirected back to task.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants in CVC words (imitation and cued production)
- Continued targeting final /p, t, m, n/ in CVC words through picture naming and play.
- Child produced final consonants with increasing consistency in imitation and with light cues (e.g., “say the last sound”) compared to prior sessions.
- Demonstrated several instances of adding a final consonant after a simple reminder rather than a full model.
- Spontaneous speech still showed frequent omissions, but there were a few unprompted correct final consonants in familiar words.

2. Initial /k/ at word level (imitation) and early phrases
- Practiced initial /k/ in single words ("car," "cow," "cat," "key") and short phrases ("go car," "my car").
- Child showed more consistent /k/ productions with moderate cues; fewer substitutions to /t/ were heard.
- In short phrases, /k/ accuracy decreased slightly compared to single words but remained improved overall.

3. Introduction of initial /f/ at isolation/syllable level
- Introduced /f/ as a “lip-bite air” sound using mirror and visual cues.
- Child was able to imitate /f/ in isolation with support but tended to add a vowel or change to /p/ without cues.
- CV syllables ("fee," "foo") were attempted in a small number of trials with mixed accuracy.

4. Functional 2–3 word phrases
- Continued to model and elicit functional phrases for requesting and commenting.
- Child imitated 2–3 word phrases readily and produced several independently during play (e.g., "more bubbles," "my car," "no more").
- Sound accuracy within phrases remains an area of focus, with ongoing final consonant and some initial consonant errors.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain focus on final consonants and initial /k/ at the imitated word level, gradually introducing more phrase-level practice as tolerated.
- Continue gentle introduction of /f/ at the isolation and simple syllable level.
- Parent encouraged to model /k/ and final consonants in daily routines and to use simple reminders like "say the end" without over-correcting every word.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent report:
- Parent indicated that child is starting to notice when words "sound funny" and sometimes tries again, especially with words like "car" and "cup."
- Reports more frequent use of short phrases at home, though some words are still hard to understand.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child was cheerful and eager to show toys brought from home.
- Participated well in both structured drills and play-based tasks; occasional rest breaks were helpful.
- Followed simple directions and responded to visual schedules.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants in CVC words (word level → beginning phrase level)
- Targeted final /p, t, m, n/ in a variety of CVC words using games and picture activities.
- Child demonstrated noticeably higher consistency in producing final consonants in imitated words with only light cues.
- Introduced simple carrier phrases ("my ___," "I see ___") for a subset of words; accuracy of final consonants decreased somewhat in phrases but remained better than in earlier sessions.
- Child occasionally self-corrected when omitting a final sound after clinician paused and used a visual cue.

2. Initial /k/ at word and simple phrase level
- Continued practice of initial /k/ words ("car," "cow," "cup," "coat") and short phrases ("big car," "my cup").
- Child produced initial /k/ with improved accuracy across trials, often requiring only a verbal reminder to use the “back sound.”
- A few spontaneous /k/ productions were heard in play when naming familiar items.

3. Initial /f/ in isolation and simple words (imitation)
- Practiced /f/ in isolation and in a small set of words ("fish," "fun," "food") at the imitation level.
- With mirror and visual cues, child produced /f/ more clearly than last session, though consistency is still emerging.
- Occasional substitutions or omissions were noted when cues were reduced.

4. Functional phrases and intelligibility in play
- Encouraged child to use 2–3 word phrases to request, protest, and comment during play.
- Child produced multiple short phrases independently; intelligibility varied depending on sound accuracy.
- Clinician modeled slightly longer 3–4 word phrases for exposure.

Plan / recommendations:
- Given moderate gains, continue to build phrase-level practice for final consonants and initial /k/ while monitoring accuracy.
- Maintain focused practice on /f/ in isolation and a small set of words.
- Parent encouraged to incorporate carrier phrases like "I see ___" and "my ___" at home, emphasizing final sounds when possible without interrupting communication.
notes/5/note_text:
Parent report:

- No concerns about changes in hearing or health this week.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly and eagerly participated in familiar routines.
- Attention and cooperation were generally good, though occasional distractibility occurred near the end of the session.
- Responded well to choices and visual supports.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants in CVC words and simple phrases
- Continued work on final /p, t, m, n/ in CVC words and short phrases (e.g., "my cup," "big cat").
- Performance was similar to last week: solid accuracy in imitated single words with light cues, with decreased consistency in phrases and spontaneous speech.
- Child occasionally added final consonants independently, but still frequently omitted them without prompts.

2. Initial /k/ in words and short phrases
- Practiced initial /k/ in familiar words and phrases using picture scenes and play.
- Accuracy remained stable compared to the previous session: generally good productions with minimal cues in single words and variable accuracy in phrases.
- Fewer fronting errors were observed in structured tasks; however, fronting still appeared during fast, connected speech.

3. Initial /f/ in isolation and word level (imitation)
- Continued practice of /f/ in isolation and in simple words ("fish," "fun," "feet").
- Child showed similar performance to last session, needing mirror and verbal reminders to sustain the correct lip position.
- Some correct productions were noted, but consistency remains emerging.

4. Functional phrases and intelligibility monitoring
- Child used multiple 2–3 word phrases spontaneously during play.
- Clinician modeled slightly longer 3–4 word sentences; child imitated some but required models and did not yet maintain sound accuracy consistently.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain current focus on consolidating final consonants and initial /k/ at word and early phrase levels.
- Continue structured practice for /f/ with high visual support.
- Parent encouraged to keep modeling final sounds and praising attempts, while allowing child to complete their message before correcting.
notes/6/note_text:
Parent report:
- Parent shared that child’s talking is "about the same" as last week; some words like "cup" and "bed" are clearer when they remind child to "use the end sound."
- Parent has been practicing /k/ words in the car and at mealtimes.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered the room happily and engaged in greeting routines.
- Participated well in structured tasks with a few brief episodes of silliness; responded to visual countdowns and turn-taking.
- Overall regulation and attention were adequate for age.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants in CVC words and short phrases
- Practiced final /p, t, m, n/ using board games and picture scenes.
- Accuracy in imitated single words with light cues remained consistent with prior sessions.
- In short phrases ("my cup," "big cat"), child continued to omit some final consonants unless reminded; a few spontaneous correct final consonants were noted.

2. Initial /k/ at word and phrase level
- Continued targeting /k/ in initial position within familiar words and 2–3 word phrases.
- Child generally produced /k/ accurately in imitated single words with minimal prompts, with variable carryover into phrases.
- Fronting errors persisted during fast or excited speech.

3. Initial /f/ at isolation and word level
- Practiced /f/ in isolation and in a limited set of words ("fish," "funny," "five").
- Child tolerated mirror work and could imitate /f/ in some trials but still required moderate verbal and visual cues.
- No clear change in consistency compared to last week, though effort and participation were good.

4. Functional phrases/sentences
- Child used many 2–3 word utterances spontaneously in play.
- Clinician modeled and elicited a few 3–4 word sentences; child imitated some but speech clarity varied due to sound errors.

Plan / recommendations:

- Maintain parent coaching on modeling final sounds and /k/ words in natural routines.
- Consider introducing more structured phrase-level tasks for final consonants once consistency increases.
notes/7/note_text:

- Parent has been less able to practice this week due to family schedule.

Behaviour/engagement:

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants in CVC words and phrases

2. Initial /k/ at word and phrase level

notes/8/note_text:

notes/9/note_text:

- They feel child is "holding steady"—no big changes, but also no regression.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered session willingly and participated in greeting routine.
- Maintained engagement in structured tasks for age-appropriate durations, with brief play breaks as needed.
- Followed directions and responded to positive feedback.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonants in CVC words and phrases
- Practiced final /p, t, m, n/ in CVC words and simple phrases ("I want ___," "see the ___").
- Accuracy in imitated single words with light cues remained similar to recent sessions.
- In phrases, child continued to omit final consonants intermittently, but was usually able to correct them when prompted.

2. Initial /k/ at word and phrase level
