notes/8/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver reported that the child is still using familiar practiced phrases ("my ball," "more bubbles") and has added a few new ones like "my car." They feel speech is stable compared with the last visit, with ongoing difficulty for people who do not know the child well.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child separated easily and engaged in shared play. Showed interest in picture cards when they were paired with preferred toys. Attention and cooperation were similar to previous sessions; the child responded well to turn-taking and praise.

Goals / session focus:
1. Bilabials (/p, b, m/) in 2–3 word phrases
- Continued work with phrases such as "my big ball," "more bubbles," and "my mommy." The child consistently produced initial bilabials in imitation and frequently did so independently during play. Overall phrase structure and clarity were comparable to recent sessions, with mostly 2-word combinations and some approximated 3-word attempts.

2. Final /p, m/ in CVC words (imitation)
- Practiced final /p, m/ with familiar items ("pop, up, map, mop, mom, ham"). The child continued to need modeling and explicit cues to close lips at the end. A mix of clear, weak, and omitted final consonants was observed, with no clear trend toward greater independence since last session.

3. Initial /t/ in simple words (imitation)
- Short drill with /t/ words ("tea, two, toe, top"). With direct tongue-tip cues and mirror support, the child produced a small number of accurate /t/ sounds, but overall performance remained variable and heavily supported, similar to prior visits.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue current targets with an emphasis on stability and confidence in practiced words and phrases.
- Encourage caregiver to maintain home practice with bilabial words and to gently cue for final sounds without pressuring the child.
- Next session: remain at the same hierarchy levels; re-evaluate if any new spontaneous final consonants or clearer /t/ productions begin to emerge.
notes/9/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver shared that the child is "talking a lot" and using many short phrases, but they do not feel that overall clarity has changed significantly since the last appointment. They continue to hear missing endings on words.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child came in with good energy and participated in both floor play and brief structured tasks. Attention was adequate with occasional prompts. The child enjoyed turn-taking games and accepted gentle corrections.

Goals / session focus:
1. Bilabials (/p, b, m/) in 2–3 word phrases
- Practiced familiar phrases ("my ball," "more bubbles," "big pop," "my mommy"). The child maintained strong accuracy for initial bilabials in both imitation and many spontaneous productions. Phrase length and structure were similar to previous sessions.

2. Final /p, m/ in CVC words (imitation)
- Continued working on final /p, m/ in the usual set of words ("pop, up, map, mop, mom, ham"). The child still required explicit modeling and lip cues. Some clearly marked final consonants were heard, but omissions and reductions remained common, with performance largely mirroring earlier sessions.

3. Initial /t/ in CV/CVC words (imitation)
- Targeted /t/ in a few high-interest words ("tea, two, top"). With mirror support and tongue-tip prompts, the child produced occasional accurate /t/ but continued to show many substitutions and omissions. Level of cueing and overall consistency were comparable to recent sessions.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain current focus on consolidating bilabials in short phrases and building awareness of final consonants.
- At home, caregiver can continue modeling slow, clear words and phrases, emphasizing both the beginning and ending sounds.
- Next session: repeat similar targets and monitor for any emerging changes before adjusting the hierarchy.
notes/10/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver reported that the child continues to use many short phrases and is generally well understood by close family. They have not noticed a big shift in how clearly the child is saying endings, though they do hear more attempts when they remind the child to "finish the word."

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly and was eager to show familiar toys. Participation in clinician-led tasks was steady with occasional prompts to stay seated. Overall behaviour and engagement were similar to prior visits.

Goals / session focus:
1. Bilabials (/p, b, m/) in functional phrases
- Reused common phrases ("my ball," "more bubbles," "my mommy," "big pop"). The child consistently produced initial bilabials in both imitated and many spontaneous phrases. Complexity and accuracy of phrases were comparable to recent sessions, with ongoing omission of some unstressed words and endings.

2. Final /p, m/ in CVC words (imitation)
- Practiced final /p, m/ in the standard set of words ("pop, up, map, mop, mom, ham"). The child required similar levels of modeling and cueing as in previous sessions. A mix of accurate and omitted final consonants was observed, with no clear change in independence.

3. Initial /t/ in simple words (imitation)
- Short practice with /t/ ("tea, two, top"). With visual and verbal cues, the child produced a few recognizable /t/ sounds, but there was still frequent substitution or omission. Performance remained at an emerging level with consistent need for support.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue current goals and practice routines to solidify skills.
- Encourage caregiver to keep using a small set of practice words at home, praising any effort to include final sounds.
- Next session: maintain the same hierarchy level and targets, reassessing for any gradual improvements across sessions.
notes/11/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver indicated that the child "sounds about the same" as in recent weeks. They continue to notice many clear bilabial sounds at the start of words but ongoing difficulty with endings and some other consonants. No new concerns raised.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child was cooperative and playful. Participated in both structured tasks and free play. Required occasional prompts to focus on the clinician's face during speech practice but overall engagement was consistent with previous sessions.

Goals / session focus:
1. Bilabials (/p, b, m/) in 2–3 word phrases
- Continued using familiar phrases ("my ball," "more bubbles," "my mommy," "big pop"). The child maintained strong accuracy for initial bilabials in both imitation and spontaneous use. Phrase complexity and overall clarity were similar to recent sessions.

2. Final /p, m/ in CVC words (imitation)
- Practiced final /p, m/ in the typical word set ("pop, up, map, mop, mom, ham"). The child still needed modeling and explicit lip cues to produce final consonants. A combination of clear, weak, and omitted endings was observed, consistent with prior performance.

3. Initial /t/ in CV/CVC words (imitation)
- Briefly targeted /t/ ("tea, two, top"). With direct tongue-tip cues and mirror, the child produced a few accurate /t/ sounds but continued to show frequent substitutions and omissions. Level of independence and cueing needs were similar to previous sessions.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to prioritize consolidation of current skills rather than advancing the hierarchy at this time.
- Recommend caregiver keep modeling slow, clear words and short phrases at home, emphasizing beginnings and endings, and provide positive feedback for any attempts at final consonants.
- Future sessions can maintain this focus, with periodic checks for emerging readiness to expand final consonant work or increase phrase complexity.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent shared that they continue to notice difficulty understanding child’s words, especially with longer words and when talking quickly. No major changes since the initial visit. Parent is motivated to work on “clearer sounds” at home.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child transitioned with some hesitation but settled quickly with preferred toys. Demonstrated good joint attention in play for 5–7 minutes at a time with light redirection. Imitation was best when activities were highly motivating (bubbles, cars).

Goals / observations:
1. Initial /f/ at syllable and word level
- Practiced CV syllables ("faa, fee, foo") in imitation. Child approximated /f/ with lip rounding but often omitted the airflow ("haa" or "baa"). With maximal verbal and visual cues ("bite lip, blow"), achieved a few clear /f/ productions in isolation and in "feet," "fish," and "fun" in imitation. Accuracy was low and inconsistent; required frequent modeling.

2. Initial /s/ in isolation and simple syllables
- Focused on /s/ in isolation using mirror and “snake sound” cue. Child could sustain a quiet airflow but often added a vowel ("suh"). Needed maximal visual and verbal support to keep tongue behind teeth. Limited success with CV syllables ("see, soo"); productions frequently distorted or substituted with /t/ or /sh/.

3. Simple CV and CVCV syllable shapes
- Targeted early consonants /m, b, p/ in CV and CVCV ("mama, baba, popo") in imitation. Child imitated several CV syllables accurately; CVCV productions were often reduced to one syllable or with unclear consonants. Required frequent modeling and pacing support.

4. Functional single-word imitations in play
- Encouraged imitation of familiar words during play ("ball, up, go, more"). Child attempted several but consonants were often omitted or replaced (e.g., "all" for "ball"). Needed direct models and excited affect to participate.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to build consistency with /f/ and /s/ at isolation and simple syllable levels with high support.
- Encourage parent to model slow, clear speech and emphasize early consonants in simple words (e.g., "more," "ball," "up").
- Provide parent with ideas for short play routines (bubbles, cars) to practice imitating 3–5 words per activity without pressure.
- Next session: continue isolation and CV work for /f/ and /s/; monitor spontaneous consonant use in play.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent noted that child is “trying more words,” but many are still hard to understand. Parent has been using the “snake sound” idea at home but finds it difficult to get child to slow down.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child entered the room more readily today and quickly engaged with toys. Attention to adult-led tasks improved; child remained with structured activities for short intervals with gentle reminders. Enjoyed mirror work briefly before moving back to play.

Goals / observations:
1. Initial /f/ at syllable and word level
- Continued CV syllables and simple words in imitation. Compared to last session, child produced more recognizable /f/ sounds when given the “bite lip and blow” cue. Still required frequent cues, but there were more spontaneous attempts at /f/ when looking in the mirror. Some clearer productions in "fish," "fun," and "four" in imitation, though accuracy remained variable.

2. Initial /s/ in isolation and CV
- Practiced /s/ in isolation and in "see, soo, saa" with mirror. Child showed better control of airflow, with fewer added vowels. Still needed significant verbal prompts to keep tongue behind teeth. A few more consistent approximations of /s/ in isolation were noted compared with prior session.

3. CV and CVCV syllable shapes
- Revisited /m, b, p/ in CV and CVCV. Child demonstrated slightly more consistent two-syllable productions (e.g., "mama," "baba"), with both syllables audible in several attempts. Some consonant blurring remained, but child needed slightly less repetition of models.

4. Functional word imitations in play
- Encouraged imitation of target words during play ("ball, more, up, go, mine"). Child imitated several of these with similar clarity to last session, though there were a few extra attempts without direct prompting. Consonant errors persisted but child appeared more willing to try.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue current targets; child is showing early but modest gains in consistency.
- Coach parent on using brief, fun mirror activities at home for /f/ and /s/, keeping practice to very short bursts.
- Encourage labeling of favorite items ("ball, car, bubbles") with slow models and waiting for child’s attempt.
- Next session: maintain focus at isolation and CV levels for /f/ and /s/ while monitoring for readiness to increase word-level trials as accuracy improves.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent reported a noticeable increase in child’s attempts to copy words at home, including trying to say names of family members. Parent feels some words are “much clearer” than a few weeks ago, but still struggles with certain sounds.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child transitioned smoothly and eagerly joined the clinician at the table with preferred toys. Maintained attention to structured practice for longer periods today and responded well to turn-taking games. Overall participation was strong with minimal redirection.

Goals / observations:
1. Initial /f/ at word level (imitation)
- Child demonstrated clear progress. After brief warm-up in isolation, practiced initial /f/ in words ("fish, feet, fun, fall, four"). With verbal cueing only (less need for tactile/visual prompts), child produced recognizable /f/ in most imitated words. A few spontaneous approximations of /f/ occurred in play (e.g., "fish" during puzzle), though not yet consistent.

2. Initial /s/ at syllable and word level (imitation)
- Practiced /s/ in isolation and in CV ("see, soo") and early word-level trials ("sun, soup, sock"). Child was able to sustain /s/ with better tongue placement and fewer distortions than previous sessions. Accuracy in imitated CVs improved, and child produced several clear /s/ in initial position with moderate verbal cues. Still required direct models but overall productions were more accurate.

3. CV, CVCV, and emerging CVC words
- Child showed more consistent production of CVCV ("mama, papa, baby") with both syllables marked in many attempts. Introduced a few simple CVC words ("mom, pop, map") in imitation. Child approximated these, though final consonants were sometimes omitted. Participation and willingness to try were high.

4. Functional words and short imitative phrases
- During play, child imitated a wider variety of single words (e.g., "more, open, go, mine, up") and a few early two-word combinations ("more bubbles," "go car") after models. Intelligibility remained variable but improved compared to earlier notes, particularly for familiar routines.

Plan / recommendations:
- Shift more of the /f/ practice to consistent word-level imitation while still supporting with cues as needed.
- Gradually increase /s/ word-level targets as tolerated, keeping practice fun and brief.
- Encourage parent to model and pause for child to imitate key words and simple two-word phrases during daily routines.
- Next session: continue building consistency at word level for /f/ and /s/, and expand early two-word combinations in play.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent shared that child is now saying “fish” and “feet” at home in familiar routines and that siblings can sometimes understand these words. Parent still notices difficulty with /s/ in words like “sock” and “sun.”

Behaviour/engagement:
Child separated from parent easily and appeared excited to see familiar materials. Sustained attention to therapist-directed tasks was good today, with only brief moments of distraction. Child was cheerful and cooperative, responding well to praise.

Goals / observations:
1. Initial /f/ at word and emerging carrier phrase level
- Child continued to perform well with initial /f/ in imitation at word level ("fish, feet, fun, food"). Accuracy was noticeably higher than prior sessions, with many clear productions given minimal verbal cueing. Introduced simple carrier phrases ("my fish," "more fish") in imitation. Child was able to maintain /f/ in the word within short phrases for several attempts, though speech rate and clarity varied.

2. Initial /s/ at word level (imitation)
- Focused on initial /s/ words ("sun, soap, sock, soup"). Child produced more accurate /s/ in imitation than in previous sessions, though there were still substitutions with /t/ or a distorted /s/ on some trials. Moderate verbal and visual cues (mirror, “snake sound”) were used. Accuracy and consistency improved within the session, but child is not yet ready for independent phrase-level work.

3. Early final consonants in CVC words
- Targeted simple CVC words with early consonants ("map, mop, mom, pop"). Child attempted final consonants in imitation with variable success; some words were reduced ("ma" for "map"). With slowed rate and tapping cues, final consonants emerged more frequently.

4. Functional two-word combinations in play
- Encouraged imitation of two-word phrases such as "more bubbles," "go car," "my turn." Child imitated several combinations with increasing ease, though individual sounds were not always clear. Phrase length is expanding, with growing willingness to combine words after a model.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to strengthen /f/ in words and short carrier phrases, gradually reducing cueing.
- Maintain focus on /s/ in initial position at imitated word level until productions become more consistent.
- Support parent in encouraging two-word combinations during play (e.g., "more + item," "go + place").
- Next session: continue current targets; consider more phrase-level /f/ practice if accuracy remains high.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent reported that child is using “my fish,” “more food,” and similar short phrases at home, though clarity varies. Parent has noticed some attempts at /s/ words but says these are “hit or miss.”

Behaviour/engagement:
Child entered willingly and quickly sought out familiar toys. Tolerated brief table-top practice and transitioned between play-based and structured tasks with light support. Participation was steady, with some playful silliness that responded well to gentle redirection.

Goals / observations:
1. Initial /f/ in words and short phrases (imitation and emerging spontaneous use)
- Child continued to show solid performance with initial /f/ at the imitated word level. Many productions of "fish, feet, fun" were clear with only light verbal reminders. In play, child independently used /f/ in a few spontaneous words (e.g., "fish" while pointing), though not consistently. Phrase-level practice ("my fish," "big fish") remained mostly imitative, with occasional approximations without a direct model.

2. Initial /s/ at word level (imitation)
- Practiced initial /s/ words ("sun, sock, soup, see"). Accuracy showed a modest improvement compared to prior session, with more clear /s/ productions when child was focused. However, substitutions and distortions were still frequent, and moderate cueing (mirror, verbal placement prompts) was required. Not yet ready to reliably carry /s/ into phrases.

3. Final consonants in simple CVC words
- Continued targeting final consonants ("map, mop, mom, pop, up"). Child marked final consonants in a slightly higher number of attempts than previously, especially with slower rate and visual cues (tapping). Some omissions remained, but there was a small increase in consistency.

4. Two-word combinations in play
- Child imitated and occasionally initiated two-word phrases ("more bubbles," "my car," "go up") during play. Length and variety of combinations are gradually increasing, though articulation errors within the phrases remain.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain current focus on strengthening consistency of /f/ in words and short phrases; continue to encourage any spontaneous /f/ use.
- Continue practicing /s/ in initial position at imitated word level with placement cues, aiming for gradually more accurate productions before moving to phrases.
- Encourage parent to notice and praise any clear final consonants (e.g., in "up," "pop") and short phrases at home.
- Next session: similar targets, monitoring for readiness to expand spontaneous phrase-level use for /f/ if consistency continues to build.
notes/5/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent shared that child’s "f" words (e.g., "fish," "food") are sounding much clearer and that family members are understanding these more often. Parent still finds /s/ words challenging to understand but feels overall speech is “a bit clearer.”

Behaviour/engagement:
Child transitioned smoothly and was eager to participate in familiar routines. Demonstrated good sustained attention for short structured drills embedded in play. Followed simple directions and responded positively to verbal praise and stickers.

Goals / observations:
1. Initial /f/ in words and short phrases (increasing independence)
- Child produced initial /f/ in a wide variety of words ("fish, feet, fall, four, fun, food") with high accuracy in imitation and many successful spontaneous productions during play. Required only occasional verbal reminders for placement. In simple carrier phrases ("my fish," "more food," "big feet"), child often maintained clear /f/ without additional cues, indicating a stable skill at this level.

2. Initial /s/ at word level (imitation)
- Continued initial /s/ words ("sun, soap, sock, soup, see"). Child produced more consistent /s/ than in earlier sessions but still needed moderate cueing. Some words were clear on multiple trials, while others showed substitutions (e.g., /t/ or distorted /s/). Compared to previous notes, improvements are present but still modest.

3. Final consonants in simple CVC words
- Practiced CVC words ("pop, map, mop, up, bus"). Child marked final consonants more frequently than in earlier sessions when given slowed rate and visual tapping cues. However, omissions remained in spontaneous attempts. Skill is emerging but not yet stable.

4. Two- to three-word combinations in play
- Child imitated and occasionally generated short phrases ("more bubbles," "my turn," "go up," "big car"). Phrase length is gradually increasing, and child appears more comfortable combining words. Sound accuracy within phrases is still variable but overall intelligibility is slightly improved in structured contexts.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain /f/ practice at phrase level with focus on generalizing to more spontaneous use across activities; begin to reduce cueing as tolerated.
- Continue working on initial /s/ at word level; once accuracy becomes more reliable, trial simple phrases.
- Support parent in recognizing clear /f/ productions and short phrases at home and responding with positive feedback.
- Next session: continue consolidation of /f/ in phrases and further practice of /s/ words and final consonants.
notes/6/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent reported that child continues to use clear "f" words in routines (e.g., asking for "food" or talking about "fish"), and this seems similar to last week. Parent has not noticed big changes with /s/ yet but feels child is talking more overall.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child entered the room with minimal prompting and engaged readily in familiar play-based activities. Attention and participation were similar to the prior session. Responded well to visual supports and short, predictable routines.

Goals / observations:
1. Initial /f/ in words and short phrases (maintenance)
- Child maintained strong performance with initial /f/ in both imitated and spontaneous words ("fish, feet, fall, fun, food"). In simple phrases ("my fish," "more food"), /f/ remained clear in most attempts without additional cueing. This represents stable maintenance of the skill rather than a notable jump from last session.

2. Initial /s/ at word level (imitation)
- Continued practice with initial /s/ words ("sun, sock, soap, see, soup"). Accuracy was similar to the previous note: some clearly produced /s/ sounds with moderate cues, along with ongoing substitutions and distortions. No clear shift in cueing level or independence was observed today.

3. Final consonants in simple CVC words
- Practiced CVC words ("pop, map, bus, up"). Child demonstrated similar performance to last session: final consonants were present in a portion of imitated attempts with support for pacing, but omissions occurred frequently in less supported trials.

4. Short phrases in play
- Child continued to use two- and some three-word combinations in structured play (e.g., "more bubbles," "my big car," "go up there") with similar clarity to the prior session. Phrase length and participation are stable.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to consolidate /f/ at phrase level and begin to look for more spontaneous use across different games and contexts.
- Maintain focus on /s/ at word level with consistent placement cues; consider adding visual supports (e.g., picture cards) to enhance engagement.
- Encourage parent to continue noticing and praising clear /f/ words and short phrases, and to gently model /s/ words during daily routines.
- Next session: similar targets with emphasis on consistency rather than advancing levels.
notes/7/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent shared that child is now using short phrases more frequently at home (e.g., “my car,” “more food,” “go outside”) and that familiar listeners are understanding more of what child says. Parent has noticed a few clearer /s/ words, such as “sun” and “sock,” with practice.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child transitioned smoothly and appeared enthusiastic about therapy activities. Showed good engagement with both table-top tasks and floor play. Tolerated brief drill-style practice when embedded in fun games (e.g., turn-taking with picture cards).

Goals / observations:
1. Initial /f/ in phrases and emerging sentences
- Child continued to produce /f/ clearly in initial position across many words with minimal to no cueing. Expanded to slightly longer phrases and early sentences (e.g., "I see fish," "more funny feet" in imitation). Child often maintained accurate /f/ within these longer utterances. Several spontaneous phrases with /f/ were noted in play ("my fish," "more food," "funny fish") without direct modeling.

2. Initial /s/ at word and early phrase level (imitation)
- Child showed a meaningful improvement with initial /s/ today. After a short warm-up in isolation, practiced words ("sun, sock, soup, see, soap") with higher accuracy than in prior notes, needing somewhat fewer placement cues. Introduced early carrier phrases in imitation ("my sock," "see sun"). Child was able to maintain /s/ in the word within these short phrases in several trials, though not yet consistently. This represents a step forward from previous word-only work.

3. Final consonants in simple CVC words
- Targeted CVC words ("pop, map, bus, up, feet"). Child more reliably marked final consonants in imitated words when supported with pacing and visual cues. A few spontaneous approximations with final consonants were observed in play (e.g., "up" with a clear /p/).

4. Two- to three-word combinations in play
- Child produced a wider range of two- and three-word phrases in play ("my big car," "more bubbles please," "go up there"). Intelligibility of these phrases improved when child slowed down, and several were understood without the need for clarification.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to support /f/ within short sentences and varied phrases, focusing on generalizing across activities.
- Further develop /s/ by consolidating word-level accuracy and practicing short carrier phrases in imitation.
- Encourage parent to highlight and repeat any clear /s/ words at home and to provide simple models such as "see sun" or "my sock."
- Next session: maintain phrase-level /f/ and expand early phrase-level /s/ as accuracy allows.
notes/8/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent reported that child is consistently using short phrases and that family is generally understanding more of child’s speech. Parent feels that /f/ words are strong and that /s/ words are “getting better,” but not dramatically different from last week.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child separated easily and participated readily in familiar routines. Engagement level was similar to the prior session, with good participation in structured tasks and playful interactions. Responded well to visual prompts and turn-taking games.

Goals / observations:
1. Initial /f/ in phrases and short sentences
- Child maintained high accuracy with initial /f/ in words and short sentences (e.g., "I see fish," "my funny feet"). Productions were generally clear and independent, requiring only occasional reminders when speech rate increased. Performance is stable relative to last session, with continued strong use of /f/ in spontaneous phrases.

2. Initial /s/ at word and short phrase level (imitation)
- Continued targeting /s/ words ("sun, sock, soup, see, soap") and short phrases ("my sock," "see sun"). Child’s performance was similar to the previous note: several accurate productions with moderate cues, along with some ongoing distortions and substitutions. Phrases containing /s/ were attempted with mixed clarity. No major change in cueing level or independence observed today.

3. Final consonants in CVC words
- Practiced final consonants in words such as "pop, map, bus, feet." Child marked final consonants in many imitated trials with pacing support, but spontaneous use remained inconsistent. This reflects maintenance of the emerging skill rather than a clear jump.

4. Two- to three-word combinations in play
- Child continued to produce a variety of two- and three-word phrases ("more bubbles please," "my big car," "go up there") with similar intelligibility to last session. Speech was generally clearer in structured contexts than in fast, excited speech.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue consolidating /f/ within sentences, focusing on carryover into new activities and settings.
- Maintain work on /s/ at the word and simple phrase level until productions become more consistent.
- Encourage parent to continue reinforcing clear productions and gently slowing child’s speech when possible.
- Next session: similar targets, with emphasis on stability and consistent practice rather than advancing the hierarchy.
notes/9/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent shared that child’s speech continues to be easier for family to understand, especially in familiar routines. Parent has noticed a few clearer /s/ words but feels progress with /s/ is gradual. No major new concerns reported.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child transitioned well and appeared comfortable in the therapy setting. Participation in structured and play-based tasks remained steady and cooperative. Some brief moments of distraction, but easily redirected with preferred activities.

Goals / observations:
1. Initial /f/ in sentences and conversation-like play
- Child continued to use clear initial /f/ in a range of words and short sentences (e.g., "I see fish," "funny feet," "more food please") with minimal to no cueing. /f/ was also noted in more conversational-style play, such as describing pictures or actions, with good consistency. This reflects ongoing maintenance of a well-established skill.

2. Initial /s/ at word and short phrase level
- Revisited /s/ words ("sun, sock, soup, see, soap") and phrases ("my sock," "see sun," "soap up"). Accuracy was comparable to last session: several correct productions with moderate placement cues, along with distortions and occasional substitutions. Some clearer productions emerged as the session progressed, but overall performance remained similar to previous note.

3. Final consonants in CVC words
- Practiced final consonants in words like "pop, map, bus, feet, cat." Child marked final consonants in many imitated trials when given pacing and visual support. Spontaneous final consonant use in play was still inconsistent, mirroring prior performance.

4. Multiword combinations in play
- Child continued to use two- and three-word phrases ("more bubbles please," "my red car," "go up there") with similar intelligibility to last session. Speech clarity improved when child was reminded to slow down.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain sentence-level /f/ practice and continue encouraging use across different games and routines.
- Continue focused practice on /s/ in initial position at word and short phrase level; consider introducing a small set of high-interest /s/ words for repeated practice.
- Support parent in modeling clear /s/ and final consonants during everyday routines and in praising any accurate productions.
- Next session: similar targets, with ongoing emphasis on consistency and generalization rather than major level changes.
notes/10/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent reported that child’s overall speech continues to be easier for familiar listeners to understand. Parent feels that /s/ words are “slowly getting there,” with a few more clear examples at home, though not yet consistent. No new concerns shared.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child entered the therapy room willingly and engaged well with a mix of structured and play-based tasks. Attention and cooperation were similar to previous sessions. Child responded positively to visual cues and simple choices.

Goals / observations:
1. Initial /f/ in sentences and conversational play
- Child consistently produced initial /f/ accurately in words and short sentences without cueing (e.g., "I see fish," "funny feet," "more food please"). /f/ remained clear during more natural play-based conversation, indicating stable generalization. Performance was in line with recent sessions.

2. Initial /s/ at word and short phrase level
- Continued targeting /s/ words ("sun, sock, soup, see, soap") and short phrases ("my sock," "see sun," "soap up"). Child demonstrated a mix of accurate and distorted productions, with moderate cues still needed for consistent tongue placement. There were a few clearer, less-cued productions compared with earlier in the block, but overall performance remained similar to the prior note.

3. Final consonants in CVC words
- Practiced final consonants in CVC words ("pop, map, bus, feet, cat"). Child continued to mark final consonants during imitated trials with pacing support. Spontaneous final consonant use remained variable, with a pattern similar to previous sessions.

4. Multiword combinations in play
- Child used multiple two- and three-word phrases during play ("my big car," "more bubbles please," "go up there now"). Intelligibility remained comparable to recent sessions, with clearer speech in slower, supported contexts.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue supporting generalization of /f/ in connected speech while maintaining focus on clarity.
- Maintain structured practice on /s/ words and phrases, with attention to tongue placement and airflow.
- Encourage parent to provide consistent models and positive feedback for any clear /s/ productions and final consonants.
- Next session: similar targets; consider formal review of progress and planning for next steps if pattern of gradual gains continues.
notes/11/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent indicated that child’s speech is generally easier to understand for family, with gradual ongoing improvement. Parent feels that /f/ is strong and that /s/ is “slowly improving,” though still not as consistent as other sounds. No new concerns raised.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child transitioned smoothly and participated well in familiar routines. Engagement and cooperation mirrored previous sessions, with good tolerance for brief drill activities embedded within play.

Goals / observations:
1. Initial /f/ in connected speech
- Child continued to demonstrate stable, accurate /f/ production in words, phrases, and short sentences (e.g., "I see fish," "funny feet," "more food please") with no need for explicit cues. /f/ remained clear in more conversational-style interactions, consistent with prior notes.

2. Initial /s/ at word and short phrase level
- Revisited /s/ words ("sun, sock, soup, see, soap") and phrases ("my sock," "see sun," "soap up"). Performance was similar to last session, with a combination of accurate and distorted productions. Moderate placement cues were still required for reliable accuracy, although there were isolated clearer productions when child was focused.

3. Final consonants in CVC words
- Practiced final consonants in words such as "pop, map, bus, feet, cat." Child continued to mark final consonants during supported imitation with pacing cues. Spontaneous use remained variable, reflecting maintenance of current skill level.

4. Multiword combinations and overall intelligibility
- Child produced a variety of two- and three-word phrases in play ("my big car," "more bubbles please," "go up there now") with similar intelligibility to recent sessions. Speech was most understandable when child slowed down and used familiar phrases.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to reinforce /f/ as a strength in connected speech and use it as a confidence-builder in conversation.
- Maintain targeted practice on initial /s/ at word and phrase level, focusing on consistent tongue placement and gradual reduction of cueing as accuracy allows.
- Support parent in ongoing home practice with a small set of high-interest /s/ words and CVC words with final consonants, keeping practice brief and positive.
- Recommend continued therapy to further develop /s/ accuracy, strengthen final consonants, and support generalization of clearer speech in less structured contexts.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver shared that they are understanding some words better at home, but overall speech is still "pretty hard" for unfamiliar listeners. No major changes since the initial visit. Family is interested in ideas for how to practice sounds during play.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned into the room with some hesitation but joined the clinician after a brief warm-up game. Needed frequent redirection from preferred toys but responded well to visual supports and turn-taking routines. Accepted cues and imitation tasks.

Goals / session focus:
1. CV and CVC syllable shapes
- Targeted simple CV (e.g., "bee," "moo") and CVC (e.g., "pop," "mom") syllables in imitation during play with bubbles and farm animals. Client produced CV forms fairly consistently when imitating; CVC productions were more variable, with frequent omission of final consonants (e.g., "muh" for "mom"). Final consonants emerged with maximal verbal and gestural cueing.

2. Final consonant production in CVC words (p, m, t)
- Practiced final /p, m, t/ in a small set of CVC words (e.g., "pop," "map," "mat," "mom") at the imitated word level. Client often reduced to CV, but when given a clear model plus tactile cue to "tap" the last sound, they produced the full word in some attempts. Overall accuracy low; requires direct modeling and repeated practice.

3. Initial /m, b, p/ in single words
- Used picture cards and toys (e.g., "ball," "baby," "more," "pop") to elicit initial bilabial sounds. Client more successful in imitation than spontaneous attempts. Occasional sound omissions or vowel-only approximations, but with slowed modeling and visual cue to "close lips," accuracy improved in several trials.

4. Early /s/ sound awareness in play
- Briefly introduced /s/ in isolation as a "snake sound" during play. Client watched clinician’s mouth and attempted the sound with some distorted productions. Focus was on exposure rather than accuracy.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue targeting CV/CVC syllables and final consonants in highly motivating play activities at the imitated word level. Provide caregiver with a small list of practice words (e.g., "pop," "up," "mom") and coach them to emphasize the last sound once or twice during play without pressure for perfection. Next session will further probe which consonants are most stimulable and begin building consistency with final /p, m, t/.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver reported that the client is "copying more" at home and will sometimes repeat short words after adults, especially during songs or when asking for snacks. Still not hearing many clear endings on words. No concerns with hearing or general health.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client entered the room more easily today and quickly engaged with cause-and-effect toys. Attention to structured tasks improved; able to participate in short drill-play segments between free play. Good tolerance for repetition when activities were fast-paced and highly motivating.

Goals / session focus:
1. CV and CVC syllable shapes
- Revisited CV and CVC syllables using a picture lotto game. CV forms (e.g., "bee," "moo," "bye") were imitated with good consistency. For CVC forms, client produced more attempts at full CVC structure, with emerging final consonants when given a visual cue (pointing to a dot for the last sound). Still frequent reductions to CV, but more consistent trials with all three sounds than previous session.

2. Final consonant production in CVC words (p, m, t)
- Practiced a small set of high-interest CVC words ("pop," "map," "mat," "mom") at the imitated word level. Client showed clearer marking of final /p, m, t/ in multiple attempts when clinician used a slow, segmented model and a tap cue for the last sound. Some independent productions of "pop" and "up" with audible /p/ at the end during bubble play. Still requires moderate cueing overall, but accuracy noticeably improved compared to baseline.

3. Initial bilabials /m, b, p/ in single words
- Used a picture book and snack routine to elicit initial /m, b, p/ ("more," "ball," "baby," "please," "pop"). Client imitated initial bilabials with higher consistency and needed fewer reminders to close lips. Several spontaneous approximations during play (e.g., "more," "pop") with recognizable initial sounds.

4. Early /s/ sound in isolation
- Briefly revisited /s/ as a "snake sound" in front of a mirror. Client showed increased willingness to try, with a few clearer /s/-like productions when cued to "smile" and keep tongue behind teeth. Still requires direct modeling.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue to build consistency with final /p, m, t/ in a limited set of CVC words, gradually fading cues as tolerated. Encourage caregiver to model and pause for the child to imitate short words with final /p/ (e.g., "up," "pop") during daily routines. Next session will begin probing initial /s/ in simple syllables if readiness continues and maintain focus on CVC word structure.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver noted that others in the family are understanding the child a bit more, especially for words like "pop" and "up." They also reported hearing more attempts at new words, though many are still unclear. No new concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was eager to enter the therapy room and quickly joined the clinician at the table. Attention to task improved; able to participate in short, structured practice blocks with brief play breaks. Followed simple directions and enjoyed turn-taking games. Good imitation willingness.

Goals / session focus:
1. CVC words with final /p, m, t/
- Targeted CVC words at the imitated word level using picture cards and a fishing game. Client produced more consistent final consonants, especially /p/ in "pop," "up," and "cup." Needed fewer tactile and gestural cues; often responded to a verbal reminder to "add your last sound". Accuracy noticeably better than previous sessions, with several correct productions in a row for familiar words.

2. Initial bilabials /m, b, p/ in single words and early phrases
- Practiced initial /m, b, p/ in single words and short two-word combinations (e.g., "my ball," "big pop"). Client imitated initial bilabials accurately in most trials at the word level and was beginning to carry them into short phrases with clinician support. Occasional spontaneous use of "more" and "pop" during play without prompting.

3. CV and CVCV syllable shapes
- Introduced simple CVCV patterns (e.g., "mama," "papa," "baby") in imitation. Client easily produced reduplicated CVCV (same consonant repeated) and showed emerging accuracy with alternating CVCV (different consonants) when given slow models. Continued good performance with CV syllables.

4. Initial /s/ in CV syllables (emerging)
- Briefly probed /s/ in simple CV syllables ("see," "soo") after reviewing /s/ in isolation. Client attempted /s/ with clearer airflow and was able to approximate "see" in a few imitative trials with a smile cue and visual model. This remains a new, early-level target.

Plan / recommendations:
Maintain focus on strengthening final /p, m, t/ in CVC words, and continue to expand initial bilabials into short, functional two-word phrases during play. Encourage caregiver to model and wait for the child to attempt phrases such as "my ball" or "more pop" during everyday routines. Next sessions will continue to probe /s/ in CV syllables while keeping practice fun and brief.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver reported that the child is using more short phrases at home (e.g., "my ball," "more pop") and that endings on some words are "popping out" more often, especially with /p/. They still find it hard for unfamiliar listeners to understand the child in longer utterances.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client entered the room independently and sought out the clinician. Demonstrated good participation in both floor play and brief table-top tasks. Needed occasional reminders to stay seated but was easily redirected with visual schedules and choice-making.

Goals / session focus:
1. CVC words with final /p, m, t/ at word and early phrase level
- Used a picture lotto and car ramp game to elicit final consonants. At the word level, client produced final /p, m, t/ with improved consistency and needed mostly light verbal cues. Several words (e.g., "pop," "map," "mat") were produced accurately across multiple trials. Began incorporating these into short carrier phrases ("my pop," "in cup") with moderate support.

2. Initial bilabials /m, b, p/ in two-word combinations
- Focused on functional two-word phrases (e.g., "my ball," "big pop," "more bubbles"). Client imitated these phrases with relatively clear bilabial onsets in many attempts. Some spontaneous phrase use observed during play (e.g., "more pop" without direct prompt). Occasional sound deletions in longer phrases, but overall clarity is improving.

3. Initial /s/ in CV and CVC syllables (imitated)
- Targeted /s/ in simple CV syllables ("see," "so," "sue") and a few early CVC words ("sun," "sock") at the imitated word level. Client watched the clinician’s mouth and responded to cues to "keep tongue behind teeth." Produced several recognizable /s/ sounds at the beginning of words, though consistency is still emerging. Some substitutions or distortions remain.

4. CVCV multisyllabic words
- Practiced high-interest CVCV words ("baby," "bunny," "puppy") in imitation. Client produced most syllables, with occasional reductions (e.g., "bey" for "baby"). With slowed models and clapping for each syllable, productions became clearer.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue strengthening final consonants in CVC words and expand use into short, functional phrases. Maintain practice of initial /s/ at the imitated syllable and word level, keeping expectations light and playful. Caregiver encouraged to model and reinforce final /p/ and /m/ in everyday words ("up," "cup," "mom") and praise any clear attempts, even if not perfect.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver shared that the child is now frequently using short phrases (e.g., "my car," "more juice") and that final sounds are "showing up" more often, though still not consistent. They have started to hear an /s/-like sound in a few words such as "see" and "sock" when the child is excited.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned smoothly and quickly joined the clinician at the table. Demonstrated improved sustained attention to structured practice with shorter breaks needed between activities. Enjoyed being praised for "strong sounds" and was motivated by sticker rewards.

Goals / session focus:
1. CVC words with final /p, m, t/ in short phrases
- Continued work on CVC words, now emphasizing their use in short phrases (e.g., "my cup," "big map," "in mat"). At the single-word level, final consonants were present in many attempts with only brief verbal reminders. Within phrases, endings were somewhat less consistent but still emerging. Client responded well to a visual cue (pointing to the last sound) to help add the final consonant.

2. Initial bilabials /m, b, p/ in spontaneous phrases
- Used play-based routines (cars, bubbles, pretend kitchen) to elicit spontaneous phrases containing bilabials ("more bubbles," "my ball," "big pop"). Client produced many clear bilabial onsets without direct modeling, particularly for "more" and "ball." Imitated slightly longer phrases (3–4 words) with good bilabial accuracy when given a model.

3. Initial /s/ in imitated words
- Focused on a small set of /s/ initial words ("sun," "sock," "see," "soap"). Client imitated these with variable accuracy but showed more consistent /s/-like productions than previously. Benefited from reminders to "smile" and keep the tongue "behind the teeth." Some productions were close enough to be clearly recognizable to the clinician.

4. CVCV and simple multisyllabic words
- Practiced CVCV words ("baby," "bunny," "puppy") and a few early multisyllabic targets ("banana," "dinosaur") in imitation. Most syllables were present, though individual consonants were not always clear. Using clapping and tapping for each syllable improved the child’s awareness of longer words.

Plan / recommendations:
Maintain current level of practice with final consonants and bilabial phrases, aiming to stabilize consistency rather than adding new sound targets. Continue gentle practice of /s/ in a limited set of words in imitation. Caregiver encouraged to highlight and praise clear final sounds and to occasionally slow their own speech to model all sounds in key words (e.g., "cuP," "soCK").
notes/5/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver reported that the child is "chatty" at home and is often using 3–4 word phrases, though not all words are clear. They have noticed that words like "pop" and "up" are usually easy to understand now, and they are starting to hear /s/ in a few more words ("see," "sit").

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was enthusiastic and requested specific toys. Demonstrated good engagement with both structured and play-based tasks. Able to follow simple two-step directions in the therapy room and participated actively in turn-taking and imitation games.

Goals / session focus:
1. CVC words with final /p, m, t/ in phrases and short sentences
- Targeted final consonants in short carrier phrases ("I see ___," "put in ___") and simple 3–4 word sentences in structured play. At the single-word level, final /p, m, t/ were typically present with minimal cueing. Within short sentences, endings were sometimes dropped but reappeared when prompted to "say the last sound." Overall, consistency continues to improve.

2. Initial bilabials /m, b, p/ in 3–4 word phrases
- Practiced bilabials in slightly longer phrases (e.g., "I want more bubbles," "my big ball," "put on backpack"). Client imitated these phrases with generally clear bilabial onsets. Several spontaneous 3–4 word phrases with accurate bilabials were heard during play. Occasional sound simplifications occurred when the child was excited or speaking quickly.

3. Initial /s/ in imitated words and early phrases
- Continued practice with initial /s/ words ("sun," "sock," "see," "soap") and introduced simple two-word phrases ("see sun," "sock on"). Client’s /s/ productions are becoming more consistent in imitation, though not yet independent. With verbal cues and a visual model, the child produced recognizable /s/ in many trials.

4. Multisyllabic words (2–3 syllables)
- Targeted common 2–3 syllable words ("daddy," "banana," "dinosaur," "open it") in imitation. Client marked most syllables, though some consonants were omitted or simplified. Using hand taps for each syllable helped maintain the overall word shape.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue consolidating final consonants in short phrases and simple sentences. Maintain practice with initial /s/ in a limited word set at the imitated level, occasionally embedding into short phrases. Caregiver encouraged to model slow, clear versions of key practice words and to play simple "I see ___" games at home to support carryover.
notes/6/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver shared that familiar family members now understand the child much more easily, especially in short sentences. They notice that /p/ and /m/ endings are usually there in favorite words, and that /s/ is "starting to sound more like a snake" in some practiced words.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client entered happily and quickly settled into the routine. Demonstrated good attention during structured tasks and accepted feedback well. Enjoyed taking "clinician" turns to listen and judge whether a word sounded "right" or "silly."

Goals / session focus:
1. Final /p, m, t/ in short sentences and structured play
- Continued using carrier phrases and short sentences ("I see map," "put it in cup," "I want pop"). Client produced final consonants more consistently within these sentences, needing only occasional reminders. During a board game, the child often produced clear final /p/ and /t/ independently when labeling pictures.

2. Initial bilabials /m, b, p/ – spontaneous use in conversation
- Focused on encouraging spontaneous 3–4 word utterances containing bilabials during open-ended play (cars, dolls, pretend kitchen). Client frequently used phrases such as "I want more," "my big ball," and "put it back" without models. Bilabial accuracy remained strong in both imitation and spontaneous speech.

3. Initial /s/ – word level and emerging spontaneous attempts
- Practiced a set of /s/ initial words and short phrases ("see sun," "silly sock," "so big") in a picture game. Client imitated /s/ with good effort and improved accuracy; many productions were clearly recognizable to the clinician. During play, the child attempted a few /s/ words spontaneously (e.g., "see," "sit") with variable clarity but without direct prompting.

4. 2–3 syllable words in short phrases
- Continued work on 2–3 syllable words ("banana," "dinosaur," "potato") within short carrier phrases. Most syllables were present, though some consonant details were still simplified. With pacing cues and tapping, the overall word shapes were generally maintained.

Plan / recommendations:
Maintain the current level of practice to solidify final consonants and bilabial accuracy within short sentences. Continue structured work on initial /s/ at the word and simple phrase level, watching for more spontaneous use. Caregiver encouraged to play simple guessing games at home ("Did I say 'see' or 'tee'?") to help the child listen for and practice the /s/ sound.
notes/7/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver reported that the child is talking "all the time" and that close family can usually figure out what they are saying. They notice that when the child slows down, speech is clearer, but when excited, some sounds drop out. They continue to hear more /s/ words, especially "see" and "sun."

Behaviour/engagement:
Client arrived in a positive mood and was eager to show toys from home. Participated well in both structured tasks and free play, though occasionally needed redirection when overly excited. Responded well to visual cues and praise for careful speech.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final /p, m, t/ in structured conversation
- Used play-based activities (pretend store, dollhouse) to elicit short, semi-spontaneous sentences containing final consonants ("put in cup," "I want map," "take it out"). Client generally maintained final /p, m, t/ in these contexts, though some omissions occurred when speech rate increased. A brief reminder to "use your last sound" helped the child self-correct in a few instances.

2. Initial bilabials /m, b, p/ in longer phrases
- Encouraged use of 4–5 word phrases containing bilabials (e.g., "I want more bubbles please," "my big blue ball"). Client imitated these with good bilabial accuracy and produced similar-length phrases spontaneously during play. Occasional cluster reductions and simplifications were present but bilabials themselves remained strong.

3. Initial /s/ in words and short phrases – consolidating
- Practiced initial /s/ in a small word set and short phrases ("see sun," "so silly," "sock on"). Client’s imitative accuracy for /s/ is relatively stable, with most productions recognizable. Spontaneous /s/ use in play was heard intermittently, but not yet consistent across all opportunities.

4. Multisyllabic words – maintenance
- Briefly revisited 2–3 syllable words in imitation. Client continued to mark most syllables, with some consonant simplifications. This area was not the main focus today but was monitored.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue to consolidate final consonant use and bilabial accuracy in more natural, play-based conversation. Maintain practice with initial /s/ at the word and short phrase level, aiming for increased spontaneous use rather than adding new /s/ contexts. Caregiver encouraged to gently remind the child to "use your last sound" in familiar practice words and to praise any clear /s/ attempts.
notes/8/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver shared that the child’s speech feels "much clearer" at home and that siblings often understand without adult help. They still notice that certain words with /s/ are tricky, and longer words can sound "squished." Overall, they are pleased with how far the child has come.

notes/9/note_text:

notes/10/note_text:

notes/11/note_text:
