notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver shared that they are starting to notice more attempts at words during play but speech is often difficult to understand. No major changes since the initial visit. Caregiver is motivated to work on sounds at home but is unsure how to cue.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned to the therapy space with some initial hesitation but warmed up with familiar toys. Attention was variable; needed frequent redirection from self-directed play back to structured tasks. Responded well to turn-taking games and simple visual supports.

Goals / session focus:
1. CV and CVC syllable shapes
- Targeted simple CV (e.g., “moo,” “bee,” “no”) and early CVC (e.g., “mom,” “pop”) in imitation.
- Client produced CV syllables in imitation fairly consistently when given a clear model and visual cue (pointing to clinician’s mouth).
- CVC words were more inconsistent; final consonants were often omitted or replaced with a neutral vowel (e.g., “muh” for “mom”). Required frequent repetition of the model and gentle verbal prompts to “finish the word.”

2. Final consonant production (p, m, t) in isolation and simple syllables
- Practiced /p/, /m/, /t/ at the sound and CV/VC level using picture cards and mirror.
- Client could approximate isolated consonants with maximal verbal and visual cueing (e.g., “lips together, pop sound,” “tap tongue at the front”).
- Final consonants in VC (e.g., “up,” “eat,” “am”) were rarely marked without simultaneous modeling. Mostly produced open syllables.

3. Initial bilabial sounds /m, b, p/ in single words (imitation)
- Targeted initial /m, b, p/ in high-interest words (e.g., “ball,” “baby,” “more,” “mine,” “pop”).
- Client imitated several initial bilabial words with emerging consistency when given slow, exaggerated mouth models and occasional tactile cue (tapping lips).
- Still required direct imitation for almost all productions; few spontaneous approximations.

4. Early two-word combinations (functional words)
- Embedded speech practice in simple play routines (e.g., “more ___,” “bye ___”).
- Client mostly produced single-word approximations; occasionally approximated two-word combinations with significant clinician scaffolding (e.g., clinician models full phrase and client repeats with reduced clarity).

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue targeting CV and CVC syllable shapes with emphasis on marking final consonants for /p, m, t/ in imitation.
- Maintain focus on initial bilabial sounds in highly motivating words.
- Caregiver coached to model slow, clear productions and to emphasize final sounds in everyday routines (e.g., “uP,” “cuP,” “maP”).
- Next session: re-check final consonants at syllable and simple word level and consider expanding to additional early sounds if consistency improves.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver reported that the child is trying to copy more words at home, especially names of family members and favorite toys. They have been practicing “up,” “pop,” and “more” as suggested. Caregiver feels speech is still hard for unfamiliar listeners but notices more attempts.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client entered the room more easily today and engaged quickly with cause-and-effect toys. Attention to structured tasks improved; able to stay with an activity for slightly longer stretches. Some silliness when tasks became more challenging but redirected with visual choices and brief movement breaks.

Goals / session focus:
1. CV and CVC syllable shapes
- Continued work on CV and CVC structures in imitation.
- CV syllables were produced accurately in most opportunities, with the client often joining in without needing repeated models.
- CVC productions showed clearer marking of final consonants compared to last session, particularly for /m/ and /p/ (e.g., “mom,” “pop”). Still some omissions for less familiar words but more consistent for practiced items.

2. Final consonants (p, m, t) in simple words (imitation)
- Focused on VC and CVC words with final /p, m, t/ using picture-based games.
- Client now marked final /p/ and /m/ in many imitated words when given a single model and a reminder to “close your lips at the end.”
- Final /t/ remained more variable; productions were sometimes backed or omitted. Required additional verbal cueing and slower pacing.

3. Initial bilabial sounds /m, b, p/ in single words (imitation to emerging spontaneous)
- Used play routines (feeding dolls, ball play) to elicit initial /m, b, p/.
- Client imitated initial bilabial words with higher consistency, and there were a few spontaneous approximations (e.g., “more,” “ball”) during play without direct modeling.
- Still benefited from visual cues (pointing to lips) but needed fewer repetitions than in Note 1.

4. Early two-word combinations
- Practiced simple combinations within routines (e.g., “more ball,” “bye car”).
- Client produced several approximated two-word phrases in imitation; clarity was variable but attempts were frequent.
- Needed full models and sometimes a slowed-down, segmented model to support both words.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue consolidating CVC words with final /p, m, t/, with extra attention to final /t/.
- Build on emerging spontaneous use of initial bilabials by embedding “more,” “my,” and “ball” in daily routines.
- Caregiver encouraged to pause expectantly after providing a model, allowing the child time to attempt the word.
- Next session: consider introducing an additional early consonant (e.g., /n/) at the syllable level if current patterns remain stable.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver shared that the child is now using “more” and “up” very frequently at home, often without a model. They also notice clearer “mom” and “pop” in familiar routines. Still concerns about overall intelligibility with people outside the family.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned smoothly and appeared eager to show familiar toys. Participation was strong with good tolerance for turn-taking activities. Required occasional breaks but generally stayed with adult-led tasks more consistently than in previous sessions.

Goals / session focus:
1. CVC words with final /p, m, t/ at the imitated word level
- Expanded CVC practice to a larger set of words (e.g., “map,” “cup,” “tap,” “ham,” “sit”).
- Client marked final /p/ and /m/ in most imitated words with only light verbal cues (e.g., “remember the end sound”).
- Final /t/ improved; still inconsistent in less familiar words but more reliably produced in practiced items (e.g., “sit,” “hot”) when given a single model and a gesture cue.

2. Initial bilabials /m, b, p/ at word level (imitation and structured play)
- Continued targeting initial bilabials in play-based tasks.
- Client produced initial /m, b, p/ correctly in many imitated words, with several clear spontaneous uses of “more,” “mine,” and “ball” during play.
- Needed fewer direct prompts; often joined in after a single model.

3. Introduction of alveolar sound /n/ in CV/CVC syllables
- Introduced /n/ in CV (e.g., “no,” “nee”) and simple CVC (e.g., “nine,” “nan”) at the imitation level.
- Client was able to imitate /n/ in CV syllables with moderate cueing to keep tongue at the front.
- CVC with /n/ remained emerging; often reduced to CV or open syllables.

4. Two-word combinations with target sounds
- Practiced two-word combinations that included bilabials and final consonants (e.g., “more pop,” “my cup,” “big ball”).
- Client imitated several two-word phrases; accuracy for individual words was better when phrases were broken into parts first and then blended together.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to strengthen final /t/ in familiar CVC words while maintaining /p/ and /m/.
- Build consistency of initial /n/ in CV syllables before expanding CVC.
- Encourage caregiver to model and recast two-word combinations (e.g., if child says “ball,” caregiver responds with “big ball” or “my ball”).
- Next session: consider early probing of another sound class (e.g., /k/) in isolation if current targets remain relatively stable.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver reported a noticeable jump in clarity at home. They shared that family members are commenting that they can understand more words, especially when the child talks about “cup,” “pop,” and “ball.” Caregiver also noted that the child is starting to combine words more frequently (e.g., “more juice,” “my ball”).

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was highly engaged today, frequently initiating play and requesting items verbally. Tolerated structured practice well and responded positively to simple feedback about sounds. Less need for redirection, with strong participation across the session.

Goals / session focus:
1. CVC words with final /p, m, t/ at word and emerging phrase level
- Practiced a wide range of CVC words in picture naming and simple games.
- Client produced final /p/ and /m/ accurately in most imitated words and began to carry these over into short phrases (e.g., “my cup,” “more pop”) with minimal cueing.
- Final /t/ accuracy improved significantly; client was able to mark /t/ in many familiar words (“sit,” “hot,” “hat”) with light verbal reminders only.

2. Initial bilabials /m, b, p/ in spontaneous and structured play
- Focused on encouraging independent use of bilabials during play.
- Client frequently used “more,” “my,” “bye,” and “ball” spontaneously with clearer consonant production than in prior sessions.
- Imitated new bilabial words (e.g., “baby,” “puppy”) with relatively high accuracy and minimal cueing.

3. Alveolar /n/ and /t/ in CV/CVC and simple phrases
- Continued /n/ and /t/ practice in CV and CVC structures, then embedded in short phrases (e.g., “no more,” “in cup”).
- /n/ in CV syllables (“no,” “nee”) was now fairly consistent in imitation, with occasional spontaneous “no” in play.
- /t/ at final position supported by a tapping gesture; client responded well, showing clearer stops at word endings.

4. Introduction of velar /k/ in isolation and CV
- Briefly introduced /k/ in isolation using visual cues (mouth open, “back sound”) and animal noises.
- Client was able to approximate /k/ in isolation intermittently with maximal cueing and modeling.
- CV combinations with /k/ (e.g., “koo,” “kah”) were attempted but often substituted with front sounds; this will remain an emerging target.

Plan / recommendations:
- Begin to incorporate more phrase-level practice using known CVC words (e.g., “big cup,” “my map”).
- Continue strengthening /n/ and /t/ in final positions with gestures to mark the end of the word.
- Keep /k/ at the isolation and simple CV level with heavy support; not yet ready to push to word-level.
- Caregiver encouraged to praise clear final sounds and model simple two- to three-word phrases during daily routines.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver reported ongoing improvement in word clarity and more frequent two-word combinations like “more cookie” and “my car.” They continue to notice difficulty with “back sounds” (e.g., /k/) and some words still sound shortened. Overall, they feel others are starting to understand the child a bit more.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client entered eagerly and quickly engaged in play-based tasks. Attention remained good, though occasional rushing led to dropped final sounds. Responded well to reminders to use “slow, strong sounds.” Cooperative throughout session.

Goals / session focus:
1. CVC words with final /p, m, t, n/ at word and simple phrase level
- Expanded final consonant practice to include /n/ (e.g., “pan,” “man,” “sun”).
- Client maintained strong performance for /p/ and /m/ in both single words and short phrases (e.g., “my map,” “more ham”).
- Final /t/ continued to improve, with clearer productions in practiced words, though still variable in less familiar vocabulary.
- Final /n/ emerged in imitation; client often approximated /n/ but sometimes shifted to /m/ or omitted.

2. Initial bilabials /m, b, p/ in short phrases and early sentences
- Targeted short phrases such as “big ball,” “my baby,” “more pop please.”
- Client imitated these combinations with generally good accuracy for initial bilabials; some reduction in clarity at the phrase level but key sounds were often present.
- Several spontaneous uses of 2–3 word combinations including bilabials were noted during play.

3. Alveolar /n, t/ in medial/final positions
- Practiced /n/ and /t/ in both final and some medial positions (e.g., “water,” “kitty,” “dino,” “sunny”) at the imitated word level.
- Client required moderate verbal cueing for medial consonants, with better success in final position.
- Some cluster reduction observed (e.g., “no” for “snow”), which was acknowledged but not a primary target this session.

4. Velar /k/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Continued work on /k/ in isolation and CV (e.g., “koo,” “kah”) using mirror and tactile cues to encourage back-of-tongue contact.
- Client showed slightly more consistent approximations of /k/ with maximal cueing but still frequently substituted front sounds.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain focus on final consonants, especially /t/ and /n/, and continue integrating them into short functional phrases.
- Keep building phrase length with known bilabial words while monitoring overall intelligibility.
- Continue /k/ at sound and CV level; not yet ready for consistent word-level demands.
- Caregiver encouraged to highlight and repeat child’s clear final sounds and to provide models with slight emphasis on ending sounds (e.g., “cuP,” “siT,” “suN”).
notes/5/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver noted that progress seems to be “slowing a bit” but that the child is still talking more and using many short phrases. They continue to hear good “p” and “m” sounds at the ends of words, but “t” and “n” are less consistent. Back sounds like /k/ remain very challenging.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was pleasant and cooperative but slightly more distractible today, frequently shifting between toys. Needed more prompts to stay with drill-based activities. Still responded well to games and short, high-interest tasks.

Goals / session focus:
1. CVC words with final /p, m, t, n/
- Reviewed previously targeted CVC words.
- Final /p/ and /m/ remained strong and consistent in imitation and short phrases.
- Final /t/ and /n/ showed similar variability to last session; some clear productions in familiar words, but omissions and substitutions persisted, particularly as phrases lengthened.
- Required similar levels of verbal and visual cueing as before to mark final alveolars.

2. Short phrases with bilabial targets
- Practiced 2–3 word phrases including bilabials (e.g., “big ball,” “more popcorn,” “my puppy”).
- Client imitated these phrases with comparable accuracy to prior session, maintaining good bilabial production but occasionally dropping other consonants.
- Some spontaneous phrases with bilabials noted, consistent with previous performance.

3. Alveolar /n, t/ in medial and final positions
- Continued practice in both final and selected medial positions.
- Client showed some correct imitations, but success was inconsistent and strongly dependent on verbal cueing and slowed models.
- No clear reduction in cueing needs compared to last session.

4. Velar /k/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Ongoing work at the sound and CV level.
- Client continued to require maximal support to approximate /k/, with frequent fronting to /t/ or /d/.
- Performance appeared similar to previous session with no clear readiness for word-level work.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue consolidating final /t/ and /n/, acknowledging that progress is gradual in this area.
- Maintain phrase-level practice with strong bilabial words, focusing on overall intelligibility rather than increasing complexity.
- Keep /k/ at isolation/CV level; monitor for any spontaneous velar attempts in play.
- Caregiver encouraged to keep practice playful and brief, using routines where final sounds can be emphasized (e.g., “in,” “out,” “up”).
notes/6/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver shared that the child is now using many short phrases throughout the day, such as “my cup,” “go out,” and “more snack.” They feel that some words are clearer, especially when the child is calm and speaking slowly, but they still notice dropped sounds at the ends of longer phrases.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned well and was more focused than in the previous session. Accepted drill tasks more readily when embedded in simple games (e.g., fishing, feeding animals). Needed occasional reminders to slow down but overall engagement was good.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final /t/ and /n/ in CVC words and short phrases
- Targeted a set of familiar and slightly new CVC words with final /t/ and /n/ (e.g., “hat,” “sit,” “sun,” “pan”).
- Client produced final /t/ and /n/ more consistently in imitation than in prior sessions, especially in highly practiced words.
- In short phrases (e.g., “my hat,” “big sun”), final consonants were sometimes maintained, particularly when cued to “use the end sound.”

2. CVC words with final /p, m/ in spontaneous phrases
- Used picture scenes and play to elicit spontaneous phrases including /p/ and /m/ final words (e.g., “big map,” “more soup”).
- Client maintained strong final /p/ and /m/ productions, with several clear spontaneous uses in 2–3 word combinations.
- Required fewer direct prompts to mark these final sounds.

3. Alveolar /n, t/ in medial position at word level
- Practiced medial /n/ and /t/ in simple words (e.g., “water,” “bunny,” “butter,” “kitty”) at the imitation level.
- Client showed a small but noticeable improvement in approximating medial consonants when given slow, segmented models.
- Still required moderate cueing but showed better carryover within the same activity.

4. Velar /k/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Continued brief practice of /k/ in isolation and CV.
- Client produced a few clearer /k/ approximations today with intensive cueing, though fronting persisted.
- Not yet ready to consistently carry /k/ into word-level tasks.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to strengthen final /t/ and /n/ in both single words and short phrases, with emphasis on high-frequency words used at home.
- Build on improved medial alveolar accuracy by incorporating these words into simple play routines.
- Maintain limited, high-support practice for /k/ at sound/CV level.
- Caregiver encouraged to cue “end sounds” with a gentle gesture (e.g., tap on table) and to praise when the child uses them independently.
notes/7/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver reported that the child is starting to be better understood by extended family, especially when talking about familiar topics (toys, food). They notice more consistent final sounds in words like “hat,” “cup,” and “sun,” though errors still occur when the child speaks quickly or is excited.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was cheerful and highly motivated to earn turns in games. Maintained attention well for both play-based and brief drill activities. Demonstrated good tolerance for feedback and was willing to repeat words when prompted.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final /t, n/ in CVC words and short phrases (imitation to emerging spontaneous)
- Revisited familiar CVC words and introduced a few new ones.
- Client produced final /t/ and /n/ with improved consistency, often needing only a light verbal reminder or visual cue.
- In short phrases (e.g., “my hat,” “big sun,” “in pan”), client more frequently retained final consonants without repeated prompting.

2. Mixed final consonants /p, m, t, n/ in phrase-level practice
- Used picture scenes and simple stories to elicit phrases containing a variety of final consonants.
- Client handled /p/ and /m/ with high accuracy and now demonstrated better stability for /t/ and /n/ in familiar combinations.
- Occasional omissions still occurred in longer phrases, but overall clarity was better than previous notes.

3. Medial /n, t/ in multisyllabic words (imitation)
- Practiced medial alveolars in 2–3 syllable words (e.g., “sunny,” “butter,” “water,” “kitty,” “dino”).
- Client imitated these words with gradually improving accuracy, particularly when the clinician tapped out syllables and slowed the model.
- Some simplification remained, but more consonants were audible within words.

4. Velar /k/ in isolation and early word attempts
- Continued /k/ practice in isolation and CV, then trialed a few simple words (e.g., “car,” “cow”) with heavy support.
- Client produced several closer approximations to /k/ in isolation and in a small number of coached word attempts, though consistency was not yet reliable.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to reinforce final consonant production across /p, m, t, n/ at phrase level, focusing on functional vocabulary.
- Gradually increase multisyllabic word practice with medial alveolars, using tapping and slowed speech.
- Keep /k/ work brief and highly supported, introducing a small set of functional /k/ words in structured practice only.
- Caregiver encouraged to notice and praise clear final sounds and to model slightly longer phrases (3–4 words) using the child’s best productions.
notes/8/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver shared that the child is now using many 3–4 word phrases at home (e.g., “I want more juice,” “my big red ball”). They feel that final sounds are “much better” than a few weeks ago, although they still hear occasional dropped endings when the child is excited or rushing.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned easily and appeared confident in the therapy setting. Engaged well with both structured tasks and pretend play. Showed good stamina for repetitive practice when tasks were embedded in games.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final /p, m, t, n/ in short sentences
- Targeted final consonants within short, functional sentences (e.g., “I want cup,” “I see sun,” “I like soup”).
- Client maintained high accuracy for /p/ and /m/ and demonstrated noticeably stronger consistency for /t/ and /n/ compared with earlier notes.
- Some endings were still dropped when sentence length increased, but the child often self-corrected with a prompt such as “use the end sound.”

2. Phrase and early sentence production with target sounds
- Encouraged 3–4 word sentences incorporating known target words.
- Client produced many intelligible short sentences in imitation and some spontaneously during play, maintaining key consonants much of the time.
- Required only intermittent cueing to slow down and emphasize important sounds.

3. Medial /n, t/ in multisyllabic words and short phrases
- Continued practice with 2–3 syllable words and added short carrier phrases (e.g., “I see kitty,” “big water cup”).
- Client showed gradual improvement with more consonants being produced in the middle of words, though accuracy remained better in single-word practice than in phrases.

4. Velar /k/ in isolation and selected words
- Briefly revisited /k/ in isolation and practiced a small set of functional words (e.g., “car,” “cookie”) with maximal cueing.
- Client produced some approximations that were closer to /k/, but fronting persisted and required frequent support.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to stabilize final consonants within short sentences, encouraging self-monitoring and gentle correction.
- Build phrase and sentence length gradually while prioritizing intelligibility over complexity.
- Maintain short, supported /k/ practice without increasing demands yet.
- Caregiver encouraged to use simple sentences at home and to prompt, “Can you say the end sound?” when endings are omitted.
notes/9/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver reported that the child’s speech continues to sound similar to last visit: many clear words and short sentences, with occasional dropped endings. They have been practicing sentence models at home and feel the child is enjoying talking more.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was cooperative and engaged, though slightly more active and chatty today. Enjoyed leading play scenarios and responded well to being gently redirected to target words within their own play themes.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final /p, m, t, n/ in short sentences
- Continued practice with functional sentences.
- Performance was comparable to previous session: strong accuracy for /p/ and /m/ and generally good but variable accuracy for /t/ and /n/ in longer utterances.
- Required similar levels of cueing to slow down and mark final consonants, with occasional spontaneous self-corrections.

2. Short sentences with target words in play-based contexts
- Used play to elicit 3–4 word sentences (e.g., “I want big cup,” “put it in pan”).
- Client produced many sentences with similar clarity to last session, maintaining target consonants in familiar phrases but occasionally simplifying in novel ones.

3. Medial /n, t/ in multisyllabic words
- Revisited previously practiced words and short phrases.
- Accuracy for medial consonants appeared stable compared with prior session, with no marked increase or decrease.
- Continued to rely on slowed, segmented models and tapping to support full word shapes.

4. Velar /k/ in isolation and selected words
- Practiced /k/ briefly in isolation and in a few target words.
- Productions were similar to previous note; some approximations were close to /k/ but fronting remained common.
- Cueing needs were unchanged.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain current focus on consolidating final consonants in short sentences rather than increasing complexity.
- Continue multisyllabic practice with medial alveolars using rhythm and tapping.
- Keep /k/ work short and highly supported; monitor for any spontaneous velar productions in conversation.
- Caregiver encouraged to keep modeling clear end sounds in everyday speech and to reinforce when the child uses them independently.
notes/10/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver stated that overall speech skills seem steady since the last visit. They still hear many clear words and short sentences, with occasional sound omissions. They are continuing home practice and feel the child is confident when talking.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client appeared comfortable and talkative in the session. Enjoyed directing play and sharing comments about toys. Needed some structure to remain on task for specific drill items but generally cooperative and responsive to clinician guidance.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final /p, m, t, n/ in short sentences
- Ongoing practice with functional sentences similar to prior sessions.
- Accuracy for final consonants remained stable, with consistent /p/ and /m/ and generally good but occasionally omitted /t/ and /n/ in longer utterances.
- Cueing to emphasize final sounds was similar to previous note.

2. Sentences with target sounds in play routines
- Encouraged the child to produce short sentences during pretend play (e.g., cooking, cars).
- Client generated many 3–4 word sentences; clarity and sound patterns were comparable to last session.
- Continued to see stronger performance with familiar phrases than with novel combinations.

3. Medial /n, t/ in multisyllabic words and phrases
- Practiced a familiar set of 2–3 syllable words and short phrases.
- Production of medial consonants remained at a similar level to last session; child responded to segmented models but did not show a clear jump in independence.

4. Velar /k/ in isolation and limited word practice
- Briefly practiced /k/ in isolation and one or two simple words.
- Productions were similar to previous sessions; fronting persisted, with occasional closer approximations under maximal cueing.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue with current targets focusing on maintaining gains and increasing stability across contexts.
- Keep integrating final consonants and medial alveolars into everyday phrases and routines.
- Maintain short, low-pressure practice for /k/ and watch for any natural emergence of velars in spontaneous speech.
- Caregiver encouraged to continue supportive modeling and to highlight when the child uses clear sounds in connected speech.
notes/11/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver reported similar communication skills compared to recent visits. The child continues to use many short sentences and is generally understood by familiar listeners, with some sound omissions still present. They are comfortable with the current home practice routine.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was relaxed and engaged, readily participating in both structured and play-based tasks. Enjoyed talking about familiar routines and toys. Needed occasional prompts to focus on specific target words but overall maintained good participation.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final /p, m, t, n/ in short sentences
- Practiced final consonants in functional sentences as in prior sessions.
- Accuracy for final /p/ and /m/ remained strong; /t/ and /n/ were produced correctly in many familiar phrases but were still occasionally omitted in longer or less practiced sentences.
- Cueing needs were consistent with previous note, focusing on reminders to “finish the word.”

2. Phrase and sentence production with target words
- Encouraged 3–4 word sentences during structured play.
- Client produced multiple short sentences with clarity comparable to earlier sessions, maintaining target consonants in common phrases.

3. Medial /n, t/ in multisyllabic words
- Continued work on a familiar set of multisyllabic words and brief phrases.
- Accuracy and cueing level remained similar to last session, with improved productions when using slowed, segmented models.

4. Velar /k/ in isolation and early word attempts
- Briefly revisited /k/ in isolation and in one or two functional words.
- Child’s approximations were similar to recent sessions; still reliant on maximal cueing with no clear shift in independence.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain current focus on consolidating final consonants and medial alveolars in functional sentences.
- Continue monitoring for spontaneous velar productions while keeping /k/ practice brief and highly supported.
- Encourage caregiver to keep reinforcing clear final sounds and to provide simple, slow sentence models at home.
- Consider future re-evaluation of goals and hierarchy levels based on ongoing stability and functional communication needs.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent shared that they continue to notice "unclear" words in everyday speech, especially when the child is excited. No major changes reported since the initial visit. Parent is most concerned about /s/ and final sounds.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child transitioned to the therapy space with brief hesitation, then engaged well with toys and picture cards. Attention was best in short, play-based activities. Some silliness and sound play were observed, but the child generally accepted redirection and modeling.

Goals and session observations:
1. Initial /s/ at the imitated syllable and word level
- Practiced /s/ in isolation with visual cueing (mirror, clinician mouth model). Child produced a clear /s/ in about half of attempts, often substituting /t/ or stopping the airflow. With imitation at the CV level ("see, soo, sah"), accuracy was inconsistent; required frequent reminders to keep tongue behind teeth and use “long air.”

2. Initial /f/ at the imitated word level
- Target words included "fish, fun, four, feet." Child often substituted /p/ or omitted the fricative portion ("pish" for "fish"). With direct imitation and visual cue to bite lip and blow, several accurate productions were noted, but support remained high. Accuracy roughly in the low range, with success mostly when the clinician slowed the pace and used verbal prompts.

3. Final consonants (CVC words) in imitation
- Introduced final /p, t, m/ in simple words ("cup, cat, mom, nap"). Child frequently omitted final consonants in spontaneous play. With immediate imitation and light tapping cues for the “last sound,” the child was able to mark some final consonants, but clarity was variable.

4. Syllable shapes (CV, CVCV) in play
- Observed productions during play (e.g., "mama, baby, doggy"). CV and reduplicated CVCV forms were frequent and generally clear. Alternating CVCV (e.g., "bunny") were less precise, with some consonant simplifications.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue focusing on establishing accurate /s/ and /f/ in isolation and simple syllables with strong visual and verbal supports.
- Encourage parent to model slow, clear /s/ and /f/ words during daily routines (e.g., "soap," "feet"), without pressuring the child to repeat.
- Next sessions will continue at the imitated syllable and simple word level, monitoring readiness to move to more consistent word-level practice.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent reported that the child is “trying more sounds” and sometimes attempts to copy /s/ and /f/ words at home, though they are still “hard to understand.” No concerns with hearing or health this week.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child separated from parent more easily and joined the clinician at the table. Good participation with turn-taking games; brief moments of distraction but responded to visual supports and praise. Enjoyed listening to their own voice in a small echo microphone.

Goals and session observations:
1. Initial /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Notable improvement in sustaining /s/ in isolation with mirror support. Child could produce a prolonged /s/ with light cues in most attempts. When moving into CV syllables ("see, soo, sah"), accuracy improved compared to last session; fewer substitutions with /t/. Verbal cueing (“snake sound,” “keep your tongue behind your teeth”) still required but less frequent.

2. Initial /s/ at the imitated word level
- Introduced simple /s/ words ("sun, soup, sock, seal"). Child imitated these with mixed accuracy, but several clear productions were heard without needing to repeat the model multiple times. Errors included stopping or reducing the /s/ portion. This represents a step forward from primarily syllable-level work.

3. Initial /f/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Child demonstrated clearer awareness of the lip-biting posture. With visual model and slow pacing, child produced /f/ in isolation in the majority of attempts. CV combinations ("fee, foo, fah") were attempted with partial success. Still needed consistent reminders to keep air flowing and not close lips fully.

4. Final consonants in CVC words (imitation)
- Practiced final /p, t, m/ in a small set of high-interest words ("cup, cat, mop, map, mom"). Compared to previous session, the child more consistently attempted the last sound rather than omitting it completely. Accuracy remains emerging, but more audible final consonants were observed.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue strengthening /s/ and /f/ at the imitated word level while maintaining practice in isolation and syllables.
- Encourage parent to play “snake sound” and “quiet air” games in front of a mirror, praising any attempts.
- Next session: expand the /s/ word list and begin probing for early phrase-level attempts if word-level accuracy continues to stabilize.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent noted a “big change” in the child’s willingness to copy words at home, especially animal names and favorite toys. They reported hearing clearer /s/ in some words like “sock” and “sun,” though not every time.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child entered the room eagerly and quickly engaged with picture cards and a farm playset. Attention was sustained for longer stretches, and the child initiated several turns in the games. Minimal redirection was needed; child responded well to specific praise for “clear sounds.”

Goals and session observations:
1. Initial /s/ at the imitated and spontaneous word level
- Marked improvement noted. Child produced initial /s/ correctly in imitation for a variety of words ("sun, soup, sock, seal, soap, sad") with fairly high consistency. Visual cues were still offered, but the child often produced the sound accurately after a single model.
- In structured play, the child began to use /s/ words spontaneously (e.g., labeling “sun,” “sock”) with noticeably clearer productions than in previous sessions. Occasional substitutions with /t/ or reduced /s/ were observed, but overall clarity was significantly better.

2. Initial /s/ in short carrier phrases
- Introduced simple two-word phrases such as “my sock,” “see sun,” and “silly snake” using picture supports. Child was able to imitate these phrases with relatively good /s/ accuracy when given a slow model. This represents a new level of complexity compared to prior work largely at the single-word level.

3. Initial /f/ at the imitated word level
- Target words ("fish, five, fun, feet, phone") were practiced. Child produced accurate /f/ in many imitated attempts with only light verbal prompts to “bite lip and blow.” Fewer substitutions with /p/ were heard, and the child appeared more confident, sometimes correcting themself.

4. Final consonants in CVC words (imitation and early spontaneous use)
- Continued practice with final /p, t, m/ ("cup, cat, map, mop, mom, seat"). Imitated productions showed clearer marking of final consonants, with fewer omissions. During play, the child spontaneously used a few CVC words with audible final sounds (e.g., “cup,” “hat”), which is a notable improvement.

Plan/recommendations:
- Maintain focus on /s/ at the word and short-phrase level, gradually reducing visual cues.
- Continue /f/ practice with high-interest picture cards, encouraging self-monitoring (“Did you feel your teeth on your lip?”).
- Suggest parent practice a few familiar /s/ and /f/ words in simple phrases ("my sock," "big fish") during daily routines, keeping practice brief and fun.
- Next sessions will build phrase-level practice and monitor for generalization into spontaneous play.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent reported that the child is using clearer /s/ in some routine words ("soap," "sun"), but consistency varies. They also noticed that final sounds in words like “cup” and “hat” are “popping out more” than before.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child transitioned smoothly into the session and showed good participation in both table-top and floor-based tasks. Mild distractibility when tasks became more challenging, but the child returned to task with brief breaks and visual choices. Overall mood was cheerful and cooperative.

Goals and session observations:
1. Initial /s/ in single words and two-word phrases
- Continued strong performance at the imitated word level with a broad set of /s/ words ("sun, soup, seal, sock, soap, sad, sand"). Accuracy remained high with only occasional cueing. The child showed improved consistency compared to last session.
- Two-word phrases ("see sun," "my sock," "silly seal") were imitated with generally good /s/ productions when modeled slowly. The child required moderate support for maintaining clarity in both words of the phrase but showed clear improvement in control and pacing.

2. Initial /f/ at word level and emerging phrases
- Practiced words such as "fish, five, fun, feet, food." Imitated productions were mostly accurate, with fewer reminders needed for lip placement. In play, the child attempted short phrases ("big fish," "my feet") after a model, with mixed but improving accuracy.

3. Final consonants in CVC words (imitation and structured play)
- Targeted final /p, t, m, n/ ("cup, cat, map, moon, man, seat, sun"). In imitation, final consonants were produced more consistently, though some omissions persisted, particularly in less familiar words. During structured play, the child produced several CVC words with audible final consonants when prompted with a carrier phrase (e.g., “my cup,” “in seat”).

4. Syllable shapes and multisyllabic words (imitation)
- Briefly probed 2–3 syllable words ("banana, turtle, spider") in imitation. Most syllables were present, though some sounds were simplified. This was exploratory and used primarily to observe tolerance and coordination; the main focus remains on simpler syllable structures.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue consolidating /s/ and /f/ at the word and short-phrase levels, focusing on consistent productions and smoother transitions between words.
- Maintain practice with final consonants in high-frequency CVC words during play at home (e.g., “cup,” “hat,” “bed”), emphasizing fun, not perfection.
- Next session will further stabilize phrase-level work and begin light probing of /s/ blends to gauge readiness, if /s/ remains strong.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent shared that familiar listeners are understanding the child a bit more, especially when the child speaks slowly or repeats. They also reported hearing some attempts at /s/ blends in words like “spoon,” though the /s/ is not always present.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child entered the therapy room independently and appeared excited to show the clinician new toys. Participation was good, with the child tolerating short periods of more structured drill mixed with play. Occasional silly vocalizations were used as a break but did not significantly interfere with work.

Goals and session observations:
1. Initial /s/ in short phrases and early sentences
- Continued work on two- and three-word combinations ("see sun," "silly sock," "my soup please"). The child imitated these with generally good /s/ productions, needing occasional reminders to keep the “long air” going. Performance is similar to last session but with slightly smoother phrase production and fewer restarts.

2. Initial /f/ in words and simple phrases
- Practiced words ("fish, four, fun, feet, phone") and two-word phrases ("fun fish," "my feet"). Accuracy at the word level remains fairly strong with light verbal cueing; phrase-level accuracy is emerging but still variable. The child sometimes reduced /f/ to /p/ in longer utterances but responded to quick reminders.

3. Final consonants in CVC words during play
- Used play-based tasks to elicit final /p, t, m, n/ ("cup, hat, map, moon, man, seat, sun"). Compared to prior sessions, the child is maintaining similar levels of accuracy. Final consonant marking is present in many imitated attempts and some spontaneous productions, though omissions still occur in connected speech.

4. Introduction of initial /s/ blends (imitated word level)
- Briefly introduced /s/ blends such as "spoon, star, stop" in imitation only. The child frequently omitted the /s/ (e.g., "poon" for "spoon"), which is typical at this stage. With maximal verbal and visual cues, there were a few emerging approximations, but this remains an early, exploratory skill.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue consolidating /s/ and /f/ at phrase level, with short, fun practice at home (e.g., “see socks,” “fun fish”).
- Maintain work on final consonants in a small, functional set of CVC words.
- Keep /s/ blends as short probes rather than a primary focus until single /s/ remains consistently strong in phrases.
- Next sessions will emphasize stability and carryover of current levels rather than advancing complexity.
notes/5/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent reported that the child’s speech seems “about the same” as last week. They continue to notice clearer /s/ in some words but still find it hard to understand longer sentences. No new concerns were raised.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child initially appeared a bit tired but warmed up with a preferred game. Attention was adequate for short activities; needed more frequent movement breaks than in previous sessions. Despite this, the child was cooperative and accepted prompts.

Goals and session observations:
1. Initial /s/ in phrases and short sentences
- Continued practice with two- and three-word combinations ("see sun," "silly sock," "my soap please"). Performance was similar to the prior session: /s/ was generally accurate in imitation with occasional slips into /t/. The child required intermittent verbal reminders but could often self-correct when cued.

2. Initial /f/ in words and phrases
- Target words ("fish, four, fun, feet, food") and phrases ("four fish," "my feet") were practiced. Accuracy at the word level was stable compared to last session, with mostly correct productions when given a model. Phrase-level /f/ continued to be variable, with some reductions to /p/ in longer combinations.

3. Final consonants in functional CVC words
- Focused on a small set of familiar CVC words ("cup, hat, map, bed, seat"). The child maintained a similar level of accuracy to last week, with many final consonants present in imitation and a few spontaneous productions in play. Omissions were still noted in faster or more excited speech.

4. Early /s/ blends (imitated word level)
- Revisited /s/ blends ("spoon, star, stop, slide") in imitation. The child again tended to omit the /s/ or simplify the blend. With maximal modeling and slow pacing, a few approximations were heard (e.g., a brief /s/ before the rest of the word), but overall performance was consistent with the previous trial.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to reinforce current skill level, focusing on consistency rather than increasing difficulty.
- Encourage parent to pause and model clear, slow phrases with /s/ and /f/, then allow the child to repeat if interested.
- Next sessions will maintain phrase-level work for /s/ and /f/ and ongoing practice of final consonants, monitoring for natural improvements in stability and attention.
notes/6/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent shared that they are hearing the child use some clearer words in routines (e.g., “my sock,” “big fish”), but overall intelligibility with less familiar listeners is still limited. They feel progress is “slow but there.”

Behaviour/engagement:
Child separated from parent without difficulty. Engagement was variable today; the child showed strong interest in certain games but needed additional prompts to stay with less preferred tasks. Responded well to choices and short, high-energy breaks.

Goals and session observations:
1. Initial /s/ in structured phrases
- Practiced familiar phrases ("see sun," "silly sock," "my soap") and added a few new ones ("see sand," "small sock"). The child’s performance remained similar to previous sessions: generally accurate /s/ in imitation with some inconsistencies. Occasional substitutions or reduced airflow were observed, particularly when the child spoke quickly.

2. Initial /f/ in words and short phrases
- Continued work with words ("fish, fun, feet, four, food") and short phrases ("big fish," "my feet"). Word-level accuracy was maintained; the child typically produced /f/ correctly after a model with minimal cueing. Phrase-level accuracy remained mixed, with some clear productions and some /p/ substitutions.

3. Final consonants in CVC words during play
- Target words included "cup, hat, map, bed, seat, moon." The child continued to mark many final consonants when imitating the clinician, with similar levels of accuracy as last week. In spontaneous play, a few final consonants were heard, but omissions still occurred frequently in longer utterances.

4. /s/ blends at the imitated word level (brief probe)
- Short probe with /s/ blends ("spoon, star, stop, slide"). The child mostly produced the second consonant only (e.g., "poon" for "spoon"), with occasional attempts at an initial /s/ that were fleeting. Overall performance was comparable to prior sessions.

Plan/recommendations:
- Maintain focus on consolidating current skills, particularly consistent /s/ and /f/ productions in familiar phrases.
- Suggest parent continue using simple scripts at home (e.g., “see sun,” “big fish”) and praise any clear sounds.
- Next sessions will continue similar targets, watching for natural gains in consistency before advancing to more complex tasks.
notes/7/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent stated that the child’s speech feels “pretty similar” to recent weeks. They notice some good days where words are clearer and other days where the child is harder to understand. They are continuing home practice with picture cards provided.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child came into the room without difficulty and selected preferred toys. Attention span was typical for this child, with good engagement in short, interactive tasks. The child was generally cooperative and responded to turn-taking routines.

Goals and session observations:
1. Initial /s/ in two- and three-word phrases
- Practiced familiar and slightly varied phrases ("see sun," "see socks," "my soap please," "silly snake"). Accuracy of /s/ in imitation was in a similar range to recent sessions. The child needed intermittent verbal and visual reminders but could produce many clear /s/ sounds when focused.

2. Initial /f/ in words and simple phrases
- Continued with high-frequency /f/ words and short phrases ("fun fish," "my feet," "four fish"). Word-level performance remained stable, with mostly accurate /f/ in imitation. In phrases, the child showed a mix of clear and reduced /f/ sounds, with no major change from previous sessions.

3. Final consonants in CVC words in structured play
- Used a game-based activity to elicit final /p, t, m, n/ ("cup, hat, map, moon, man, seat"). The child continued to mark many final consonants in imitation, with occasional omissions. Spontaneous final consonants were heard intermittently in play, consistent with prior observations.

4. /s/ blends (brief imitative practice)
- Repeated a small set of /s/ blend words ("spoon, star, stop, slide"). The child generally omitted the /s/ or simplified the cluster. With maximal cues, a few approximations with a short /s/ onset were noted, similar to previous sessions.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue reinforcing the current level of skills, focusing on clear, slow productions rather than adding new complexity.
- Encourage parent to highlight clear sounds when they occur (e.g., “I heard your good /s/ in ‘sun’!”) to support self-awareness.
- Next sessions will maintain similar targets and monitor for signs of increased independence or readiness for more complex tasks.
notes/8/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent reported that the child is starting to be better understood by close family members, especially when using short phrases. They still notice frequent sound errors but feel there has been gradual improvement overall.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child entered the room with a smile and quickly engaged in a picture-based board game. Participation was good, with the child taking turns, following simple directions, and tolerating short periods of focused practice. Only minimal redirection was needed.

Goals and session observations:
1. Initial /s/ in structured phrases and short sentences
- Continued practice with two- and three-word combinations ("see sun," "silly socks," "my soap please," "see the snake"). Performance was comparable to recent sessions: /s/ was generally accurate in imitation with occasional substitutions or reduced airflow when the child rushed. The clinician supported with pacing cues and reminders.

2. Initial /f/ in words and phrases
- Targeted /f/ in familiar words ("fish, four, fun, feet, food") and phrases ("four fish," "my feet," "big fish"). Word-level /f/ remained strong with minimal cueing. Phrase-level productions were mixed but within the same range as prior sessions, with some clear /f/ and some /p/ substitutions.

3. Final consonants in CVC words in structured play and simple phrases
- Practiced final consonants in words like "cup, hat, map, bed, seat" and short phrases ("my cup," "on seat"). The child continued to produce many final consonants in imitation and some in spontaneous play. Occasional omissions persisted, particularly when the child was more excited.

4. /s/ blends (imitated word level)
- Briefly revisited /s/ blends ("spoon, star, stop, slide, snow"). The child mostly produced the second consonant only, with a few fleeting attempts at including /s/. Overall performance resembled previous sessions.

Plan/recommendations:
- Maintain the current hierarchy, with emphasis on clear /s/ and /f/ in functional phrases and consistent final consonants.
- Suggest parent use short, repetitive phrases during daily routines (e.g., “my cup,” “see socks”) to support carryover.
- Next sessions will continue similar targets, looking for subtle increases in independence and generalization before introducing more demanding tasks.
notes/9/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent excitedly reported that they are now hearing the child use clearer /s/ and /f/ sounds on their own in everyday speech (e.g., “see sun,” “my feet”), without being asked to repeat. They also noted that grandparents are understanding the child a bit more over video calls.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child transitioned smoothly and showed high motivation to participate, frequently requesting “more” of preferred activities. The child attended well to both play-based and slightly more structured tasks, and appeared proud when producing clear sounds.

Goals and session observations:
1. Initial /s/ in phrases and early connected speech
- The child produced /s/ accurately in a variety of two- and three-word phrases ("see sun," "silly socks," "my soap please," "see the snake") with less frequent need for cues. Several spontaneous phrases containing initial /s/ were heard in play (e.g., “see sun,” “my sock”), with good clarity. This reflects a meaningful increase in independence and carryover compared to prior sessions.

2. Initial /f/ in words, phrases, and short sentences
- Word-level /f/ remained strong across a range of items ("fish, four, fun, feet, food, fire"). In structured activities, the child imitated and sometimes spontaneously used /f/ in short sentences (e.g., “four fish here,” “my feet up”) with improved accuracy and fewer /p/ substitutions. Verbal cues were needed less often, mainly as occasional reminders.

3. Final consonants in CVC words and short phrases
- Target words ("cup, hat, map, bed, seat, moon") were practiced in both single-word and phrase contexts ("my cup," "on seat," "big hat"). The child produced final consonants more consistently, including in spontaneous comments during play. While not perfect, there was a clear improvement in how often final sounds were audible and accurate.

4. /s/ blends at the imitated word level
- Revisited /s/ blends ("spoon, star, stop, slide, snow, swing"). Compared to earlier sessions, the child showed more frequent attempts to include the /s/ portion of the blend. With moderate cueing (slow models, segmenting “s-poon”), several approximations were produced that included an audible /s/ and the following consonant.

Plan/recommendations:
- Build on this breakthrough by increasing opportunities for spontaneous use of /s/ and /f/ in short, functional sentences during play.
- Encourage parent to model and expand on the child’s clear productions at home (e.g., child: “see sun”; parent: “Yes, see sun in sky”).
- Next sessions will continue to strengthen phrase and early sentence-level accuracy and gradually increase practice with /s/ blends as tolerance allows.
notes/10/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent reported that the child continues to use clearer /s/ and /f/ sounds in many everyday words, though some days are “clearer than others.” They have noticed more final sounds in words like “cup” and “bed,” especially when the child is speaking slowly.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child entered the room eagerly and maintained good attention for the majority of the session. Occasional distractibility was observed but was easily managed with brief breaks and choice-making. The child appeared confident and enjoyed listening to their own clear productions.

Goals and session observations:
1. Initial /s/ in phrases and short sentences
- The child maintained strong /s/ productions in two- and three-word phrases ("see sun," "silly socks," "my soap please") and short sentences ("I see sun," "see my sock"). Accuracy and independence were similar to last session, with the child often producing clear /s/ without prompts. Minor slips into /t/ were occasionally observed when speech rate increased.

2. Initial /f/ in functional sentences
- Practiced /f/ in short sentences during play ("four fish here," "my feet up," "funny fish swim"). Performance was comparable to the previous session: generally accurate /f/ in both imitated and some spontaneous productions, with only occasional need for verbal reminders.

3. Final consonants in CVC words and simple phrases
- Continued targeting final /p, t, m, n, d/ in words like "cup, hat, map, bed, moon, man" and short phrases ("my cup," "on bed"). The child produced final consonants at similar levels to last week, with many accurate productions in imitation and an increasing but still variable presence in spontaneous speech.

4. /s/ blends (imitated word level and early phrases)
- Practiced /s/ blends such as "spoon, star, stop, slide, snow" in imitation. The child continued to attempt the /s/ portion more often than in earlier blocks, with several approximations containing both /s/ and the following consonant. In a few structured phrases ("my spoon," "stop car"), the child attempted the blend with support, though accuracy remained inconsistent.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to consolidate gains at the phrase and short-sentence levels, focusing on maintaining clarity as utterances become slightly longer.
- Recommend parent continue reinforcing clear /s/, /f/, and final consonants at home by repeating and expanding on the child’s clear words.
- Next sessions will maintain this level of complexity and provide additional practice with /s/ blends as the child’s attention and interest allow.
notes/11/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent stated that the child’s speech has been fairly consistent over the past week. They continue to hear clearer /s/ and /f/ in many familiar words and short phrases, with some ongoing difficulty in longer sentences or when the child is very excited.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child transitioned smoothly into the session and participated well in both structured and play-based activities. Engagement was steady, and the child responded well to praise and visual supports. No significant behavioural concerns were noted.

Goals and session observations:
1. Initial /s/ in phrases and short sentences
- Continued practice with familiar phrases and short sentences ("I see sun," "see my socks," "my soap please"). The child’s performance was similar to the last session, with frequent accurate /s/ productions and occasional slips when speaking quickly. Cueing needs remained low and consistent.

2. Initial /f/ in functional sentences
- Targeted /f/ in short, meaningful sentences ("my feet are wet," "four fish swim," "funny fish here"). Accuracy remained comparable to previous sessions: mostly correct /f/ productions in both imitated and some spontaneous utterances, with minimal verbal prompting.

3. Final consonants in CVC words and phrases
- Practiced final consonants in a familiar set of words ("cup, hat, map, bed, moon, man, seat") and short phrases ("my cup," "on seat," "big hat"). The child continued to mark many final consonants in imitation and some in spontaneous comments, with an overall pattern that matched last week’s performance.

4. /s/ blends at the imitated word level and simple phrases
- Revisited /s/ blends ("spoon, star, stop, slide, snow") in imitation and in a few simple phrases ("my spoon," "stop car"). The child produced approximations similar in quality to last session, with partial inclusion of the /s/ segment in several trials but ongoing variability.

Plan/recommendations:
- Maintain current goals, with ongoing emphasis on consistent, clear productions in functional phrases and short sentences.
- Encourage parent to continue brief daily practice using favorite picture cards and routine phrases, focusing on praising clear productions rather than correcting every error.
- In upcoming sessions, the plan is to keep reinforcing stability at this level and to gradually expand the variety of words and contexts, especially for /s/ blends, as the child is ready.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver shared that speech is still "hard to understand" for unfamiliar listeners. Noted that child is starting to try more words during play at home but often leaves off the ends of words (e.g., "dah" for "dog"). No other changes reported.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned slowly into the therapy room, initially clinging to caregiver but separated with reassurance. Once settled, engaged well with cause-and-effect toys and farm play. Attention was variable; needed frequent redirection from self-directed play back to speech tasks. Responded well to visual reinforcement and brief movement breaks.

Goals and observations:
1. Final consonant production (CV → CVC)
- Targeted final /p, t, m/ in simple CVC words (e.g., pop, up, cat, map, mom) at the imitated syllable/word level.
- In simultaneous imitation with strong verbal and visual cues (e.g., “close your lips at the end”), child approximated final consonants on about half of opportunities; often added a neutral vowel after the consonant (e.g., "puh").
- In delayed imitation, final consonants were usually omitted. Required maximal verbal models, slowed rate, and tactile cues to the lips to achieve clearer closures.

2. Initial /p, b, m/ in single words
- Practiced initial bilabials in isolation and CV syllables (e.g., "pa, ba, ma") and then in simple words (e.g., ball, baby, more) at the imitated level.
- Production in isolation and CV was generally accurate with visual modeling and mirror support.
- At the word level, child frequently reduced to a vowel or produced a glottal-like onset. Needed direct modeling plus cues to "put lips together" to achieve more consistent bilabial closure.

3. Early syllable shapes (CV, CVCV)
- Observed in play: frequent use of CV and reduplicated CVCV syllables (e.g., "mama", "dada", "wawa").
- When given models of alternating CVCV forms (e.g., "baby, bunny"), child tended to simplify to one repeated syllable.
- Used clapping and tapping to highlight syllables; child showed emerging ability to imitate two-syllable words with heavy modeling.

4. Functional single-word use in play
- Encouraged use of single words to request (e.g., "more", "up") and comment (e.g., "go", "pop").
- Child primarily relied on gestures and vocalizations; accepted models and occasionally imitated single words directly after the clinician.
- Spontaneous clear words were limited but included a few approximations (e.g., "up", "more", "go") when highly motivated.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue targeting final consonants and initial bilabials at the imitated CV/CVC level with strong visual and tactile cues.
- Maintain focus on building a small core set of functional words for requests during motivating play routines.
- Home practice: caregiver to model and emphasize final sounds in familiar words (e.g., "up", "pop", "cat") and encourage child to watch their mouth in a mirror for "lip sounds" (/p, b, m/). Keep practice brief and playful.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver noted child is "trying to say more words" and is vocalizing frequently, especially during play. Reported hearing clearer "up" and "pop" at home, though endings are still inconsistent. No concerns with hearing or health.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child separated more easily from caregiver today and joined the clinician at the table with minimal hesitation. Maintained attention well for short, structured activities and responded positively to turn-taking games. Needed occasional prompts to stay seated but was generally cooperative.

Goals and observations:
1. Final consonant production (CVC words: /p, t, m/)
- Practiced final /p, t, m/ in high-interest words (e.g., pop, up, hot, hat, map, mom) at the imitated word level.
- In simultaneous and immediate imitation with visual cues, child produced clearer final consonants in most attempts, with fewer added vowels (e.g., "pop" instead of "puh-puh").
- In delayed imitation, final consonant marking improved compared to last session, though still inconsistent. Required moderate verbal prompts ("remember the sound at the end") and slowed rate.

2. Initial bilabials (/p, b, m/) in single words
- Targeted initial /p, b, m/ in picture naming and play-based tasks (e.g., bubbles, ball, more, baby) at the imitated word level.
- Accuracy in CV syllables remained strong; at the word level, child produced more consistent bilabial closures, especially for /m/ ("more", "me").
- Needed fewer tactile cues to the lips and responded well to visual modeling alone for several trials, indicating improved awareness of lip closure.

3. Early two-syllable words (CVCV)
- Focused on simple two-syllable words (e.g., "mama", "baby", "bunny", "puppy") in imitation.
- Child imitated several two-syllable words with both syllables present when provided with slow, exaggerated models and gestural tapping.
- Occasional reductions to one syllable persisted, but overall there were more complete productions compared to baseline.

4. Functional word use in play
- Integrated target words into play (e.g., bubbles, cars, blocks) and encouraged child to request with single words rather than gestures alone.
- Child produced several imitated requests ("more", "up", "pop") and a few emerging spontaneous approximations during highly motivating activities.
- Still relies heavily on gesture, but is beginning to pair gesture with vocal word attempts more frequently.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue work on final consonants in familiar CVC words, gradually increasing delay between model and child’s production to encourage independence.
- Maintain emphasis on initial bilabials within functional vocabulary.
- Home practice: caregiver to model and encourage words like "more", "up", "pop", "mom" during daily routines, praising any attempts and gently highlighting end sounds (e.g., "poP").
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver reported that others are starting to recognize a few of child’s words (e.g., "up", "more", "ball"), especially during play. Noted that child sometimes repeats words after adults without being prompted.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered the room easily and appeared eager to explore toys. Demonstrated good participation in both floor play and brief table tasks. Followed simple play routines and accepted turn-taking with minimal protest. Occasional silliness, but redirected with visual cues and choices of activities.

Goals and observations:
1. Final consonant production in CVC words (/p, t, m/)
- Practiced final /p, t, m/ in a variety of familiar words (e.g., pop, cup, hat, hot, map, mom) at the imitated word level and began to probe for carrier phrases ("my ___", "more ___") with maximal support.
- At the imitated word level, child marked final consonants in most attempts with moderate cues; productions were clearer and more consistent than previous sessions.
- In highly practiced words (e.g., "pop", "up"), child occasionally produced accurate final consonants with only a visual reminder or even spontaneously during play.

2. Initial bilabials (/p, b, m/) in single words and short phrases
- Continued work on initial /p, b, m/ in words (e.g., ball, baby, more, puppy) and introduced simple two-word combinations in imitation (e.g., "more bubbles", "my ball").
- Word-level accuracy for initial bilabials was good with visual models; child required fewer tactile prompts and benefited from gestural cues (tapping lips) only intermittently.
- In short imitated phrases, initial bilabials were often maintained in the first word but occasionally reduced or omitted in the second; needed repetition and slowed rate.

3. Two-syllable words (CVCV, CVCV) in imitation
- Targeted two-syllable words such as "baby", "bunny", "daddy", "puppy", "happy" at the imitated level.
- Child produced both syllables in many attempts when provided with clear modeling, tapping for each syllable, and slowed speech.
- Fewer reductions to a single syllable were observed; accuracy still variable but clearly improved compared to initial note.

4. Functional single-word and early phrase use in play
- Encouraged use of words to request and comment during play with bubbles, animals, and vehicles.
- Child produced a small set of recognizable words spontaneously (e.g., "up", "more", "ball", "pop") and imitated additional words and two-word combinations with support.
- Increased willingness to attempt new words when prompted, showing growing confidence.

Plan/recommendations:
- Begin to more systematically introduce carrier phrases (e.g., "more ___", "my ___") with familiar CVC target words while continuing to reinforce accurate final consonants.
- Continue building a functional core vocabulary with initial bilabials and two-syllable words.
- Home practice: caregiver to model short two-word combinations using child’s known words (e.g., "more bubbles", "big ball"), emphasizing final sounds and keeping practice playful and brief.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver shared that child is now frequently saying "more" and "up" independently at home. Reported hearing a few new words (e.g., "car", "hat") and noticing that endings sometimes sound clearer, especially when they slow down and repeat with the child.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly into the session and quickly joined in play. Maintained attention well for short structured tasks at the table and enjoyed turn-taking games. Needed minimal redirection from preferred toys back to targeted activities.

Goals and observations:
1. Final consonant production in CVC words and early phrases (/p, t, m/)
- Worked on final /p, t, m/ in CVC words (e.g., pop, cup, hat, hot, map, mom) at the imitated word level and expanded into simple carrier phrases (e.g., "my cup", "more pop") with visual supports.
- At the single-word level, child marked final consonants consistently in many practiced items with only light verbal reminders.
- In carrier phrases, final consonants were present in a good portion of attempts but sometimes dropped when the phrase length increased; benefited from modeling and slight pauses before the last word.

2. Initial bilabials (/p, b, m/) in words and two-word phrases
- Continued targeting initial /p, b, m/ in common words (e.g., ball, baby, more, puppy) and two-word combinations (e.g., "more ball", "big pop").
- Word-level accuracy for initial bilabials was strong with minimal cues; child often produced these sounds correctly once given a model.
- In two-word phrases, child frequently maintained accurate bilabials in both words when provided with a slow model and visual cueing; occasional omissions occurred when excited or speaking quickly.

3. Two-syllable words and early multisyllabic attempts
- Focused on two-syllable words (e.g., "puppy", "baby", "bunny", "daddy") and introduced a few longer words in imitation (e.g., "banana") for exposure.
- Child imitated several two-syllable words with both syllables clearly present when given visual and rhythmic cues.
- Longer words were often reduced, but child showed interest and willingness to attempt them.

4. Functional communication in play (single words and short phrases)
- Integrated target words into play routines (e.g., feeding animals, cars on a ramp) and encouraged child to use words or short phrases to request and comment.
- Child produced multiple spontaneous words and a few short word combinations (e.g., "more ball", "my car") during motivating activities, though speech clarity varied.
- Caregiver observed part of the session and commented that these productions resemble what they are beginning to hear at home.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue strengthening final consonant production at the word level and in simple carrier phrases, focusing on consistency.
- Maintain emphasis on initial bilabials and two-syllable words within functional phrases.
- Home practice: caregiver to model and encourage short phrases such as "my cup", "more pop", and to gently emphasize end sounds in everyday words during play and mealtimes.

Goals and observations:

- Practiced final /p, t, m/ in CVC words (e.g., pop, cup, hat, hot, map, mom) and increased focus on short phrases and early sentences (e.g., "my hot cup", "more pop please") in imitation.

- Child maintained accurate initial bilabials across most words in imitated phrases with only light verbal and visual support.
- Demonstrated improved carryover of these sounds into short, functional sentences during structured play (e.g., "more bubbles please") when provided with a model.

3. Two-syllable and early multisyllabic words

4. Functional communication: spontaneous words and short phrases
- Throughout play, encouraged child to use words and short phrases to request, comment, and protest.
- Child used several spontaneous words and some short spontaneous combinations (e.g., "more bubbles", "my car") with increasing clarity.

- Expand use of initial bilabials and two-syllable words in functional short sentences.

notes/7/note_text:
Parent report:
- Caregiver shared that child is "talking more" and seems eager to try new words. Reported hearing clearer short sentences at home such as "more bubbles please" and "my big ball", though not always perfectly articulated. Noted that familiar listeners are understanding child better.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child entered the session happily and quickly engaged in shared play. Demonstrated sustained attention for structured speech tasks and followed directions well. Enjoyed being given "helper" roles (e.g., choosing pictures, handing out cards), which supported participation.

Goals and observations:
1. Final consonant production in phrases and simple sentences
- Targeted final /p, t, m/ in CVC words embedded in short phrases and simple sentences (e.g., "I want pop", "my hot cup", "mom has hat") at the imitated and cued spontaneous levels.
- Child maintained accurate final consonants in many imitated phrases with minimal cues; errors occurred mainly when speaking quickly or when phrases were novel.
- Began to see some spontaneous carryover of final consonants during play (e.g., "pop" and "up" produced clearly at the end of short sentences), although consistency varied.

2. Initial bilabials and early alveolars in short sentences
- Expanded focus to include initial /p, b, m/ and early alveolars /t, d/ in functional sentences (e.g., "my big ball", "I want more", "dad has cup") at the imitated level.
- Child produced initial bilabials accurately across most words with minimal support and showed emerging accuracy with initial /t, d/ in imitated contexts.
- Benefited from visual models and slowed rate to maintain clarity across all words in the sentence.

3. Two-syllable and simple three-syllable words in context
- Practiced two-syllable words ("puppy", "baby", "bunny", "daddy") and simple three-syllable words (e.g., "banana") in both single-word imitation and short carrier phrases ("I see ___").
- Two-syllable words were generally produced with both syllables present; occasional sound distortions occurred but overall rhythm was good.
- Three-syllable words remained challenging but child was able to approximate all three syllables with strong modeling and tapping cues.

4. Functional communication and intelligibility in play
- Encouraged child to use short sentences for requests and comments during play (e.g., car ramp, kitchen set).
- Child produced a number of imitated and some spontaneous short sentences (e.g., "more bubbles please", "my car go") with improving clarity, especially in familiar routines.
- Caregiver observed that these sentence-level productions were similar to those heard at home and commented that it is becoming easier to understand child in those contexts.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to practice final consonants and clear initial sounds within short, functional sentences, gradually increasing independence.
- Maintain work on two- and three-syllable words in simple carrier phrases.
- Home practice: caregiver to use predictable sentence frames such as "I want ___", "my ___", and "more ___ please" at home, modeling clear beginnings and endings and encouraging child to repeat or fill in the blanks.
