notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver shared that they are starting to notice a few more attempts at words during play, but speech is still hard to understand for unfamiliar listeners. No major changes since the initial visit. Parent is motivated to work on sounds at home and asked for ideas for simple games.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned with mild hesitation but joined the session with encouragement. Preferred to explore toys independently at first, then gradually participated in structured tasks with frequent redirection. Attention was variable; short bursts of good focus with play-based activities.

Goals / observations:
1. Initial /p, b, m/ at syllable and word level
- Practiced CV syllables (e.g., "pa, ba, ma") in imitation. Client produced bilabial sounds with fair consistency in isolation, but accuracy decreased when adding vowels (approx. half of attempts were clear). Needed visual models and occasional tactile cue to help close lips fully.

2. Initial /k, g/ at syllable level
- Targeted /k/ and /g/ in isolation and simple CV syllables using visual placement cues ("tongue back" and mouth model). Client could approximate /k/ and /g/ in isolation with heavy cueing; productions in syllables were mostly backed glottal sounds or omitted consonants. Imitation only; not yet emerging spontaneously.

3. Early final consonant production (final /p, m/ in VC/CVC)
- Introduced final consonants using simple words in play (e.g., "up," "pop," "map"). Client often omitted final sounds, producing mainly open syllables. With simultaneous production and gestural cue (tapping for the last sound), occasional final consonants were heard but not consistent.

4. CV and CVCV syllable shapes
- Observed spontaneous vocal play including vowels, CV ("ba," "da") and reduplicated CVCV ("baba," "mama"). Alternating CVCV (e.g., "bada") not yet consistent. Modeled varied syllable shapes during toy play; client imitated a few but needed repetition and high engagement.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue building accuracy for bilabial sounds in CV syllables and increase awareness of final sounds using tactile/visual cues.
- Maintain focus on establishing back sounds (/k, g/) in isolation before pushing to word level.
- Coach parent in using simple imitation games (e.g., "pop" with bubbles, "up" on the swing) to encourage final consonant attempts in natural play.
- Next sessions will continue at the syllable and simple word level with a strong play-based framework.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver noted that the child is "making lots of sounds" at home and trying to copy more words from family members. Still difficult for others to understand, but parent feels there are more attempts overall. They have been practicing "up" and "pop" with bubbles.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client separated more easily from caregiver today and was curious about the toys. Engagement improved when activities were fast-paced and highly visual. Needed occasional breaks from direct imitation tasks but returned to work with encouragement.

Goals / observations:
1. Initial bilabials /p, b, m/ in CV and early word level
- Client produced /p, b, m/ in isolation with good consistency given a visual model. At CV level, accuracy improved compared to last visit, with many clear productions of "pa, pee, boo, me" in imitation. At single-word level ("ball, pop, baby"), accuracy was emerging; some words were approximated but recognizable. Still requires modeling and occasional verbal cueing.

2. Initial /k/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Notable improvement in producing /k/ in isolation with jaw support and "tongue back" cue. Client was able to imitate /k/ independently several times without glottal substitution. In CV syllables ("ku, ka"), approximations were more consistent, though still required modeling and slow rate. A few clearer /k/ productions were heard during play (e.g., approximating "car"), representing new emerging skill.

3. Final consonant awareness for /p, m/ in VC/CVC words
- Continued work with "up, pop, map, mop" in structured play. With simultaneous production and gestural cue, client produced some recognizable final /p/ and /m/ (more success with /p/). Still omits many final sounds, but there were more successful trials compared to prior note, and child began to anticipate the "last sound" when cued.

4. Syllable shapes (CVCV alternating)
- Modeled alternating CVCV (e.g., "bada, papi, mabu") in songs and routines. Client imitated a small number of these sequences with partial accuracy. Clearer productions seen with reduplicated CVCV ("mama, baba"). Overall, slightly more varied syllable structure compared to previous session.

Plan / recommendations:
- Maintain current focus on improving consistency of bilabial CV and simple words, while continuing to shape /k/ at CV level.
- Provide caregiver with ideas for car-themed play to practice /k/ (e.g., "go, car, key") using slow models and "tongue back" cue.
- Continue targeting final /p/ and /m/ with bubbles and simple board games, emphasizing "one more sound" at the end.
- Next session may begin to introduce a small set of highly familiar words for /k/ if CV accuracy remains stable.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver reported a "big jump" in attempts at words over the past week. They notice clearer "pop" and "up" during play and feel the child is trying to say "car" and "cookie" more often. Family members are starting to recognize a few specific words.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client entered the room readily and sought out favorite toys. Demonstrated good participation in structured tasks when embedded in play (cars, bubbles, animal farm). Imitation was strong today; client frequently watched the clinician's mouth and tried to copy.

Goals / observations:
1. Initial bilabials /p, b, m/ at single-word level
- Marked improvement in clarity and consistency at the word level. In imitation, client produced a small set of bilabial words (e.g., "pop, baby, ball, more, mine") with generally good accuracy; only occasional distortions. Spontaneous use of "more" and "pop" during play with minimal prompting. Cueing needs were reduced compared to prior session.

2. Initial /k/ at CV and word level
- Client produced /k/ in CV syllables ("ku, ki, ka") with improved accuracy and less jaw support. Introduced a small set of /k/ words ("car, key, cow, cookie") at the imitated word level. Accuracy was variable but clearly better than before; several productions of "car" and "key" were recognizable. Needed verbal reminders to "put tongue in the back" but could correct with cueing.

3. Final /p/ and /m/ in VC/CVC words
- Continued with "up, pop, map, mop" using play-based routines. Client now produced final /p/ in "up" and "pop" with moderate consistency in imitation and occasional spontaneous use in context. Final /m/ still emerging, but a few clearer productions were heard in imitation (e.g., "mom"). Visual/tactile cue (tapping for last sound) remained helpful but less intensive than previously.

4. CVCV alternating syllable shapes
- Client imitated several alternating CVCV sequences in songs (e.g., "bada, papi") with improved rhythm and more distinct consonants. Syllable marking was more consistent, though some consonant substitutions remained. This reflects a small but clear advancement in sequencing skills.

Plan / recommendations:
- Expand practice set of bilabial words at the word level and begin to shape into simple two-word combinations (e.g., "more pop").
- Continue strengthening /k/ at imitated word level with high-interest items (cars, animals, snacks).
- Maintain emphasis on final /p/ in meaningful routines (going "up" the stairs, popping bubbles) to support generalization.
- Provide caregiver with a short list of 5–6 target words (mix of bilabials and /k/) to practice in daily routines using slow, clear models.
notes/3/note_text:

Caregiver shared that the child is now regularly saying "more" and "pop" at home and has started to attempt short combinations like "more car" and "my ball." They still feel many words are unclear but notice less frustration when the child tries to communicate.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was eager to engage and frequently initiated play with the clinician. Attention to models was good; the child often leaned in to watch the clinician’s face. Needed minimal redirection and responded well to visual supports.

Goals / observations:
1. Bilabial words in simple phrases (two-word combinations)
- Built on previously established words ("more, my, pop, ball"). Client imitated two-word phrases such as "more pop," "my ball," and "more bubbles" with moderate accuracy. Consonants were generally present, though some vowel distortions and reduced clarity occurred. A few spontaneous phrases ("more pop," "my ball") emerged in play without direct prompting, representing small but meaningful progress.

2. Initial /k/ at word level and early carrier phrases
- Practiced /k/ words ("car, key, cow, cookie, coat") in imitation. Accuracy at the word level was improving; several words were produced clearly with minimal cueing. Introduced simple carrier phrase "my ___" with /k/ targets (e.g., "my car"). Client could imitate the full phrase with support, though /k/ occasionally fronted to /t/ or omitted. Still required verbal cueing for back tongue placement.

3. Final /p, m/ in VC/CVC words within play
- Continued focus on "up, pop, map, mom" during routines. Client produced final /p/ consistently in imitation and occasionally independently when excited (e.g., loudly saying "pop" during bubble play). Final /m/ was heard more clearly in "mom" and "ham" with direct modeling. Cueing shifted toward lighter verbal reminders rather than full simultaneous productions.

4. Syllable structures in functional words (CVC, CVCV)
- Observed more consistent marking of all syllables in CVCV words (e.g., "baby, mommy, bunny") in imitation. For CVC words ("pop, map, ball"), consonants were often present, though not always precise. This reflects gradual strengthening of word shapes.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue practicing two-word phrases using familiar bilabial and /k/ words, emphasizing clear consonants at the beginning and end.
- Maintain /k/ at the word level and expand the carrier phrase practice as tolerated.
- Encourage caregiver to respond positively to any phrase attempts, modeling the correct production rather than requiring repetition.
- Next sessions will continue at word and short-phrase levels, monitoring readiness to increase spontaneous phrase production.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver reported that the child is "talking a lot more" at home and is starting to put two and sometimes three words together (e.g., "more car please," "my big ball"). They notice that some sounds are clearer, especially /p/ and /m/, but /k/ is still inconsistent.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned smoothly and was highly engaged throughout the session. Enjoyed turn-taking games and often initiated communication to request items. Demonstrated good stamina for both play-based and slightly more structured tasks.

Goals / observations:
1. Two-word and early three-word phrases with bilabials
- Targeted phrases such as "more pop please," "my big ball," and "more bubbles" in structured play. In imitation, client produced these phrases with generally good consonant accuracy on bilabial sounds and emerging prosody. Some spontaneous use of two-word phrases ("more pop," "my ball," "more bubbles") was observed with only a visual cue (holding up the item). This indicates meaningful clinical progress at the phrase level.

2. Initial /k/ in words and short phrases
- Continued practicing /k/ words ("car, key, cookie, cat, coat") in imitation. Accuracy at the word level improved, with many clear productions of /k/ at the beginning when cued. Introduced more consistent carrier phrases ("my car," "big car," "more cookie"). Client imitated these phrases with moderate success; /k/ was present more often than previously, though still occasionally fronted. Needed fewer tactile cues and responded well to verbal reminders and visual models.

3. Final consonants /p, m, t/ in CVC words
- Expanded final consonant practice to include /t/ (e.g., "hot, hat, out"). Client produced final /p/ accurately in most imitated trials and several spontaneous productions. Final /m/ and /t/ were emerging with clear attempts, though not yet fully consistent. The child showed improved awareness of the "last sound" when given a light gestural cue.

4. Multisyllabic early words (2–3 syllables)
- Introduced simple 2–3 syllable words relevant to play ("banana, cookie, teddy, open it"). Client approximated these with most syllables present, though some consonant accuracy was reduced. Syllable marking improved compared to earlier sessions, especially in high-interest words like "cookie" and "teddy."

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue building phrase length with familiar vocabulary, focusing on maintaining bilabial clarity and emerging final consonants.
- Maintain and gradually increase demands for /k/ in phrases, while avoiding overload; continue using clear models and "tongue back" cue.
- Suggest that caregiver model short phrases during routines (e.g., "my car," "more cookie please") and praise any attempts, even if not perfect.
- Next sessions will monitor consistency of /k/ and final consonants to determine readiness for more spontaneous phrase-level practice.
notes/5/note_text:

Goals / observations:

- Practiced phrases such as "more pop please," "my big ball," and "put it up" within play routines. Client imitated these phrases with similar accuracy to the previous session: bilabial consonants were typically present, and final /p/ in "up" and "pop" remained fairly consistent when cued. Some spontaneous phrases were heard, but quality was comparable to last note.

2. Initial /k/ in words and short phrases
- Continued work with /k/ words ("car, cookie, cat, coat") and simple phrases ("my car," "big car"). Accuracy at the word level was similar to the prior session: several clear /k/ productions in imitation, with occasional fronting to /t/ or omission. Phrases required verbal cueing and visual modeling; performance did not yet reflect a clear jump in independence.

3. Final consonants /p, m, t/ in CVC words
- Revisited CVC words such as "pop, map, mom, hat, hot." Client produced final /p/ reliably in structured tasks; final /m/ and /t/ remained variable but were attempted. Cueing level and consistency were comparable to previous visit, indicating consolidation rather than notable advancement.

notes/6/note_text:

Goals / observations:

- Continued targeting phrases like "more bubbles please," "my big ball," and "put it in." Performance was similar to the prior session: bilabials remained clear in most imitated phrases, and final /p/ and /t/ were present in many structured trials. Spontaneous phrases were frequent but at roughly the same clarity level as before.

2. Initial /k/ at word and phrase level
- Practiced /k/ words ("car, cookie, coat, cat") and phrases ("my car," "more cookie"). Client produced a mix of accurate and fronted /k/ sounds in imitation, with overall pattern similar to previous note. Continued to require verbal cues ("tongue back") and visual models; independence has not yet shifted noticeably.

notes/7/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver shared that the child is using many of the practiced phrases at home, especially around play and snack times (e.g., "more bubbles," "more cookie," "my car"). They still notice that /k/ is not always there, but overall intelligibility with family feels similar to previous weeks.

Goals / observations:

- Continued practice of phrases like "more bubbles," "more pop please," and "put it in/out." Client produced these in imitation with accuracy comparable to prior visits; bilabials remained a strength. Final /p/ was usually present in "up" and "pop" during games, with similar cueing needs as before. Spontaneous phrase use in the session mirrored caregiver report.

2. Initial /k/ in words and phrases
- Practiced /k/ targets ("car, cookie, cat, coat, key") and simple phrases ("my car," "big car," "more cookie"). Client continued to show a mix of correct and fronted productions. Some clear /k/ productions occurred with verbal cueing, but overall independence and accuracy at the phrase level remained largely unchanged compared to recent notes.

3. Final consonants in mixed CVC words
- Revisited CVC words with /p, m, t/ ("pop, map, mom, hat, hot") and added a few new items ("cup, gum"). Client showed similar accuracy patterns: final /p/ remained strongest, with /m/ and /t/ still variable. Gestural cueing for "one more sound" was needed at about the same rate as previously.

notes/8/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver reported that the child is still using many short phrases but they have not noticed big changes in clarity recently. They feel the child understands a lot and is eager to communicate, but some words are still hard for others to catch.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client separated easily and chose familiar toys. Participation remained good across structured and play-based tasks. The child was cooperative with imitation and seemed to enjoy practicing favorite words and phrases.

Goals / observations:
1. Short functional phrases with target sounds
- Practiced phrases such as "more bubbles," "my big ball," "put it up," and "more cookie." Bilabial sounds and final /p/ in "up" and "pop" remained consistent strengths in structured contexts. Spontaneous phrases occurred frequently but at a similar clarity level to prior notes; no clear shift in independence or hierarchy.

2. Initial /k/ in familiar words and phrases
- Continued focus on /k/ in words ("car, cookie, cat, coat, key") and phrases ("my car," "more cookie"). Client produced some accurate /k/ sounds in imitation with verbal cues, but fronting and omissions were still present. The overall profile and cueing requirements were comparable to the last several sessions, indicating maintenance.

3. Final consonants /p, m, t/ in play
- Used picture cards and play items to elicit CVC words like "pop, map, mom, hat, hot, cup." Final /p/ remained the most reliable; final /m/ and /t/ were heard inconsistently. Client continued to benefit from gestural cues and slowed rate, similar to previous sessions.

4. Longer words and syllable marking
- Practiced multisyllabic words ("banana, butterfly, open it, teddy bear") within routines. Client marked most syllables, with consonant simplifications similar to earlier notes. No major change in complexity level or support needed.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue reinforcing accurate productions at the current level, especially for /k/ and final consonants.
- Provide caregiver with a brief home list focusing on 2–3 high-value /k/ words and 2–3 final consonant words, encouraging short, fun practice rather than drilling.
- Next sessions will remain at the same task level, aiming to strengthen consistency before introducing more complex targets.
notes/9/note_text:

Client was cheerful and cooperative throughout the session. Demonstrated good turn-taking and was willing to imitate words and phrases when prompted. Attention was sustained for longer activities than earlier in the block.

- Rehearsed phrases like "more bubbles," "more pop please," "put it in," and "put it up." Bilabial sounds remained clearly produced, and final /p/ in "up/pop" was accurate in most imitated trials. Spontaneous use of these phrases in play was similar to prior sessions, suggesting stable performance at this level.

2. Initial /k/ in high-interest words and phrases
- Targeted /k/ in "car, cookie, coat, key, cat" and associated phrases ("my car," "more cookie," "coat on"). Client produced a mix of accurate and fronted /k/ sounds, with accuracy comparable to previous notes. Continued to require verbal and visual cues to maintain back tongue position; no clear reduction in cueing.

- Practiced CVC words with final /p, m, t/ ("pop, map, mom, hat, hot, cup"). Final /p/ remained solid; /m/ and /t/ were produced inconsistently but attempted when cued. This reflects maintenance of skills with gradual consolidation rather than a distinct step up.

4. Multisyllabic functional words
- Used multisyllabic words ("banana, butterfly, open it, teddy bear, bubbles") during routines. Client continued to mark most syllables, with consonant simplifications similar to prior sessions. The level of support and overall accuracy were stable.

notes/10/note_text:
Parent report:

Behaviour/engagement:
Client appeared relaxed and quickly joined the clinician in play. Participation in tasks remained good, with the child often initiating favorite games that involve practiced words. Imitation was accepted without resistance.

Goals / observations:

- Continued work on phrases such as "more bubbles," "my big ball," "put it in," and "more cookie." Bilabial productions remained consistently clear, and final /p/ was produced accurately in many imitated and some spontaneous productions. Overall phrase clarity and independence were similar to prior sessions, indicating ongoing stabilization.

2. Initial /k/ in familiar contexts
- Practiced /k/ words and phrases ("car, cookie, coat, key," "my car," "more cookie"). Client continued to demonstrate a mix of accurate and fronted /k/ productions. Verbal and visual cues remained necessary to elicit best productions, with no notable reduction in support level compared to recent notes.

- Elicited CVC words ("pop, map, mom, hat, hot, cup") during structured play. Final /p/ remained a strength; final /m/ and /t/ were variably present, with the child showing awareness of the need for an "ending sound" but not yet consistently marking it. Performance was in line with previous sessions.

notes/11/note_text:

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was cheerful and cooperative, easily transitioning into the therapy space. Enjoyed familiar routines and actively requested favorite toys, often using short phrases. Attention to the clinician’s models remained good.

- Revisited phrases such as "more bubbles," "more pop please," "put it in," and "put it up." Bilabials continued to be produced clearly, and final /p/ in "up" and "pop" was consistently accurate in structured activities. Spontaneous phrase use was similar to previous sessions, suggesting stable maintenance at this level.

2. Initial /k/ in words and phrases
- Continued targeting /k/ in words ("car, cookie, coat, key, cat") and short phrases ("my car," "more cookie"). Client produced a combination of accurate and fronted /k/ sounds, with overall performance and cueing needs mirroring recent notes. No clear shift in hierarchy level was observed.

3. Final consonants in CVC words
- Practiced CVC words with final /p, m, t/ ("pop, map, mom, hat, hot, cup"). Final /p/ remained a strength; final /m/ and /t/ continued to be variably present but were attempted when prompted. The child responded to familiar gestural cues and benefited from slowed rate, as in prior sessions.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver reported that speech is still "hard to understand" for unfamiliar listeners. No major changes noticed since the evaluation, though caregiver feels the child is trying to talk more. Caregiver noted frequent use of "h" or "b" sounds in place of other consonants.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned to the therapy room with some hesitation but joined play activities with support. Attention was variable; needed frequent redirection from preferred toys to structured tasks. Responded well to visual supports and turn-taking games.

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /p, b, m/ at syllable level (CV)
- Practiced simple syllables ("pa, ba, ma, boo, bee, moo") in imitation. Client was most consistent with /m/ and /b/, with emerging accuracy for /p/. Several productions were breathy or omitted the consonant. Required frequent verbal models and visual cues (watching clinician’s mouth). Accuracy roughly in the low–moderate range, with best success during high-interest play.

2. Early /k/ in isolation
- Introduced back-of-the-mouth sound using tongue-back cue and animal sound play ("k-k-k" like a coughing sound). Client was able to approximate /k/ in isolation a few times with maximal verbal and tactile cues but frequently reverted to /t/ or glottal sounds. This remains a new and challenging target.

3. Simple CVCV syllable shapes
- Targeted reduplicated syllables ("mama, baba, papa") in imitation. Client could imitate some sequences when highly engaged in songs and routines. Consonants were sometimes omitted or replaced with /h/. Needed models every time; no spontaneous productions of targets noted.

4. Functional single words in play
- Encouraged production of familiar words (e.g., "more," "go," "up") within play routines. Client produced a few approximations (e.g., "uh" for "up," "mo" for "more") with modeling and gestural prompts. Speech remained unclear but attempts were frequent.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue focusing on early-developing sounds (/p, b, m/) in CV and CVCV syllables, and maintain early exposure to /k/ in isolation. Encourage caregiver to model simple sound–syllable combinations during daily routines (e.g., "ma-ma," "ba-ba") and praise any attempts. Plan to continue at the syllable and simple word level next session, with emphasis on consistent articulatory placement and increased attempts.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver shared that the child is "making more sounds" and is starting to copy some words at home, especially during songs. Still difficult for others to understand, but caregiver feels there are more vocal attempts overall.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client entered the room more easily and engaged quickly with cause-and-effect toys. Sustained attention to structured tasks was somewhat improved; responded to short, play-based drills. Enjoyed copying silly faces and sound effects.

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /p, b, m/ at CV and CVCV syllable level
- Notable improvement in imitation. Client produced /m/ and /b/ in CV syllables ("ma, me, moo, ba, boo") with relatively good consistency when given a model. /p/ remained less stable but was present in multiple clear productions. Expanded to simple CVCV words ("mama, baby, puppy") in imitation; several productions were recognizable, though some consonants were reduced or blurred. Needed intermittent verbal reminders but less continuous cueing than previous session.

2. /k/ in isolation and simple syllables
- Client was more successful eliciting a backed tongue position with mirror and visual cueing. Produced a fair number of recognizable /k/ sounds in isolation, though still mixed with /t/ at times. Introduced CV syllables ("ku, ka") in imitation; a few accurate productions noted with moderate verbal and visual cues.

3. Early CVC word approximations
- Introduced high-interest words ("pop, mom, beep") at the imitated word level. Client attempted each item; several approximations were close (e.g., clear final /p/ in "pop" in some trials). Still required direct models and reminders to "close lips" at the end.

4. Functional words in play
- Encouraged "go," "up," "more" within play. Client imitated "go" and "up" multiple times during turn-taking games. A few approximations were produced with only a gestural cue (e.g., reaching plus brief eye contact), indicating early movement toward more independent use.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue reinforcing /p, b, m/ in CV/CVCV structures and build consistency for /k/ in isolation and CV. Begin to increase practice of simple CVC words where lip closure can be emphasized. For home, encourage caregiver to pause and wait expectantly during routines (e.g., before pushing a car to cue "go") and accept approximations, modeling clear targets afterward.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver reported that the child is starting to say a few recognizable words, such as "mama" and "bye," and is copying words from favorite videos. Still notes many unclear attempts, but feels overall speech has "opened up" compared to a few weeks ago.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client separated from caregiver without difficulty and was eager to explore toys. Participated well in turn-taking and sound games. Needed occasional redirection when very excited, but overall engagement was strong and cooperative.

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /p, b, m/ at single word level
- Moved to more consistent practice at the imitated single-word level ("ball, baby, more, bubble, pop"). Client produced many clear approximations with recognizable initial consonants, particularly /b/ and /m/. /p/ improved, with more audible lip popping and fewer omissions. Required fewer direct models and sometimes maintained accuracy across several trials after one model.

2. /k/ initial in CV and word level
- Built on improved /k/ isolation from previous session. Client produced /k/ in CV syllables ("ku, ka, kee") with moderate success when watching the clinician’s mouth. Introduced simple words ("car, key, cow") in imitation. Several attempts had a clear /k/, though some reverted to /t/. Needed verbal and visual cues, but the number of accurate productions increased compared to last session.

3. Simple CVC words with bilabial consonants
- Targeted words such as "pop, mop, mom, beep, map" in imitation. Client produced recognizable CVC forms for many items, with clearer final consonants emerging. Some final consonant deletions persisted, but with cueing ("close your lips at the end") the client could mark final /p/ and /m/ in a fair portion of trials.

4. Functional early vocabulary
- During play, encouraged use of "go, up, more, open, bye". Client imitated these words readily and produced "go" and "bye" a few times spontaneously in context (e.g., when cars moved, when leaving the room). Speech remained somewhat imprecise but more intelligible to the clinician.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue at the single-word level for /p, b, m/ and expand the set of CVC words to solidify final consonant use. Maintain focused practice of /k/ initial in CV and simple words, using mirror and tactile cues as needed. At home, caregiver is encouraged to model and wait for word attempts before giving preferred items, reinforcing especially clear productions with labeled praise (e.g., "Nice 'pop'!").
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver shared that the child is now using several single words throughout the day (e.g., "ball," "up," "more"), and family members are understanding more of what is said. Caregiver also notices attempts at animal sounds and simple labels during book reading.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was enthusiastic and talkative on arrival. Quickly engaged in both structured tasks and play-based activities. Tolerated short drill segments well when they were embedded in games. Needed occasional prompts to slow down and repeat when speech became rushed.

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /p, b, m/ at single word and emerging phrase level
- At the imitated single-word level, client produced targets such as "baby, bubble, more, mommy" with generally good clarity. Accuracy for initial /b/ and /m/ was high across a variety of words, with /p/ still slightly less consistent but clearly improving. Introduced short carrier phrases ("my ball," "more bubbles") in imitation; client was able to imitate several two-word combinations while maintaining initial consonant accuracy in many attempts.

2. /k/ initial at single word level
- Continued practice with "car, key, cow, cookie" at the imitated word level. Client produced more consistent /k/ in initial position with moderate verbal and visual cues. Some /t/ substitutions remained, but there were multiple clear productions within play. Able to self-correct in a few instances when given a reminder to "use the back sound."

3. CVC words with bilabial final consonants
- Practiced words such as "pop, map, beep, mop" in imitation. Client marked final consonants more reliably than in previous sessions, though final consonant deletion and vowel prolongation still occurred at times. With a cue to "close lips" and slow rate, client could achieve accurate CVC productions in many trials.

4. Functional requests and labels in play
- Encouraged use of "go, up, more, open" and simple labels ("car, ball, bubbles"). Client produced a mix of imitated and a few spontaneous words during routines. Spontaneous productions were sometimes less precise than imitated ones but still increasingly understandable in context.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue consolidating single-word accuracy for /p, b, m, k/, with intermittent two-word phrase practice where the client is successful. Maintain emphasis on final consonants in CVC words. For home, suggest short, predictable routines (e.g., bubble play, snack time) to practice two-word combinations like "more ___" and "my ___" while keeping expectations flexible and praising all attempts.
notes/4/note_text:

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned smoothly and was eager to play with familiar toys. Participated well in structured tasks, though occasionally became silly and needed reminders to focus. Overall cooperation and effort were consistent with recent sessions.

1. Initial /p, b, m/ at word and short phrase level
- Continued work on words such as "baby, bubble, more, mommy" and simple two-word phrases ("my ball, more bubbles"). Accuracy for initial /b/ and /m/ remained strong in imitation, with /p/ still somewhat less consistent but similar to last session. Client required similar levels of verbal modeling and visual cues, with some successful carryover into short phrases.

- Practiced "car, key, cookie, cow" at the imitated word level. Performance was comparable to the previous session: client produced several accurate /k/ productions with moderate cueing, but /t/ substitutions persisted. Self-correction occurred occasionally when prompted, but no major change in cueing needs or accuracy.

notes/5/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver indicated that speech is "about the same" as last week. The child continues to use familiar words and some short phrases, but strangers still have difficulty understanding. Caregiver has been practicing words from home activities and feels the child enjoys the practice.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client arrived in a positive mood and participated readily. Tolerated brief drill segments interspersed with play. Attention was adequate; needed typical prompts to stay with the activity but no significant behavior concerns.

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /p, b, m/ at word and short phrase level
- Repeated practice with words like "baby, bubble, more, mommy" and phrases such as "my ball, more bubbles, big bubble." Accuracy remained generally strong for /b/ and /m/ at the imitated word level, with /p/ slightly less consistent but similar to prior sessions. Phrase-level productions showed some variability, with occasional sound omissions when excited. Cueing needs were comparable to last visit.

2. /k/ initial in words
- Practiced "car, key, cookie, cow" in imitation and simple play contexts. Client continued to produce recognizable /k/ in many attempts when given verbal and visual cues. /t/ substitutions and reduced back placement were still present but did not appear significantly different from recent sessions.

3. CVC words with final bilabial consonants
- Focused on "pop, map, beep, mop" and similar items. Final consonant marking remained emerging but not yet consistent. With explicit cues to "close lips" and slow rate, the client was able to produce several accurate CVC words in each set. Overall, performance resembled previous sessions, suggesting ongoing consolidation of this skill.

4. Functional communication in play
- Continued to encourage "go, up, more, open" and object labels. Client used a combination of imitated and spontaneous words; intelligibility was similar to prior sessions. Some approximations were clearer in familiar routines.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue with the current targets to support consolidation of skills, particularly final consonants and consistent initial /p/ and /k/. No shift in task level is recommended yet. Caregiver advised to keep using predictable routines (e.g., snack, bubbles, cars) to model and elicit target words, and to emphasize clear endings on words like "pop" and "map."
notes/6/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver reported that over the past week, the child has been using more short phrases spontaneously (e.g., "more bubbles," "my car") and seems a bit easier to understand at home. Family members are noticing clearer words, especially for familiar items.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client entered eagerly and initiated interaction with the clinician. Demonstrated good participation in both structured tasks and play. Tolerated slightly longer practice sets today and responded well to praise and visual feedback.

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /p, b, m/ at phrase and early sentence level
- Built on strong word-level performance by practicing two- to three-word combinations ("my baby, more bubbles, big ball, pop it"). Client maintained good accuracy for /b/ and /m/ in many phrases with only light verbal cues. /p/ remained somewhat variable but showed improved clarity in contexts like "pop it" and "push it." Fewer direct models were required, and the client occasionally produced short phrases independently during play.

2. /k/ initial in words and short phrases
- Continued with "car, key, cookie, cow" and expanded to simple phrases ("my car, big cookie"). Client demonstrated more consistent /k/ productions, especially when given an initial reminder to "use the back sound." Some /t/ substitutions persisted but were less frequent. Able to maintain /k/ in a few short phrases following a single model.

3. CVC words with final bilabial consonants
- Targeted "pop, map, beep, mop, nap" in imitation and simple carrier phrases ("my pop, big map"). Final consonant marking was more reliable with moderate cueing, and client produced several accurate CVC words without needing repeated prompts. Some deletions were still observed, but overall clarity improved compared to previous sessions.

4. Functional communication in structured play
- During play routines (cars, bubbles, snack), client used a mix of imitated and spontaneous two- to three-word combinations. Intelligibility within context was stronger, and the child often initiated with words rather than gestures alone.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue to strengthen phrase-level accuracy for /p, b, m, k/ and final consonants. Begin incorporating simple, repetitive carrier phrases (e.g., "I see ___," "my ___") as tolerated. At home, caregiver is encouraged to model and expand the child’s phrases (e.g., if child says "my car," caregiver can model "my big car") while maintaining clear articulation.
notes/7/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver shared that the child is "talking a lot" and putting more words together, though some words are still hard to understand. Caregiver notices that certain sounds like "k" are clearer in some words (e.g., "car") than before.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was energetic and social, initiating conversation and play themes. Required a few prompts to slow down and listen to directions but was generally cooperative. Enjoyed using phrases during pretend play.

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /p, b, m/ at short phrase level and beginning simple sentences
- Practiced two- to three-word phrases and early sentences such as "my big ball," "more bubbles please," and "I pop it." Client produced many phrases with accurate /b/ and /m/ sounds and fairly consistent /p/ when cued to "make the popping sound." Needed some verbal reminders but overall maintained sound accuracy across multiple phrase repetitions.

2. /k/ initial in phrases and short sentences
- Targeted /k/ words ("car, key, cookie, cat") within simple phrases ("my car, big cookie, I see cat"). Client showed gradually improving consistency for /k/, with fewer /t/ substitutions than in earlier sessions. Still required visual and verbal cues at the start of activities, but once cued, could produce several accurate /k/ words in a row. Phrase-level productions were clearer, though not yet fully independent.

3. CVC words with final bilabial consonants in phrases
- Practiced words like "pop, map, nap, beep" in short phrases ("my pop, nap time"). Client marked final consonants in many attempts when reminded to "close lips" at the end. Some deletions still occurred, especially when speaking quickly, but overall production remained similar to last session with steady practice rather than a large change.

4. Functional communication and play-based language
- Encouraged the child to use phrases to request, comment, and protest (e.g., "more bubbles," "no, my car"). Client frequently initiated two- and three-word phrases independently. Speech clarity was improved in familiar contexts, but some sounds and endings remained less precise.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue practice at the phrase and simple sentence level for /p, b, m, k/ and final consonants, emphasizing slower rate and clear endings. Home practice can include simple sentence frames such as "I see ___" or "I want ___" during daily routines. Caregiver encouraged to gently model clearer /k/ and final consonants without over-correcting.
notes/8/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver reported that speech this week was similar to last week. The child continues to use many short phrases and sentences in daily routines. Some words are still unclear, especially in longer utterances, but no major changes were noted.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was cheerful and cooperative. Participated in structured tasks and pretend play with typical levels of support. Needed occasional reminders to slow down and take turns but remained engaged throughout the session.

Goals / session focus:
1. /p, b, m/ in phrases and short sentences
- Continued to target phrases such as "my big ball," "more bubbles please," and "I pop it." Client maintained generally good accuracy for /b/ and /m/ at this level. /p/ productions were acceptable in many contexts, with some omissions or weak productions when excited. Cueing requirements and overall accuracy were similar to previous session.

2. /k/ initial in phrases and short sentences
- Practiced /k/ words in phrases ("my car, big cookie, I see cat"). Client produced /k/ correctly in many imitated phrases when cued to "use the back sound" at the start of practice. Occasional /t/ substitutions persisted. No substantial change in independence or hierarchy level from last visit, though performance remained solid.

3. CVC words with final bilabial consonants in connected speech
- Used words like "pop, nap, map" within short phrases and play-based sentences ("I pop it, nap time"). Final consonant marking was present in a fair number of attempts with verbal reminders. Some deletions continued, especially in longer utterances or when the child was focused on the message rather than form.

4. Functional communication in play and routines
- Encouraged a range of phrases for requesting and commenting. Client used many spontaneous two- to three-word phrases, with intelligibility aided by context. Error patterns were consistent with prior sessions.

Plan / recommendations:
Maintain current focus on phrase- and sentence-level production for /p, b, m, k/ and final consonants, aiming for more consistent clarity rather than advancing the hierarchy. Caregiver encouraged to continue modeling slower, clear speech and to highlight key words by slightly emphasizing initial and final sounds during daily interactions.
notes/9/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver indicated that speech remains fairly steady. The child is talking a lot and using many short sentences, but some words, particularly with "k" and final sounds, are still not always clear. No new concerns or changes at home.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned easily and chose preferred toys. Participated in structured tasks with usual levels of cooperation. Attention and motivation were similar to recent sessions, with occasional silliness but overall good engagement.

Goals / session focus:
1. /p, b, m/ in short sentences
- Continued work on 3–4 word sentences (e.g., "I pop the bubble," "my big ball is red"). Client generally maintained accurate /b/ and /m/ across sentences with verbal modeling. /p/ remained somewhat variable but was acceptable in many contexts, especially when rate was slowed. Performance appeared consistent with prior sessions.

2. /k/ initial in short sentences
- Practiced sentences such as "I see the car," "my cookie is big," and "I see a cat." Client produced /k/ correctly in many imitated sentences when provided with a brief cue at the start of the activity. /t/ substitutions occurred intermittently. No clear reduction in cueing or major change in hierarchy level was observed this session.

3. CVC words with final bilabial consonants in sentences
- Embedded target words like "pop, nap, map" into simple sentences ("I pop it," "I nap here"). Client marked final consonants with mixed consistency, similar to recent sessions. With prompts to "finish the word" or "close your lips," accuracy improved on repeated trials.

4. Functional communication and intelligibility
- During free play, client produced a variety of spontaneous phrases and short sentences. Intelligibility to the clinician was moderate; context supported understanding. Error patterns (particularly with final consonants and some /k/ productions) were similar to previous visits.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue reinforcing clear productions in short sentences, focusing on /k/ initial and final bilabial consonants. Maintain current hierarchy and gradually increase variety of sentence frames as tolerated. At home, caregiver can practice target words and sentences during predictable activities (e.g., snack, bedtime routines) and provide gentle reminders to "finish the word" for final sounds.
notes/10/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver reported that the child’s speech this week seemed similar to last session. The child continues to use many short sentences and is generally understood by family, though some words with "k" and word endings remain unclear for less familiar listeners.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was cooperative and playful. Demonstrated consistent willingness to participate in both structured and play-based tasks. Needed typical prompts to slow rate of speech and listen to directions.

Goals / session focus:
1. /p, b, m/ in short sentences and connected speech
- Targeted 3–4 word sentences incorporating /p, b, m/ (e.g., "I pop the bubble," "my baby is sleeping"). Client showed stable accuracy for /b/ and /m/ in these contexts. /p/ remained somewhat variable but generally recognizable. Cueing and performance levels were comparable to previous session.

2. /k/ initial in short sentences
- Continued practice with sentences such as "I see the car," "my cookie is yummy," and "I see a cat." Client produced many correct /k/ sounds when given initial verbal and visual cues. Occasional /t/ substitutions persisted, especially in faster speech. No marked change in independence or accuracy from last week.

3. CVC words with final bilabial consonants in sentences
- Embedded words like "pop, nap, map" in simple sentences. Client continued to mark final consonants in some attempts and omit them in others, particularly when excited or talking quickly. Reminders to "finish the word" improved accuracy on prompted trials.

4. Functional communication
- In play, client used a range of spontaneous phrases and short sentences to request, comment, and protest. Intelligibility to the clinician remained moderate and consistent with recent sessions, with context supporting understanding.

Plan / recommendations:
Maintain current therapy focus on improving consistency of /k/ and final consonant production within sentences. Continue to emphasize slower speech and clear word endings. Caregiver encouraged to keep practicing target words and sentences at home and to reinforce when the child produces especially clear words (e.g., specific praise such as "I heard your /k/ in 'car'!").
notes/11/note_text:
Parent report:
Caregiver noted that speech continues along the same pattern as in recent weeks. The child talks frequently and uses short sentences, with family understanding most of the time. Some words with "k" and final sounds are still harder for unfamiliar listeners.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client arrived in good spirits and engaged easily with therapy materials. Demonstrated consistent participation in structured and play-based tasks, with attention and cooperation similar to prior sessions.

Goals / session focus:
1. /p, b, m/ in short sentences
- Practiced 3–4 word sentences such as "I pop the bubble," "my big ball rolls," and "baby is sleeping." Client maintained good accuracy for /b/ and /m/ across multiple trials. /p/ productions were adequate in many contexts, with occasional weak or omitted consonants when speaking quickly. Support needs and performance levels were in line with previous sessions.

2. /k/ initial in short sentences
- Continued sentence-level work with /k/ words ("car, cookie, cat") in frames such as "I see the car," "my cookie is big." Client produced /k/ accurately in several sentences with verbal and visual cues at the beginning of the activity. Intermittent /t/ substitutions and reduced back placement persisted, particularly in more spontaneous utterances. Overall performance appeared stable rather than showing a major change.

3. CVC words with final bilabial consonants in sentences
- Embedded target words ("pop, nap, map, beep") within simple sentences ("I pop it," "I nap here"). Client continued to mark final consonants inconsistently, improving with reminders to "finish the word" or "close your lips." Accuracy and cueing needs were similar to the last few sessions.

4. Functional communication and generalization
- During open play, client used many spontaneous short sentences to request and comment. Intelligibility within familiar contexts remained moderate to good, though some sound errors and final consonant omissions reduced clarity for less familiar listeners.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue working at the sentence level on /p, b, m, k/ and final bilabial consonants, with emphasis on consistency and generalization into more spontaneous speech. Caregiver encouraged to keep modeling clear productions and to provide occasional, gentle reminders to slow down and "finish" words during daily routines.

notes/0/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent shared that they continue to notice that familiar listeners understand most of what the child says, but new people still have difficulty. No big changes since the initial visit. Parent reported that they hear substitutions like /t/ for /k/ (e.g., “tat” for “cat”) and that /s/ blends are usually reduced (e.g., “top” for “stop”).

Behaviour/engagement:
Child transitioned into the therapy room with brief hesitation, then engaged well with toys and books. Attended to adult-directed tasks for short periods with visual supports and frequent breaks. Responded well to turn-taking games and was generally cheerful.

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /k/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Practiced /k/ in isolation with mirror and “back sound” cue. Child was able to imitate a clear /k/ intermittently with maximal verbal and visual cueing. Accuracy was low and inconsistent.
- At CV level (“koo, kay, kee”), productions were mostly fronted to /t/, even with modeling. Occasional accurate productions when the child watched the clinician’s mouth closely.

2. Initial /f/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Introduced “lip bite, gentle air” cues for /f/ in isolation. Child could imitate a recognizable /f/ sound in some attempts with intensive verbal and gestural support.
- At CV level (“fee, foo”), productions frequently shifted to /p/ or omitted the fricative portion. High level of modeling and repetition required.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation
- Attempted /s/ in isolation using snake imagery and mirror. Child produced mostly distorted /s/ (slushy or voiced) with occasional approximations when cued to keep tongue behind teeth.
- Child tolerated some tactile cueing to help with lip and jaw stability.

4. Syllable shapes (CV, CVCV) in play
- Observed spontaneous productions of CV and CVCV syllables during play (e.g., “mama,” “baba,” “nana”). Some CVC forms were reduced (final consonant often omitted).

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to build consistent production of /k/, /f/, and /s/ at the isolation and simple syllable level with high support.
- Encourage parent to model “back sound” /k/ in daily routines (e.g., “car, cookie”), emphasizing slow, clear productions without pressure for the child to imitate.
- Next sessions will continue at the same level, focusing on establishing accurate productions before moving to word-level practice.
notes/1/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent reported that the child has started to imitate a few of the practice words from last week (e.g., something closer to “car” and “key”), though still often says “tar/tee.” Parent noted that the child enjoys practicing “snake sounds” but they are not yet clear in everyday speech.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child entered the room more easily today and appeared excited to see familiar toys. Maintained attention to structured tasks for slightly longer stretches than last session with minimal redirection. Accepted cues and modeling without frustration.

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /k/ – isolation, CV, and early word level
- Child showed a noticeable jump in accuracy for /k/ in isolation with visual cues and “back of the tongue” prompts. Able to produce a clear /k/ multiple times in a row when imitating.
- At CV level (“koo, kah, kee”), child produced more consistent /k/ at the start of the syllable, with occasional fronting to /t/ when excited.
- Introduced a few highly familiar words at the imitated word level (e.g., “car, key, cow”). Child could imitate a recognizable /k/ in these words in many attempts when given a slow model and mirror.

2. Initial /f/ – isolation and CV syllables
- Child was more responsive to the “lip bite + air” visual cue. Produced clearer /f/ in isolation across several trials with moderate verbal support.
- At CV level (“fee, foo, faa”), approximations were more consistent, though some attempts were still stopped to /p/. Parent commented that the /f/ sound was easier for them to hear today.

3. Initial /s/ – isolation and simple syllables
- Continued practice of /s/ in isolation with snake visuals. Child could hold a quieter, more controlled airflow briefly with modeling. Distortions were still frequent, but a few productions were close to a clear /s/.
- Combined /s/ with simple vowels (e.g., “see, soo”) at the imitation level. Productions were often slushy or partially voiced; however, the child maintained tongue position behind teeth more consistently with reminders.

4. Syllable shapes – CVCV and early CVC
- During play, child produced several CVCV words (e.g., “nana, baba”) clearly. Some CVC attempts (e.g., “cup, cat”) continued to show final consonant deletion.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to strengthen /k/ and /f/ productions at the imitated word level, maintaining isolation/CV practice as needed.
- Maintain focus on improving /s/ clarity in isolation and CV, with ongoing use of mirror and visual supports.
- Parent to practice a small set of /k/ and /f/ words at home (e.g., “car, key, cow,” “feet, food”), using slow, exaggerated models and praising any approximations.
- If gains with /k/ and /f/ remain stable, consider expanding to a wider word list in upcoming sessions.
notes/2/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent reported that the child is now sometimes correcting themselves from “tar” to a closer “car” when reminded. They also notice a few clearer /f/ words like “fish” and “food” at home, mainly after they model them first. No major changes reported for /s/ or blends in everyday speech.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child separated smoothly from caregiver and joined therapy activities readily. Demonstrated good participation in turn-taking games and tolerated structured drill tasks for short sets before needing movement breaks. Cooperative and playful throughout.

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /k/ – imitated word level
- Practiced a set of initial /k/ words (e.g., “car, key, cow, cup, cat”) in imitation. Child produced recognizable /k/ in many attempts with moderate verbal cueing (“back sound”) and visual models.
- Fewer substitutions to /t/ compared to previous session. When errors occurred, the child was usually able to correct with a reminder.

2. Initial /f/ – imitated word level
- Focused on initial /f/ words (e.g., “fish, feet, fun, four”). Child imitated a fairly clear /f/ in a majority of trials, often with just a brief cue to bite lip and blow.
- Still occasional substitutions to /p/ when speaking quickly, but child responded well to slowing down and watching the clinician’s mouth.

3. Initial /s/ – isolation and CV/word attempts
- Continued work on /s/ in isolation; the child is beginning to produce a quieter, more forward airflow more consistently with modeling.
- Introduced a small set of imitated /s/ words (“sun, soup, sock”). Productions remained inconsistent and often distorted or substituted with /d/ or /sh/. Needed substantial cueing for tongue placement and airflow.

4. Final consonants – awareness in play
- During play, clinician highlighted final sounds in words like “cup, boat, hat.” Child attempted to imitate these but often omitted the final consonant or added a neutral vowel (e.g., “cup-uh”).

Plan/recommendations:
- Maintain focus on /k/ and /f/ at the imitated word level, aiming for stronger consistency before moving to phrases.
- Continue shaping /s/ in isolation and simple words, with emphasis on controlled airflow.
- Parent encouraged to pick 3–4 practice words each for /k/ and /f/ and use them during daily routines (e.g., bath time, mealtimes), offering gentle reminders for “back sound” and “lip bite + air.”
- Consider beginning very simple carrier phrases with /k/ or /f/ words once accuracy is more stable.
notes/3/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent shared that the child is using clearer /k/ and /f/ words more frequently without being asked, such as “car,” “cookie,” and “fish.” They feel family members are understanding the child a bit more easily in these contexts. Parent has not noticed much change yet with /s/ sounds or clusters.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child arrived in good spirits and transitioned into the therapy room independently. Showed strong engagement with picture cards and game-based tasks. Needed occasional redirection when excited but responded to visual schedules and brief movement breaks.

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /k/ – word and early carrier phrase level
- Child produced initial /k/ words (e.g., “car, key, cow, cookie, cat”) with fairly good consistency in imitation, needing only light verbal reminders.
- Introduced simple carrier phrases such as “my ___” and “go ___” (e.g., “my car,” “go car”). Child was able to imitate several short phrases with recognizable /k/ in the target word.
- A few spontaneous phrases with /k/ were noted in structured play (e.g., “my car,” “go car”) with minimal prompting.

2. Initial /f/ – word and early carrier phrase level
- Practiced initial /f/ words (“fish, feet, fun, food, four”) at the imitated word level. Child was mostly accurate, occasionally needing a quick cue to bite the lip.
- Expanded to short phrases (e.g., “my fish,” “big feet”) in imitation. Child could maintain the /f/ sound in many attempts when the phrase was kept slow and supported.

3. Initial /s/ – isolation and imitated word level
- Continued to refine /s/ in isolation. Child produced several clearer /s/ sounds when cued to smile and keep tongue behind teeth.
- At the word level (e.g., “sun, soup, sock”), accuracy remained variable. Distortions and substitutions with /d/ were still common, but a few clearer productions emerged when the child watched the clinician’s mouth closely.

4. Final consonant awareness – CVC words
- Used picture cards to highlight final sounds in simple CVC words (e.g., “cup, hat, boat”). Child attempted to imitate the full word structure, but final consonant deletion persisted frequently.
- Some success when clinician tapped out the sounds or exaggerated the final consonant.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue advancing /k/ and /f/ in short carrier phrases and structured play, while monitoring for spontaneous use.
- Maintain targeted practice on /s/ in isolation and simple words, focusing on consistency rather than complexity at this time.
- Parent encouraged to model short phrases with /k/ and /f/ words at home (e.g., “my car,” “big fish”) and praise any attempts.
- Next sessions will continue at similar levels, with gradual increases in phrase complexity as tolerated.
notes/4/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent reported steady use of clearer /k/ and /f/ words in daily routines (e.g., “car,” “cup,” “fish,” “feet”). They noted that the child will sometimes correct themselves if reminded gently. Parent has not yet noticed a clear /s/ in conversation, though they hear some attempts when practicing.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child was cooperative and eager to show favorite toys. Participated in structured tasks with good effort, needing only occasional prompts to stay on task. Enjoyed turn-taking games and responded well to specific praise.

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /k/ – carrier phrases and structured play
- Focused on using /k/ words within short carrier phrases (e.g., “my car,” “I see a cat,” “go car”). Child imitated these phrases with good success and required minimal cueing for the /k/ sound.
- In structured play with cars and animals, the child independently used several /k/ words and short phrases (e.g., “car go,” “my car”) without direct prompting.

2. Initial /f/ – carrier phrases and early sentences
- Practiced /f/ words in simple phrases such as “big fish,” “my feet,” and “find food.” Child’s /f/ productions were mostly accurate in imitation, with occasional reminders for lip placement.
- Child produced some short, more spontaneous phrases containing /f/ (e.g., “my fish,” “funny fish”) during play with minimal supports.

3. Initial /s/ – imitated word level
- Continued work on /s/ words (“sun, soup, sock, sand”). Child produced a mix of clearer /s/ approximations and distorted productions. Still needed frequent cueing for airflow and tongue position.
- Used mirror and “quiet snake” imagery; child was able to briefly sustain a more accurate /s/ in isolation, though this did not consistently carry over into words.

4. Final consonants – CVC words in imitation
- Practiced CVC words (e.g., “cup, hat, boat, bed”) in imitation. Child showed occasional marking of final consonants, especially when the clinician tapped out the final sound.
- Final consonant deletion remained common, but there were a few clearer productions when attention was specifically drawn to the last sound.

Plan/recommendations:
- Maintain current level for /k/ and /f/ in phrases and short sentences, aiming for greater spontaneous use across different activities.
- Continue targeting /s/ at the imitated word level, reinforcing successful approximations and using visual/tactile cues.
- Parent to keep modeling short phrases with /k/ and /f/ and to highlight final sounds gently in everyday words (e.g., “cup,” “bed”) without pressuring the child.
- Next sessions will continue similar goals, monitoring for readiness to expand /s/ practice if consistency improves.
notes/5/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent shared that the child’s “car” and “fish” words remain quite clear at home. They feel overall intelligibility is slowly improving for familiar listeners, but strangers still have difficulty. Parent has not noticed many new sounds this week but feels the child is more willing to try.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child transitioned smoothly and quickly engaged with table-top activities. Showed good participation in drill-style tasks when paired with games and stickers. Needed occasional prompts to slow down speech when excited.

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /k/ – phrases and short sentences
- Continued practice using /k/ words in short phrases and emerging sentences (e.g., “I see a cat,” “the car goes,” “my cookie here”). Child maintained fairly consistent /k/ productions in imitation with minimal cues.
- Several spontaneous uses of /k/ words in play were observed (e.g., “my car,” “more cookie”). Accuracy was generally similar to last session.

2. Initial /f/ – phrases and short sentences
- Practiced /f/ in phrases/sentences such as “big fish swim,” “wash my feet,” “find food here.” Child imitated these with mostly accurate /f/ sounds.
- Spontaneous use of /f/ in play (e.g., “funny fish,” “fall down”) was noted but comparable to last session; occasional substitutions to /p/ persisted when speaking rapidly.

3. Initial /s/ – imitated word level and limited phrases
- Repeated work on initial /s/ words (“sun, soup, sock, sand, sit”). Productions remained mixed, with some clearer approximations but frequent distortions and substitutions.
- Briefly attempted simple phrases (e.g., “sun up,” “see soup”) in imitation. Child could sometimes carry over a closer /s/ into the phrase when heavily cued, but this was not consistent.

4. Final consonants – CVC words
- Continued practice with CVC words (e.g., “cup, hat, boat, bed”). Final consonant deletion was still common, though a few accurate final productions were observed when the clinician used tapping or slowed speech significantly.

Plan/recommendations:
- Maintain current targets and levels; focus on consolidating /k/ and /f/ in phrases/sentences and slowly increasing opportunities for spontaneous use.
- Continue intensive support for /s/ at the word level; hold on advancing /s/ hierarchy until productions are more stable.
- Parent encouraged to keep home practice short and positive, emphasizing a few familiar /k/ and /f/ phrases and gently modeling /s/ words like “sun” and “sock.”
- Next sessions will revisit these same goals, monitoring for gradual changes in consistency.
notes/6/note_text:
Parent report:
Parent reported that the child continues to use clearer /k/ and /f/ words throughout the day, particularly when talking about cars and food. No major new words or sounds were noted this week. Parent feels speech is “about the same” compared to last session.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child entered willingly and participated in both floor play and table-top work. Attention to tasks was adequate with intermittent prompts. The child responded well to visual timers and frequent praise.

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /k/ – phrases and play-based contexts
- Practiced /k/ words in short phrases during a car race game (e.g., “go car,” “my car fast”). Child’s /k/ productions remained generally accurate with light cueing, similar to prior sessions.
- Spontaneous use of /k/ words occurred during play (“car go,” “more cookie”), with accuracy comparable to previous note.

2. Initial /f/ – phrases and short sentences
- Used picture scenes to elicit phrases such as “funny fish,” “wash feet,” “find food.” Child imitated these with mostly correct /f/ sounds, requiring occasional reminders for lip placement.
- Spontaneous use of /f/ words (“fall,” “funny,” “food”) was observed and remained similar in frequency and accuracy to last week.

3. Initial /s/ – imitated word level
- Continued targeted practice on /s/ words (“sun, soup, sit, sock, soap”). Productions remained inconsistent, with some improved approximations but also many distorted or substituted sounds.
- Child continued to require substantial verbal and visual cues to achieve clearer /s/, and these gains did not yet generalize to spontaneous speech.

4. Final consonants – CVC words

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue at current levels for all targets; focus remains on steady practice rather than hierarchy changes at this time.
- Parent encouraged to keep reinforcing /k/ and /f/ in natural play and to gently highlight final sounds (e.g., “cup,” “bed”) using slow, clear models.

- Next sessions will revisit the same word lists and phrase structures, looking for gradual improvement in consistency.
